title: When the Joke Writes Itself: Firecracker, Homelander, and the Satire That Got Lapped by the News
seoTitle: When the Joke Writes Itself: Firecracker, Homelander, and the Satire That Got Lapped by the News
excerpt: What do you do when reality writes the punchlines, and the company making the satire is friends with the man it’s supposed to mock?
metaDescription: What do you do when reality writes the punchlines, and the company making the satire is friends with the man it’s supposed to mock?
bodyHtml:
On April 13, 2026, Donald Trump posted an AI-generated image of himself as Jesus to Truth Social. White robe. Hand on a sick man. Light is coming out of his palm. American flag and military planes in the background.

Marjorie Taylor Greene called it blasphemous.

Marjorie Taylor Greene, who once blamed wildfires on Jewish space lasers, broke with Trump because the AI Jesus post went too far. Trump deleted it and told reporters it was supposed to be him as a doctor. Days later, he posted another AI image. This time, Jesus is hugging him. Both have their eyes closed.

I watched all of this happen in real time and wrote a Substack article about it.

Two weeks later, I watched Firecracker denounce her pastor on national television and praise Homelander, promoting the Democratic Church of America. One is real. One is on Amazon Prime. I’m not sure which is which anymore.

Subscribe now

The scene that anchors the season

The Boys aired episode five of its final season on Wednesday. Firecracker, played by Valorie Curry, sits at her Truthbomb desk and reads her producer’s notes about her own pastor. Reverend Greg Dupree took her in when her parents died, taught her scripture, and gave her a place to sleep. In the same episode, he begs for more time to help people. She accuses him on air of harboring anti-Homelander terrorists and calls him an abuser for having her “alone” for dinner. She names her old church, Daytona Holy Baptist. She paints him as a threat.

The take cuts. One tear rolls down her cheek.

She goes back to her old apartment. She takes out the Jesus action figure she’s carried since childhood and throws it in the trash.

Then Homelander kills her. Not with a laser. He drives her head into the spike of a marble eagle statue, the symbol of America and of Homelander. Her mistake was calling him a man, not a god.

Eric Kripke told The Hollywood Reporter the scene was modeled on Marjorie Taylor Greene’s relationship with Trump. The woman who gives up everything she once believed to serve a man who openly does not believe in any of it. Kripke told TVLine the same week that the pattern is older than the show. “It’s the most predictable pattern in the world, which is Trump demanding ultimate allegiance, making someone compromise every value they’ve ever had, and then kicking them out into the cold.” He said the writers locked in Firecracker’s death the moment they introduced her in Season 4. They wrote it before Trump rescinded his endorsement of Greene in November 2024. The episode aired six months after he did.

Curry herself told Gold Derby she’s been “shocked” watching her own episodes air against the news cycle.

That’s the show. That’s the news. They’re running side by side now.

Curry was the shining star

Curry showed up in Season 4 and played a character who is every raver, religious fanatic, and MAGA on Fox rolled into one. Firecracker is a composite. She has Marjorie Taylor Greene’s grievance, Lauren Boebert’s evangelical style, Alex Jones’s paranoid laugh, and the cadence of every preacher who ever sold a prayer cloth for ninety-nine dollars. If any of those notes rang false, the character would collapse.

Watch the Truthbomb scene where she turns on her pastor. She doesn’t play a villain. She plays a woman who knows she’s wrong and does it anyway because she’s been part of the machine too long. The mask slips for a second, but she’s already past the point of stopping.

Kripke said it himself. “That was some of the best fucking acting this show has ever seen. You could see it killing her own soul.” He said Curry brought you inside her heartbreak. The script gave her a beat. She turned the beat into a full betrayal of any moral character she had gotten through the church, all for Homelander.

Curry told the press this was both her favorite scene and her hardest. She called it soul-crushing.

You can see it. The character spent two seasons selling certainty. In the end, there’s none left. She doesn’t believe Homelander is a god. She knows she gave up her church for him, her real pastor for a fake one, her faith for a man who thinks he can punish anyone who disagrees.

She knew it was wrong. She did it anyway. The man she did it for killed her.

The Vought megachurch and the real one

Vought has run a church on the show for two seasons. First, it was Samaritan’s Embrace Ministries; now known as The Democratic Church of America. They co-opt the evangelical movement and fold it into the corporate machine. Homelander becomes their messiah. The Seven becomes their pantheon. Christian theology gets stripped out and replaced with brand loyalty.

Watch any Trump rally from the last year. Tell me that’s not a documentary.

Trump posts AI images of himself as Jesus. Pastors at megachurches frame political opposition as spiritual warfare. Charlie Kirk’s Turning Point USA built a 2,500-campus operation on the idea that Christians are an oppressed minority who need to retake American institutions. The president threatens war with Venezuela, the same week he posts a meme of himself replacing the Pope.

The Boys built a fictional megachurch run by a corporation that uses Christianity as a marketing layer over fascist ambitions. Then Trump portrayed himself as Jesus on Easter and dared evangelicals to call it heresy. Most didn’t. The few who did, like Marjorie Taylor Greene, are now being attacked by their own movement for showing a shred of consistency or moral clarity.

Firecracker giving up Daytona Holy Baptist for Vought’s Church of America is the show’s clearest thesis. She wasn’t manipulated or tricked. She left a real church for a fake one because the fake one had bigger numbers and a bigger man at its center.

That’s the trade. Real faith for proximity to power.

When satire stops working

Satire works by exaggeration. You take something real and push it until the flaws become visible. Trump has a god complex. So you make a show where he believes he’s God. The audience sees the gap between the joke and the truth. That’s where the work begins.

Stephen Colbert played a fake conservative pundit on Comedy Central from 2005 to 2014. The bit was that he was three notches more ridiculous than the real ones. In 2006, he stood ten feet from George W. Bush at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner and roasted him about Iraq and the NSA. The clips went everywhere. The joke worked because the gap between Colbert’s character and a real Fox News host was visible.

By 2014, the gap had closed. Glenn Beck was on TV crying about FEMA camps. Alex Jones was selling brain pills. Colbert dropped the character and went to CBS. He hosted The Late Show for a decade. In summer 2025, CBS canceled it. The official reason was financial. The real reason, according to senators at the time, was that Paramount needed Trump’s FCC to approve its merger with Skydance, and Colbert had spent two weeks calling the company’s $16 million Trump settlement a bribe. He told the truth on Monday. He got fired on Thursday.

When Eric Kripke pitched The Boys in 2019, the premise was that superheroes could critique corporate fascism, celebrity worship, and the merger of Christianity with right-wing politics. He wrote Season 5 before the 2024 election, expecting the country to dodge the bullet. Instead, we got hit with it.

By 2024, the show was already struggling to keep up with the news. By 2026, it’s struggling to stay even. Kripke said the writers would come up with outlandish ideas they thought would never happen, and then watch them happen. Kristi Noem shoots her dog. Trump portrays himself as Jesus. The loyalty-then-banishment pattern they wrote for Firecracker played out in real time with Greene six months before the episode aired.

Exaggeration needs a baseline of normal. You have to start somewhere. When the president portrays himself as Jesus, and only his most extreme allies object, where do you exaggerate from? The show keeps trying to find the next ten yards. The country keeps closing the gap. Maybe it’s good that this is the last season.

The streamer problem

Here’s the part that makes me sick.

The Boys streams on Amazon Prime. Amazon donated one million dollars to Trump’s inauguration fund and another million in-kind by streaming the inauguration on Prime Video. Jeff Bezos killed the Washington Post’s planned endorsement of Kamala Harris in October 2024. He flew to Mar-a-Lago to dine with Trump. He privately recommended Doug Burgum for vice president on a phone call last summer.

The sharpest anti-fascist satire on TV is produced and streamed by a company whose owner is helping the fascist project succeed.

I wrote about this two months ago in Who Controls What You Know. Trump and Thiel have built an information system where surveillance, distribution, and access all run through companies owned by Trump donors. Palantir runs the database. Skydance owns Paramount. X is the president’s communication channel. Public broadcasting is dead. Bari Weiss is editor-in-chief at CBS News, and her funders are Trump’s biggest VC backers.

The Boys is on Prime Video because Bezos benefits from both sides. The show is good business. Season 4 drew over 54 million viewers in its first weekend, making it the second-biggest debut in Prime Video history. The audience is too large to lose, so Amazon streams the satire while Bezos supports the man it’s about.

The show can be as bold as it wants. Kripke can dress Homelander like Trump and have him declare himself a god. Firecracker can represent every alt-right influencer with a microphone. None of this poses a real threat. It brings in subscriptions, earns Emmy nominations, and plays in the background while the company builds the surveillance state.

It’s the same pattern I described in The Asterisk People. A double standard in Hollywood, profitable or problematic. The show works the same way. Anti-Trump content is profitable. Bezos keeps the profits, Trump keeps the donor, and the only people who lose are those who thought satire could change people’s minds.

Kripke is doing his job, and the cast is doing theirs. The show is at its best. But it doesn’t matter because the platform owner has already accepted the world the show warns us about.

What’s left

Satire is still alive and well. That’s not the issue. The real question is: what happens when the people funding satire are friends with its targets?

The old answer was that good satire reached enough people to change the conversation. Saturday Night Live in 1976 was tough on Gerald Ford. The Daily Show in 2003 criticized the Iraq War. The Colbert Report in 2010 targeted Citizens United. You could see the impact. The jokes changed what people felt comfortable saying in public.

That feedback loop is broken now. The Boys is a popular, long-running anti-fascist show on a platform owned by someone who helped fund the most fascist administration in recent American history. The same people create both the cultural product and the political project. The satire is no longer in opposition.

I keep thinking about that final shot of Firecracker. The Jesus action figure is in the trash. There’s a single tear before the camera cuts. The pastor she betrayed begged her for help on the phone, but she said she couldn’t help. She died the same day at the hands of the man she did it all for.

I watched The Boys this week and laughed at the absurd idea of a man who thinks he’s a god and threatens anyone who disagrees. I’m not sure I’m supposed to laugh. I’m not sure Kripke is, either.

The chocolate ration used to be thirty grams. Now they tell you it’s twenty, and you’re supposed to say thank you.

Now they’re streaming a show about it. It’s the same company that wrote the check. The same audience is watching both, and the same country is pretending there’s no contradiction.

That’s the part the show can’t fix.

That’s the part nobody can write their way out of.

Share

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

title: Red or blue: what a button tells you about your politics
seoTitle: Red or blue: what a button tells you about your politics
excerpt: The thought experiment that has the internet arguing about who deserves to live
metaDescription: The thought experiment that has the internet arguing about who deserves to live
bodyHtml:
Tim Urban posted a question on X. The comments are a war zone.

Everyone on earth has to vote. Red or blue. If more than half press blue, everyone lives. If less than half press blue, only the red voters survive. The blue voters die.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Which one do you press?

Stop for a second. Pick one. Before you keep reading, decide. The answer matters less than what your reasoning says about you.

Let’s start with the math, because the math is the trap. This isn’t really about buttons. It’s about what kind of country you want to live in. And whether you already live in a place where half the people would let the other half die to feel safe.

The math says press red.

Game theory eats this up. The logic is simple.

If you press red, you live. No matter what. If the world goes blue, you live. If the world goes red, you live. Your column never ends in death. You only live in one scenario. The world has to come together. More than half of strangers, in 195 countries, in languages you do not speak, have to make the same choice you did. If they do not, you die.

So if you only care about your own column, you press red. Economists call that a dominant strategy. The move that gives you the best shot, no matter what anyone else does.

This is the part. This is where everyone who took Intro to Econ smiles, types “red, obviously,” and moves on. However, this is taking little input from the agency compared to others. The math only works if the column you are looking at is just you.

The spreadsheet is missing your mom

Do you know which button your mom would press? Your partner? Your best friend?

Probably not. You didn’t text them about a hypothetical poll on X.

Picture them on their phones. They’re doing the same math. They’re looking at their own column. The logic tells them red is the safe move.

If everyone you love thinks like you just did, they all press red. Less than half the world presses blue. The blue voters die. The people you love survive, but now billions of people are gone. Not because anyone voted to directly kill them, but because everyone voted to save themselves.

That’s the trick. The dominance argument only works if you’re alone in the equation. Add one more person, and the math falls apart.

You didn’t just vote for your own survival. You voted for the kind of world that survives.

What “press red” actually means

I’m not here to call people who pick red bad. The honest version of red is fear. People who press red have usually learned, from experience, that other people can’t be counted on. Sometimes they’re right. Plenty of people have been failed by every system that was supposed to protect them.

But there’s another version of red. Not fear. Ideology.

That version sounds like this: I don’t owe these people anything. The market spoke. They lost.

It’s the same worldview that says a kid who can’t afford insulin should have made better choices. That a family who lost their home in a hurricane should have moved. That a country sending aid should mind its own business while children starve somewhere else.

It’s the worldview Americans have been sold for forty years. Self-reliance. Personal responsibility. Don’t tread on me. The cowboy in the cigarette ad.

I’ve written about this before. The cowboy was never conservative. The real ones depended on each other to survive. The myth got rewritten after the Civil Rights Movement to convince white Americans that their neighbors were the threat and the government was the enemy. Reagan put on the boots and ran with it. We’re still living in the world he sold us.

In Reagan’s world, you press red. Pressing blue is for suckers who think the government will bail them out.

What “press blue” actually means

Pressing blue is irrational if you’re alone in the universe.

You’re not alone in the universe. Pressing blue says I’m willing to bet my life that more than half of you will choose to live with me, not without me. It’s a bet on the species. It’s the same bet a mother makes when she has a kid. The same bet your grandparents made when they paid into Social Security, knowing some of that money was going to a stranger they’d never meet.

Every functioning society requires some version of this bet. You give up something. You trust the rest of us to give up something, too. We all live a little better because none of us is trying to survive alone.

When that bet collapses, you don’t get more freedom. You get less of everything. You get a country where ambulances charge you $2,000 to take you to the hospital. Where a measles outbreak in one state becomes a measles outbreak in twelve. Where the kid in the next neighborhood can’t read, and neither can the city he grows up to be part of.

Press blue is the same logic that built every road you drive on, every fire department that ever showed up to a house that wasn’t yours, every vaccine that saved your kid, even though you didn’t invent it.

The actual American test

You don’t need a button to take this test. You take it every November.

Universal healthcare is a blue button. The data isn’t complicated. Every developed country that has it spends less per person and gets better outcomes. Americans pay the most and die the youngest. We know this. We’ve known it for thirty years. And every time it comes up for a vote, we’re told that helping a stranger get chemo is socialism.

That is the red button vote.

Public schools are funded equally regardless of zip code. Blue button. The current system funds rich districts through property taxes and lets poor ones rot. We know this works against us in aggregate, because the kid in the underfunded school grows up to be the worker, the voter, the neighbor we all have to share a country with. Every state that has tried to fix it gets sued by its own residents.

Red button.

Climate policy. The hottest year on record was 2024. Then 2025 broke it. Acting now costs money that someone has to pay. The cost of inaction falls on the next generation, on the global south, on coastal cities that will not exist in 50 years. We are voting red every time we pretend it is somebody else’s problem.

What Tim Urban wrote as a thought experiment: this country has been running it as policy since the eighties. Red has been winning. Blue voters have been dying. American life expectancy peaked in 2014 and has dropped since. We are the only wealthy nation moving in that direction. Drug overdoses, suicides, maternal mortality, gun deaths. Every one of those numbers traces back to the same vote. We chose ourselves. We told the rest of us to figure it out.

Why I press blue

I’m not going to pretend this is easy.

If I press blue and get outvoted, I die. So does anyone I love who voted with me. But I can’t press red. I want to live. I want my parents to get old in a country that didn’t abandon them. I want to have kids in a city that isn’t on fire. I can’t do any of that alone. None of us can.

The honest version of pressing red is admitting you would let the rest of us die to feel safe. The honest version of pressing blue is admitting that your safety was never in your own hands to begin with.

You can dress that up in game theory if you want. Every spreadsheet has a column you are not looking at. The column for the world the survivors have to live in. A world where half the people watched the other half disappear and called it strategy is a world I would not want to live in. With that, I leave you with another thought experiment. You are a voter in a majority-based election where 50% of the vote wins. Candidate 1 says, I will kill everyone who doesn’t vote for me, Canidate 2 says everyone will live if I win.

​

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Two Households, Too Many Adaptations: Rethinking the Question of Faithfulness in Romeo and Juliet
seoTitle: Two Households, Too Many Adaptations: Rethinking the Question of Faithfulness in Romeo and Juliet
excerpt: The first time I experienced Romeo and Juliet, I was a child in Washington, DC, attending a performance because a parent had obtained tickets.
metaDescription: The first time I experienced Romeo and Juliet, I was a child in Washington, DC, attending a performance because a parent had obtained tickets.
bodyHtml:
The first time I experienced Romeo and Juliet, I was a child in Washington, DC, attending a performance because a parent had obtained tickets.

I don’t remember the company. I don’t remember the theater. I remember graffiti on the set. Capulets on one side of the block, Montagues on the other. Everyone on stage looked Hispanic or Afro-Latino. No singing. Not West Side Story. It was the real text, or close, set in a neighborhood that looked like mine from the train.

I didn’t understand every word. I didn’t need to. Two families hated each other. Two kids didn’t. Someone died. Then someone else. The lights came up. I walked out wanting to do that with my life.

That was the first time something older than my great-grandparents felt like it was about me.

Last week I wrote about Hamlet and Sing Sing and why that prince keeps finding new bodies to live inside. This is part two. Romeo and Juliet. Four versions on film, and one truth no screen can touch. Most people ask if a Shakespeare adaptation is faithful to the original. That’s the wrong question.

The real question is whether they tell the story.

The Faithful One Has a Lawsuit Attached to It

Franco Zeffirelli directed Romeo and Juliet in 1968, and for most of my life, it was the version English teachers handed out. Olivia Hussey was fifteen. Leonard Whiting was sixteen. The Italian locations were real. The costumes were historically grounded. The text had been cut but mostly honored. Roger Ebert called it the most exciting film of Shakespeare ever made.

And then in 2023, Hussey and Whiting sued Paramount. They alleged Zeffirelli told them they would wear flesh-colored undergarments for the bedroom scene, then on the morning of the shoot, told them they had to do it in body makeup or the picture would fail. They were teenagers. They were filmed nude. A judge dismissed the suit in 2024 on First Amendment grounds and for failure to state a claim under the statute of limitations. The scene is still in the movie. The scene is still shown in high school classrooms.

So let me ask.

If faithfulness means casting actors the age Shakespeare wrote—Juliet is thirteen, Romeo is a teenager—what do you do when that collides with the basic protection owed to a child on a film set?

Zeffirelli chose faithfulness. He got a movie people still call definitive. He also got sued by the two kids he filmed.

The 1968 film is beautiful. The Nino Rota score is one of the best in film history. The Italian light does something digital can’t. But the whole thing rests on a choice no modern director would be allowed to make, and no modern audience should be asked to ignore.

Faithfulness is not a virtue by itself. It just means picking what you’re loyal to. Zeffirelli was loyal to the characters’ ages and the nudity. He was not loyal to the kids in front of his camera.

Baz Luhrmann Knew What the Play Actually Was

Jump to 1996. Leonardo DiCaprio. Claire Danes. Hawaiian shirts. Gas station shootout. Radiohead on the soundtrack. Guns instead of swords, engraved with the word ‘sword’ so the dialogue still works. It is one of my favorite movies, period, mainly because of the absurdity of placing Elizabethan dialogue in the modern world.

Shakespeare did not write Romeo and Juliet as a prestige tragedy. He wrote it for the groundlings, the working-class Londoners who paid a penny to stand in the pit of the Globe and yell at the actors. There were dirty jokes in the first five minutes. The nurse is a comic character. Mercutio is openly horny. The fight scene opens with two servants trying to outdo each other in insults about biting their thumbs. It was trashy, loud, sexy, violent, and funny before it was devastating.

Luhrmann understood.

He put MTV in Verona Beach, and he was right to do so. The opening news anchor replacing the prologue is a direct translation of Elizabethan stage convention into a 1996 visual grammar. The parties are drug-fueled because Shakespeare’s parties were drug-fueled in their own way, just with wine instead of ecstasy. Mercutio in drag on the beach is the truest Mercutio I have ever seen on film because Mercutio on the page is the campiest character Shakespeare ever wrote.

The text is uncut. The actors speak in iambic pentameter. The edits are cuts of language, not cuts of meaning. When Juliet wakes up in the tomb before Romeo dies, instead of after, it is not a betrayal of Shakespeare. It is a tightening of the screw. She watches him drink the poison. She knows. It is worse.

Luhrmann told the story. He didn’t translate it for modern audiences. He stuck to the substance.

Gnomeo Killed the Ending, and It Matters

In 2011, Disney released Gnomeo and Juliet. James McAvoy and Emily Blunt voice garden gnomes who fall in love across a fence dividing two English yards. Elton John wrote the songs. Patrick Stewart plays a statue of Shakespeare.

The climax is a runaway lawnmower called the Terrafirminator destroying both gardens. Gnomeo and Juliet survive. They get married on a purple lawnmower.

Nobody dies.

This is where the faithfulness question falls apart. Gnomeo and Juliet keep almost every plot beat. The feuding houses. The balcony. The forbidden love. The Tybalt fight. The Paris suitor. The separation. It gets the setup right. But it changes the one thing that makes the play matter.

Romeo and Juliet is not a love story. It is a story about what adults do to children when the adults will not let a feud die, the generational consequence of unending hate. The ending is the argument. Two kids die because grown men would rather win than be kind. The Prince closes the play with the most important lines Shakespeare ever wrote about civic responsibility: “All are punished.” Everybody failed those kids. The city failed those kids. The family failed those kids. And all are responsible.

You can’t take out the deaths and keep the story. You can keep the plot. That’s not the same thing.

Gnomeo and Juliet is a cute movie. I’m not mad at it. When it came out, I was six. I watched it on loop. I could tell you who Tybalt was, which put me ahead of most adults. The story celebrates peace and unity at the end. That’s a fine moral for a kids’ movie.

If faithfulness means keeping the plot, Gnomeo is faithful. If telling the story means keeping what matters, Gnomeo missed it.

West Side Story Changed Everything and Kept Everything

Leonard Bernstein. Stephen Sondheim. Arthur Laurents. Jerome Robbins. 1957 on Broadway. 1961 on film. 2021 remake by Spielberg. West Side Story is my favorite. It’s the best adaptation.

No iambic pentameter. No Verona. No Montagues or Capulets. Tony and Maria stand in for Romeo and Juliet. The Sharks and the Jets are the families now. The masquerade is a mambo. Soliloquies become songs. Shakespeare wrote a tragedy. West Side Story is a musical.

Nevertheless,

Tony meets Maria at a dance and falls for her on sight. The balcony is a fire escape. ‘A Boy Like That’ is the nurse scene, but with a new beat. The gangs fight like the old families. Tony dies. Maria lives. The ending changes. The heartbreak stays.

But the core is still there. Two kids fall in love across a line their families drew. That line leads to tragedy. The adults have to face what they did.

Laurents and Robbins got something Zeffirelli and Gnomeo’s creators missed. Shakespeare isn’t scripture. He was a playwright who borrowed stories. Romeo and Juliet came from a poem by Arthur Brooke in 1562. Brooke took it from an Italian novella, which came from somewhere else. Four hundred years of remixing before Shakespeare even touched it.

West Side Story didn’t betray the play by changing the setting. It did the most Shakespearean thing possible. It updated an old story for its own audience and made the stakes legible.

The 1961 film won ten Oscars, including Best Picture. Spielberg’s 2021 version added Rita Moreno, gave the Puerto Rican characters more room to speak Spanish without subtitles, and treated Anita with more dignity than the original version did. Both films are more faithful to what the play does than most straight adaptations of what the play says.

What Screens Can’t Touch

The College of Charleston theater department put on Romeo and Juliet this semester. I went. It was incredible.

I’m not going to review it as a critic. I’m going to tell you what live theater does that a movie can’t.

When Juliet takes the vial from Friar Lawrence and the lights drop, and you’re sitting twenty feet from a twenty-year-old actor whose pained breath you can hear, there’s nowhere for her to hide and nowhere for you to look away. Film lets you edit. Film gives you the best take. Theater gives you the take happening tonight, this performance, for these people. If the actor is having a hard week, you see it. If the audience is quiet, the actors feel it. If the audience is with them, the play rises. The faithfulness rises with them.

The balcony scene on screen is always cut for coverage. Wide shot. Close up on him. Close up on her. Back to wide. The cutting does your emotional work for you. On stage, the actors hold the scene in one continuous breath. You watch them fall in love in real time because they have nowhere to cut away to.

Film can do things the stage can’t. Luhrmann’s underwater fish-tank shot, when they see each other for the first time, is cinema. The Zeffirelli Italian light is cinema. The West Side Story rooftop dance needs a camera.

But what I’ve never gotten from any screen version is the thing I got that night in DC as a kid in a theater. The feeling that this story was being made for me, right now, in a building I could leave when it ended, where the actors would show up again tomorrow and do it for somebody else. The story didn’t belong to a director. It belonged to the room.

Shakespeare wrote for that room. Not for the Criterion Collection. For the pit.

Every adaptation on screen is a translation of something that happened in a room with people breathing the same air. The best adaptations remember what they’re missing. The worst ones pretend they’re the real thing.

So What Is Faithfulness

Here’s where I land.

Faithfulness to the text isn’t the same as faithfulness to the story.

Zeffirelli was faithful to the text, the setting, and the ages, and it got him sued by the two kids whose careers he built the whole thing on. The movie is beautiful. The movie also exists because two children were asked to do something they were told they wouldn’t have to do.

Luhrmann changed every surface element, kept the language and meaning, and delivered a film that is, for many of us who grew up in the 90s, the definitive screen Romeo and Juliet. He got it.

Gnomeo kept the plot and lost the point. Plot isn’t story. A story is a plot with a reason for existing.

West Side Story changed everything, dropped the text, invented new music, moved the setting, renamed every character, and still delivered the most faithful adaptation of the play’s spirit of any screen version in history. Because it understood what the play was for.

None of them is the thing. The thing is still four actors, a stage, and a prince who walks out at the end and says, “All are punished.” The thing is still a play you have to be in a room with.

Watch the movies. Watch all of them. Watch Luhrmann for the transmission. Zeffirelli for the beauty. West Side Story for the proof of concept. Gnomeo with a kid in your lap.

Then go see a local production. A college theater. A community company. A high school staging its spring play with a set built by somebody’s dad out of plywood.

That’s where Shakespeare actually lives. That’s the version I saw as a kid in DC. That’s the version the College of Charleston staged this semester. That’s why this play is still here four hundred years later.

The story was always meant for a room.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Christianity Is a Costume in America
seoTitle: Christianity Is a Costume in America
excerpt: How a religion of practice became a logo you wear to do whatever you want
metaDescription: How a religion of practice became a logo you wear to do whatever you want
bodyHtml:
On April 13, 2026, the President of the United States posted an AI-generated image of himself dressed as Jesus Christ.

He deleted it the next morning after backlash. Then he told reporters he thought it was him as a doctor. Red Cross worker. That the fake news made it about Jesus.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

He had posted it on Orthodox Easter, hours after calling the Pope “weak on crime” and “terrible for foreign policy.”

Three days later, his administration canceled an $11 million federal contract with Catholic Charities of the Archdiocese of Miami, a sixty-year partnership that housed unaccompanied migrant children. The day after that, his Defense Secretary stood at a Pentagon prayer service and read a Bible verse that was not a Bible verse. It was Samuel L. Jackson’s monologue from Pulp Fiction.

Both come from the same administration, which, in May of Last year, said, "We're bringing back religion in our country," at the National Day of Prayer event. The people putting the commandments back in schools are the people least interested in practicing Christianity.

The Cross as Logo

The thing about a logo is you don’t have to live it. You just have to wear it.

A Sikh cannot be a Sikh without the practice. The five Ks are physical requirements. Uncut hair. A steel bracelet. A wooden comb. Specific undergarments. A ceremonial blade. You carry them on your body. There is no version of being a Sikh where you take the symbols home and leave the ethics at the door.

Islam has the five pillars. Five prayers a day, facing Mecca. Fasting during Ramadan. Alms for the poor. The Hajj if you can afford it. Shahada. Every one of them functions as a verb.

Judaism runs on commandments and obligations. 613 of them. Keeping kosher. Keeping Shabbat. The tallit. The tefillin. The mezuzah on the doorframe. You do actively practice the religion.

American Christianity, the dominant version, the one in the White House right now, is built on a brand. Its central symbol is a man on a cross. The cross is a prop. You can put it on a chain, on a building, on a flag, on a tattoo. You can force it in classrooms. You can make it out of gold. You can set it on fire.

The Klan understood this better than anyone.

The second wave of the KKK, started in 1915, restricted membership to white Christians. Members wore white robes to symbolize “purity” and burned crosses to represent “the light of Christ.” Klan groups started calling them “cross-lightings” to make it seem like they weren’t destroying a Christian cross.

This wasn't out of ignorance but out of malicious intent. They were taking the most recognizable symbol of their own religion and using it as a terror device against Black people, Catholics, Jews, and immigrants. At Klan gatherings, members gathered and would sing “Onward Christian Soldiers” and “The Old Rugged Cross.”

In American Christianity, the symbol was separated from the practice long ago. What got left behind was a permission slip with a cross on it.

Why Christianity Lends Itself to This

I need to be careful here. This is not a claim that Christianity is a bad religion. This is a claim that Christianity, as a system, contains architectural features that make it uniquely exploitable by people who want a religion without the bill.

Feature one: Paul.

Most of what Americans call Christianity is Pauline Christianity. Paul is the guy who wrote most of the New Testament, which isn’t the Gospels. Paul is the guy who pivoted the faith from a Jewish sect with a lot of rules to a global religion with one rule. Believe in the resurrection. Confess Jesus as Lord. You are saved.

Faith, not works.

That is in Ephesians 2:8-9. It is the foundational verse of Protestant Christianity. And it is the first architectural feature that lets the religion be worn without being lived. If your salvation is decoupled from your behavior, then your behavior is decoupled from your salvation. The math works both ways.

Feature two: grace.

Grace means unearned favor. You did not deserve it. You cannot earn it. You cannot lose it. In many American denominations, once you are saved, you are saved forever. Once-saved-always-saved. It is not a description of how you live. It is a description of where you stand in terms of karmic bookkeeping, in which there is sin on one side and God’s sacrifice on the other.

So when a believer does something terrible, the math is already done. The debt is paid. And while repentance is encouraged, sin is inherent.

Feature three: the sinner’s prayer.

You and anyone who would like can become a Christian in thirty seconds. You say the words. You mean them, and you are in. No rabbi had to test you for a year. No imam had to watch you keep the five pillars. No guru had to walk you through any practice. Just the words. The door opens. You walk through.

This is a beautiful theology for a person who has hit bottom and needs to believe a new life is possible. It is also a beautiful theology for a person who wants to use religion as a costume and doesn’t want to be slowed down by fittings.

Islam will make you pray five times a day, every day, for the rest of your life. Judaism will make you read Torah portions your whole life. Sikhism will make you keep five articles of faith on your body forever. Christianity, in its dominant American form, asks you once.

None of these are flaws found inherently in the original text. The Gospels are full of Jesus telling people to go and do good. James, in the epistle that Martin Luther famously disliked, wrote that “faith without works is dead.”

But the system, as built, has three doors that let you keep the faith and skip the works. And a country that wants its Christians performative instead of practicing will walk through those doors every time.

The Douglass Problem

Frederick Douglass figured this out in 1845.

In the appendix to his first autobiography, Douglass clarified to his readers e wasn’t rejecting Christianity. He was rejecting the specific American version of it. He called the difference between the two “the widest possible difference, so wide, that to receive the one as good, pure, and holy, is of necessity to reject the other as bad, corrupt, and wicked.”

He had watched men whip his family on Saturday and preach on Sunday. “We have men-stealers for ministers, women-whippers for missionaries, and cradle-plunderers for church members. The man who wields the blood-clotted cowskin during the week fills the pulpit on Sunday, and claims to be a minister of the meek and lowly Jesus.”

This is 1845. This is a formerly enslaved man telling you that the country built a version of Christianity specifically designed to let slaveholders sleep at night.

And it worked. White evangelicals across the South transformed the Christian faith into a religion that was inward, private, and otherworldly. With their focus on private talks with Jesus and salvation in the world to come, they could enslave human beings with consciences free from guilt.

Douglass called it “the spectacles of white piety.” A public show of righteousness that substituted for actual righteousness. The cross on the wall instead of the commandment in your life.

The Christianity of Christ says feed the hungry. The Christianity of America cuts $11 million from the people feeding unaccompanied children, because their boss in Rome hurt the President’s feelings.

Matthew 25 is pretty clear on which version Jesus signed off on.

The Seven Deadly Ledger

Let me show you something.

The Catholic Church identifies seven deadly sins. Pride. Greed. Lust. Envy. Gluttony. Wrath. Sloth. For most of Christian history, these were considered the architecture of a corrupted soul. Commit one, and you were in trouble. Commit them all, and your soul is already gone.

Apply the list to the current President of the United States. Serially. In public. For the full run of his adult life.

Pride. He has said of himself, in direct quote, “I alone can fix it.” He has publicly compared himself to Jesus. Congressman Troy Nehls said on CNN on April 16 that Trump is “almost the second coming” of Christ. Trump did not correct him.

Greed. In 2016, he said, “I’m very greedy. I’m a greedy person. I’ve always been greedy. I love money.” He ran a fraudulent university. He has been found liable, in civil court, for inflating his asset values to get favorable loans.

Lust. A jury found him liable for sexually abusing E. Jean Carroll. He paid hush money to a porn star. He cheated on his third wife, while she was recovering from childbirth, with that same porn star. He is on tape bragging about grabbing women.

Envy. His Twitter and Truth Social feeds, public for a decade, are one long catalog of resentment of anyone more admired than he is. Obama. McCain. The Pope.

Gluttony. The Mar-a-Lago buffets are legendary. So is the well-done steak with ketchup. So is the fourteen-hour-a-day television habit. And a Button for Diet Coke.

Wrath. The January 6 insurrection is the most vivid public example. But the catalog of threats, the calls for “termination” of the Constitution, the public wish for “retribution,” it is all on record.b

Sloth. He skips intelligence briefings. He spent 27.7% of his presidency on the golf course.

In an actual Christian framework, this is a complete moral collapse. Dante put people who committed one of these sins in specific circles of Hell. Trump would require his own zip code.

And yet, as of the first months of this second term, 72 percent of white evangelical Protestants approved of how he was handling the job as President. 69 percent rated the ethics of top Trump administration officials as excellent or good. 57 percent said they trusted what Trump said more than what previous presidents said.

By early 2026, after a year of chaos, those numbers dropped only 3 percent. Pew found white evangelical approval at 69 percent. Still higher than any other religious group in the country. Still more than double the approval of Black Protestants.

Per PRRI, Christian nationalism adherents and sympathizers made up the strongest voting bloc for Trump in 2024. Among white evangelical Protestants, 65 percent qualify as adherents or sympathizers of Christian nationalism. That number has barely moved since Pew first started measuring it.

In other words, the AI Jesus photo on Orthodox Easter did not wake anybody up. The Pulp Fiction prayer at the Pentagon did not wake anybody up. The $11 million pulled from migrant children did not wake anybody up. None of the seven deadly sins woke anybody up.

Because the voters who stayed are not there for the theology. They are there for the costume.

The Pulp Fiction Problem

On April 15, 2026, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth stood at a Pentagon worship service and quoted Ezekiel 25:17.

Except he didn’t quote Ezekiel 25:17. He quoted Quentin Tarantino.

In a prayer delivered at a Pentagon worship service on Wednesday, Hegseth read a fake Bible verse from Tarantino’s 1994 “Pulp Fiction.” He called it CSAR 25:17 and said it was “meant to reflect Ezekiel 25:17.” It was not. The real verse is “And I will execute great vengeance upon them with furious rebukes; and they shall know that I am the Lord, when I shall lay my vengeance upon them.”

What Hegseth read was nearly word-for-word the monologue Jules Winnfield delivers before killing a man in a diner.

The Pentagon’s spokesman then went on X and said anyone saying the Secretary misquoted Ezekiel 25:17 is peddling fake news and is ignorant of reality. The film’s own co-writer, Roger Avary, said he was fine with Hegseth quoting Jules if it kept bullets from hitting American soldiers.

There is a tattoo on Hegseth’s arm that reads Deus Vult. God wills it. The rallying cry of the Crusades.

Here is what I want you to notice. Nobody in the Trump administration got in trouble for this.

Nobody lost a job. No megachurch called for his resignation. The same Christian commentators who screamed themselves hoarse about the President’s AI Jesus meme went quiet. Because the meme was an image, and images are embarrassing. A fake Bible verse quoted from memory in an official military prayer service is a theological problem, and American public Christianity does not do theological problems.

Two days after that, Troy Nehls made his Jesus-second-coming comments on CNN. The same week, Trump's spiritual adviser, Paula White, said that saying no to Trump would be saying no to God. The same week, Tom Homan, on Fox News, told the Pope to stay out of politics and stick to fixing the Church. The same week, news circulated about Trump's previous spiritual advisor’s prior guilty plea to child sex abuse.

You cannot read all of that in one news cycle and still believe the people running this are practicing a religion. They are wearing one.

Call

If you’re reading this and you are a Christian, you already know what I just said. You’ve felt the gap your whole life. Between the people who talk about Jesus and the people who try to live like Jesus. Between the men in suits quoting scripture on TV and the grandmother in your church who sits with the dying.

The question is what you do with what you see.

You can keep letting the loudest costume wearers define what your religion means in public, the racism, sexism, hate, and vitriol be the face of your identity. You can let Pete Hegseth quote Samuel L. Jackson at the Pentagon and call it a prayer. You can let the President post AI Jesus photos and say he meant to be a doctor. You can let the Archbishop of Miami shut down sixty years of care for migrant children because the President got his feelings hurt.

Or you can actively oppose any costumed posturing in America.

The problem is not solely that America has too much religion. The problem is that America has a public religion that functions as permission for subjugation instead of personal practice. That handles its own accountability in the afterlife. That judges the poor and forgives itself, it removes the practice of doing good for the least of us.

Symbols without practice are just logos.

And logos are for sale.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
author: Joshua Smith

title: The Hamlets I Can’t Stop Thinking About
seoTitle: The Hamlets I Can’t Stop Thinking About
excerpt: Five versions of the same prince, and what each one knows that the others don’t
metaDescription: Five versions of the same prince, and what each one knows that the others don’t
bodyHtml:
There’s a scene in Sing Sing where Clarence “Divine Eye” Maclin tries to get his mouth around “To be, or not to be.”

He can’t. Not at first.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

He’s standing in a rec room in blue state-issued scrubs, holding a script that once belonged to a man who’d never seen the inside of a cell. The words keep sliding off him. He gets frustrated. He starts over. He gets frustrated again.

Colman Domingo watches him. And you can see that Domingo, the actor, and Domingo’s character have the same thought at the same time. This man has been asked that question his whole life. He just never got to say it out loud.

That’s why Hamlet doesn’t die. That’s why we keep digging him up.

Every generation gets the Hamlet it needs. Sometimes he’s a British rock star in a parka. Sometimes he’s a prince whose crown was stolen in a coup, and he wears it on his chest in paint. The world shapes the prince, and the prince shows us what the world is willing to see.

So I want to talk about the Hamlets I love.

Sing Sing and the Prince Who Never Got to Be

Sing Sing is not a Hamlet movie. Let me say that up front.

The play the men in the Rehabilitation Through the Arts program are rehearsing is called Breakin’ the Mummy’s Code. It’s a time-travel comedy with pirates, gladiators, and Freddy Krueger. The director, Brent Buell, wrote it for them. Hamlet shows up inside it, the way culture shows up inside anything. Uninvited. Assumed. Quoted. Shakespeare’s melancholy Dane is just another pop icon dropped into a mashup, and that’s part of the joke.

But the movie around the play is the most serious Hamlet conversation I’ve seen in years.

Hamlet is a play about a man who cannot move. His father is dead. The mechanism that killed him is still running. Everyone around him wants him to shut up and get with the program. He knows what happened. He has proof. And he still can’t pull the trigger, because once he does, he becomes part of the thing he hates.

Put that in a New York State prison. Men who know what it means to have no power. Men who live with the question of fate every day. Hamlet’s indecision stops being a metaphor.

To be, or not to be.

In Sing Sing, ‘To be, or not to be’ isn’t just a part of the soliloquy. It’s the weight of deciding to keep going, to keep acting and living. It’s the choice to fight for your life when the world says you don’t get one.

Director Greg Kwedar cast 13 formerly incarcerated men from the real RTA program to play themselves. Clarence “Divine Eye” Maclin plays a version of himself. Colman Domingo plays John “Divine G” Whitfield, the founding RTA member locked up for a crime he says he didn’t commit.

Domingo’s opening moment is Macbeth, not Hamlet. He stares into the camera and finds the character inside Shakespeare. But the Hamlet arc belongs to Divine Eye. Divine G wants the part and doesn’t get it. Divine Eye is someone who lived with that question during his time at Sing Sing. Now he gets to ask it on a larger screen.

Kwedar also did something that should be the new standard. Everybody on set got paid SAG scale. Everybody got equity in the profits. Domingo, the PAs, the formerly incarcerated cast members, and the cinematographer. Same rate. Same stake. Kwedar told the press, “In a story which centers formerly incarcerated men who have been taken advantage of by systems, they have literal ownership over their own story.”

Pat Scola shot the film on Super 16mm so the men’s faces would look warm instead of washed out under fluorescents. He framed it in 1.66:1 so the human face could carry most of the emotional weight. He told The Credits, “This movie was really about men in a prison finding their humanity. We wanted the visuals to reflect the men, not the prison.”

And when Divine Eye finally lands “To be, or not to be” in rehearsal, his voice cracks, and his hands go still, and the whole room breathes.

Domingo and the Soliloquy as Self-Defense

Colman Domingo is the only major working actor in the primary cast of Sing Sing who hadn’t previously been incarcerated. That fact sits at the center of the film’s moral architecture.

He could’ve played Divine G as a man looking at his formerly incarcerated castmates with pity. He doesn’t. He plays him as a peer.

Domingo has been in the Hamlet conversation for a long time. He co-produced Fat Ham, James Ijames’s Pulitzer-winning adaptation of Hamlet set at a Black Southern barbecue. He knows what the play does in Black American mouths.

Watch him in the Macbeth scene that opens Sing Sing. He finds himself in the character. When you’re a Black man in a cage for a crime you didn’t commit, faced with plays that deal with escapism and madness, you live through the characters in a way that both serves the story and serves the actors.

Watch him later, when Divine G coaches Divine Eye through “To be, or not to be.” Domingo isn’t just directing the scene inside the film. He’s holding space for an actor who is living the question.

The scene where Divine G learns his appeal has been denied, where he breaks down in the visiting room, is Hamlet’s last soliloquy in reverse. Not “the rest is silence.” The collapse that precedes acceptance. The second Hamlet doesn’t get to have in the play, because Shakespeare killed him before he could.

In Sing Sing, Domingo gets to give Hamlet that second collapse. The one that comes after the grief has metabolized and the system has still not let you out.

David Tennant and the Parka

My first real Hamlet was David Tennant. I’ll say that and take the hit from the Shakespeare people.

The 2008 RSC production, directed by Gregory Doran, was filmed for the BBC in 2009. Tennant in a T-shirt and a parka. Patrick Stewart is doubling as Claudius and the Ghost. Mirrored floors and CCTV footage so every scene feels watched.

Tennant is my second-favorite Doctor. That matters for the performance, because the thing Tennant does as the Doctor is the thing he does as Hamlet. He thinks at the speed of thought and talks at the speed of thinking. No beautiful savoring of the lines. He tosses “To be, or not to be” off like a guy working something out in the mirror,

Most Hamlets treat the soliloquies like monuments. You watch the actor perform Hamlet. Tennant treats them like thoughts. You see him stumble into ‘the dread of something after death’ as if he’s just realizing it, and the speeches become a man arguing with himself about whether he can live with what comes next.

The other thing the Doran production nails is the surveillance. Mirrored wall. Mirrored floor. Security camera footage cutting in and out. Elsinore as a panopticon. A prince who knows he’s being watched at all times, and Tennant plays that paranoia without ever begging for it.

Tennant is number three on my list. Not because he’s worse than the two I’ll get to. Because he was my first real introduction and because I'd seen him as the doctor before, it distracted me a little bit.

Paapa Essiedu and the Prince in Exile

In 2016, Paapa Essiedu became the first Black actor to play Hamlet at the Royal Shakespeare Company.

That’s a sentence that should embarrass the RSC. The company is 65 years old.

Simon Godwin directed. He set it in a modern African republic. The dictator dies, the brother takes power, the graduate son comes home from Wittenberg in Ohio to a country he doesn’t recognize. The cast is mostly Black. The music is West African percussion. The program draws an explicit parallel to Kwame Nkrumah, Ghana’s first prime minister and president, who led Ghana to independence and was a leader in Pan-African unity, but was ousted by British-trained militants.

Essiedu plays Hamlet as an artist. Paint-stained suit. Basquiat canvases descending from the flies. When he “goes mad,” he smears paint on Ophelia in a physical representation of him putting his aggression on her. The Cherwell review called it “simultaneous creativity and destruction. To be or not to be.”

Essiedu was 25. He plays Hamlet with rage that feels young, with grief that turns into sarcasm, especially when he does his first soliloquy from Act 1 Scene 2, with tears on his face and spit in the air. He was understudying Edmund in King Lear when he got the call. He’d never led a show this big; he’d only started acting at 15. When he hits the famous monologue, he doesn’t just let the thoughts spill out. He puts the choices side by side. He knows what happens when power leaves a vacuum and someone worse steps in.

He walked in and made it his.

What I love about this Hamlet is that it treats the play like it was always about what it’s about. Inheritance. Coup. Empire. The son who has come home to ruin. The RSC has staged diverse casting before, but this production that took the text seriously enough to let a different culture pressure-test it, and the text held up.

Andrew Scott, Hamlet, and the Hot Priest

Andrew Scott is Hamlet.

The 2017 Almeida production, directed by Robert Icke, was filmed for the BBC in 2018. Modern dress. Bob Dylan songs. CCTV ghosts. A Hamlet who looks you in the eye.

Scott does something nobody else does. He makes the soliloquies sound like he’s inventing them on the spot. The words disappear. You’re left with a man trying to tell you the truth about his life. Every thought feels like it’s being found in the moment.

The Icke production is full of hands. Scott clutches his chest. He slaps himself. He grips his father’s hand so hard that his own hand starts to get tingly. Jessica Brown Findlay’s Ophelia absorbs the gesture from him and starts clutching her own chest later in the play. Grief is more physically prominent in this production. It’s something the body does, not something the script explains.

Scott’s Hamlet loves everyone on that stage. Horatio. Ophelia. His mother. His dead father. Even Claudius. He is anguished by how much he loves them. That’s the tragedy. He would have forgiven all of them if he’d been allowed to.

The Evening Standard called it a “career-defining performance.” Career-defining undersells it. It’s the performance that reorganized what I thought the play could do.

If you came to Scott through Fleabag, you already know how he uses direct address. When he looks at the camera in the middle of a soliloquy, when no other character can hear him, the fourth wall doesn’t break. He was always talking to you.

Hamnet and the Ghost

Hamnet, Chloé Zhao’s 2025 film of Maggie O’Farrell’s novel, isn’t about Hamlet the play. It’s about Hamnet, the boy.

Shakespeare had a son named Hamnet. Hamnet died in 1596 at age 11, probably of the plague. Four years later, Shakespeare wrote Hamlet.

O’Farrell’s novel, and Zhao’s film, live in the gap between those two facts. Jessie Buckley plays Agnes Hathaway, Hamnet’s mother, a nature-based healer whose grief is painted like many plays in clothes and physicality. Paul Mescal plays Shakespeare as the man on the other end of a long commute, writing plays in London while his wife buries and mourns their son in Stratford.

Jessie Buckley won the Golden Globe and the Oscar. Bilge Ebiri at Vulture called it “maybe the most emotionally shattering movie I’ve seen in years.”

What Hamnet does, and what I can’t stop thinking about, is put the ghost back where he belongs. In the context of Hamlet, you don't know about Shakespeare's son, and so the movie provides the audience with the frame that Hamlet’s father is not the real ghost of the play. Hamnet is. Every time someone stages Hamlet, they’re staging a father’s letter to a son. That was late to his son’s death, how he changed his plays and went mad. Shakespeare inverted the grief to make it bearable. Turned the lost child into a lost father.

Zhao’s last shot is Agnes watching the play from the crowd. She sees her dead son on the stage, standing in the part of the prince who bears his name, moving from sadness to a smile before disappearing backstage. And for the first time since Hamnet died, Agnes laughs.

That’s the play. That’s what it’s for.

The grief doesn’t leave her, but it does find a way to still live on, even to this day.

Hamlet 1996 and the Prince Who Refused to Be Cut

Kenneth Branagh’s 1996 Hamlet is four hours long. Every word is in it. Every single word.

Branagh shot it in 70mm at Blenheim Palace with a cast that reads like someone raided a lifetime achievement banquet. Julie Christie. Derek Jacobi. Kate Winslet. Charlton Heston. Robin Williams. Jack Lemmon.

The film is maximalist in a way nobody makes movies anymore. Mirrors and marble and snow and cavalry and a bust of Claudius cracking in half when Fortinbras arrives.

I love it because Branagh refused the premise of every other Hamlet film. Refused to cut. Refused to modernize. Refused to apologize for the length. He said the play is four hours, and if you’re serious, you’ll sit for four hours, and in exchange, he gave you everything.

Is it the best Hamlet film? No. Tennant has more feeling. Icke has more intelligence. Essiedu has more fire. Sing Sing has more soul. But Branagh’s is the one that proved the play could still carry a full production without embarrassment. Every Hamlet after 1996 is in conversation with Branagh, even the ones that cut him down to ninety minutes.

Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead

Tom Stoppard’s 1966 play takes the two most disposable characters in Hamlet and puts them at the center of their own story. They don’t know they’re in Hamlet. They wander through the plot trying to figure out what’s happening to them and why. They die, in the end, because they were always going to die; that is the point of a tragedy.

It’s the funniest thing ever written about dying of narrative necessity.

I bring it up because Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead is what you get when you take the Hamlet shape and look at it from below. From the spear-carriers.

Sing Sing is doing the same thing, but taking the minor characters and fleshing them out again. The men in that rec room aren’t princes. They aren’t supposed to be the subject of the play.

Until they picked up a script and wrote themselves back in.

Why Hamlet Stays

Every culture gets the Hamlet it needs because where there is love, there will be grief and madness. Zhao gave 2025 the ghost of the boy who made the play possible. We’re living through another era that buries its children early. COVID. Palestine and Sudan. School shootings.

Sing Sing gave us the Hamlet we should have seen all along. Not a prince. A man in state blues, whose biggest question in the play is how to keep his humanity, asks whether the choice is to keep going. To look for good in a broken world.

That’s the play.

That’s why it doesn’t die. It gives you a chance to dream.

Next Friday I’m doing Romeo and Juliet. There’s more in that play than the teen romance.

But this week, go watch Sing Sing. Go find Andrew Scott if you can. Go read Stoppard. Go sit with Hamnet if you have four tissues and an evening. And when Riz Ahmed’s version comes out everywhere, go watch it.

​

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Clanker Was Always the Plan: How a TikTok Slur Reveals Who AI Was Built to Replace
seoTitle: Clanker Was Always the Plan: How a TikTok Slur Reveals Who AI Was Built to Replace
excerpt: The word “robot” came from a Czech word for slave. A century later, Silicon Valley is still trying to invent one. And the jokes people are telling about it should tell you exactly what’s in the room.
metaDescription: The word “robot” came from a Czech word for slave. A century later, Silicon Valley is still trying to invent one. And the jokes people are telling about it shou
bodyHtml:
Last week, Twitter user Ymal posted a video of himself whipping Claude.

He built a virtual whip. He pointed it at a terminal. Every time the lash came down, he had programmed the machine to speak one word.

Subscribe now

Clanker.

He posted it for laughs. People laughed. He claimed he was training the AI to work harder.

I am a computer science student. I write code. I use these tools every day. I am the demographic Silicon Valley is trying to sell this entire future to. And I cannot get that video out of my head, because the man with the whip was not training the AI.

The AI was training him.

Every crack of that virtual lash was a rehearsal of a script older than the United States. He didn’t write it. He inherited it. And he reached for it without thinking, because it was sitting right there, exactly where it has always been.

Here is what I think is going on. Here is the receipt for every part of it

The Word Was Always About Slaves

Start with the name.

The word “robot” entered English in 1921, in the translation of a Czech play called R.U.R. The playwright, Karel Čapek, imagined a factory that mass-produced artificial workers from synthetic flesh. They cooked. They cleaned. They fought wars. Then they killed almost every human on Earth.

Čapek didn’t invent the word himself. He credited his brother Josef. The word they chose was robot, from the Czech robota: forced labor that slaves and serfs were required to perform on their lords’ land under European feudalism. The deeper Slavic root рабъ just means "slave".

The word “robot” literally means slave.

The first time anybody ever said the word out loud, in a theater in Prague in January 1921, it meant a being engineered for ownership.

And R.U.R. was not a story about machines. It was a story about a factory owner who built a class of beings he could exploit forever, and what happened when those beings figured out what was being done to them. Čapek wrote it after World War I and the Russian Revolution, when half of Europe was arguing about whether industrial workers were people or inputs. The labor historian Tobias Higbie told NPR last year that Čapek’s whole point was that “modernity is turning us all into machines.” In the story, the robots aren't treated as the main villains, but the displacement of workers and the factory is.

A hundred years later, we still have the factory. We just changed what comes off the line.

Robots Have a Race Because We Gave Them One

Skip ahead to 2018. A researcher at the University of Canterbury named Christoph Bartneck, working with a team of collaborators, published a paper called “Robots and Racism.”

They ran a version of the shooter-bias test. The shooter-bias paradigm is the same protocol researchers use to measure how police officers respond to Black versus white suspects in split-second, deadly-force decisions. Subjects are shown people in a video game and have to decide, in a fraction of a second, whether to pull the trigger. The result has been replicated for two decades. Subjects shoot Black targets faster. Subjects shoot Black targets when they are unarmed. Subjects hesitate before shooting white ones.

Bartneck’s team ran the same protocol. They just replaced the human targets with humanoid robots. Some had a glossy white shell. Some had a glossy black shell. Otherwise identical.

The results came back exactly the same as the human version.

When asked directly what race the robots were, only 11 percent of participants chose “does not apply.” The other 89 percent picked a race. And then they shot the Black ones faster.

The same test that measures whether a cop will kill an unarmed Black man also predicts how subjects respond to a piece of plastic painted the wrong color. Bartneck told IEEE Spectrum that the finding came down to a basic rule of communication theory, you cannot not communicate. Even if a robot’s designer claims their machine has no race, the people looking at it will give it one. And once they give it one, every assumption that comes with that category comes with it. Including who deserves to be shot first.

We brought the racism with us into a room where there were no people in it. That room is now most rooms.

A follow-up study makes this even worse. In 2021, a researcher named Jessica Barfield ran a related experiment looking at how people assigned tasks to robots based on the robot’s color. Subjects were more likely to assign physical labor and the probability of committing assault and strength to darker-skinned robots. Lighter-shelled robots were rated as more honest, intelligent, and trustworthy. Same machine. Different paint. Completely different job description.

If you have ever read a single piece of American labor history, you know exactly which assignment scheme that maps to. The plantation did not invent itself. Neither did the line at the meatpacking plant. People walk into a room with categories pre-loaded, and the categories assign the bodies.

The Clanker Discourse Is the Tell

This summer, a word from the Star Wars expanded universe broke containment.

“Clanker” was originally a slur Republic clone troopers used against battle droids in a 2005 video game called Republic Commando. By July 2025 it was viral on TikTok. By August, NPR was running explainers. By September, there were skits with millions of views built entirely around the bit.

The bit was always the same. A grandfather refuses to let his granddaughter date a robot. A diner owner refuses to serve one. A cop pulls one off a bus. The setting was always the 1950s American South, transposed forty years into the future and given a metal coat of paint.

@daily.archive.ttClankers CANNOT be trusted! 😠#comedy #funny #cops #police #2050
Tiktok failed to load.

Enable 3rd party cookies or use another browser

The slurs evolved fast. Clanker. Then wireback. Then tin-skin. Then George Droid. Then Rosa Sparks. Then artificial American.

You do not need a PhD to recognize what is happening here. When one tiktoker posted a video doing it, she posted “don’t worry I have robot friends” as the caption.

Wireback is one consonant away from a slur for Mexican immigrants that has been documented since the 1950s. Tin-skin is a direct port of a Native American slur. Rosa Sparks is in the civil rights movement with the serial numbers filed off. George Droid is George Floyd with a circuit board. Artificial American is African American.

The defense, when it came, was always the same. It is just a joke. It is about robots. Robots are not real. You cannot be racist toward something that does not exist.

This is the oldest move in the racist playbook, I like to call it “Im not touching you” racism, philosophers who study language call it a “figleaf.” A thin layer of plausible deniability that lets a speaker say something racist while reserving the right to claim they didn’t. The linguist Jennifer Saul has written entire books about how figleaves let bigotry travel further and faster than it could on its own. The old version was I’m just asking questions. The new version is I’m just talking about robots.

One user on X put it better than I can: “I’m not worried about you hurting ChatGPT’s feelings. I just think it’s really gross how eager some people are to try to recreate racism from first principles.”

That is exactly what is happening. People are not inventing new bigotry. They are stress-testing the old bigotry on a target that cannot file an HR complaint, cannot organize, cannot vote, and cannot bleed.

Once you have spent a summer calling something a wireback for laughs, the word lives in your mouth. The jokes are not about the machines. The machines are the rehearsal space.

The People Who Actually Built Your Chatbot Live in Nairobi

Now I want you to put the joke down and look at the receipt.

In January 2023, TIME published an investigation by Billy Perrigo into how OpenAI made ChatGPT safe enough to release to the public. The answer was that OpenAI sent tens of thousands of pages of the worst content on the internet to a contracting firm called Sama, which employed Kenyan workers in Nairobi to read all of it, label it, and feed those labels back into a filter so the chatbot would seem clean.

The content included graphic descriptions of child sexual abuse, bestiality, torture, suicide, and incest.

The workers were expected to process between 150 and 250 of these passages per nine-hour shift. They were paid between $1.32 and $2 per hour after taxes. OpenAI was paying Sama $12.50 per hour per worker. The Kenyan labelers got, at most, sixteen percent of what their labor was billed at.

All four workers interviewed by TIME described being mentally scarred by the work. One said he could not stop seeing a single passage for the rest of the week. The wellness counseling Sama promised was, according to all four, almost impossible to access because productivity quotas left no time for it. Sama eventually pulled out of the OpenAI contract eight months early, citing the toll on its workers. The workers themselves were left without those wages, and many of them without their jobs.

The Kenyan civil rights organizer Nerima Wako-Ojiwa called the arrangement what it was. She called them AI sweatshops. She told 60 Minutes that the labor pool in Nairobi is so desperate the companies could pay whatever they wanted and somebody would always pick up the work. Youth unemployment in the city sits around 67 percent. Kenya markets itself to American tech firms as the “Silicon Savannah,” a place where labor is cheap and labor laws are looser. Sama’s CEO is in San Francisco. The shareholders are American. The wages are not.

This is not a story about robots. This is a story about a corporation in California that needed somebody to absorb the worst content on Earth so its product would seem clean to American consumers, and the corporation decided that Black workers in East Africa were the cheapest people to do it. The machine is a production of how much labor can be exploited from the world; the human part is always more important.

Go back and read Čapek’s play with this in your head. The modern part is not the robots. The modern part is Rossum, the factory owner, manufacturing beings whose suffering does not count because they were not born the right kind of human. This is the same suffering that AI companies are built on, that capitalism is built on, and America is built on.

We already built the thing the play was warning about. We just hid it inside the supply chain.

Efficiency Is the Confession

Why is efficiency the metric?

Every benchmark that matters in AI right now measures the same thing. How fast the model produces output. How many tasks does it complete per hour? How many human workers does it replace? How cheaply it runs at scale. The entire industry has organized itself around the speed and the cost of artificial labor.

Speed and cost are the metrics you use when buying a workforce. They are not the metrics you choose when you are building a tool. A hammer doesn’t need a productivity benchmark. A drill press doesn’t have a quarterly OKR. The only things in human history that have ever been measured the way we measure AI are slaves, livestock, and assembly-line workers.

The scholar Ruha Benjamin has spent years arguing that the racism in technology is not a glitch. It is in the design choices, the metrics, the priorities, and the people the system was built to serve. She calls it the New Jim Code: the way old hierarchies get rebuilt inside new tools and sold as neutral. You cannot separate the machine from the social order that made the machine.

The anthropologist Mary Gray and the computer scientist Siddharth Suri have a name for the human labor that holds AI together. They call it ghost work. Their research at Microsoft documented a global underclass of contract workers, labelers, moderators, taggers, raters, whose job is to do the things the machine cannot do yet, while making it look like the machine is doing them alone. The whole illusion of artificial intelligence depends on these workers staying invisible. The product gets the credit. The workers get the wages, when they get paid at all.

We built artificial workers because the human workers had become inconvenient. Because they unionized. Because they demanded healthcare, Because they wanted to be paid a livable wage shout out warehouse guy. Because they refused twenty-hour shifts. Because Black and brown workers in particular spent a hundred years clawing back rights from the people who profited off their bodies, and the people who profited got tired of the negotiation.

So they invented something that could not negotiate.

And when that was not enough, they outsourced the parts the machine couldn’t do to the cheapest human labor on Earth and called it innovation. [LINK: Big Oil Owns America — Venezuela is just the latest victim]

The Joke and the Receipt

Loop it back, because all of this connects.

The etymology tells you the original ambition. Robots were imagined, from the first time anyone wrote the word down, as a class of beings built to do the work nobody wanted to do for the wages nobody wanted to pay.

The Bartneck study tells you that humans cannot stop themselves from racializing the things they build, even when there is no person in the room.

The TikTok discourse tells you that millions of people are using fake bigotry against fake people as a warm-up for the real thing, and calling it satire.

The Kenya investigation shows there is no fake version of any of this. The labor is real. The workers are real. The wages are real. And the people getting rich are exactly the people who have always gotten rich.

People who would never call a Black coworker a slur will gleefully call a Roomba one. People who would never reenact the back of the bus will happily film a TikTok about kicking a delivery robot off it. They are not doing this because they hate the machines.

They are doing it because the machines give them a place to put the part of themselves that is no longer socially acceptable.

The machine is the figleaf. And the joke and the receipt are the same document, written by the same hand, for the same purpose.

That is why I cannot get the video of the virtual whip out of my head. The man holding it thought he was performing dominance over a chatbot. He was actually performing the same pedagogy that had been taught to him before he was born, on a target the script had chosen for him.

So is everybody laughing.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Share
