author: Joshua Smith

title: You Love the Culture. You Just Don’t Know the Story.
seoTitle: You Love the Culture. You Just Don’t Know the Story.
excerpt: How TikTok Turns Black Pain Into Content and Calls It a Trend
metaDescription: How TikTok Turns Black Pain Into Content and Calls It a Trend
bodyHtml:
On December 30, 2025, a TikToker named @foreverhumblemarc96 posted a video set to the saxophone theme from Boyz n the Hood. The caption read something like “POV: You in a 90s hood movie about to move out the trenches but you hear them saxophones going crazy so you know you finna get slimed.” It got over 800,000 views in a week.

By January 2026, the trend was everywhere. Millions of videos. Millions of laughs. People running down hallways. SpongeBob edits. Draymond Green jokes.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Almost none of those videos mentioned what the saxophone was actually playing over: a 22-year-old Black man getting shot in the back while running for his life.

That is the scene. Ricky Baker, played by Morris Chestnut, a high school running back with a scholarship offer, is sprinting through an alley in South Central Los Angeles while Stanley Clarke’s saxophone score swells behind him. He never makes it. The bullet catches him. Tre, played by Cuba Gooding Jr., holds his body in the street. His mother screams.

John Singleton directed that scene in 1991. He was 23 years old. He became the youngest person and the first Black person ever nominated for the Academy Award for Best Director. He made a film about Black boys dying in a neighborhood that most of America would refuse to look at otherwise.

That is what the saxophone means.

And now it is a meme

Share

The Sound Without the Source

The saxophone trend follows a pattern that repeats so often you can set a clock by it. A piece of Black art gets created. It carries weight. It comes from a specific history and a specific pain. The internet discovers it, strips the context, and turns it into content. The original meaning disappears. By the time someone asks where it came from, the damage is done.

Take the Precious sound.

Mo’Nique delivered one of the most devastating performances in modern American cinema as Mary Jones, a woman who knew her daughter was being raped by her own father and did nothing, who then also abuses her daughter. The film is based on the novel Push by Sapphire. It won Mo’Nique the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress in 2010. Gabourey Sidibe earned a Best Actress nomination for playing Precious, a role that Hollywood almost never writes for Black women, a dark-skinned, plus-sized teenager surviving abuse in Harlem.

In the film’s climactic scene, a social worker, played by Mariah Carey, confronts Mary about what she allowed to happen. Mary, cornered, deflects. She delivers the now-famous line about how, “since you got your degree and you know everything.”

Before that scene starts, Precious meets Carey’s character, who asks her hard questions about her life, Precious deflects, turning the tables, and “says something like what are you, some kind of Black? Hispanic?” She is looking at a lighter-skinned woman sitting across from her in a welfare office and deciding that this woman does not get to hold her accountable because she does not look Black enough.

On TikTok, the whole scene became a punchline.

Both lines went viral. The “degree” line gets used when a friend gives unsolicited advice. When a partner corrects your grammar. When a coworker gets a promotion. The “Black Hispanic” line gets used to question anyone’s credibility about anything. A scene about child sexual abuse, about a mother standing by while it happened, about colorism tearing apart a room where a little girl’s life is being discussed, turned into a joke about gatekeeping.

The original audio went viral in March 2022 after a TikToker used it as a joke about a friend being too honest. Since then, hundreds of thousands of videos have used the sound. Almost none reference the film. Almost none mention Mo’Nique’s Oscar or the novel it was based on.

The same double standard that ranks Black skin in a welfare office follows Black women into casting rooms, newsrooms, and boardrooms. That complexity disappears when the line becomes a sound bite. The colorism becomes a vibe. The abuse becomes background noise. The pain gets extracted. The context gets deleted.

The Assembly Line

This pattern has a history much older than TikTok. American culture has always processed Black art the same way.

Rock and roll was Black music until it was not. Elvis performed Black songs to white audiences and became the King. Little Richard, Chuck Berry, and Sister Rosetta Tharpe built the genre. The recording industry made sure they did not own it.

Jazz was Black music until it became “America’s classical music.” The marketing changed. The credit did not follow.

Hip hop was Black music until corporations figured out that white suburban teenagers were the biggest consumers. Now the most-streamed rap playlists on Spotify are curated by algorithms that treat Blackness as an aesthetic and strip it of everything else. Even Jack Harlow can make an R&B album, with the title being a homophone for the n-word.

TikTok is the latest version of this machine. The platform’s algorithm does not care about context. It promotes engagement.

Jalaiah Harmon was 14 years old when she choreographed the Renegade dance on September 25, 2019. She posted it on Funimate, a small video-sharing app, and then on Instagram. It ended up on TikTok in October after another creator reposted a version with a few moves changed. Then it exploded. Charli D’Amelio and Addison Rae performed it. Lizzo performed it. Kourtney Kardashian performed it. They got the brand deals, the followers, the magazine features.

Harmon got nothing.

She commented on posts trying to tell people she made it. Nobody listened. She told the New York Times she could have gotten money, promos, recognition, but none of it happened because nobody knew she was the creator.

Then on February 15, 2020, the NBA invited D’Amelio and a group of TikTok creators to perform the Renegade at the All Star slam dunk contest. Harmon was not invited. It took public backlash for the NBA to give her a solo performance during the All Star Game the following night.

A 14-year-old Black girl had to fight for credit for her own choreography while white creators built careers off her work. Think about that.

Producer Polow da Don told the New York Times that the pattern is built into the platforms themselves. He said Dubsmash catches things at the roots when they are culturally relevant, and TikTok is where suburban kids take those things and bring them to their own community. The creation happens in one place. The credit lands in another.

In June 2021, Black creators held what amounted to a strike on TikTok. They refused to choreograph a dance for Megan Thee Stallion’s new single, protesting the lack of credit and compensation for Black labor within the creator economy. The fact that Black creators had to organize a work stoppage to be acknowledged on a platform that runs on their creativity tells you everything about who benefits from the system.

The Renegade is the template. Black creators build the trend. Non-Black creators scale it. The platform rewards the scale. And by the time anyone traces it back, the original creator is buried under a million copies.

BTS, Howard, and the Quiet Erasure

On March 12, 2026, BTS released an animated teaser for their fifth studio album, Arirang. The video depicted Howard University in 1896, referencing seven Korean students who attended the historically Black college and recorded what are believed to be the first Korean voices ever captured on audio in the United States. Three of the students recorded traditional Korean songs, including Arirang, with ethnologist Alice Fletcher. Those recordings now sit in the Library of Congress.

The story itself is worth telling. Howard University, founded in 1867, opened its doors to people who faced racial barriers at other institutions. Korean students found a home there because Howard had already built a tradition of welcoming international students of color. According to WETA’s Boundary Stones project, the men were known for their musical abilities and stunned the Howard community with a singing performance on their first night on campus.

But the animated teaser showed something different. The crowd on Howard’s campus was mostly white. At a school where the vast majority of full-time undergraduates are Black. At a school that formerly educated enslaved people. At a school that has been the center of Black intellectual life in America for over 150 years.

Howard’s own campus publication, The Dig, addressed it directly. They wrote that most of the people depicted in the audience on The Yard were not Black, and that this contradicted the institution’s history as one of the country’s foremost colleges with a predominantly Black student body. They also noted that the video showed the iconic Founders Library in the background, a building that was not constructed until the 1920s, decades after the events depicted.

A Howard student told The Hilltop that this has been a repeated pattern in the K-pop industry, pointing to a lack of nuance and a failure to educate themselves on different practices, hairstyles, appropriation, and what she called downright racism.

Alexander Hong, a Korean American Howard student, told The Dig that he admires how Howard opened its doors when no one else would accept the seven Korean students. But he also felt the animation used Howard more as a backdrop than as an institution with its own identity. He said they could have done a better job at showcasing what Howard actually is.

The story of seven Korean students recording Arirang at Howard in 1896 is a beautiful piece of shared history between two communities that both faced racial exclusion in America. You do not get to borrow Howard’s legacy to tell your story while making Howard’s students disappear from the frame.

Shaun Harper, a professor at the University of Southern California, wrote in Inside Higher Ed that companies and studios employ too few Black professionals in decision making capacities. Cultural errors like depicting Howard as a predominantly white campus are more likely when Black people are not meaningfully involved in content creation or invited to give input on projects that involve Black history.

The problem is systemic. It always is.

The Vocabulary You Borrowed

The extraction goes beyond sounds and visuals. It extends into the language itself.

AAVE, African American Vernacular English, is the backbone of internet culture. “Slay.” “No cap.” “It’s giving.” “Period.” “Ate that.” “Understood the assignment.” Every phrase that defines how people talk online came from Black communities. These expressions get adopted by the mainstream, stripped of their origin, and relabeled as “internet slang” or “Gen Z language.” The communities that created them get nothing.

A 2021 Newsweek report documented how non-Black creators on TikTok were mimicking Black women’s speech patterns and mannerisms to generate engagement. A Baylor University professor called it modern-day blackface, saying that non-Black people were assuming the identity of Black people online, the same way performers used to put on makeup and pretend.

Here is the part that never makes it into the TikTok discourse. The same language that gets celebrated on the internet gets punished in institutions.

In 2013, Rachel Jeantel took the stand in the trial of George Zimmerman, the man who killed Trayvon Martin. Jeantel was 19 years old. She was on the phone with Martin moments before he died. She testified for nearly six hours. She was the closest thing the prosecution had to a witness for Trayvon’s final moments.

She spoke in AAVE. The same dialect that wins Grammys when Kendrick Lamar uses it. The same syntax that goes viral when someone says “ate and left no crumbs” on TikTok. The same grammar that built every slang phrase the internet cannot stop repeating. Even 67, which originated with a rapper talking about his dead friend.

The jury did not bring up her testimony once during 16 hours of deliberation. Not once. Stanford linguists John Rickford and Sharese King later analyzed the case and found that jurors could not understand her. A juror told Anderson Cooper she found Jeantel “hard to understand” and “not credible.” Attorney Jonna Spilbor went on Fox News and called her “brutally ignorant.”

Rickford wrote that Jeantel spoke a highly systematic form of AAVE, with possible Caribbean influence, and that the features of her speech are shared by working-class African Americans across the country. He argued that the disregard for her speech reflected both dialect unfamiliarity and institutionalized racism.

The same language that makes a TikTok trend made a murder witness invisible.

The dialect that makes everyone laugh online gets a 19-year-old girl called ignorant on national television while she tries to tell a courtroom how her friend was killed.

America loves what Black people make. America does not love what Black people are.

What Knowing the Story Actually Looks Like

This is not a call to stop engaging with Black culture. That argument leads nowhere. Black culture is American culture. It built this country’s music, language, fashion, humor, athletics, and art. You cannot separate the two.

But engagement without knowledge is consumption. And consumption without credit is theft.

If you use the Boyz n the Hood saxophone as a joke and you have never seen the film, you are not sharing Black culture. If you animate a video at a historically Black college and fill the frame with white faces, you are overwriting the very history you claim to honor.

Knowing the story means understanding that the “degree” line and the “Black Hispanic” line come from the same conversation. A conversation about abuse, survival, and generational trauma packed into a welfare office. Mo’Nique and Gabourey Sidibe gave performances that Hollywood almost never makes room for. When you strip those performances down to a sound effect, you do to their art exactly what Mary does to Carey’s character in that scene. You take what you want and dismiss the rest.

You do not have to write a thesis every time you post a TikTok. But you do have to know what you are touching.

Every example in this piece follows the same structure.

When you laugh at the saxophone and do not know about Ricky, when you use the sound and do not know about Precious, when you borrow the language and do not know about Rachel Jeantel, you are doing what America has always done.

Taking the culture. Leaving the people.

Share

Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

author: Joshua Smith

title: AI Isn’t the Problem, The problem is who's using it and Why.
seoTitle: AI Isn’t the Problem, The problem is who's using it and Why.
excerpt: 3.2 Million People Are Watching Cartoon Fruit Date on TikTok. That’s the Crisis.
metaDescription: 3.2 Million People Are Watching Cartoon Fruit Date on TikTok. That’s the Crisis.
bodyHtml:
On March 14, 2026, a TikTok account called @ai.cinema021 posted the first episode of “Fruit Love Island.” AI-generated anthropomorphic fruits flirting, fighting, and coupling up in a knockoff dating show. Strawberina. Bananito. Orangelo. A banana with abs and a personality disorder.

Nine days later, the account had 3.2 million followers. Each episode averages 15 million views. The comment sections are full of people rooting for their favorite couples. “KIWILO AND MANGELLA NEED TO LOCK IN BROOOO,” one user wrote, with full sincerity, about two pieces of cartoon fruit generated by a language model.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

And on March 25th, 2026, OpenAI announced that it is shutting down Sora, its AI video-generation app. Six months after launch. Disney pulled out of their billion-dollar partnership. The app peaked in November at 3.3 million downloads and bled users every month after. The official reason is cost reallocation. The real reason is that the product had no reason to exist.

Good.

But Sora's death doesn’t fix the problem. The problem was never just the tool. The problem is the 3.2 million people who chose cartoon fruit over anything a human being has ever made.

Everyone is fighting about AI’s side effects. Water. Carbon. Art theft. Environmental racism. Those fights are real. Some of them matter. But they’re all about the branches. Nobody is talking about the root.

The root is that people can’t think anymore, not about the real water use, carbon use, or environmental racism. And AI is making it worse. I said this on the first day I started this Substack: the algorithm won’t save us. I didn’t know how right I was.

The Side Effects Everyone Argues About

Let me deal with these quickly because they’re clogging the conversation.

Water. A typical American golf course uses 312,000 gallons of water per day. There are almost 16,000 golf courses in the United States. Google’s entire global data center operation used 6.4 billion gallons in 2023, the equivalent of about 43 golf courses. According to a Morgan Stanley report, global annual water consumption by AI data centers is set to reach 1,068 billion liters by 2028, which is still less than that of golf courses in America. The water usage is high, but it is also proportional to the rise of the internet; the data centers that run AI also run Spotify, Netflix, Instagram, and your iCloud backups. Although some data centers use open systems that pump steam into the air, most use closed-loop cooling. The water cycles from the server to a radiator and back. And while the U.S. drops these facilities in the driest parts of the country, China launched the world’s first commercial underwater data center off the coast of Hainan in 2025, using seawater as a passive cooling system that cuts cooling energy use by up to 90%. The technology to do this better exists. America just doesn’t use it.

Environmental racism. This one is the biggest for me. Elon Musk’s xAI built its Colossus supercomputer in Boxtown, Memphis, a majority-Black neighborhood founded by formerly enslaved people after the Civil War. The facility ran 35 unpermitted gas turbines. Nitrogen dioxide levels spiked 79%. The area’s cancer rate is four times the national average. Across the South, more than $200 billion in planned data center infrastructure targets communities that have been treated as sacrifice zones for a century. Black Americans are 75% more likely to live near hazardous waste facilities. This is the same pattern I wrote about in Your Doorbell Is a Cop, it is the infrastructure of surveillance and extraction that always lands in the same neighborhoods. But that statistic comes from redlining, not from ChatGPT. Factories, highways, refineries, wastewater plants, all of them got there first. The same system that lets Big Oil own foreign policy lets Big Tech own Black neighborhoods. The data center is the latest tenant in a building that was always zoned for exploitation. If AI disappeared tomorrow, the next industry would move in by Tuesday.

Art. AI art is bad. It’s aesthetically dead. It has no intention, no perspective, no feeling. In an era where contemporary art is already devalued by those who cannot think critically about art. AI makes that worse. But the common claim that AI “steals” from artists is a misunderstanding of how these models work. They learn patterns from training data, the way a human art student studies other artists. The difference is that the human student produces something with a point of view. AI produces something that looks as if it were made by someone who has never felt anything. The real harm is that companies use AI art because it’s cheaper, which means real artists lose real work, and art that, for all of human history, has been a tool to fight fascism is no longer there. The problem isn’t theft. The problem is that bad “art” became good enough for people who never cared about art in the first place. It is also the gateway to deepfakes harming real people, misinformation campaigns, and, most unfortunately, harassing sexual content. It is why I jumped up and down when it was being shut down.

Share

Every one of these problems is real. And none of them is THE problem.

The Real Problem Is You Can’t Think Anymore.

If you are reading my Substack, I figure you can read and think, but AI is making it harder.

On January 29, 2025, American Airlines Flight 5342 collided with a military helicopter over the Potomac River. Sixty-seven people died. Within hours, AI-generated images of the crash flooded social media. Fake wreckage. Fake survivors. Fake news articles claiming relatives of Kid Rock and Bob Dylan died on board. People shared them, believed them, and engaged with them. One Louisville man who watched the AI slop spread said it was, “so ridiculous, but then you start looking at the comments on it, and people are actually believing this.”

In June 2025, Air India Flight 171 crashed in Ahmedabad, killing 269 people. Within hours, AI-generated videos of the burning wreckage went viral on TikTok. One man’s cousin died in the crash. He found out about the AI-generated video of her cremation before he finished grieving. People used the Ghibli filter to generate AI versions of a survivor sitting next to her dead partner after the Pahalgam attack. Content creators swapped the face of a murdered doctor in Kolkata into their videos for engagement. Real suffering turned into content. Real death turned into engagement bait.

On February 25, 2025, the President of the United States posted a 33-second AI-generated video to Truth Social. It showed the bombed-out ruins of Gaza transforming into a luxury resort called “Trump Gaza.” Golden statues of himself. Elon Musk dancing on a beach under a rain of cash. Shirtless Trump and Netanyahu sipping cocktails. Children running through rubble into a skyline of skyscrapers. The White House called the president “a visionary.” This is the same administration I wrote about in Who Controls What You Know, the one building a surveillance state with Palantir while defunding public media. Now they’re using AI-generated propaganda to sell ethnic cleansing as a real estate deal. Nobody in Gaza has consistent internet access. Tens of thousands of Palestinians have been killed. The territory is rubble. And the leader of the free world posted an AI cartoon of it as a real estate pitch.

Descartes said cogito ergo sum. I think, therefore I am. The very foundation of Western philosophy rests on the premise that the act of thinking is what makes you real.

What happens when 3.2 million people choose not to?

Your Brain Is Atrophying, and There’s Data to Prove It

For most of the 20th century, humanity was getting smarter. IQ scores rose about 3 points per decade from the 1930s to the 1990s. Researchers called it the Flynn Effect, after James Flynn, the political scientist who documented it. Better nutrition. Expanded education. Urbanization. The conditions that make people think harder were improving, so people thought harder.

Then it stopped.

Starting in the mid-1990s, IQ scores began declining in Norway, Denmark, Finland, France, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom. A 2018 study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences analyzed data from 730,000 Norwegian men and found that brothers born just a few years apart showed significant differences in IQ. Younger siblings scored lower. The decline couldn’t be explained by genetics. Even people from the same family, raised in the same house, were getting measurably less sharp. The researchers concluded the cause was environmental. The timing lines up almost exactly with the rise of Google, smartphones, and constant digital access.

Now layer AI on top of that.

A 2025 study by Michael Gerlich at SBS Swiss Business School surveyed 666 participants across age groups and found a significant negative correlation between AI tool use and critical thinking scores (r =- 0.68). The more someone used AI tools, the worse they performed on independent reasoning tasks. Younger users, ages 17 to 25, showed the strongest dependence on AI and scored the lowest. The study also found that increased trust in AI-generated content led to reduced independent verification of information. People stopped checking whether what they were reading was true. They just accepted it.

In research, this is called cognitive offloading. You let the machine do the thinking, and your brain stops practicing. In the same way, your legs atrophy if you stop walking.

MIT’s Media Lab ran an experiment in 2025 in which it split participants into three groups. One used ChatGPT to write essays, another used search engines, and the third used nothing but their own brains. The ChatGPT group finished faster. They also showed weaker brain connectivity, lower memory retention, and a fading sense of ownership over their own work. The researchers measured this with an EEG. The brains of the AI users were literally less active. The scariest part was that even after the AI group stopped using ChatGPT and tried to write on their own, their brain activity stayed sluggish. Once you outsource your thinking, your brain doesn’t just pick back up where it left off. It stays in low gear.

When students skip the steps of encoding, retrieval, and consolidation needed in essay writing, the actual brain work of learning and remembering, they build weaker neural pathways. They guess more. They grasp things less deeply. They think less flexibly.

A RAND Corporation survey found that nearly 7 in 10 middle and high school students said they were concerned that using AI for schoolwork was eroding their critical thinking skills. The students know this is hollowing them out. They do it anyway. Because it’s easier.

Harvard’s Gazette ran a piece in November 2025 asking faculty across disciplines whether AI is making us dumber. A Senior Philosphy Research Scholar wrote in the study, “I am very worried about the effects of general-use LLMs on critical reasoning skills. We already know that the tools we’re using during cognitive labor can change the ways that we do that work.” We already knew that taking notes by hand leads to better recall than typing. We already knew predictive text changes your word choices, and it's even worse when it's not just your essays but information and entertainment.

Fruit Love Island isn’t content. It is nothing, generated by nothing, consumed by people whose brains have been trained to accept nothing as entertainment. One TikTok commenter joked, “We ain’t gonna have no clean water next month because y’all wanna watch fruit love island.” The water thing isn't my biggest issue, but something is drying up.

The Honest Version

I work in tech. I use AI every day. I run an implementation checker that flags errors and security vulnerabilities in my code updates, and even if there aren't any, it helps. I’m not pretending I don’t benefit from this technology.

And I can see where it does really well. Doctors are using AI to run clinical trials on new drugs. Insilico Medicine reported positive Phase IIa results in 2025 for a fully AI-designed therapeutic for pulmonary fibrosis, marking the first time an AI-discovered molecule demonstrated clinical efficacy in human trials. AI can compress early drug discovery timelines by 30 to 40 percent, making a 4-year process take 13 months. Engineers use machine learning to model systems too complex for manual calculation. Researchers are processing genomic data, protein structures, and molecular interactions at speeds that would have taken decades of lab work ten years ago.

AI is good when smart people use it as a tool to extend what they already know how to do. An educated doctor who uses AI to cross-reference thousands of case studies with a patient’s symptoms to combat their own bias is practicing better medicine. An engineer who uses AI to simulate structural loads, combined with human mathematical checks, before building a bridge is doing safer engineering.

The problem is when the tool replaces the thinking instead of extending it. When a student uses ChatGPT to write an essay, they are supposed to struggle through it and learn the importance of literary works, critical thinking, and conducting research. When a company uses AI art, it’s cheaper than hiring someone to produce work with a unique perspective. When 3.2 million people watch a language model generate fruit drama and call it entertainment.

AI has been made the scapegoat for problems that capitalism created. AI is the latest tool in a system that was already broken. We blame the product for dying when the system was designed to strip it down to its parts. Blaming the tool lets the system off the hook. But AI is accelerating the one problem that is consistently at the front of my mind. We are becoming a society of people who cannot think for themselves. And we are choosing it. Voluntarily. Because it’s convenient.

The Convenience Trap

Every generation makes tradeoffs. We chose cars over public transit because cars were faster and more individual, even though they made cities unlivable. We chose streaming over theaters because streaming was easier, even though it hollowed out an entire industry. We chose social media over newspapers because feeds were free, even though that choice destroyed journalism's economic model. Every time, the tradeoff was quality, community, and critical engagement for speed and ease.

But there was always something on the other side of the deal. Netflix gave us entertainment. Cars gave us mobility. Social media gave us a connection, even if it was a degraded version.

What does Fruit Love Island give you? What does an AI-generated video of a plane crash give you? What does a cartoon of Gaza as a luxury resort give the people living under rubble?

UNESCO published a paper in 2025 about what they called “a crisis of knowing.” Their researchers argued that synthetic media isn’t just a misinformation problem. It’s an epistemological one. It destabilizes how humans establish what is true. When you can’t tell the difference between a real video and a generated one, you don’t just lose trust in the fake. You lose trust in everything. The Generative AI market is projected to grow 560% between 2025 and 2031. That means more content, produced faster, with less human involvement. Not better content. More content. And every piece of it makes it harder for you to tell what’s real.

That’s the tradeoff with AI. You get speed. You lose the ability to know what’s true.

What Actually Needs to Happen

The problem is education. Not STEM education. Not “learn to code” education. Philosophy. Ethics. Media literacy. The ability to look at a piece of content and ask, who made this, why did they make it, what does it want from me, and is any of it true? I watched a TikTok where a teacher asked students to write an essay, and this student used Wikipedia as a primary source.

In 2024, a Gallup survey found that Americans’ confidence in higher education hit a record low, with only 36% expressing strong confidence. Philosophy departments have been gutted across the country. The humanities are treated as less valuable. And the result is a population that watches AI-generated fruit date and calls it entertainment.

But it goes deeper than curriculum. We need to teach people how their brains actually work, including how they retrieve, integrate, and recognize patterns. When you repeatedly pull information out of your own head, your brain builds durable pathways. Those pathways are what let you reason, spot errors, and make connections across domains. When you skip that process and instead ask a chatbot, you lose capacity.

Schools need assignments that require students to think without AI. The same way you can’t get stronger by watching someone else lift weights, you can’t get smarter by watching a language model generate your arguments for you. Gerlich’s study found that higher education was associated with better reasoning abilities, even among heavy AI users. Education works, but it has to be the right kind.

We need to stop treating memorization like it’s beneath us. Somewhere along the way, American education decided that rote learning was old-fashioned. Oakley’s team found that when students rely on external tools too early, before they’ve built foundational knowledge, they skip the cognitive steps that build flexible thinking. You have to know things before you can think critically about them. A doctor who Googles every symptom is not the same as a doctor who has internalized thousands of cases and can see patterns without looking them up. The same applies to every field. Including mine.

And we need to get honest about what AI is for. AI is good at processing data. Translating languages. Running simulations. Screening millions of compounds for drug candidates. It is bad at thinking because it doesn’t think. It predicts the next word in a sequence based on patterns it absorbed from text written by people who did think. When you use it as a shortcut for the work for your brain, you’re being negligent with the one organ that makes you human.

Sora is dead. Thank God. But the thing that made Sora possible, a culture that values convenience over thought, speed over craft, engagement over meaning, is still running. And no amount of carbon offsets or closed-loop cooling systems will fix it.

The crisis isn’t artificial intelligence.

The crisis is a natural kind. The kind between your ears. The kind you’re choosing not to use.

author: Joshua Smith

title: Everything Except the Crown: How the Academy Keeps Rewarding Black Art Without Rewarding Black Directors
seoTitle: Everything Except the Crown: How the Academy Keeps Rewarding Black Art Without Rewarding Black Directors
excerpt: Ryan Coogler won his first Oscar. Michael B. Jordan won his first Oscar. The most nominated film in Academy history won four. And still, no Black director has ever won Best Director. Not once.
metaDescription: Ryan Coogler won his first Oscar. Michael B. Jordan won his first Oscar. The most nominated film in Academy history won four. And still, no Black director has e
bodyHtml:
On March 15, 2026, Paul Thomas Anderson walked to the stage at the Dolby Theatre and picked up his first Oscar for Best Director. He thanked the Academy. He joked about the wait. “You make a guy work hard for one of these things,” he said, referencing four previous nominations across a career that includes There Will Be Blood, Phantom Thread, and Licorice Pizza.

He earned it, even with my critiques of the movie. One Battle After Another is one of the most critically acclaimed films of the year. PTA adapted Thomas Pynchon into something coherent, political, and funny. That alone is worth at least a chance at the trophy he’s deserved.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

But I need to tell you what else happened that night.

Ryan Coogler’s Sinners came into the ceremony with 16 nominations. The most for any single film in Oscar history. It won four. Coogler took home Best Original Screenplay. Michael B. Jordan won Best Actor. Autumn Durald Arkapaw became the first woman to ever win Best Cinematography. Ludwig Goransson won Best Original Score.

Coogler is an Oscar winner. Jordan is an Oscar winner. History was made in multiple categories.

And for the 98th consecutive year, a Black director did not win Best Director.

I wrote about this pattern in

The Pattern Has a Name

Three nights. Three decades. The same result.

1990. Driving Miss Daisy wins Best Picture. A film about a white woman and her Black driver. Directed by Bruce Beresford. Four Oscars, including Best Picture.

That same year, Spike Lee released Do the Right Thing. A film about racial tension, reflected in the heat in Brooklyn, that ends with a man being killed by police. Radio Raheem choked to death on screen in 1989. The Academy does not nominate it for Best Picture. Does not nominate Lee for Best Director.

Driving Miss Daisy is about a white woman learning that her Black driver is a person. Do the Right Thing is about a neighborhood that burns because the system never bothered to learn that first.

The Academy picked the driver.

2019. Green Book wins Best Picture. Peter Farrelly directs a film where a racist Italian learns tolerance over a road trip, and the Academy calls it progress. Don Shirley’s family said the film distorted his life. The movie is named after The Negro Motorist Green Book, but it is not about Victor Hugo Green. It is about a white man’s feelings.

That same year, Spike Lee’s BlacKkKlansman loses. A film that connects 1970s Klan infiltration to the 2017 Charlottesville rally. A film that ends with real footage of Heather Heyer’s murder. A film that does not let you leave the theater comfortable.

Backstage, Lee holds up his champagne glass. “Every time somebody is driving somebody, I lose.”

I wrote about this loss and the history of it in one of my first articles:

2026. One Battle After Another wins Best Picture and Best Director. Sinners, with more nominations than any film in history, wins four but not the top two. PTA swept the precursor circuit. He won the DGA, the BAFTA, the Critics Choice, and the Golden Globe. The statistical case is airtight.

But here is the context in which those stats live: Ryan Coogler became the seventh Black director nominated for Best Director. Seven. In nearly a century. The full list is John Singleton, Lee Daniels, Steve McQueen, Barry Jenkins, Jordan Peele, Spike Lee, and now Coogler.

Seven nominees. Zero winners.

The question is not whether PTA deserved his Oscar. He did. The question is why, when a Black film dominates every other category, the one award that says “you led this” keeps going to someone else.

The Trauma Economy

There is another pattern running underneath the Best Director drought. And it explains a lot about how the Academy treats Black art.

The Academy has a preference for which Black stories it rewards. And that preference has a name.

Trauma.

Hattie McDaniel became the first Black person to win an Oscar in 1940. She played Mammy in Gone with the Wind. She was forced to sit separately from her white castmates at the ceremony. The Academy’s first recognition of Black talent came through a role built on the stereotypes Black actors would spend decades trying to escape.

That was the template.

Denzel Washington has two Oscars. He lost for Malcolm X, where he played a fundamental Black revolutionary who transformed himself and his community. He won for Training Day, one of my favorite 90s movies, where he played a corrupt cop spiraling into destruction. The Academy told him, “We don’t want to watch you lead. We want to watch you fall.”

Halle Berry won Best Actress for Monster’s Ball in 2002. She remains the only Black woman to win that award. Twenty-four years later. The role required her character’s trauma and exploitation to fuel a white character’s redemption arc. After she won, she said, “While I was wildly proud of it, I was still Black that next morning.”

Lupita Nyong’o won for 12 Years a Slave, playing an enslaved woman brutalized on screen for nearly two hours. When she starred in Jordan Peele’s Us six years later, playing a complex, terrifying dual role in a genre film comparable to what MBJ just won for? The Academy didn’t nominate her.

Mo’Nique won for Precious. Octavia Spencer for The Help. Viola Davis for Fences. Suffering. Servitude. Domestic pain. The Academy knows what it likes.

A study from the BlackLight Collective and McKinsey found that the majority of Oscar nominations that go to Black writers, directors, and producers are for films about racism in America. More than half featured storylines about the criminal justice system. Ammanuel Zegeye from McKinsey put it plainly, “People either want poverty or ‘Wakanda,’ and nothing in between.”

And the films that don’t just center on pain? Do the Right Thing. Eve’s Bayou. Love Jones. Girls Trip. Sorry to Bother You. Black films that don’t focus on suffering have been consistently overlooked by the Academy. Not because they lack quality, most of these sit above 90% on Rotten Tomatoes. The pattern reflects how Hollywood taste, shaped by assumptions about what “serious” Black art looks like, determines which stories get recognition.

This is what Sinners has most effectively broken the mold on.

Sinners is a horror film. It has vampires. It has a juke joint. It has blues music, dancing, community, humor, and Black people being fully alive before the monsters show up. Yes, it is set during the Jim Crow era. Yes, the vampires are a metaphor for colonization and exploitation. But the heart of the movie is not suffering. The heart is the music. The connection. The joy of being in a room full of Black people creating something that belongs to them.

Coogler made a film that refuses to translate its cultural references or soften its Black specificity. It does not ask for permission. It does not cause pain, so the Academy feels comfortable giving it a trophy.

And the Academy gave it everything except the crown.

When Black films perform trauma, the Academy says, “this is important.” When Black films perform joy, the Academy says, “This is entertaining.” Neither gets Best Director. But the trauma gets closer. And that is its own kind of cage.

Marsai Martin has a “No Black Pain” rule at her production company. Jordan Peele builds horror films that comment on race without romanticizing suffering. Coogler made a vampire movie where the scariest thing is not the monsters. It is the history the monsters represent.

Can the Academy recognize Black art that does not ask them to feel sorry for Black people? Sinners proved the audience is ready. $368 million says so. Sixteen nominations say so.

The directors’ branch said no.

What They Give You Instead

The Academy has developed a specific way of honoring Black filmmakers. It celebrates the work. It hands out writing, acting, scoring, and cinematography awards. And it withholds the one award that says, “you were the best director in this room.”

Spike Lee. Never won Best Director. Won Adapted Screenplay for BlacKkKlansman in 2019. Received an honorary Oscar in 2016. The Academy said, We love your career. We love your legacy. We will never say you directed a film better than everyone else in a given year. He has been making films since 1986. He is 69 years old. He will not win Best Director. That window is probably closed.

Barry Jenkins. Moonlight won Best Picture in 2017, after the La La Land envelope mix-up made it the most dramatic moment in Oscar history. Jenkins lost Best Director to Damien Chazelle for La La Land, making him the youngest winner. The film that the Academy said was the best picture of the year was apparently not well enough directed to win Best Director, despite 72 of the 93 awards going to that category. The same voters who ranked Moonlight number one on their Best Picture ballot gave the directing award to the film that came in second.

Steve McQueen. 12 Years a Slave won Best Picture in 2014. McQueen lost Best Director to Alfonso Cuaron for Gravity. Same pattern. The Black film wins. The Black director doesn’t.

Ryan Coogler. Sinners got 16 nominations. It won four Oscars. Coogler won the WGA, the BAFTA for Original Screenplay, and the Critics Choice. One Battle After Another won six, including Best Picture, Best Director, Best Adapted Screenplay, Best Supporting Actor, Best Casting, and Best Editing.

The Academy will celebrate the work. It will reward the writing, the acting, the score, and the cinematography.

It will not say “a Black man directed this better than everyone else this year.”

This Night Was Special

I need to stop here and say something before this article becomes only about what didn’t happen. Because what did happen on March 15 was real. I felt it.

Michael B. Jordan won Best Actor.

I screamed. My roommate did not jump, and I appreciate him for that. From Fruitvale Station to Creed to Black Panther to here. Five films with Coogler. A partnership that began at Sundance and led to the Dolby Theatre.

The press room lost it. A CBC reporter who has covered the Oscars for over a decade said members of the press were on their feet. Jordan started his speech with “God is great,” and the room responded like a church service. “That’s right.” “Speak on it.”

He thanked Coogler. “You gave me the opportunity and space for me to be seen.” He thanked the ancestors. He asked his cast and crew to stand. From the kid on The Wire and The Sopranos to the man holding an Oscar for playing twin brothers in a vampire movie set during Jim Crow. That is a career.

And this was a genre film. Horror. Vampires. The last actor to win for something close to a genre role was probably Joaquin Phoenix for Joker, and that film shed most of its comic-book identity to fit into a dramatic mold. Before that, Anthony Hopkins in The Silence of the Lambs. Sinners did not shed anything. It leaned in. Jordan won for a horror film that is proudly, unapologetically a horror film.

Autumn Durald Arkapaw became the first woman and Black person to win Best Cinematography.

That the Academy has existed for nearly a century and never given this award to a woman is its own disgrace. But Arkapaw’s win was the right way to break it open. The first film to combine 70mm IMAX and Ultra Panavision 70. She gave Black life the cinematic grandeur it always deserved.

When her name was called, Coogler ran to the back of the theater and brought her son Aidan to the front of the stage so he could watch his mom make history. I don’t know what the room sounded like from inside the Dolby, but from my bed, it sounded like a revival. She thanked Coogler first. “Whenever I say thank you to Ryan, he replies and says, ‘No, thank you. Thank you for believing in me and thank you for trusting me.’ That’s the kind of guy I get to make films with.”

Ludwig Goransson won Best Original Score. His third Oscar. His second for a Coogler film. A live band performed with a Black conductor. These two met at USC film school. They have made every film together since. Blues, gospel, and Delta South are the foundation and lifeblood of Sinners, making the score inseparable from the movie.

Coogler won Best Original Screenplay. The WGA confirmed it. The BAFTA confirmed it. The Academy confirmed it. Coogler made it work, and he far and away earned it. If you want to read my thoughts about the movie, there are other articles for that.

And the “I Lied to You” performance. Miles Caton and Raphael Saadiq on stage. Misty Copeland. Christone Kingfish Ingram. Bobby Rush. Buddy Guy. Brittany Howard. Crip walkers, ballet dancers, and Zaouli performers sharing the same stage. I got the same chills I got the first time I heard it in the theater. That performance showed you what Sinners is about better than any acceptance speech could.

Four Oscars. First Black Best Actor win for a horror film. First woman to win Cinematography. Coogler’s first Oscar. Goransson’s third. It was an amazing night for film.

Quick Notes on the Ceremony

Conan O’Brien was good for year two. Opened dressed as Aunt Gladys from Weapons, got chased through clips by schoolchildren. The energy was lighter than last year. He earned the “Host for Life” joke at the end.

The tribute to Rob Reiner broke me. Billy Crystal eulogized his friend. They brought out actors from his films. The Princess Bride is still my favorite. I watch it with my dad every time I go home.

Barbra Streisand closed the In Memoriam segment with a tribute to Robert Redford. She reminded the room that Redford created Sundance, the institution that launched the careers of both PTA and Coogler. Both of the night’s biggest winners started at the same festival, built by the same man.

The International Film presenter stood up and said “no to war” and “Free Palestine.” I liked him immediately.

Jessie Buckley won Best Actress for Hamnet. Complete sweep. She won every major precursor. One of my favorite films of the year, based on one of my favorite works of literature. I am claiming my 10% Irish with this win.

KPop Demon Hunters won Best Animated Feature. Golden won Best Original Song. The first K-pop song to win. I knew that when Lionel Richie kept his composure during the presentation, it was going to be golden, not “I lied to you”.

Mr. Nobody Against Putin won Best Documentary Feature. A reminder that people have a responsibility to stand up when fascism is on the rise. The same forces in that film are the same ones reemerging here.

I went 12 for 21 on predictions. Not bad. Not perfect. A good night.

Where This Goes

This was the best night for Black film at the Oscars in years. Four wins for Sinners, and all of them historic.

And it was the 98th year a Black director did not win Best Director.

People are already comparing Coogler’s trajectory to Spielberg’s. Spielberg made Jaws, Raiders of the Lost Ark, E.T., and The Color Purple before he won Best Director for Schindler’s List. That took nearly 20 years. PTA joked about the long wait during his speech. The assumption is that Coogler will get another shot. Black Panther 3 is reportedly next.

But that framing asks Black filmmakers to be patient with an institution that has had nearly a century to get this right. Spike Lee has been making films for 40 years. He directed Malcolm X, Do the Right Thing, 25th Hour, and BlacKkKlansman. He never won Best Director. He is not going to. The window closed while the Academy was telling him to wait.

John Singleton was nominated at 24 for Boyz n the Hood. He remains the youngest and first black Best Director nominee in history. He died at 51. He never won.

The patience argument assumes the system will correct itself. The history says it won’t. Not without pressure. Not without the kind of work Coogler, Jordan, Arkapaw, and Goransson did this year. Not without audiences showing up the way they showed up for Sinners, making sure theaters were sold out. Not without writers, critics, and people on Substack pointing out the gap between what the Academy celebrates and what it rewards at the top.

I watched Michael B. Jordan say to the audience, “I love you,” and mean it. I watched Autumn Durald Arkapaw’s son see his mother make history. I watched Ryan Coogler run through the Dolby Theatre to bring that boy to the front of the stage. I watched Ludwig Goransson conduct a live band and make the whole room feel moved by something that no acceptance speech could.

Those are real victories. And they happened within a system that has never once acknowledged that a Black director was the best director in the room.

I predicted this in The Asterisk People. Audiences already answered the question. $368 million. Sixteen nominations. The actors voted for Jordan. The writers voted for Coogler. The cinematographers voted for Arkapaw. The composers voted for Goransson.

The directors voted for PTA.

Everything except the crown.

Share

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
author: Joshua Smith

title: The Pitt Breakdown: Joyce St. Claire and the Assumption That Black Pain Is Fake
seoTitle: The Pitt Breakdown: Joyce St. Claire and the Assumption That Black Pain Is Fake
excerpt: How sickle cell disease is underdiagnosed and undertreated, and what that reveals about how the system ignores Black pain.
metaDescription: How sickle cell disease is underdiagnosed and undertreated, and what that reveals about how the system ignores Black pain.
bodyHtml:
The Pitt is the best drama on television right now. Not hyperbole.

The show follows the staff of a Pittsburgh emergency room in real time. One season. One shift. Fifteen hours. Every episode covers one hour on the clock. Season 1 won five Emmys, including Outstanding Drama Series, Lead Actor for Noah Wyle, and Supporting Actress for Katherine LaNasa. It became the first medical procedural to win best drama since ER in 1996. It swept two Golden Globes. Season 2 premiered in January 2026 to 5.4 million viewers in three days, a 200% increase from the series debut. It now averages roughly 12 million viewers per episode and has already been renewed for a third season.

It earned all of it.

What makes it different from every medical drama you have watched? The Pitt does not just show you sick people getting treated. It shows you why they got sick. Why did they wait so long? Why did the system that was supposed to catch them let them fall? Every patient is an American policy failure walking through the door.

Season 1 takes place over one day in September. I am going to break it down piece by piece. The characters. The conditions. The real data behind what you are watching.

This is The Pitt Breakdown.

Subscribe now

A Black woman is wheeled into the emergency room screaming. She is in so much pain that she cannot hold still. She is restrained. She is begging for medication.

The paramedics have already made their diagnosis. They describe her as drug-seeking. Uncooperative. Combative. There are two white men delivering a Black woman in agony, and the story they are telling is that she is an addict looking for opioids.

Her name is Joyce St. Claire. She has sickle cell disease. She has had it her entire life. And the first people tasked with getting her to a hospital decided she was faking it before they ever checked.

What Happens on the Show

Joyce arrives at Pittsburgh Trauma Medical Center in Season 1, Episode 2. The paramedics describe her to the staff the way you describe a problem, not a patient. She was kicked off a city bus for disrupting other passengers. She has been screaming for narcotics. She called 911 because her medications at home stopped working.

Dr. Samira Mohan takes her case. Mohan listens. She asks Joyce what is wrong. Joyce tells her she has sickle cell disease. Mohan does not hesitate. She administers IV Dilaudid and morphine. She orders an exchange transfusion. She asks about Joyce’s pain regimen at home. Joyce takes 90 milligrams of extended-release morphine every 12 hours with oxycodone for breakthrough pain, and it has not been enough.

This is not a woman looking for a high. This is a woman whose red blood cells are shaped like crescent moons instead of discs, jamming together in her blood vessels like a traffic accident happening inside her body, cutting off oxygen to her organs, and producing pain that patients describe as feeling like their bones are being sawed apart. She needs medication because she is in a medical crisis. The paramedics looked at a Black woman in pain, asking for painkillers, and saw an addict.

Mohan does something else in this episode. She turns to her colleagues and names the bias. Many white doctors, she explains, do not have experience treating sickle cell disease and underestimate the severity of the pain. That lack of familiarity combines with racial bias to create a pattern where sickle cell patients are consistently undertreated.

Later in the season, Mohan encounters a white male patient she suspects is drug-seeking and gives him opiate blockers without his knowledge. The show does not let the audience off the hook. The same doctor who correctly identified bias against a Black sickle cell patient made her own judgment call about a white patient’s pain. The line between clinical instinct and bias is thinner than anyone wants to admit.

The Disease Nobody Funds

Sickle cell disease affects approximately 100,000 Americans. About 1 in every 365 Black American births results in sickle cell disease. About 1 in 13 Black American babies carries the sickle cell trait. It is overwhelmingly a disease of Black people in this country.

That fact explains almost everything about how the disease is treated, studied, and funded.

Sickle cell research receives less than half the federal funding per patient than cystic fibrosis research. Cystic fibrosis predominantly affects white Americans of Northern European descent. It affects roughly 40,000 people in the US, compared to sickle cell disease’s 100,000. The disease with more patients and fewer dollars is the one that affects Black people.

People with sickle cell disease live an average of 20 years less than the general population. There are over 200,000 sickle cell-related emergency department visits every year in the United States. The ER is often the only option because only 20 percent of family physicians say they feel comfortable treating sickle cell disease. When your primary care doctor does not know how to manage your condition, the emergency room becomes your doctor’s office. And the emergency room is where the bias lives.

A national study of ER visits from 2003 to 2008 found that patients with sickle cell disease waited 25 percent longer to see a physician than the general patient population, despite presenting with higher pain levels and being assigned more urgent triage ratings. When researchers compared sickle cell patients specifically to patients with long bone fractures, another extremely painful condition, sickle cell patients waited 50 percent longer. And the disparity held even after controlling for triage level.

The researchers found that both the patient's race and the disease itself contributed to the delay. Being Black made you wait longer. Having sickle cell made you wait longer. Being a Black person with sickle cell disease combined both penalties, and makes this gap worse.

Share

The Pain Gap

In 2016, a study at the University of Virginia surveyed white medical students and residents. Half of them endorsed at least one false belief about biological differences between Black and white patients. Some believed Black people’s nerve endings were less sensitive. Some believed Black skin was literally thicker. These are not fringe beliefs held by bad doctors taught by bad professors. They are embedded in the training.

The medical professionals who believed Black patients felt less pain were less likely to prescribe adequate pain medication to Black patients. The bias was not a feeling. It was a dosage.

For sickle cell patients, the consequences are specific and documented. Medical providers have described sickle cell patients using language like “over-reporting of pain,” “drug-seeking behavior,” and “frustrating to care for.” Both adults and adolescents with sickle cell disease report feeling stigmatized, having their pain dismissed, and being labeled as addicts in healthcare settings. Because you're Black, they try to assume that you're coming there to get like pain medicine and stuff, when really you just wanna get treated for the pain.

The opioid crisis made it worse. Every guideline has tightened its stance on pain medication. Every protocol added another layer of scrutiny. And the scrutiny fell hardest on the patients who were already being undertreated and seen as drug seeking. The signs of drug-seeking that doctors are trained to look for include asking for opioid medications by name, making multiple visits for the same complaint, having symptoms out of proportion to what an exam shows, and seeking treatment at a hospital. Every single one of those criteria describes a sickle cell patient in crisis. The disease itself looks like addiction to a doctor who does not know better. And most doctors do not know better.

Dr. Sylvia Owusu-Ansah, an emergency physician at UPMC Children’s Hospital of Pittsburgh who consulted on The Pitt, told producers about a 17-year-old sickle cell patient who was unfairly restrained. When she watched the first episode of the show, she saw that moment reflected almost exactly as she had described it. The show did not invent this story. It copied it from real life.

What the Show Is Actually Saying

Joyce St. Claire is in the emergency room for maybe twenty minutes of screen time. She is not a main character. She does not return. But the show uses her to establish something that carries through both seasons, the American healthcare system does not believe Black pain.

That is the foundation. Everything else that The Pitt builds about racial disparities in healthcare sits on top of that scene. Jackson Davis is being tased during a psychotic break. The Black woman whose eating disorder goes undiagnosed until Dr. King catches it. The ongoing absence of Dr. Collins, the Black senior resident, has left a gap that the show has struggled to fill. All of it traces back to the same root. Black bodies in distress are often not read as patients but as problems.

Mohan is the correction the show offers. A doctor of color who recognizes what the paramedics missed because she has the clinical knowledge and the lived experience to see past the assumption. The show makes a specific argument through her character that diversity in medicine should not be overlooked; it can help save patients. Mohan caught the sickle cell because she knew what it looked like. The paramedics missed it because they only knew what addiction looked like.

But The Pitt is honest enough to complicate that argument, too. Mohan is not perfect. Her own biases show up with the white patient later. The show refuses to let any character be the clean hero. Everyone carries assumptions. The question is whether the system corrects for them or amplifies them.

For Joyce St. Claire, the system amplified them. She was restrained, dismissed, and labeled before anyone listened. It took one doctor who knew better to change the outcome.

The next sickle cell patient who walks into an ER without a Samira Mohan on shift does not get that story.

That is what The Pitt wants you to think about.

Subscribe now

Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Share

author: Joshua Smith

title: Pray For Me, Not With Me: Knives out Wake Up Dead Man, The Most Honest Film About American Christianity in Years
seoTitle: Pray For Me, Not With Me: Knives out Wake Up Dead Man, The Most Honest Film About American Christianity in Years
excerpt: Rian Johnson made a murder mystery. He also made the best argument for faith I have seen on screen.
metaDescription: Rian Johnson made a murder mystery. He also made the best argument for faith I have seen on screen.
bodyHtml:
Halfway through Wake Up Dead Man, Father Jud gets a phone call. He and Benoît Blanc are in the middle of an investigation. They need a lead from a construction company about who ordered equipment to open a family tomb. The clerk who answers is Louise, played by Bridget Everett. She is chatty. She cannot get to the point. Blanc is in the background making “wrap it up” gestures. The scene plays for comedy, and it drags just enough that the audience starts to fidget, too.

Then Louise pauses.

“Hey, Father, could I ask you something?”

Jud, still in detective mode, Blanc still hovering, says something like, “If you can make it quick.”

“Father, could you... would you...”

She trails off. The line goes quiet. Then, barely above a whisper. “Oh God.”

Jud says her name. “Louise.”

“Will you pray for me?”

Not with me. For me.

“Pray with me” is a communal phrase. It puts you on semi-equal footing. “Pray for me” is something else entirely. It says I cannot do this myself. I need someone to carry this to God on my behalf. I am too broken, too tired, too alone to make the trip.

Her mother has a brain tumor. They had a fight. Louise said things she cannot take back and fears they are the last words she will ever say to her mom. She is not a churchgoer. She once attended one of Monsignor Wicks’ masses and was ousted by his sermon. And now she is on the phone with a stranger who happens to be a priest, asking him to do the one thing she cannot do for herself.

Jud’s entire demeanor changes. He turns away from Blanc. Away from the investigation, the clue, his own legal peril. He walks into the next room. Closes the door. Asks Louise for her mother’s name.

And he prays.

She says she feels alone, and he responds, “You are not alone. I am right here.”

Two people on a phone line. No stained glass, no pulpit, no offering plate. Just a clerk in an office and a priest in a stranger’s house, asking God to be present for someone who doesn't believe.

That is more church than Monsignor Wicks ran in sixty years. And that phone call is the argument of the entire film.

The Woman at the Gas Station

I wrote about this before. In my essay on Kendrick Lamar’s “How Much a Dollar Cost,” a homeless man at a gas station in South Africa asks Kendrick for sixty cents. Kendrick says no. Runs the man through every calculation we have been taught to run, deciding whether this person deserves his time. The man reveals himself as God. The cost of that refusal is Kendrick’s spot in heaven.

Louise is the woman at the gas station.

She is not asking for money. She is asking for someone’s time. The test is the same. Do you see the person in front of you, or do you see an inconvenience?

When Louise calls, Jud has been getting swept up in Blanc’s game. Sorting the guilty from the innocent. Playing detective. He is so deep in the game that he shattered Christ on a table and did not flinch. Louise pulls him back. She reminds him that his purpose as a priest is not to fight the wicked but to serve the wounded.

A Catholic priest who consulted on the script wrote that he has started calling moments like these “Louise moments.” The times when you are in a rush, in the middle of something important, and someone needs you. The instinct is to push through. The calling is to stop.

That is the only thing the film is trying to convince you of. It does not care whether you believe in God, or whether the church is good, or whether the rituals mean anything. It cares about this: when someone is alone and afraid and asks you to pray for them, you stop what you are doing and pray.

If you cannot believe in God, believe in loving people.

The Song and the Question

All three Knives Out films borrow their titles from songs. Radiohead. The Beatles. This one takes from U2’s “Wake Up Dead Man,” where Bono, a lifelong Christian, begs Jesus to return. He is not reverent about it. He is exhausted. It is one of the few times U2 uses profanity in their discography. The world is full of hurt and silence, and he wants to know if anyone is listening.

The film asks the same thing. And the answer it gives has nothing to do with the church.

The Fighter and the Fraud

Father Jud, played by Josh O’Connor, is a young priest and former boxer transferred to a dying parish in upstate New York called Our Lady of Perpetual Fortitude. He is there as punishment for punching a deacon. That is how the movie starts. A priest throwing hands.

The reason he became a priest was guilt. He killed a man in the ring. He did not stop when he should have because he had hate in his heart, and that is his original sin. He carried it out of the ring and into the priesthood. He went from fighting against something to fighting for something.

He arrives to find Monsignor Jefferson Wicks, played by Josh Brolin, running the church like a cult leader. Wicks preaches fear. He singles out newcomers until they leave. He manipulates a woman with chronic pain into giving her savings by implying God will heal her if she pays enough. He uses confession as a weapon, graphically detailing fake sins to Jud just to mess with him. He calls the mother of his own illegitimate son “a loose woman of no significance.” The man who preached purity from the pulpit fathered a child out of wedlock and dismissed the woman as nothing.

You have seen this version of Christianity before. On cable news. In megachurches where the pastor drives a Bentley while the congregation goes hungry. In school board meetings where someone waves a Bible while banning books. Rian Johnson grew up evangelical and has said the film is deeply personal. The church he depicts is fictional. The Christianity Wicks practices is not.

Wicks’ followers do not practice Catholicism. There are no sacraments shown, no communion, no real liturgy. They follow Wicks, not the faith. That is the distinction the film draws between a church built around Christ and a church built around one man’s ego.

And this is what separates the two priests. Wicks treats sin as a stain. You break the rules, you get punished. The world is full of wolves, and the job of the faithful is to find them and cast them out. His God keeps score. His forgiveness comes with conditions.

Jud treats sin as a wound. You are hurt, and you need tending. The world is full of wounded people, and the job of the faithful is to sit with them and carry what they cannot carry alone. His God is a healer.

“You start fighting wolves,” Jud says in his first real line, “and before you know it, everyone you don’t understand is a wolf.”

Wicks spent sixty years looking for wolves. Jud just sees people.

The Tattoo and the Cross

Here is one of the film’s best visual details. Jud has a tattoo on his neck. A devil and an angel, with the word “Serendipity” underneath, peeking out from behind his priestly collar. His past, always visible, never fully covered. O’Connor described it as a physical representation of his spirit being a fighter, that little bit of his history that will not stay hidden.

In one of the debates between the two priests, Jud explains why he believes. God did not hide him. God did not fix him. God loves him while he is guilty. That is the version of Christianity this film argues for. Not the version where you perform righteousness and hide your sins in confession, but the version where you carry your hurt openly and let grace meet you there.

Wicks, by contrast, refused to replace the crucifix his mother destroyed years ago. He left the outline on the wall as a reminder of her shame. His theology is punishment. Scorekeeping. Making sure everyone remembers who sinned and how badly.

And then there is the Christ figurine. During the frantic search for clues, Jud shatters a porcelain figurine of Jesus without hesitation. He does not think twice. He is so caught up in Blanc’s detective game, sorting the guilty from the innocent, that he destroys the very image of the God he serves. It surprises even Blanc. When you stop being a priest and start being a prosecutor, the first casualty is Christ himself.

He realizes what he has done. And in the final scene, he carves a new crucifix by hand from a tree. He places Eve’s Apple, the diamond at the center of the mystery, inside the body of Christ on the cross. All that sin, all that greed, all that generational destruction, sealed within the figure that was supposed to carry it all along.

The Names Tell You Everything

The names of the two most important women in the movie carry their entire stories.

Grace Wicks, played by Annie Hamilton, was the woman Wicks called a “harlot whore” from the pulpit for decades. Her name is the theological virtue that means both forgiveness and atonement. She was trapped by her father’s false promise of an inheritance, kept at the church against her will. When the money turned out to be a lie, she destroyed the place and died at the door of a mausoleum. The church branded her a demon. Jud is the first person in sixty years to recognize what she went through.

He renames the church after her. Our Lady of Perpetual Fortitude becomes Our Lady of Perpetual Grace.

Martha Delacroix, played by Glenn Close, goes deeper. Martha means “lady of the house” in Aramaic, and two biblical Marthas matter here.

The Martha in Luke 10 served while Mary sat at Jesus’ feet. So busy maintaining the household that she missed the point of who was in the room. Martha Delacroix served Wicks for decades. Ran the church, managed the secrets, controlled the narrative. She was the house. And like the biblical Martha, she was so busy keeping the operation running that she missed everything. Also, a representation of the overuse of women’s labor in the church without praise.

The Martha in John is the sister of Lazarus. The first to say she believes. Martha Delacroix knows the truth of what Wicks did, what Grace suffered, what the church covered up for sixty years. She is also the first to declare the resurrection of “Wicks” from the tomb, even if she planned it. She carries both roles. The servant and the witness.

The way she dies matters. Martha commits suicide, an act Catholic doctrine treats as a grave sin because there is no time for reconciliation. You die in the process. She knows this. Chooses it anyway. Even dying, she says, “Forgive Wicks, and Samson.” Jud has to encourage her to forgive Grace as well. To give Grace. She dies saying, “that poor girl.”

Johnson holds back the words of absolution throughout the film. Two hours of sin, manipulation, murder, and confession, and the words that release all of it arrive only when Martha is dying.

Jesus Help Me

Near the end, after the confessions and the poison and sixty years of lies, Jud stands alone. Tears in his eyes.

“Jesus help me. Show me the way through this. Please.”

He is not reciting liturgy. He is begging.

The door opens.

Benoît Blanc walks in. A detective. An atheist. A man who, by his words, kneels at the altar of the rational.

But he shows up. He walks through the door. He is the answer to the prayer.

Both an irony and a reconing that God does not appear in a cloud of light. God shows up as the person who is with you when you have nothing left. Sometimes that person does not even believe.

But they came.

The Light Follows Faith

Johnson lights the final exchange between Jud and Blanc with intention.

Blanc has seen the institution. The history. Slavery justified by scripture. Women controlled by doctrine. Children abused by clergy. Wealth hoarded by tax-exempt organizations while their people starve. He is not wrong. I am not convinced he is wrong either, and I think Johnson knows that most of his audience feels the same way.

But Jud sees all of it, too. He is standing in a church where every woman has been harmed. Simone spent her savings begging for healing. Vera was handed a child to raise and told to shut up. Grace was imprisoned, slandered, and erased. Martha spent her life serving a man who did not deserve it.

Jud sees all of that. And he still believes. Not because he is naive, but because something met him on the other side of killing a man that he cannot explain any other way except through the story of a God who loves the guilty.

Blanc presses him on religion as a form of storytelling. Costumes and rituals. Jud does not disagree. He says yes, it is storytelling. And then he asks,

Do these stories convince us of a lie? Or do they resonate with something deep inside us that is profoundly true, that we cannot express any other way?

Blanc says the truth is the opposite. But the opposite of one profound truth is not a lie. It is another profound truth. It is two honest people looking at the same broken world and arriving at different answers. Both answers cost something. Both answers are earned.

The lighting in this scene helps frame both men's arguments. When Blanc speaks about the horrors of Christianity, the room goes darker. The warmth fades. The sunlight streaming through the stained-glass windows is a physical reminder of the darkness that rationality brings. When Jud responds, the light returns.

Throughout the film, sunlight through the glass casts halos behind whoever delivers a monologue grounded in genuine belief. There is a reason churches are built facing east. You are facing the sunrise. You are watching the light arrive.

Johnson builds every frame of Jud’s faith with that light. He lets a believer be warm in a film that has every reason to tear the institution apart. He does not ask you to believe what Jud believes. He asks you to notice that Jud’s belief makes him kinder. And then he asks whether that kindness is enough.

By the end, Blanc still doesn't believe in God. But he believes in Jud. When Martha is dying, Jud asks what to do, and Blanc answers, “Be her priest.” He has not converted. He just watched someone whose faith made them better.

That is more than enough. And I think that is the only argument for religion that has ever made sense to me. More than theology and the institution, or the promise of heaven or the threat of hell. Just the observable fact that some people’s belief in God makes them gentler with the living.

The Church Still Standing

Jud renames the church. Carves a new cross by hand from a tree. Takes Eve’s Apple, the diamond, all that sin and greed and generational destruction, and hides it inside the body of Christ on the cross. Changes the sign to “All are welcome.”

He is a few beads short of a rosary, as someone in the film puts it. He punches people. He curses. He gets drunk and throws a devil’s head through a church window. He is still a fighter even though he left the ring.

But when Louise called, he stopped everything and prayed.

My mom always prays for me. I do not believe in the God she is talking to. I have not been convinced. The last time I stepped into a church was after my friend died. I am not Jud. I am closer to Blanc, at the altar of the rational.

But I know what it feels like when someone prays for you. Not with you. Nor for any reason of self-righteousness or pretentious thought. For you. When your mom bows her head and talks to someone on your behalf, and you do not share her faith, but you feel held by it anyway.

You do not love God by worshipping some imagined figure. You do not love God by going to church. You love God by being kind to the world around you. By unconditionally loving what you love and making that circle ever-expanding.

That is what the film argues from its first frame to its last, where the diamond, caked with sin, shines in the middle of the carving of Jesus.

If I cannot believe in a loving God, I believe in my loving Mom. If Blanc cannot believe in God, he believes in Father Jud. In Matthew, Jesus says, " Where two or three are gathered in my name, I am there among them.” I’d like to believe that can mean love as well.

Wake Up Dead Man is a great theological movie; it effectively asks what the church is supposed to be when you strip away everything else.

A kind person on the phone. Saying you are not alone. Saying you are not hopeless. Saying I love you when you are guilty.

Meaning it.

Dedicated to my Mom for naming me Josh, the names of both of our main actors.

Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.
