title: The Pitt Breakdown: Orlando Diaz and the American Healthcare Insurance System.
seoTitle: The Pitt Breakdown: Orlando Diaz and the American Healthcare Insurance System.
excerpt: A construction worker rations insulin. A doctor calls an Uber. The system works exactly as designed.
metaDescription: A construction worker rations insulin. A doctor calls an Uber. The system works exactly as designed.
bodyHtml:
The Pitt is the best drama on television right now. Not hyperbole.

The show follows the staff of a Pittsburgh emergency room in real time. One season. One shift. Fifteen hours. Every episode covers one hour on the clock. Season 1 won five Emmys, including Outstanding Drama Series, Lead Actor for Noah Wyle, and Supporting Actress for Katherine LaNasa. It became the first medical procedural to win best drama since ER in 1996. It swept two Golden Globes. Season 2 premiered in January 2026 to 5.4 million viewers in three days, a 200% increase from the series debut. It now averages roughly 12 million viewers per episode and has already been renewed for a third season.

It earned all of it.

What makes it different from every medical drama you have watched? The Pitt does not just show you sick people getting treated. It shows you why they got sick. Why did they wait so long? Why did the system that was supposed to catch them let them fall? Every patient is an American policy failure walking through the door.

Season 2 takes place over the Fourth of July. I am going to break it down piece by piece. The characters. The conditions. The real data behind what you are watching.

This is The Pitt Breakdown.

Share

What Happens on the Show

Orlando Diaz is a construction worker. He has a wife. He has a daughter named Ana. He works two jobs. Neither offers health insurance.

He faints on a job site on the Fourth of July and ends up in the emergency room with a blood sugar of 500 and a diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis. That is when your blood becomes acidic because your body does not have enough insulin. It can kill you.

Here is what Orlando has been hiding from his family. He lost his previous job. When he lost that job, he lost his insurance. When he lost his insurance, he lost access to the doctor managing his diabetes. So he started rationing insulin. Cutting doses in half to stretch the supply. That is how you end up unconscious on a construction site.

His wife, Lorrie, races to the hospital. Dr. Samira Mohan diagnoses him and recommends an insulin drip. That is when the math starts.

The Diazes make too much for Medicaid. Too little to afford private insurance. Multiple part-time jobs with no coverage. Mohan asks about the ACA marketplace. The case manager looks into it. Nothing fits.

Then the second secret, Orlando is already $100,000 in medical debt.

He tries to leave. Not because he does not need treatment. Because every minute he stays adds to a bill he already cannot pay. “Every minute I stay is a meal, shoes, school supplies,” he tells Mohan. His teenage daughter overhears the conversation and offers to pick up extra shifts to help cover it.

She is a child offering to work more hours so her father can afford not to die.

Mohan gathers medications and insulin supplies to send him home with something. When she comes back to his bed, he is gone. He left against medical advice. He went to his second job.

Dr. Jack Abbot pays out of his own pocket for an Uber to deliver the supplies to Orlando’s home.

A doctor used a ride share app and his own money to do what the American healthcare system would not.

Subscribe now

The Gap

The Affordable Care Act was supposed to fix this. The plan was simple. Medicaid would expand to cover all adults up to 138% of the federal poverty level. For everyone above that, subsidized marketplace insurance would fill the gap. No one would fall through.

Then the Supreme Court broke it. In 2012, the Court ruled that states could not be forced to expand Medicaid. As of 2025, 10 states still have not expanded.

That decision created the coverage gap. If you live in one of those states and you earn too much for traditional Medicaid but less than the federal poverty level, you qualify for nothing. Too poor for help. Too “rich” for assistance.

According to the Kaiser Family Foundation, 1.4 million uninsured adults are stuck in that gap right now. Nearly all of them live in the South. Texas, Florida, and Georgia account for roughly three-quarters of that population. People of color make up about 61% of everyone trapped in it.

It kills people. A 2023 study in Health Affairs found that states refusing Medicaid expansion saw roughly 7,000 preventable deaths per year compared to states that expanded. Not projections. Deaths that already happened, in states that chose to let them happen.

The uninsured population in America sat at 25.3 million in 2023, according to the Census Bureau. That number dropped after ACA implementation. But it stopped dropping around the same time expansion stalled. The floor is the floor because ten states decided it was.

The ACA assumed every state would expand. That is why it did not create subsidies for people below the poverty line. When conservative states opted out, the law had no backup plan. Those states decided their poorest residents did not deserve coverage. The federal government did not override them.

Orlando Diaz works two jobs. His wife works. Their daughter works. They do everything right. The system still has no place for them.

The Debt

Even when you dont’t get treated, the bill comes.

Medical debt is the number one cause of bankruptcy in America. Research published in the American Journal of Public Health found that 66.5% of all bankruptcies are tied to medical issues. Total medical debt in the United States is estimated at $220 billion.

The insulin piece is specific. A 2025 Yale study published in the Journal of General Internal Medicine found that one in four patients prescribed insulin still rationed it due to cost in 2024. That rate was unchanged from 2017. Seven years of policy debate and legislative action have moved nothing for the people actually living this. When researchers broadened the definition to include insurance delays and pharmacy shortages, 37% of patients reported rationing over the past year.

Kasia Lipska, the Yale endocrinologist who led the study, said,

“When people don’t have enough money for insulin, they may ration it, meaning they take less than is prescribed to make it last longer, or skip it altogether.”

That is what Orlando did.

Rationing insulin does not just make diabetes harder to manage. It causes DKA. The exact condition that put Orlando on the floor of that construction site.

The most common forms of analog insulin cost multiple times as much as 10 times in the United States as in any other developed country. The uninsured pay the most. They ration the most. They get the sickest.

Orlando rationed his insulin. That made his diabetes worse. The worsened diabetes sent him to the emergency room. The ER visit costs more than a year's supply of insulin would. The cycle starts again.

The Pitt does not let you look away from the math.

Other medical dramas mention insurance in passing. A character says they do not have coverage, and then the scene moves on to the dramatic surgery or the love triangle. The Pitt stays in the room. It shows you the conversation between Mohan and the case manager. It shows you Lorrie discovering the debt. It shows you Ana doing the math in her head to figure out how many extra shifts she needs.

Supriya Ganesh, who plays Dr. Mohan, pitched this storyline to showrunner R. Scott Gemmill between seasons. She wanted to explore what happens when a patient cannot afford the care they need, and medicine cannot fix that.

That is why the moment she realizes the family has no insurance hits so hard. She assumed they did. She is a doctor in an emergency room, and it did not occur to her that the family in front of her had no way to pay for insulin. Even the good doctors operate inside a system that treats insurance as a default. When the default does not apply, there is no protocol; Mohan improvises. She begs Orlando to stay. It does not work.

And Dr. Abbot does the only thing left. He reaches into his own pocket.

This scene is an indictment of the system we’re in. When a doctor needs a ride share app and a personal credit card to deliver medication to a patient who fled because he could not afford to be there, every layer of the system has failed. The insurance failed. The employer failed. The state legislature failed. The federal safety net failed.

Orlando did not leave because he was noncompliant. He left because compliance costs money he does not have. The Pitt does not blame him for that.

Why Medicare for All Never Passes

You watched a doctor use a ride-share app to deliver medication to a patient who fled the hospital because he could not afford to stay. You probably asked yourself why this keeps happening.

Here is the answer.

About 70% of Americans support Medicare for All, according to Kaiser Family Foundation polling. That number has held for years. The majority supports. Consistent. Clear.

It never passes.

The reason is not public opinion. The pharmaceutical industry spent $294 million lobbying Congress in 2024. Up from $100 million in 2000. The health insurance industry spent another $170 million that same year. Together, nearly half a billion dollars in a single year to make sure the system that just failed Orlando Diaz stays exactly the way it is.

When Medicare was finally allowed to negotiate drug prices, the pharmaceutical industry had already spent decades and billions fighting the proposal.

Political scientists Martin Gilens and Benjamin Page studied 1,779 policy outcomes over 20 years and reported that when average Americans want something that corporations oppose, they lose. Every time. The pattern holds for gun reform, climate policy, drug prices, and healthcare. Public opinion does not predict outcomes. Donor preferences do.

You want a healthcare system that does not abandon Orlando Diaz. The insurance industry wants to keep collecting premiums. Pharma wants to keep charging what the market allows. Congress does what it is paid to do.

Orlando Diaz paid the price. They cashed the check.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Subscribe now

Dedicated to: Granddad, and My Scout Master

title: 98 Years and Counting: My Oscar Predictions for March 15
seoTitle: 98 Years and Counting: My Oscar Predictions for March 15
excerpt: No Black director has won Best Director. Ever. Here are my picks for who changes that and everything else on Oscar night.
metaDescription: No Black director has won Best Director. Ever. Here are my picks for who changes that and everything else on Oscar night.
bodyHtml:
This is the most exciting Oscar race in years. Two Warner Bros. films, Sinners and One Battle After Another, have spent the entire awards season trading body blows. One swept every precursor. The other set the all-time nomination record. Both are masterpieces. Only one wins Best Picture.

Conan O’Brien is hosting for the second time. Ten films are nominated. And for the first time in 98 years, a Black director has a real shot at winning Best Director.

I have thoughts. Here are my predictions.

Best Director: Ryan Coogler, Sinners

I’m breaking from the prediction models here. PTA won the DGA, and only eight times since 1948 has the DGA winner not gone on to win the Oscar. But the Academy is a different body from the DGA. The momentum shifted. The BAFTA moment, the NAACP response, the SAG sweep, the WGA win. All of it put Sinners at the center of the conversation in the final days of voting.

PTA is owed an Oscar. That part is true. Boogie Nights. There Will Be Blood. Phantom Thread. His body of work is among the strongest in American cinema, and One Battle After Another is among his best. The Academy has a history of giving overdue directors their flowers. Scorsese for The Departed. Spielberg for Schindler’s List. The Coen Brothers for No Country.

But PTA will make another great film. He always does. And there is a version of this Oscar night where the Academy looks at the man who directed the most nominated film in their history, an original horror film set in Jim Crow Mississippi that made $368 million and changed what voters thought was possible, and decides this is the year. The year that the award goes to a Black filmmaker for the first time in 98 years.

I think they have decided.

Best Actor: Michael B. Jordan, Sinners

The SAG win changed everything. Chalamet was the frontrunner for months. Marty Supreme was seen as his coronation. Jordan winning means the actual actors in the Academy, the largest voting bloc, chose him. That is not a fluke.

He plays two roles. Both performances are distinct and extraordinary. The way he wore his shoes, his eyebrows, and his dimples. He consulted real-life twins before delivering two of his best performances, playing against himself throughout the film. One brother haunted by loss. The other by love. Coogler and Jordan have been building toward this since Fruitvale Station. Five films together. Five different genres. This is the one.

The BAFTA moment matters here too. Voters watched Jordan stand on that stage, absorb what happened, and finish the job. Then they watched him win SAG two weeks later. The goodwill around him right now is real and earned.

Best Supporting Actress: A Split Decision

I think Teyana Taylor takes this for One Battle After Another.” And I think it’s fair. She gave a performance that made people forget she was not always an actress. She earned it.

But I would love to see Wunmi Mosaku get her flowers. Her work in Sinners is the kind of acting that holds an entire film together without ever asking you to notice. The BAFTA voters noticed. The NAACP voters noticed. I hope the Academy does too.

Taylor probably wins mainly because the academy hates horror, but both of the women deserve it, and if it does happen, Wunmi deserves it just as much.

The rest of my predictions, including Best Picture, Best Supporting Actor, Best Original Screenplay, Best Cinematography, and my picks across the technical categories, are below for paid subscribers.

I keep all my deep dives and essay articles free. But if this work means something to you, paid subscriptions support the writing and give you access to the subscriber chat. I appreciate everyone who reads, shares, and shows up. I will make these public the night after, so if you wanna wait and see if I get any of them right, that’s fine too.

Best Picture: Sinners

The SAG Ensemble win is the freshest signal before the ceremony. CODA won SAG Ensemble and then won Best Picture. Everything Everywhere All at Once won the SAG Ensemble Award and then Best Picture. Oppenheimer won the SAG Ensemble Award and then Best Picture. I have seen this movie before.

Best Picture uses a preferential ballot. Sinners has the broadest coalition of any film this year. Voters who love it rank it first. Voters who respect it rank it high. That is how you win a preferential ballot. One Battle After Another has people who love it and people who find it cold. Sinners has almost no detractors.

The PGA win for One Battle After Another is real. That award uses the same preferential system as the Oscars, has roughly the same size voting body, and has matched the Best Picture winner 17 of the last 22 years. Five years running before this, the PGA winner went on to win Best Picture. That is a hard wall to run through.

But here is what changed. SAG Ensemble. ACE Eddie. WGA. No film has held all three and lost Best Picture since SAG began. Sinners holds all three heading into Sunday.

The split people keep predicting, Sinners for Picture, PTA for Director, is one the Academy has chosen before. I wrote about this pattern in my 2019 piece on the Green Book win. Black directors make films that the Academy celebrates in every category except the one that credits them personally.

I don’t think it goes that way this year. I think Sinners takes both.

Best Supporting Actor: Delroy Lindo, Sinners

He is 73 years old. This is his first Oscar nomination. He has been one of the best actors working for four decades, and the industry finally noticed. Joe Turner’s Come and Gone. Crooklyn. Da 5 Bloods. Five years ago, his performance in the Spike Lee film should have been nominated. It was not.

The BAFTA moment put a spotlight on his grace. The NAACP's standing ovation showed what his peers think of him. He told NPR this week he is “claiming the victory in this process, no matter what happens.”

Sometimes the Oscar goes to the career as much as the performance. This is one of those times. And the performance deserves it on its own. Delta Slim is the heartbeat of Sinners. Lindo found that character in the music, in the itinerant life of 1930s blues musicians, and he gave a performance so deep and grounded that it anchors the entire film.

If not him, my next pick is Sean Penn, who plays the satirical right-wing military officer in One Battle After Another. He does a very good job.

Best Original Screenplay: Ryan Coogler, Sinners

The WGA confirmed this last night. Coogler won Best Original Screenplay at the Writers Guild Awards. He already won this category at the BAFTAs and at Critics Choice. The script for Sinners should not work on paper. A period horror film about vampires and blues music set during Jim Crow? That premise requires a writer who can balance genre, history, and character for over two hours without losing a step. Coogler did it.

The competition is strong. Sentimental Value is the kind of European intimate drama the Academy loves. It Was Just an Accident is a critic’s favorite. But Sinners is the one that rewrote what the industry thought was possible for an original screenplay.

Best Adapted Screenplay: Paul Thomas Anderson, One Battle After Another

PTA won this at the WGA, the BAFTAs, Critics’ Choice, and the Golden Globes. Adapting Thomas Pynchon into a coherent film is a feat on its own. Making it one of the best films of the year, on top of that, is why PTA is PTA. This one feels locked.

Best Cinematography: Train Dreams

I know Sinners is the favorite here, and I understand why. Autumn Durald Arkapaw made history as the first woman of color nominated in this category, and she shot the film on 70mm IMAX combined with Ultra Panavision 70. The first film to ever combine those formats. She could surprise me, and she would absolutely deserve it. I would love to eat my words on this.

But Train Dreams is a visually stunning film, and cinematography is one of its only nominations. When that happens, voters tend to give that category to the film whose craft work is the entire argument for its existence. Adolpho Veloso shot the film using 99% natural light: fire, candles, and daylight. He used a narrower 3:2 aspect ratio, comparable to an old photograph, so the film feels like a memory. And it does.

Best Actress: Jessie Buckley, Hamnet

This feels like the one lock of the night. Everyone says so. I say so too. The movie was amazing, and she was amazing in it. It was one of my favorite films of the year, based on one of my favorite plays.

The Technical Categories

Best Makeup and Hairstyling: Sinners. The period detail in this film is stunning. 1933 Mississippi was brought to life with precision. The makeup designer talked about giving Stack dimples and Smoke none because Stack was always smiling. That is the level of character work happening in the makeup chair. I would also love a Frankenstein win here. Jacob Elordi's transformation is comparable to some of the greatest in the horror franchise. He is unrecognizable.

Best Original Score: Sinners. Ludwig Goransson built a score that David Ehrlich at IndieWire called inextricable from the texture of the film. He was right. Blues, gospel, and Delta South sounds that feel both modern and rooted in their era. Goransson and Coogler have been collaborating since USC film school on projects as ambitious as Black Panther, but their connection shines brightest in this movie. For the first time, 1930s music is heard without the disruption of older recording methods.

Best Casting: Sinners. This is the first year the Academy is giving out this award. The Sinners ensemble, from Jordan to Lindo to Miles Caton to Wunmi Mosaku, is one of the best assembled casts in years.

Best Film Editing: One Battle After Another. The pacing is amazing, and that is an achievement. I don’t think there is a moment wasted in this movie.

Best Production Design, Best Costume Design: The Frankenstein costume was intricate and beautifully constructed. The production design builds a world that feels handmade while also putting you into the story very well. These are the categories where Frankenstein collects its Oscars.

Best Animated Feature: KPop Demon Hunters. I really hope Little Amelie or The Character of Rain can pull it off. That film is visually stunning and explores the scars of postwar Japan while also telling a beautiful coming-of-age story. But I think it goes to KPop Demon Hunters. It is one of those years where the popular animated feature wins. I love the movie and was glued to my seat for it, it was fun and action-packed, even if I do not think it was the best of the year.

International Film: The Secret Agent. Sentimental Value did well in the precursor season, but I think this is where the upset happens. My favorite of the nominees is The Voice of Hind Rajab. If you have not seen it, please go watch it. It is heartbreaking and moving.

Best Visual Effects: Avatar. It's great; I don’t need to say more.

Best Sound: F1, This is probably the only win for F1 on Oscar night, and it is the right one. The sound design puts you in the car. It is a loud, immersive, technical achievement in a category that rewards exactly that.

Best Documentary Feature: The Perfect Neighbor. A haunting reminder of racism in America.

Best Original Song: Golden. Everyone is predicting this, and I agree. Golden became a phenomenon, reaching the Hot 100. Nine songs have won both the Academy Award for Best Original Song and the Grammy for Song of the Year. The other strong contender is "I Lied to You," and what makes it great is not the lyrics alone but the story around it. It is a theatrical number that lives in the climax of its film. But Golden is more listenable, more modern, and the kind of song voters play in their cars on the way to the ballot

Oscar night is March 15. I will be watching and writing.

If you want to talk predictions, the paid subscriber chat is open. I want to hear where you think I am wrong.

Thanks for reading.

Joshua, if you are reading this far and are free, you used your free preview, for which I say thank you.

If you are a paid subscriber, thank you from the bottom of my heart.
author: Joshua Smith

title: You Are Shining: What Viola Davis, Herald Loomis, and Michael B. Jordan Tell Us About How Black People Root for Each Other
seoTitle: You Are Shining: What Viola Davis, Herald Loomis, and Michael B. Jordan Tell Us About How Black People Root for Each Other
excerpt: The conversations about the upcoming oscars has been split but why and the explanation of "rooting for everybody black"
metaDescription: The conversations about the upcoming oscars has been split but why and the explanation of "rooting for everybody black"
bodyHtml:
On March 1, 2026, Viola Davis stood at the podium of the Shrine Auditorium in Los Angeles. She was presenting Best Actor at the SAG Awards. Most of the room expected Timothée Chalamet to win for Marty Supreme. He was the frontrunner. The presumptive lock.

Davis opened the envelope. She did not just read the name.

She gasped. She jumped. She screamed. And before anyone in that room processed what was happening, she shouted five words that sent half the internet to Google and the other half to tears.

“You are shining, Herald Loomis!”

Then she screamed Michael B. Jordan’s name with a joy that jumped through the screen and into living rooms across the country.

Jordan looked stunned, and he wiped his eyes. Delroy Lindo, sitting next to him, was the first person he hugged; then his mom; and then he made his way to the stage. “I wasn’t expecting this at all,” Jordan said from the podium. He thanked his mother for driving him back and forth to New York for auditions when they didn’t have enough money to pay the Holland Tunnel toll.

Most people in that room didn’t know who Herald Loomis was.

Subscribe now

Who Is Herald Loomis

That line comes from August Wilson’s 1984 play Joe Turner’s Come and Gone. It is part of Wilson’s Pittsburgh Cycle, a ten-play cycle that chronicles the Black American experience across every decade of the twentieth century. Joe Turner is set in 1911, during the Great Migration. It takes place in a boardinghouse in Pittsburgh where travelers, workers, and the displaced cross paths.

Herald Loomis is a man searching. He spent seven years in forced labor under Joe Turner, a real white man in the post-Reconstruction South who illegally forced Black men into years of servitude. When the play begins, Loomis is free, but he doesn’t know who he is anymore. He’s looking for his wife. He’s looking for himself. He carries the weight of what was taken from him like a second body.

At the end of the play, Loomis stops waiting for someone else to tell him he is whole. He slashes his own chest, lets the blood run, and reclaims himself. The character Bynum, who has watched this man struggle the entire play, looks at him and says the line.

“Herald Loomis, you shining! You shining like new money!”

Wilson’s stage directions describe the moment when Loomis has found his song. The song of self-sufficiency. He is free from any encumbrance other than the workings of his own heart.

That’s the line Viola Davis chose. Not “Congratulations.” Not “And the winner is.” She pulled from a 40-year-old August Wilson play and gave Michael B. Jordan something the room couldn’t give him. Context.

Here is what makes it land even deeper.

Davis’s first professional acting gig was as Mattie in Joe Turner’s Come and Gone. Delroy Lindo, who was standing right next to MBJ when his name was called, earned a Tony nomination for playing Herald Loomis in the 1988 Broadway production. Davis won her Oscar for Fences, another August Wilson play. A new Broadway revival of Joe Turner starring Taraji P. Henson opens on March 30.

This wasn’t just a quote, out of thin air. It was a whole lineage passing in real time. Three generations of August Wilson’s work standing in the same room, one of them being told he is shining by another who has been there before.

Why Viola Said That and Not Something Else

Ira Madison III wrote on his Substack the morning after the ceremony that Davis’s reference “appeared more instinctive than intentional, but that doesn’t strip away its meaning.”

He’s right, Viola Davis didn’t rehearse that. She saw the name, and the first thing that came out of her was Wilson. The first thing that came out of her was a reference that connected Jordan’s win to four decades of Black theater, to a story about a man who stopped waiting for permission to exist.

That’s what Black solidarity looks like on full display, a connection to history and a gift to the future. It’s Viola Davis standing at a podium in front of a room full of Hollywood’s biggest names and deciding to make MBJ’s moment bigger than the room.

“Man, I Love Being Black”

The night before the SAG Awards, at the 57th NAACP Image Awards in Pasadena, Jordan won Entertainer of the Year. He also won Best Actor. Sinners swept the ceremony with 13 wins out of 18 nominations.

When Jordan accepted his Best Actor award, he talked about sneaking into the NAACP Image Awards

@bet“I used to come here as a kid…sneaking in through the back as best [as] I could.” We are so proud of you, Michael and younger Mike would say the same. Congratulations on your ‘Outstanding Actor’ win! #NAACPImageAward
Tiktok failed to load.

Enable 3rd party cookies or use another browser

He talked about feeling seen there, feeling like the people in that room poured into him before anyone else did. Then he paused. He watched the In Memoriam segment. He dedicated the award to Chadwick Boseman.

“As my brother, our time on this planet is short,” he said. “That’s why I want to be here and be as present as I can with you guys as much as possible.”

Then he said it.

“Man, I love being Black. I love y’all.”

The entire auditorium rose to its feet.

Context matters here; if you are a subscriber for more than a week, you know where this is going, if not read this substack. One week before the NAACP Image Awards, at the 2026 BAFTAs in London, Jordan and Delroy Lindo were presenting the award for Best Visual Effects when John Davidson, a Tourette's syndrome advocate who was in the audience as the executive producer of the BAFTA-nominated film I Swear, shouted a racial slur. The N-word. Directed at two Black men standing on a stage in front of the world.

Davidson’s outburst was involuntary. He has Tourette's syndrome. That is the medical reality, and it matters. But the institutional failure that followed was not involuntary. The BAFTAs were recorded on tape delay. The BBC had two hours to edit the slur out of the broadcast. They edited out a separate outburst. They left the N-word in.

Jordan and Lindo stood on that stage, absorbed in it, and kept presenting. Lindo told Vanity Fair afterward that they “did what we had to do.” He also said he wished someone from BAFTA had spoken to them afterward. Nobody did.

Production designer Hannah Beachler, who won an Oscar for Black Panther, wrote on social media that she was subjected to a separate slur during the evening. She called the host’s on-air apology a “throwaway.”

So when MBJ said “I love being Black” at the NAACP Image Awards, he wasn’t just celebrating. He was answering something. Black people in that auditorium already knew what he was responding to.

Before the ceremony even started, Regina Hall paused to address Jordan and Lindo directly. “Take a moment for the two kings who are in this audience,” she said. “I just sent you so much love for your class.” The room gave them a standing ovation before a single award was presented.

Later, Lindo took the stage with Ryan Coogler and addressed the BAFTA incident publicly for the first time. “We appreciate all the support and love we have been shown in the aftermath of what happened last weekend,” he said. Then he added the line that said everything.

“It’s a classic case of something that could be very negative becoming very positive.”

He called it an honor to be “here amongst our people.”

Coogler followed,

“There’s something powerful about standing in this room. A room where we don’t have to explain ourselves, or our stories aren’t footnotes. They’re the main text.”

That room in Pasadena was doing something the BAFTAs never could. It was absorbing the blow and converting it into fuel. That's the context you need to understand what happened the next night at the Shrine.

Share

The Sinners vs. One Battle After Another Split

Issa Rae said, “I’m rooting for everybody Black” at the 2017 Emmys.

@varietymagazine#Emmys 2020 are only 18 days away. Here’s #IssaRae from the Emmys #redcarpet in 2017! #ProveWhatsPossible #fyp #insecure
Tiktok failed to load.

Enable 3rd party cookies or use another browser

People clowned her for it. They acted like it was divisive, but she was pointing to a real cultural phenomenon: Black audiences and Black creatives have to make a different calculation than everyone else when they walk into these rooms.

No Black director has won the Oscar for Best Director. Ever. In 98 years. Seven Black directors have been nominated. John Singleton. Lee Daniels. Steve McQueen. Barry Jenkins. Jordan Peele. Spike Lee. Now Ryan Coogler. None of them won.

The only two films by a Black director to win Best Picture, Moonlight(which was terribly presented) and 12 Years a Slave, won Best Picture but not Best Director. The Academy gave the movie the award, but not the person who made it.

Sinners broke the record for the most Oscar nominations in history with 16; the next-highest is 14, held by All About Eve, Titanic, and La La Land. Sixteen. A horror film set in 1933 Mississippi about twin brothers and vampires that made $368 million worldwide. Best Picture. Best Director. Best Actor. Best Supporting Actor. Best Supporting Actress. Best Original Screenplay. Every technical category it competed in.

One Battle After Another, Paul Thomas Anderson’s film, won the PGA and the DGA. It won the Golden Globe. It won the Critics’ Choice. It won Best Film and Best Director at the BAFTAs.

Let me lay out both films’ awards packages because the numbers tell a story.

One Battle After Another has one of the strongest precursor runs in recent Oscar history. It won the Golden Globe. It won the Critics’ Choice Award. It won the DGA. It won the PGA. It won Best Film and Best Director at the BAFTAs. It won the National Board of Review, the New York Film Critics Circle, and the LA Film Critics Association. PTA’s film swept the season as Oppenheimer did two years ago. The PGA Award alone is the gold standard precursor. It uses the same preferential ballot as the Oscars, has roughly the same size voting body, and has matched the Best Picture winner 17 of the last 22 years. Five years running, the PGA winner went on to win Best Picture. One Battle After Another has every institutional stamp.

The main things that set these apart are timing. The DGA ballots closed on February 6. The PGA ballots closed on February 3. The BAFTA vote ran through February 17. Most of those wins tell us what voters were thinking in January. They don’t tell us what voters are thinking now.

The award that tells us what voters think now is the SAG Award for actors. Those ballots closed on February 25. And Sinners won. Not just the Ensemble prize, which went to the full Sinners cast over One Battle After Another’s stacked lineup of DiCaprio, Del Toro, Penn, Hall, and Taylor. Jordan also won Best Actor, beating Chalamet.

That SAG Ensemble win is not a small thing. CODA won SAG Ensemble and then won Best Picture. Everything Everywhere won the SAG Ensemble and then won Best Picture. Oppenheimer won the SAG Ensemble Award and then Best Picture.

Sinners also earned a 97% on Rotten Tomatoes and the first “A” CinemaScore for a horror film in 35 years, besting Get Out, with an A-. It was the highest-grossing original film domestically in 15 years.

The split development is one the Academy has chosen before. Sinners for Picture. PTA for Director. But the split is also racial, more often than not. If you look at any interview for these nominations, black people have predominantly chosen sinners, and white people have predominantly chosen OBAA; in Hollywood, this split could be the deciding factor for the Oscars.

If that happens, Ryan Coogler will have directed the most nominated film in Oscar history and still not won Best Director. The pattern I wrote about in my 2019 Oscars piece on Green Book will have repeated itself. Black directors make films that the Academy celebrates in every category except the one that personally credits them.

I don’t think it goes that way this year. Here is why.

Paul Thomas Anderson is owed an Oscar. That is true. His body of work from Boogie Nights through There Will Be Blood(one of my favorite movies) to Phantom Thread is among the strongest in American cinema. One Battle After Another is one of his best films. And the Academy has a history of giving overdue directors their flowers, Scorsese for The Departed, Spielberg for Schindler’s List, the Coen Brothers for No Country.

But PTA will make another great film. He always does. And there is a version of this Oscar night where the Academy recognizes that the man who directed the most nominated film in their history, an original horror film set in Jim Crow Mississippi that made $368 million and changed what voters thought was possible, deserves the award that has never gone to a Black filmmaker in 98 years.

Here are my predictions for March 15 (my explanations for each of these pics will be in the paid Substack on Tuesday).

Best Picture: Sinners.

Best Director and Original Screenplay: Ryan Coogler.

Best Actor: Michael B. Jordan.

Best Supporting Actor: Delroy Lindo.

My hope is that Makeup and Ensemble Cast all go to Sinners. Cinematography goes to Train Dreams, though Autumn Durald could surprise me, and if she does, she earned it. Best Supporting Actress is Teyana Taylor’s to win, and I think that’s fair, even if I want Wunmi Mosaku to get her flowers. When Isa Rae said, “I’m rooting for everybody black,” she recognized that between 1966 and 2014, fewer than 5% of lead acting nominations in comedy or drama were awarded to Black actors. So when we go into the Oscars, I think about all of the contextual history of these films as ways to tell the story of Hollywood award shows from the perspective of their makers.

What All of This Is Actually About

None of these moments was scripted by a publicist. They happened because Black excellence doesn’t just celebrate. It witnesses. It carries, as described in the black national anthem, the “hope that the present has bought us”. It passes things forward.

When Viola Davis quoted Wilson, she wasn’t just congratulating a winner. She was connecting Jordan to a lineage. Loomis to Jordan. Wilson to Davis. The 1988 Broadway stage to the 2026 Shrine Auditorium. This moment didn’t start with you, and it won’t end with you, and that’s what makes it matter.

Viola Davis made a roomful of people who didn’t know Herald Loomis go find out.

You should, too. Joe Turner’s Come and Gone opens on Broadway on March 30. Go see it.

And if Sinners wins Best Picture on March 15, remember that it didn’t start with Ryan Coogler. It started with every Black filmmaker who was nominated and didn’t win. Every Black director who made something extraordinary and watched someone else hold the trophy.

Herald Loomis found his song. Now we see if the Academy is ready to hear it.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid (it’s the same button as the regular subscribe one). Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Subscribe now
author: Joshua Smith

title: The Pitt Breakdown: Jackson Davis and the Criminalization of Black Psychosis
seoTitle: The Pitt Breakdown: Jackson Davis and the Criminalization of Black Psychosis
excerpt: How the same psychiatry that criminalized civil rights is still diagnosing Black men as dangerous.
metaDescription: How the same psychiatry that criminalized civil rights is still diagnosing Black men as dangerous.
bodyHtml:
The Pitt is the best drama on television right now, and I am not being hyperbolic. The show follows the staff of a Pittsburgh emergency room in real time. One season. One shift. Fifteen hours. Every episode covers one hour on the clock. It won five Emmys, including Outstanding Drama Series. It earned that.

But what makes this show different from every medical drama you have ever watched? The Pitt does not just show you sick people getting treated, nor does it feature spicy romance like Dr. Steamy (rest in peace, Eric Dane). It shows you why they got sick. Why did they wait so long? Why did the system that was supposed to catch them let them fall? Every patient is an American policy failure walking through the door.

Season 2 takes place over the Fourth of July. I am going to break it down aswell as the first season. The characters. The conditions. The real data behind the storylines. One piece at a time.

This is The Pitt Breakdown.

Share

What Happens on the Show

Jackson arrives at Pittsburgh Trauma Medical Center on a stretcher, restrained at four points, taser barbs still in his neck. The paramedics describe him as a “combative college kid” who “flipped out in the library.” He is screaming the number 2901 over and over, thrashing against his restraints. The EMTs have already decided what he is. Not a patient. A problem.

Tony Chinchiolo, the campus security guard who tased him, shows up at the ER with a head wound from a chair Jackson threw. He parks himself in the waiting room, waiting for the police, telling anyone who will listen his theory. “Probably meth,” he says. “F*cking animal.”

Dana, the charge nurse, listens to this with visible skepticism. The doctors sedate Jackson. They run a tox screen. It comes back clean. No meth. No drugs. No substances at all.

Over the next several episodes, the picture fills in. Jackson is a law student. He has been hearing voices for months. “They don’t want me to pass the bar,” he tells the doctors. “That’s what they told me.” His sister Jada arrives and is blindsided. His parents eventually learn that their son is showing symptoms consistent with either bipolar disorder or schizophrenia. There is no simple test to tell the two apart. The family is terrified.

The security guard faces no consequences onscreen. The cops came to take his statement, not to check on Jackson. The system processed the Black man in crisis as the aggressor and the white man with a badge as the victim.

The History They Don’t Teach You

Chinchiolo looked at a Black man in a psychotic break and saw a drug addict. That assumption did not come from nowhere. It came from sixty years of American psychiatry treating Black distress as Black aggression.

Before the 1960s, schizophrenia in America was largely associated with white women. The diagnosis carried connotations of sensitivity and emotional fragility. It was considered nonthreatening. Then the civil rights movement happened.

Psychiatrist Jonathan Metzl documented this transformation in his book The Protest Psychosis: How Schizophrenia Became a Black Disease. At the Ionia State Hospital for the Criminally Insane in Michigan, Black men who had participated in civil rights protests were institutionalized and diagnosed with schizophrenia at alarming rates. Their hospital charts cited their political activity as evidence of their psychosis. Paranoid against his doctors and the police, one chart read. Would be a danger to society were he not in an institution.

In 1968, the American Psychiatric Association released the second edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual. The DSM-II redefined the paranoid subtype of schizophrenia using language about hostility and aggression. That language, applied in a society that already coded Black men as dangerous, turned a psychiatric diagnosis into a weapon. Black resistance became Black pathology.

That was sixty years ago. The pattern has not changed.

A 2018 analysis of 52 studies found that Black Americans are 2.4 times more likely to be diagnosed with schizophrenia than white Americans. Other research puts the rate at three to four times that. A Rutgers study found that Black patients with severe depression were being misdiagnosed with schizophrenia because clinicians overemphasized psychotic symptoms and underweighted mood symptoms in Black patients.

Dr. Stephen Strakowski, a co-author of that research, wrote

Black Americans show up sicker because it takes longer to get them into care, which makes their symptoms more dramatic, which causes clinicians to be less careful in their evaluations. “In the United States we’re just not treating people fairly if they are not white people.”

The misdiagnosis has real consequences. A person diagnosed with schizophrenia when they actually have bipolar disorder will not receive mood stabilizers that could change their life. They will receive heavier antipsychotic medications with worse side effects that only serve to exacerbate problems. They will carry the stigma of a schizophrenia diagnosis, which closes doors that a bipolar diagnosis does not.

Jackson Was Lucky. Others Are Not.

Here is where Jackson's story diverges from a sad reality for many.

Jackson Davis ended up in an ER with doctors who ran the right tests, took their time, and gave his family honest answers. He got tased, but he lived. That is not always the outcome. For many Black people experiencing a mental health crisis, the first responder is not a doctor nor even campus security. It is a cop. And the intervention is not a tox screen. It is a bullet.

In 2024, police in the United States killed 1,365 people. That is the highest number on record since Mapping Police Violence began collecting data. Black people were 2.9 times more likely than white people to be killed by police. And according to Johns Hopkins, 23 percent of all police shootings involved someone experiencing a mental or behavioral health crisis. Two out of three of those shootings were fatal.

You can name them. Laquan McDonald. Chicago, 2014. A 17-year-old is walking away from officers. Shot 16 times. Walter Wallace Jr., Philadelphia, 2020. His family called 911, asking for an ambulance because he was having a bipolar episode. A cop showed up instead. Shot him in front of his mother. Daniel Prude. Rochester, 2020. Naked in the street during a psychotic episode. Officers put a hood over his head and pressed his face into the pavement until he stopped breathing. Sonya Massey. Springfield, Illinois, 2024. Called 911 to report a prowler at her home. She was in a mental health crisis. A deputy shot her in her own kitchen after she said, “I rebuke you in the name of Jesus.” He is now serving 20 years. Ryan Gainer. California, 2024. A Black teenager with autism. His family called for help during a behavioral crisis. Police showed up and killed him. In his home.

Human Rights Watch compiled a partial list of Black men and women killed by police during mental health emergencies. It is not short.

The pattern is the same every time. A Black person is in crisis. Someone calls for help. The help arrives armed. The armed help sees a Black body in distress and reads it as a Black body threatening violence. The situation escalates because the only tool the responder carries is a weapon. The person in crisis dies. The family buries them. And we have the same conversation we had last time about training and reform and alternatives that never materialize.

Jackson Davis got a taser to the neck. That is the version of this story where the system shows restraint, not because it chose empathy, but because it was a security guard with a taser, not a cop with a gun.

What the Show Is Actually Saying

The Pitt does something most television refuses to do with this storyline: it doesn't gloss over it.

Jackson is not a one-episode tragedy. He is not a case-of-the-week designed to make you feel sad for 42 minutes and then move on. His story stretches across the entire season. You watch his family sit in that waiting room. You watch the doctors work through the differential diagnosis. You watch the slow, terrifying process of a young Black man learning that his brain is changing in ways he cannot control, and he will have to live with. And you watch the system that was supposed to help him choose to hurt him first.

Two things can be true at once. Jackson threw a chair and hurt someone. And a campus security guard decided a Black student having a medical emergency was a drug user who deserved to be electrocuted. The show holds both of those truths without collapsing them into a simple narrative. Jackson was dangerous in that moment. He was also a patient who needed help. The system only saw the first one.

Nicole Steadman, a black social worker on the show, speaks to Jackson’s parents from personal experience about her own daughter’s schizophrenia diagnosis at age 20. That scene is the hinge. In the middle of the parents’ panic, someone offers them a vision of a future that is not defined by the diagnosis. It is one of the most human moments in any medical drama in years.

But the show does not let you forget what happened before that moment. Before the compassion, there was a taser. Before the diagnosis, there was an assumption. Before anyone asked Jackson what was wrong, they decided he was wrong, that he was a “f*cking animal.”

That is how Black mental health crises are handled in America. Not as emergencies. As threats. The intervention comes with voltage, or worse. The diagnosis comes later, if it comes at all.

Jackson Davis was in a library when his world came apart. The first person who responded did not ask if he was okay.

He just pulled the trigger.

The Pitt wants you to remember that.

Share

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot. Click to subscribe or upgrade.

Subscribe now
author: Joshua Smith

title: One Family, Every Screen: How the Ellison's Paramount–Warner Takeover Finishes What Trump Started
seoTitle: One Family, Every Screen: How the Ellison's Paramount–Warner Takeover Finishes What Trump Started
excerpt: A look at how the Paramount–Warner merger reshapes American media power and what it means when political loyalty and corporate consolidation collide.
metaDescription: A look at how the Paramount–Warner merger reshapes American media power and what it means when political loyalty and corporate consolidation collide.
bodyHtml:
On Thursday, February 26, 2026, Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos walked into the White House for a meeting about his company’s $83 billion bid to buy Warner Bros. Discovery’s studio and streaming assets. He walked out with nothing.

Less than two hours later, Warner Bros. Discovery’s board declared Paramount Skydance’s $110.9 billion offer a “superior proposal.” Netflix didn’t counter. Didn’t negotiate. Didn’t ask for more time. The company that had spent months defending the deal called it a “nice to have at the right price, not a must have at any price.”

The same day Sarandos was leaving the White House looking, by multiple press accounts, defeated, Paramount CEO David Ellison was celebrating. Two days earlier, Ellison had attended Trump’s State of the Union address as the personal guest of Senator Lindsey Graham. They posed for a photo. Thumbs up.

In my first essay in this series, I laid out how Peter Thiel’s Palantir was building a surveillance database of American citizens, how the Corporation for Public Broadcasting was being killed, and how the combination created an information architecture designed not to inform you but to predict and control you. The surveillance layer. The distribution layer. The access layer.

Now there’s a fourth layer. Call it what it is.

The content layer.

Share

What the Ellison Family Actually Controls

Here is what one family, tied directly to the sitting president, now controls or is positioned to control.

Paramount Skydance. CBS. CBS News. Paramount Pictures. Paramount+. Nickelodeon. MTV. BET. Comedy Central. Showtime. Pluto TV. The studio behind Top Gun, Mission Impossible, and Star Trek.

TikTok’s U.S. operations. In December 2025, ByteDance signed a deal giving Oracle and a group of investors an 80.1% stake in a new American TikTok entity. Oracle, the software and data company run by Larry Ellison, serves as the “trusted security partner” responsible for auditing TikTok’s algorithm, managing American users’ data, and overseeing content moderation. That means the Ellison family now influences what 170 million Americans see on the country's most powerful social media platform.

And now Warner Bros. Discovery. HBO. HBO Max. CNN. Cartoon Network. DC Comics. TNT. TBS. Discovery Channel. HGTV. Food Network. The Warner Bros. film studio. New Line Cinema. The Harry Potter franchise. Game of Thrones. Every piece of it. The deal is expected to close between September and December 2026, pending regulatory and shareholder approval.

As Senator Elizabeth Warren said

“First Paramount/CBS and now TikTok. Trump wants to hand over even more control of what you watch to his billionaire buddies.”

This isn’t Disney being big, though I have problems with that. This isn’t Comcast owning a lot of stuff. Disney doesn’t have the president’s number saved in its phone. Comcast didn’t settle a sham lawsuit with the White House to get its merger approved. This is one politically aligned family, directly tied to the sitting president, with a documented pattern of editorial concessions, controlling multiple chokepoints of American culture, news, and information simultaneously.

How the Bidding Was Rigged

The mechanism matters. Pay attention to how this actually happened.

In September 2025, David Ellison showed up at WBD CEO David Zaslav’s home with an unsolicited offer. Nineteen dollars a share. Rejected. He raised it. Rejected again. He reportedly offered Zaslav a personal compensation package worth “several hundred million dollars.” Zaslav told his board it was “inappropriate to discuss.”

In December 2025, Netflix announced its $83 billion deal for WBD’s studio and streaming assets. Days later, Paramount launched a hostile takeover bid for the entire company at $108 billion.

Then the DOJ got involved. On January 22, 2026, the Department of Justice opened an antitrust review of the Netflix deal. Both companies received a formal “second request” for information, pausing the statutory waiting period. The probe cited potential violations of Section 7 of the Clayton Act and Section 2 of the Sherman Act, investigating whether Netflix’s acquisition would “substantially lessen competition or tend to create a monopoly.”

On February 12, the DOJ fired its own antitrust chief.

Gail Slater, confirmed with bipartisan support, Trump’s own pick, spent 11 months on the job overseeing the review. CBS News reported she was ousted by Attorney General Pam Bondi and Deputy AG Todd Blanche after she “lost their trust.” Her replacement, acting chief Omeed Assefi, immediately escalated the antitrust investigation into Netflix. Not Paramount. Netflix.

The DOJ sent civil investigative demands to filmmakers and producers across Hollywood, asking whether Netflix’s deal would create a monopoly. The deadline for responses? March 23. Three days after the scheduled shareholder vote on the Netflix deal.

Meanwhile, Paramount’s regulatory waiting period expired without challenge. No second request. No antitrust probe. No civil investigative demands. Their chief legal officer, Makan Delrahim, ran the DOJ Antitrust Division during Trump’s first term. He knows the filing timelines. He knows the people.

Senator Warren called Slater’s removal “corruption.” She said,

“a small army of MAGA-aligned lawyers and lobbyists have been trying to sell off merger approvals to the highest bidder.”

Then Trump demanded Netflix fire board member Susan Rice, a former Obama administration official, or “pay the consequences.”

Then Sarandos went to the White House. Then Netflix walked away.

Connect this to the pattern from my prior essay on the Quiet Capture of American Information. Trump sued Paramount over 60 Minutes. Threatened network licenses. Pressured ABC into a settlement. Paramount paid $16 million to settle a baseless lawsuit with the president to get FCC approval for its Skydance merger. This is the same playbook, just running in both directions. Punish enemies. Reward allies.

Subscribe now

Antitrust Was Never the Point

Antitrust law exists to prevent this. The Clayton Act. FCC media ownership rules. State attorney general authority. The legal infrastructure is there.

But the people who built the fence are now selling the gate.

Delrahim, who once argued against consolidation as Trump’s first-term antitrust chief, now works for Paramount. Slater, who was supposed to enforce the rules, got fired when she apparently wouldn’t bend them fast enough. FCC Chair Brendan Carr, a Trump loyalist, blessed the Paramount-Skydance merger after Ellison promised to end DEI programs and install a conservative ombudsman at CBS News.

California Attorney General Rob Bonta said it clearly, “Paramount/Warner Bros is not a done deal. These two Hollywood titans have not cleared regulatory scrutiny. The California Department of Justice has an open investigation, and we intend to be vigorous in our review.”

But the federal apparatus? It’s been captured.

The Fourth Layer

In my January 2026 essay, I outlined three layers being constructed.

Surveillance layer. Palantir is building a centralized database that consolidates data across agencies, enabling real-time tracking of behavior. A private company, founded by a Trump megadonor, with employees now staffing DOGE and former employees embedded throughout the administration.

Distribution layer. Broadcast networks are disciplined through regulatory threats and lawsuits. Corporate media self-censoring to protect merger approvals. Colbert canceled. Kimmel suspended. Networks donating to the White House. 60 Minutes’ investigation killed three hours before broadcast.

Access layer. Public broadcasting defunded. The one media system that wasn’t dependent on corporate advertising or billionaire ownership. Eliminated after 60 years.

Now add the fourth.

Content layer. The Ellison family controls what fills the screen once the other three layers have done their work. Palantir builds a file on you. The distribution system gets disciplined into compliance. Public media gets killed. And the content that pours into the remaining space is owned by people who owe the president everything.

You don’t need state TV if loyal billionaires own the only TV left.

What Already Happened at CBS

You don’t have to guess what Ellison ownership looks like. We already have the evidence.

After taking over Paramount in August 2025, David Ellison paid $16 million to settle Trump’s lawsuit against CBS over a 60 Minutes interview with Kamala Harris. Legal experts widely described the suit as baseless. Paramount paid anyway.

Ellison canceled The Late Show with Stephen Colbert. Colbert was the top-rated late-night host on network television. He’d called the settlement a “big fat bribe.” He was off the air within weeks.

Ellison bought Bari Weiss’s outlet, The Free Press, for $150 million and installed her as editor-in-chief of CBS News. Weiss had no experience in television reporting or news production.

Ellison promised FCC Chair Carr he would end all DEI initiatives across Paramount and appoint an ombudsman to field complaints of ideological bias. He named the former head of a conservative think tank to that role. Carr praised the moves.

Then came December 21, 2025. CBS had spent months working on an investigation into CECOT, the maximum-security prison in El Salvador where Trump sent hundreds of Venezuelan migrants. Reporter Sharyn Alfonsi interviewed former inmates about torture and abuse. The story was screened five times and cleared by CBS attorneys and Standards and Practices.

Hours before it was set to air, Weiss killed it.

Alfonsi wrote in an internal email that the decision was “political” and not editorial. “These men risked their lives to speak with us,” she wrote. “We have a moral and professional obligation to the sources who entrusted us with their stories. Abandoning them now is a betrayal of the most basic tenet of journalism: giving voice to the voiceless.”

The timing? The segment was scheduled to air the night before David Ellison raised his bid for Warner Bros. Discovery. David Dayen of The American Prospect wrote: “It’s hard to ignore that this happened just as Paramount’s hostile bid for Warner Bros. was slipping away. Time to please the king again.”

This is what happened at CBS. Now think about what happens to CNN.

What Happens to HBO, CNN, and DC

Jon Klein, a former top executive at both CNN and CBS News, told NPR: “It’s tech giants becoming media giants.”

Eight Democratic senators have already sent David Ellison a letter demanding he preserve all communications with Trump and administration officials about the Warner Bros. bid. They asked directly: Did you promise Trump changes to CNN in exchange for favorable treatment?

So here’s what’s on the table.

CNN. The network Trump has attacked more than any other major news outlet in the American media. The network, whose parent company now needs regulatory approval from Trump’s DOJ to finalize the deal. If CBS killed a CECOT investigation to keep the White House happy, what happens to CNN investigations into the administration when the same family signs the checks?

HBO. The network that produced some of the most significant documentary journalism and prestige television of the past three decades. Ellison has already said he plans to merge HBO Max with Paramount+ if the deal goes through. What gets greenlit when the owner’s priority is not angering the White House? What documentaries get shelved? What stories never get told?

DC Comics and Cartoon Network. These reach children. The characters kids grow up with, the stories that shape how they understand heroes and power and justice. All of it now filtered through the risk of political retaliation from a president who has shown he will use regulatory power to punish media companies that displease him.

Self-Censorship Doesn’t Need a Memo

You don’t have to tell journalists to back off. You just have to make the incentive structure clear.

Your CEO needs merger approval. Your parent company’s owner is a Trump donor. Your colleague at the network down the hall just watched her investigation get killed hours before broadcast. The president demanded a board member of the competing bidder be fired “or pay the consequences.” The DOJ’s antitrust chief got removed for apparently not moving fast enough in the right direction.

You figure it out.

Michel Foucault wrote about the Panopticon, a prison designed so guards could watch inmates at any time, but inmates could never tell when the guards were watching. The point wasn’t that someone was always watching. The point was that you could never be sure they weren’t. That uncertainty disciplines behavior automatically.

The same incentive structure now extends to every newsroom, every writers’ room, every development meeting at the combined Paramount-Warner company.

Netflix Wasn’t the Hero

I want to be direct about this. A Netflix-Warner combination would also be a problem. More consolidation, less competition, higher prices, less leverage for workers and independent creators. And my personal nightmare, less release window, if they got their hands on Sinners, it would have been in theaters for only 3 weeks. The DOJ’s formal antitrust concerns about Netflix’s market power weren’t invented out of thin air.

Two things can be true.

Netflix being too big is a market problem. Ellison's control over Warner is a democracy problem. The difference is that Netflix doesn’t have a documented record of killing investigations to appease the president. Netflix’s merger approval doesn’t depend on staying in Trump’s good graces. Netflix didn’t attend the State of the Union as a senator’s personal guest two days before winning a $111 billion deal.

The administration didn’t choose the lesser evil. It chose the one it could use.

The Oligarch Operating System, Version 3

Follow the money. Same structure I laid out in January, extended.

Peter Thiel. Palantir co-founder. $15 million donor to JD Vance. Federal contracts nearly doubled from $541 million to $970.5 million in 2025. His company builds the surveillance infrastructure.

Larry Ellison. Oracle co-founder. Net worth $201 billion. Trump supporter and adviser. Financed his son’s takeover of Paramount. Personally guaranteed $43.3 billion toward the Warner acquisition. Controls a major stake in TikTok’s U.S. operations through Oracle. Lives 20 minutes from Mar-a-Lago. His company controls the content infrastructure.

David Ellison. Paramount CEO. Attended Trump’s State of the Union as Lindsey Graham’s guest. Made conservative editorial changes at CBS. Hired Bari Weiss. Settled a baseless lawsuit with the president. Promised to end DEI across Paramount. Now positioned to run the largest combined media company in American history.

Makan Delrahim. Former DOJ Antitrust Division chief under Trump’s first term. Now, Paramount’s chief legal officer is navigating the exact regulatory apparatus he used to run.

Same architecture. Different sector. The playbook is: identify a strategic resource, rig the rules in your favor, and hand control to loyal capital. Oil. Data. And now narrative.

What You Can Actually Do

Contact your state attorney general. California AG Rob Bonta has already opened an investigation. State AGs have the authority to challenge this merger even if Trump’s DOJ won’t. That is the most direct pressure point right now. If you live in a state with a Democratic AG, call their office. Tell them this deal matters.

Support antitrust reform that accounts for political power, not just consumer prices. The current legal framework measures harm in streaming bundle costs. It needs to measure harm in the concentration of democratic speech. Senator Warren and others have introduced language addressing this. Push your representatives to co-sponsor it.

Be intentional about where your screen time goes. As a funding decision. Independent outlets, worker-owned publications, and local journalism survive on subscriber dollars. Every subscription to an independent outlet is a vote for media that doesn’t need a phone call with the White House to go well before it publishes.

And pay attention to the Senate. An antitrust subcommittee hearing is scheduled for March 4. Senator Cory Booker has called for Ellison to testify. Ellison has so far declined. Public pressure makes that harder to sustain.

Pirate. This may seem extreme, but piracy is illegal on the hosting side. A lot of outlets have already removed shows and TV that are ethical or diverse. Scream 7 is AI-made and fired its main actor for talking about Palestine.

The Night the Screen Goes Quiet

A story breaks. Something the administration doesn’t want covered. A deportation gone wrong. A contract awarded to a donor. A database used to target a critic.

And every outlet with reach is owned by someone who needs a phone call to go well. CBS already killed the story once. CNN’s new owner attended the State of the Union two days before winning the bid. TikTok’s algorithm is audited by the same family’s company.

The screen doesn’t go dark. It stays on, but the story never reaches the front.

You scroll through TikTok. You flip to HBO. You check CNN. Three different apps. Three different brands.

One family. One president’s number is saved in the phone.

This is the architecture I’ve been writing about since January. The surveillance layer tracks you. The distribution layer disciplines the messengers. The access layer eliminates alternatives. And now the content layer fills the screen with whatever the people in power want you to see.

You’re not a citizen anymore.

You’re a product in someone else’s political project.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.
