author: Joshua Smith

title: New Sections. Same Mission. Here’s How I’m Organizing This Space.
seoTitle: New Sections. Same Mission. Here’s How I’m Organizing This Space.
excerpt: What You See. What You Hear. What You Think. What About Me.
metaDescription: What You See. What You Hear. What You Think. What About Me.
bodyHtml:
I started this Substack two months ago with one rule: write about whatever bothers or excites me enough to research. That worked more than I expected. 600 of you now. But the archive was getting messy, and I want you to find what you came for without scrolling through 20 articles and 40 notes, hoping the algorithm does its job.

So I’m organizing.

Four sections. Each one covers a different way I process the world. Here’s the breakdown.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Whacha See?

This is a film. TV. Visual media. The stories Hollywood tells and the ones it refuses to.

If you came here because of the Sinners piece, this is your section. Same for the Green Book/BlacKkKlansman essay, the Mercy copaganda breakdown, or the Ted Lasso piece.

I write about what’s on screen, like Ted Lasso and my appreciation for it. But more than that, I write about who controls the screen. Who gets the prestige red carpet rollout and who gets the quiet release? Who gets called a genius and who gets called “promising.” What the camera shows you and what it leaves out on purpose.

The Wayans brothers built modern comedy and got written out of their own story. Ryan Coogler made an original film that grossed $368 million, and Variety questioned whether it was a real success. Jordan Peele reclaimed the cowboy from conservative mythology. These are “What You See” stories.

If you watch movies and TV and something feels off but you can’t name it, this section is for you.

Whacha Hear

Music. Sound. Language. The spoken word.

Kendrick Lamar lives here. So, does the piece on how “ain’t nobody wrong” captures an entire culture in three words that grammar textbooks call a mistake?

I’m a person who believes music tells truths that essays can’t always reach, mainly due to the gatekeeping in academia. When Kendrick raps about buying a homeless man a meal and still getting judged by God, that’s theology. When a blues riff shows up in a vampire movie set in 1933 Mississippi, when Delta Slim remembers the lynching of his brothers, that’s history you feel before you understand it.

This section is about what you hear and what it means. The music that shaped us. The language that defines us. The sounds that carry more weight than any essay I could write on politics.

Whacha Think

Systems. Money. Power. Who benefits.

This is the political economy section. Private equity is gutting businesses. Big Oil running foreign policy. Super PACs buying elections. Peter Thiel building a surveillance database while the government defunds public media. ICE killing a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis and calling it an immigration raid.

If the question is “why does this keep happening,” the answer is usually in the money. Follow it far enough and you find the system. That’s what this section does.

I also put the tech surveillance pieces here. Ring doorbells feeding footage to police. Flock cameras are tracking your license plate. The College of Charleston is quietly removing LGBTQ+ housing protections. These are all the same story: institutions choosing control over people.

Stats, media literacy, and the manufactured outrage machine live here too. If you read the piece about how TPUSA creates fake martyrs on college campuses, you’ve already been here. If you read the one about how crime statistics are really policing statistics, same.

This section exists because most of the things you’re angry about were designed to make you angry. The question is: who designed them and why.

What About Me

Personal essays. Philosophy. Book reviews. The writing craft. Poetry. Alpha Phi Alpha history.

This is the section where I stop analyzing larger systems and start analyzing myself and my thoughts about the world, and my experiences in it.

About Time and learning that grief isn’t something you fix. Book reviews of Travels with Epicurus and Ways to Live Forever. The piece about writing faster without writing worse.

My Alpha history pieces live here, too. The MLK Day speech. The seven Alpha men who won thirteen medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. Fritz Pollard, the first Black head coach in the NFL.

These feel personal because they are. The fraternity pieces are about my people. The film essays in this section are about my life. The craft pieces are about my process.

If the other three sections are me looking at the world, this one is me looking in the mirror.

Why This Matters

I write about culture, power, and the space between what we’re told and what’s true. That hasn’t changed. But now there’s a map.

You don’t have to read everything. Some of you are here for the film criticism. Some of you want the political analysis. Some of you showed up for one Kendrick essay and stuck around. All of that is fine.

The sections help you find what you want. They help me see what I’m building. And they give new readers a front door instead of a pile of posts.

Same mission. Better architecture.

See you in the next one.

Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work. If you are already subscribed, thank you, and to my paid subscribers, thank you so much, you mean the world to me.

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Heated Rivalry, Olympic Sexism, and The Tkachuk Brothers
seoTitle: Heated Rivalry, Olympic Sexism, and The Tkachuk Brothers
excerpt: A TV show brought millions of new fans to hockey. The league’s own stars, its president, and its FBI director just reminded those fans they were never welcome.
metaDescription: A TV show brought millions of new fans to hockey. The league’s own stars, its president, and its FBI director just reminded those fans they were never welcome.
bodyHtml:
On Sunday night, the U.S. men’s hockey team beat Canada 2-1 in overtime to win their first Olympic gold medal in 46 years. Jack Hughes scored the game-winner. The locker room exploded. Beer everywhere. Guys screaming. The kind of moment you dream about when you’re six years old, lacing up skates for the first time.

Then the FBI director walked in.

Kash Patel, who flew to Milan on a government jet his office swore was for “official business,” was in the locker room chugging beer, spraying it across the room, pounding on tables, and singing Toby Keith’s “Courtesy of the Red, White and Blue” while a player draped a gold medal around his neck. Matthew Tkachuk’s gold medal. On the FBI director’s neck. Nancy Guthrie, an 84-year-old woman and mother of a national news anchor, has been missing for over three weeks in a case his agency is investigating. An armed man was shot dead breaching the perimeter at Mar-a-Lago earlier that day.

That’s the scene. Hold it in your head. We’ll come back to it.

Subscribe now

Heated Rivalry Changed the Sport. The Sport Didn’t Change Back.

A television show about two closeted hockey players falling in love just became the biggest television event in the sport’s 108-year history. Heated Rivalry, adapted from Rachel Reid’s novel series, premiered on Crave and HBO Max in late November 2025. By Christmas, it had exploded. Over 600 million minutes watched and 10.6 million viewers in the US alone. An 83% increase in NHL TikTok followers. A 114% jump in average daily follower count on the league’s X account. The show’s stars, Hudson Williams and Connor Storrie, went from unknown actors to Golden Globe presenters and official Olympic torchbearers for the Milan 2026 Winter Games.

Queer hockey leagues across the country reported surges in new players. Chicago Pride Hockey received 100 new player requests since January. Boston Pride Hockey added more than 10 new players in a single month. The New York City Pride Hockey Alliance saw a wave of interest from people who’d never laced up skates before. Women filled arenas in what TikTok started calling “the boy aquarium.”

The NHL was thrilled. Commissioner Gary Bettman said he binged the show in one night. A league spokesperson told The Hollywood Reporter that Heated Rivalry was “the most unique driver for creating new fans” in the NHL’s history.

Here’s the problem.

The same week Bettman praised Heated Rivalry, he was at the White House to meet with the Trump administration. The same league celebrating new women and queer fans just spent three years making those fans feel unwelcome. And the two brothers the NHL chose as the face of its real rivalry, the one playing out on Olympic ice right now, are working against the same communities that Heated Rivalry brought through the door.

I’ve watched this movie before. So have you.

The Tkachuk Brothers Are the Face of the NHL’s Moment. Look at What That Face Is Telling You.

Matthew and Brady Tkachuk are the center of the NHL’s Olympic story. Matthew plays for the Florida Panthers in america. Brady plays for the Ottawa Senators. Their rivalry, brother against brother, country against country, is the marketing hook the league has been riding since the Four Nations Face-Off in February 2025. They launched a podcast, Wingmen, produced by the same company behind the Kelce brothers’ New Heights. They’re the NHL’s answer to Travis and Jason.

Except Travis Kelce didn’t join a presidential council committed to banning trans athletes from sports. Matthew Tkachuk did.

On August 1, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order revitalizing the President’s Council on Sports, Fitness, and Nutrition. Matthew Tkachuk was named to the 20-member council alongside Wayne Gretzky, Gary Bettman, and Harrison Butker. The council’s stated goals, per the White House, include “restoring tradition to college athletics” and “keeping men out of women’s sports.”

When the Florida Panthers visited the White House in January 2026 after winning back-to-back Stanley Cups, Tkachuk stood next to the president and said the walk around the White House “might’ve trumped” winning two championships. “Being an American,” he told Trump, “nothing beats this.”

The NHL said nothing.

Three episodes into the Wingmen podcast, in early December 2025, a clip went viral. Matthew Tkachuk said that when people outside hockey use player nicknames, it bothers him. But Tkachuk didn’t say “people.” He said “girls.”

“Nothing worse than when girls call a player by their nickname,” Matthew said on air.

Saying ‘girls’ rather than ‘fans’ implies that one person cannot be both.

The NHL said nothing.

The Locker Room Call

Now we’re back to Sunday night. Milan. Gold medals around necks. Kash Patel is holding his phone up on speaker so the president can talk to the boys.

Trump congratulated the team. He praised goaltender Connor Hellebuyck, who stopped 41 shots. He invited the entire team to the State of the Union address on Tuesday. Military plane. The works. The players cheered.

Then Trump got to the women.

“I must tell you, we’re going to have to bring the women’s team,” Trump said. He started laughing. “You do know that. I do believe I probably would be impeached.”

The locker room laughed with him.

The president of the United States treated the gold medal-winning women’s hockey team as a legal obligation. Not as athletes who earned the same thing the men earned. Not as a team that beat the same opponent, Canada, in the same fashion, overtime, three days earlier. As a chore. A political liability. A punchline.

And every man in that room laughed.

The women’s team is coached by the Hughes brothers’ mother, a former Olympic star herself, which means when the locker room laughed, they weren’t just laughing at “the women.” They were laughing at her. My mom would have slapped me into next week.

The Women’s Team Won First. Won Bigger. And Got Treated as an Afterthought.

The U.S. women beat Canada 2-1 in overtime on Thursday, February 19th. Captain Hilary Knight scored the tying goal with just over two minutes left in regulation. Knight is now the all-time leading scorer in U.S. women’s Olympic hockey history with 15 career Olympic goals. Megan Keller scored the game winner in overtime. The broadcast averaged 5.3 million viewers on USA Network and Peacock, peaking at 7.7 million in overtime.

It was the most-watched women’s hockey game in history.

No phone call from the president. No FBI director in the locker room spraying beer. No State of the Union invitation. No military plane.

Three days later, the men did the same thing. Same opponent. Same score. Same overtime drama. And the full weight of the federal government showed up to celebrate.

The numbers from all of the Olympics tell the bigger story. U.S. women won six gold medals at the 2026 Winter Olympics. The men won four. The women won 17 total medals. The men won 12. U.S. women have now out-medaled the men at three consecutive Winter Olympics and four consecutive Summer Olympics.

This is the same administration that claims to protect women’s sports by targeting trans athletes. The same president who says he’s fighting for fairness in women’s athletics. And when the women actually win, on the biggest stage in the world, at the sport this administration is most publicly attached to, they’re an afterthought to a locker room phone call.

The women’s team got the message.

On Monday, the U.S. Women’s Hockey Team declined Trump’s State of the Union invitation. A USA Hockey spokesperson told NBC News the team was “sincerely grateful for the invitation,” but unable to attend “due to the timing and previously scheduled academic and professional commitments.”

The Show Told a Love Story. The League Told a Different One.

Heated Rivalry works because it’s honest about what the NHL is. In the show’s fictional world, players are terrified of being outed. Rachel Reid, the author, told The Hollywood Reporter that some aspects of hockey culture made her “sad and angry.” She said one of the biggest was homophobia.

“I kept thinking about how difficult it would be to be a closeted NHL player.”

She was right.

The NHL is the only men’s professional sports league in North America with no active or retired player who has come out publicly as gay. Zero. Not one. The Professional Women’s Hockey League has 30 out queer players as of 2025. The men’s side has zero.

Hudson Williams, who plays Shane Hollander, told SiriusXM Radio that he’s received DMs from closeted professional athletes across hockey, football, and basketball since the show aired. They reach out through Reid, who relays “lovely, anonymous emails.” Sometimes they message Williams privately through Instagram.

Anonymous. Because coming out in the NHL isn’t safe.

In 2023, the NHL banned specialty warm-up jerseys, including Pride jerseys designed by local queer artists for teams. They also banned Pride tape on players’ sticks. Commissioner Bettman called Pride jerseys a “distraction.” The tape ban was reversed after player backlash, but the same commisioner profiting off of Heated Rivalry things actual progress is distracting and is hand in hand with the Trump administration.

So when the NHL celebrates Heated Rivalry’s ratings, we have to remember the show is popular because it depicts a world the league built and refuses to change. The fiction is aspirational. The institution is not.

While the Tkachuks Got Cameras, Jason Robertson Got Shown the Door

The Tkachuk brothers, who sit on a presidential anti-trans council and told women fans they don’t belong, are the face of the NHL’s Olympic moment, but they aren’t the best in the NHL. The highest-scoring American-born player in 2025 watched the Olympics from home.

Jason Robertson, a 26-year-old left wing for the Dallas Stars, scored 49 goals and 98 points in 2025. More than any other American. At the time of the roster announcement, he had 48 points through 41 games. He led the eligible American player pool in every major offensive category. By the Olympic break, he was up to 32 goals and 66 points in 57 games, tied for third in the entire NHL in goals.

He was not selected for Team USA.

JT Miller of the Rangers made the team with 22 points. Vincent Trocheck made it with 9 goals through 29 games. Robertson had 24 goals and 24 assists. The math is not close.

Robertson is Filipino-American. Born in Arcadia, California.

This was not the first time. Robertson was also left off the Four Nations Face-Off roster. He was excluded from the 2025 IIHF World Championships, where Team USA won gold without him.

The official explanation was “team balance” and “veteran leadership.” Robertson addressed it on January 3rd. “I thought that this year was different from last year,” he told reporters. “Obviously last year, I didn’t think I was good enough. Now, I think I did. It’s their choice and their decision, and that’s it.”

A man who outscored every other American in 2025 had to stand in front of cameras and accept that his production wasn’t enough. He said he loves the game. He said he won’t change how he plays.

That’s not acceptance. That’s the language of someone who knows the system won’t bend for him.

The NHL Can’t Support Its Own Moment

Here’s what I keep coming back to. The league has three separate cultural events right now that are pulling new audiences to hockey.

Heated Rivalry brought women and queer fans to the sport at a rate the league has never seen. The US-Canada Olympic rivalry, boosted by geopolitical tension and the return of NHL players to the Olympics for the first time since 2014, is drawing casual sports fans. And the Tkachuk brothers’ Wingmen podcast is the league’s attempt to replicate the Kelce magic, who, on their podcast, congratulated Layla Edwards on their podcast, who was the first black woman to play for the USA senior team, and Layla Edwards is the first black person to win gold in ice hockey.

All three are happening at once. And all three are in conflict.

The show tells a story about queer love surviving in a homophobic sport. The brothers tell women fans they don’t belong and sit on a council targeting trans athletes. The Olympic roster excludes the best American scorer because team balance apparently matters more when the scorer is Filipino-American. And the FBI director who facilitated the president’s locker room call is the same man who chugged beer on a government trip while an 84-year-old woman remains missing.

MAGA influencers shared Tkachuk clips from the Four Nations Face-Off as nationalist content. The league didn’t distance itself from any of it.

I wrote about this pattern in my piece on the cowboy myth. Conservative media took an identity that was historically multiracial, stripped it of its real history, and turned it into a symbol of white nationalism. Hockey didn’t need the stripping. The cost barriers, the geography, the locker room culture. The infrastructure was already there. So when Matthew Tkachuk stands next to Trump and the league doesn’t flinch, it’s because their audience and their stars are aligned in a way other leagues don’t have to manage.

That’s what $10,000-a-year youth hockey produces. A fan base that looks like Matthew Tkachuk. A roster that looks like Matthew Tkachuk. And an institution that protects what Matthew Tkachuk represents, even when the rest of the culture is moving somewhere else.

Two Things Can Be True

Heated Rivalry is beautiful. The hockey is incredible, who doesnt want to see people fight each other on ice. The Olympic rivalry is real and people are having a good time. Jack Hughes scoring the overtime winner on the 46th anniversary of the Miracle on Ice is the kind of story sports exist to tell.

And the institution hosting all of this excitement employs zero out gay players, left its most productive Asian-American skater at home, gave its biggest star a pass for backing anti-trans policy, stayed quiet when that star told women they don’t belong in hockey’s language, banned Pride tape two years ago, operates with a 90% white player base, let the FBI director turn a gold medal locker room into a MAGA rally, and watched the president of the United States treat the women’s gold medal team as a punchline.

Kyle McCarthy, a 32-year-old player for an LGBTQ+ hockey association in Vancouver called The Cutting Edges, quit hockey after coming out at 19. He was convinced that being openly gay and playing were not compatible. Asked whether Heated Rivalry could make hockey more welcoming, he told Canadian Affairs: “I hope it can. I don’t know that it will.”

What the NHL’s Moment Actually Costs

The question isn’t whether hockey deserves attention. The question is who gets written out of the story when the sport gets its spotlight.

Robertson sits at home with 32 goals and 66 points while players with half his stats wore the flag. Trans athletes are told there’s no room by a council that includes the league’s most-promoted player. Women fans get reminded that their fandom is conditional. The women’s team wins gold, breaks viewership records, out-medals the men across the entire Olympics, and gets treated as a political obligation. Queer fans stream a show about closeted players and then walk into arenas where Pride tape was banned 18 months ago. And the Tkachuk brothers get another White House visit, a podcast deal, and the FBI director putting a gold medal around their necks.

I keep coming back to what I wrote in my piece on Super PACs and lobbying. The system doesn’t fail. It performs. It performs exactly as designed, for exactly the people it was designed to serve. The NHL’s Olympic moment isn’t a rarity; the men's team locker room was filled with the same spirit that has kept hockey straight and white for the last 40 years.

It’s just not working for everyone. And the people it’s not working for are the exact same people who just made hockey the most talked-about sport in the country.

Subscribe now

Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

title: The BAFTAs Could Mute Palestine But Not The N-Word
seoTitle: The BAFTAs Could Mute Palestine But Not The N-Word
excerpt: A pre-recorded ceremony with a two-hour editing window chose what harm to air and what to silence. That tells you everything.
metaDescription: A pre-recorded ceremony with a two-hour editing window chose what harm to air and what to silence. That tells you everything.
bodyHtml:
Yesterday, on February 22, 2026, Michael B. Jordan and Delroy Lindo walked onto the stage at London’s Royal Festival Hall to present the first award of the night at the 79th BAFTA Film Awards. Best Visual Effects. Routine stuff. Jordan opened with the standard pleasantries. Then someone in the audience shouted the N-word at them.

The camera stayed on Jordan and Lindo. You could see it on Lindo’s face. His eyes widened. He froze for a beat. Then both men did what Black professionals have been trained to do since birth: they composed themselves and kept going.

The person who shouted the slur was John Davidson, a Tourette’s syndrome campaigner and the real-life inspiration behind the BAFTA-nominated film I Swear. Davidson has coprolalia, a specific type of Tourette’s that causes involuntary outbursts of obscene or socially inappropriate language. He also shouted “shut the fuck up” at BAFTA chair Sara Putt during her opening remarks, yelled “boring” during housekeeping announcements, and “bullshit” when guests were asked not to curse.

About 25 minutes in, Davidson left the ceremony on his own.

The host Alan Cumming told the audience, “You may have noticed some strong language in the background. This can be part of how Tourette’s syndrome shows up for some people.” He thanked the crowd for “understanding and helping create a respectful space for everyone.”

Later, Cumming added

“We apologize if you are offended tonight.”

He did not name the slur. He did not name the people it was directed at. He did not apologize to Jordan or Lindo specifically. He apologized if anyone was offended, which is the non-apology that corporations and institutions have perfected over the last two decades. It puts the burden on the people who were harmed to justify their own pain.

This happened during Black History Month. At an awards ceremony, Ryan Coogler became the first Black man to win Best Original Screenplay. Where Wunmi Mosaku won Best Supporting Actress and spoke about Black women feeling seen. Where Jordan and Lindo are both Oscar-nominated for Sinners, a film about the forces that have drained Black communities for generations.

Two things can be true at once. And both of them are.

Subscribe now

Coprolalia Is Real. So Is The Harm.

To be clear, I am not questioning Davidson’s diagnosis. Coprolalia is a neurological condition. It is involuntary. The Tourette Association of America reports that it affects approximately 10% of people with Tourette’s syndrome. The CDC confirms that number. The outbursts are not chosen. They are not reflections of a person’s character or beliefs.

The condition is also well documented in how it functions. Research published in Deviant Behavior in 2025 found that people with coprolalia face a double stigma, navigating Tourette’s while embodying the very stereotype others in the Tourette’s community try to dispel.

The Tourette Association of America’s own resource page on coprolalia explains it as

“Particularly embarrassing for some individuals with coprolalia are involuntary outbursts within social contexts, such as racial or ethnic slurs in the company of the very people who would be most offended by such remarks.”

The leading Tourette’s organization in America acknowledges that slurs are often triggered in the presence of the people they target. The condition is involuntary. The harm is predictable.

Davidson is a lifelong campaigner for Tourette’s awareness. He received an MBE in 2019 for his advocacy. He knows what his tics involve. BAFTA knows. The BBC knows. The producers of the ceremony know. Everyone in that room knew what was possible when they seated Davidson in the audience at a live, microphone-heavy event.

So the question is not whether Davidson chose to say it. He did not. The question is, why did the people who organized this event choose not to prevent it?

The Editing Room That Silences What It Wants

The fact is that the BAFTAs are pre-recorded, which turns this from an unfortunate incident into an institutional failure.

The ceremony runs approximately three hours. The BBC edits it down to a two-hour broadcast. They have time. They have tools. They have editorial discretion.

They used that discretion this year. Akinola Davies Jr., who won the Outstanding British Debut award for My Father’s Shadow, ended his acceptance speech by dedicating the win to people under occupation and persecution, and said, “Free Palestine.” The BBC cut that from the broadcast. A BBC insider told Deadline that avoiding a repeat of the Glastonbury controversy, where Bob Vylan’s set included chants the BBC later said it regretted streaming, was a central concern in planning the ceremony.

They had contingency plans for politics. They had no contingency plan for a racial slur shouted at two Black Oscar nominees on a stage in front of the Prince and Princess of Wales, from a man whose film is about shouting obscenities.

“Free Palestine” from a debut filmmaker? Cut. A white man screaming the N-word at two Black actors? Aired.

The BBC’s official statement on the edits: “The live event is three hours and it has to be reduced to two hours for its on-air slot.” They chose to keep this in. This is what institutional power looks like: the organization decides whose speech is a problem and whose pain is acceptable.

Impact Over Intent Is Not Conditional

We teach this to children. We teach it in workplaces. We teach it in schools. Impact matters more than intent. If you step on someone’s foot, it hurts whether you meant to or not. You apologize.

The online response to the BAFTAs incident split into two camps almost immediately. One side defended Davidson; his tics are involuntary, he cannot control them, this is what Tourette’s looks like, and we need to be more understanding. The other side pointed out that two Black men were called the N-word in a room full of their peers, on camera, during what was supposed to be a celebration, and nobody apologized to them directly.

Both of those things are true. And only one group is being asked to sit with its discomfort in silence.

There is a legal precedent, at least in America, for this exact tension. In a federal case documented by employment law firm HR Simple, a clinic employee with coprolalia repeatedly used the N-word around a Black coworker. The coworker filed a racial harassment claim. The court ruled that the employer could take action against an employee with Tourette’s syndrome if “the disability-caused behavior is disruptive to co-workers.” The court also rejected the argument that the coworker had no right to complain, noting that “complaining to one’s employer about being called the N-word in the workplace is protected activity.”

The law does not treat involuntary harm as harmless. The BAFTAs should not either.

Davidson left the ceremony after the slur. That suggests he understood the gravity of what happened. But there has been no public apology from Davidson to Jordan and Lindo. No statement from BAFTA acknowledging the specific harm done to two Black men on their stage. No statement from the BBC addressing why the slur aired, but political speech did not.

Share

What Should Have Happened

Davidson’s presence at the BAFTAs was not random. He was there because I Swear was nominated; it is a great film. Robert Aramayo, who plays Davidson in the film, won Best Actor that night and the Rising Star award. The film won for Best Casting. Davidson was part of the story being celebrated. His invitation made sense.

But invitations come with responsibilities. If BAFTA knew Davidson had coprolalia, and they did, then they had a duty to mitigate risk. The Tourette Association of America documents strategies people with coprolalia use, muffling outbursts, positioning away from microphones, and pre-arranged exit plans. They are established practices.

BAFTA could have seated Davidson away from hot microphones. They could have provided a private viewing area with a quick exit. They could have assigned a liaison. They could have discussed the risks with Davidson and his team beforehand and built a plan that honored both his presence and the safety of everyone else in the room.

None of that appears to have happened. Davidson was seated centrally. Microphones picked up everything. The first 25 minutes of a pre-recorded ceremony were disrupted, and the production team’s response was to have the host read disclaimers.

Inclusion without accommodation is not inclusion. It is a spectacle.

The Pattern

This is not the first time the BAFTAs have fumbled on race. In 2020, every acting nominee was white. Joaquin Phoenix used his acceptance speech to call out systemic racism in the industry. The year before, the Oscars chose Green Book, a white savior film, over BlacKkKlansman. I wrote one of my first articles about this topic. The pattern across award ceremonies on both sides of the Atlantic is that Black art is celebrated when it is convenient and sidelined when it becomes uncomfortable.

What happened yesterday was not just about one man’s tics. It was about an institution that had every tool to prevent harm, chose not to use them, and then responded with vague language designed to avoid accountability.

BAFTA can mute politics. BAFTA can edit speeches. BAFTA can cut three hours to two and decide what fits.

They decided a racial slur fit.

Jordan and Lindo deserve a direct, named apology from BAFTA. They deserve a direct acknowledgment from Davidson. And every awards ceremony going forward needs a protocol for exactly this situation, because the existence of a disability does not erase the existence of racism, and no institution should be allowed to hide behind one to avoid addressing the other.

The tics are involuntary. The editorial choices were not.

Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

author: Joshua Smith

title: Ted Lasso and the Persistence of Hope: Fatherhood, Masculinity, and the Price of Redemption
seoTitle: Ted Lasso and the Persistence of Hope: Fatherhood, Masculinity, and the Price of Redemption
excerpt: How Ted Lasso Turns Broken Fathers, Silent Wounds, and Second Chances into a Blueprint for Better Men. This article is dedicated to my dad.
metaDescription: How Ted Lasso Turns Broken Fathers, Silent Wounds, and Second Chances into a Blueprint for Better Men. This article is dedicated to my dad.
bodyHtml:
I finished Ted Lasso last week, and I thanked my lucky stars for my dad, specifically for the ending. The finale song is “Father and Son” by Cat Stevens.

Not a fight song. Not a victory anthem. A song about a father who cannot explain the world to his son and a son who cannot wait to leave it behind, even if he doesn’t know what he will do. That tells you everything about what this show was really about. It was never about soccer.

Ted Lasso is a three-season examination of what happens to people when their fathers fail them. And what it takes to build something better from the wreckage. There will be spoilers, but it doesn’t spoil the work.

Subscribe now

The Wound That Runs Through Richmond

Here is what connects almost every character at AFC Richmond: a broken relationship with a father.

Ted Lasso’s dad killed himself when Ted was sixteen. Jamie Tartt’s father beat him into becoming a football prodigy, then showed up drunk to mock him after losses. Nate Shelley’s father sat across from him at dinner for decades and never once told him he was proud of him. Rebecca Welton’s father cheated on her mother and taught her that love was a transaction. Sam Obisanya is the exception, and we will get to why that matters.

Fathers are the backstory of almost every characterization on Ted Lasso.

Every act of cruelty, of self-sabotage, every wall a character builds traces back to the same source. A father who was absent, abusive, unavailable, or some combination of all three.

Share

Ted: The Father Who Stayed By Leaving

Ted Lasso is a good man. That is the first thing the show shows you and the last thing it confirms. But good men carry damage too.

His father “quit” on his family. That is how Ted describes it when asked; the reality is his dad died by suicide. Ted found him. He was sixteen years old.

Then Ted spent the next twenty years making sure no one around him ever felt abandoned or unheard. He became relentlessly positive. Aggressively kind. Every biscuit box, every folksy metaphor, every corny joke was a brick in the wall between Ted Lasso and the grief he refused to face.

Ted has panic attacks he hides from everyone. He resists therapy. He smiles through his divorce, smiles through his separation from his son, smiles through every crisis because stopping means feeling, and feeling means going back to that room where he found his father. Or, as people who love the show say on TikTok, he embodies “depression cannot hit a moving target.”

Dr. Sharon Fieldstone, the team’s sports psychologist, finally cracks it open. Ted does not want to talk. He does not want to process. He wants to keep coaching, keep helping, keep being the dad to everyone else that his own father never was. The show makes the point outright.

When Roy and Ted sit in a pub, a server asks, “You two are father and son, right?” Roy says, “What? No.” Ted says, “I’m his former coach.” The server shrugs: “It’s all the same thing.”

Two things are true at once. Ted is a great surrogate father to the men at Richmond. And Ted uses that role to avoid dealing with his own son, his own marriage, and his own grief. His relentless focus on making everyone else okay is the same emotional suppression patriarchy teaches every man. Just wrapped in a nicer package.

Kevin Roy, a masculinity researcher at the University of Maryland who has studied fatherhood for nearly three decades, wrote in the Baltimore Sun: “My father struggled with the legacy of his abusive father, and he died without talking with me about the toll that it took on him.” That is Ted. A man who watched his father die from the weight of unspoken pain, then spent decades doing the exact same thing with a smile on his face, and making sure others aren’t him.

The show’s resolution is honest. Ted does not fix himself by fixing Richmond. He fixes himself by choosing to go home. By choosing to be present for his actual son instead of hiding behind the surrogate family he built three thousand miles away. The hardest thing Ted does in three seasons is not win a football match. It is admitting that his kindness was also a form of avoidance.

Jamie Tartt: The Boy Who Became His Father’s Fist

Jamie walks into season one as the most hateable person on the show. Arrogant. Selfish. Cruel to his teammates. Then you meet his dad.

James Tartt Sr. is a violent, alcoholic bully who turned his son into a weapon and punished him for not being lethal enough. Jamie passes the ball, and his dad throws a shoe at his head. Jamie’s entire personality, the cockiness, the refusal to pass the ball, the need to be the center of every moment, is the mechanism of a kid who learned that his value was tied to his performance. Score goals, and Dad notices you. Miss and Dad remind you what you are.

The show’s most powerful scene happens in season two, episode eight. Richmond loses to Manchester City in the FA Cup semifinal. The team is crushed. Jamie’s father, drunk and belligerent, walks into the locker room to berate his son in front of everyone.

Jamie punches him in the face.

Coach Beard drags the father out. The locker room goes silent. Jamie is shaking. And then Roy Kent, the man who spent two seasons openly despising Jamie Tartt, walks across the room and hugs him.

Jamie collapses into Roy’s arms and sobs.

That moment is the turning point of the entire show. Not just for Jamie. For everyone.

Roy does not hug Jamie because he likes him. Roy hugs Jamie because he recognizes something. Roy’s niece Phoebe has a father Roy describes as a “living piece of shit.” Roy knows what it looks like when a kid is drowning under the weight of a terrible dad. He sees it in Jamie, and he does the only thing that matters. He shows up.

By season three, Roy is Jamie’s coach. And coaching, in the world of Ted Lasso, is just fatherhood with a whistle. Roy pushes Jamie to be better the way a good father would. Not through fear. Through accountability.

In season three, Jamie goes back to Manchester City. Not as a player. As a man returning to the place where his father’s shadow is longest. He visits his mother. He has the conversation he needed to have. Then, injured, he walks onto the pitch and scores the winning goal as both sets of fans cheer.

The show mirrors Roy’s own final game in that moment. The old man and the young man. The surrogate father and the son who never asked for one but needed one more than anyone. Jamie’s redemption is not about football. It is about a boy who finally stopped trying to earn his father’s love and started accepting love from the people who actually offered it, and more importantly, learning to love himself.

The Good Fathers: Roy, Sam, and the Blueprint

Ted Lasso does not just show you broken fatherhood. It shows you the alternative.

Roy Kent is a father figure twice over, first, to his niece Phoebe, whose actual father is absent and useless. Roy picks her up from school. Handles the parent-teacher conferences. He makes sure she knows she is loved every single day. He is gruff about it, but that’s just Roy. He swears constantly. But he shows up. That is the whole job.

Because Roy practices good fatherhood with Phoebe, he has the tools when Jamie needs him. The hug in the locker room does not come from nowhere. It comes from a man who already learned that strength means being present when someone is falling apart.

Sam Obisanya is the show’s most positive point about fatherhood. Sam has a great dad. His father calls him. Supports him. Gives him advice that Sam actually follows. When Sam’s restaurant gets destroyed, his father tells him to fight forward rather than fight back.

That phrase matters. Fight forward. Not against the thing that hurt you. Toward the thing that is right.

Sam is the only major male character who does not carry a father wound. And look at the result. He is kind without being performative. Confident without being arrogant. He takes principled stands without needing validation from anyone.

Sam is the show’s answer to the question it keeps asking. What does a man look like when his father gets it right?

Higgins: The Man Who Stopped Running

Nobody talks about Leslie Higgins. He does not have a redemption arc. He does not punch his father in the face. He does not betray anyone or break down sobbing in a locker room. He just shows up every day, does his job, loves his family, and somehow that makes him the most emotionally healthy man at AFC Richmond.

The scene is in “Man City.” The episode we talked about ealier S2E8, NPR affectionately dubbed “Father Figures.”

Jamie needs tickets for his dad. His dad and his mates want to come to the FA Cup semifinal at Wembley. They will be rooting for Manchester City against Jamie’s own team. Jamie’s father is making his son arrange seats so he can watch his son lose and enjoy it.

Before Jamie goes to Higgins for the tickets, he overhears Sam on the phone with his father. Mr. Obisanya is warm. Encouraging. Present. He is doing the thing fathers are supposed to do and making it look easy. Jamie watches this, and you can see it on his face. Not anger. Envy. The quiet, gut-level envy of a kid who never got that and knows he never will.

Jamie asks Higgins to put his dad in the worst seats possible. Higgins gets them VIP seats instead because Higgins understands something Jamie does not yet. You cannot punish your father into being different. You can only decide how you hold it.

And then Higgins says:

“I try to love my dad for who he is and forgive him for who he is not.”

The show does not tell us much about Higgins’ dad. It does not need to. The line tells you everything. Higgins had a father who was neither perfect nor enough. And instead of letting that define him, he made a choice. He would not spend his life angry about the father he wanted. He would accept the father he got and build something different.

That is why Jamie goes to Higgins. Not Ted. Not Roy. This is a man who figured something out about fathers that everyone else is still wrestling with. You do not get the dad you deserve. You get the dad you get. And the only choice that matters is what you build after that.

The episode puts three father-son relationships side by side. Sam’s father, who gets it right. Jamie’s father, who gets it catastrophically wrong. And Higgins, who had a flawed father and chose peace over war. Three options. The show is asking you which one you are going to be.

Sometimes the bravest thing a man can do is not fight. It is accepted. Quiet, unglamorous, clear-eyed acceptance. And then go be better.

Nate: The Resentment That Silence Builds

Nate Shelley is the character people have the hardest time with. They describe his arc as undeserved or surprising. That is the point.

When we meet Nate, he is the kit man. Invisible. Bullied by the players. Ted sees him and does what Ted does. Lifts him up. Promotes him to assistant coach. Tells him his ideas matter. Makes him feel, for the first time in his life, like he is important.

Then Nate burns it all down.

Unlike Jamie, the show does not give us a monster for Nate’s father. Nate’s father is quiet. Present. He shows up to dinner. He sits across from his son. He just never tells Nate he is proud.

There is a subplot about Nate trying to get the window table at his father’s favorite restaurant. He wants his dad to see him. Not in the literal sense. Nate wants his father to look at what he has become and say the words. And his father, for reasons the show suggests are generational and cultural, cannot do it.

This is the subtle genius of Nate’s arc. His father is not a villain. His father loves his son, but was never taught how to show it. Nate, starving for that validation, assumes the silence means disapproval.

So when Ted gives Nate what his father never did, Nate latches on. Ted becomes the father figure. And when Ted, dealing with his own panic attacks and divorce, pulls back even slightly while promoting another person, Nate interprets it as abandonment.

“You made me feel like I was the most important person in the whole world,” Nate tells Ted, “And then you abandoned me.”

That line is not about Ted. That line is about Nate’s father. Every word of it.

Nate does terrible things. He leaks Ted’s panic attacks to the press. He betrays the team. He goes to work for Rupert. I do not excuse any of it. But the show asks you to understand its origins.

An insecure person who gets pulled up by others but never does the internal work to address their doubts will externalize their inadequacy. Dr. Jorge Rodriguez, a researcher on generational trauma, identified this when analyzing Nate’s character. The cycle repeats. A father who cannot show love produces a son who cannot receive it. That son inflicts his confusion on everyone around him.

Nate’s redemption in season three is quieter than Jamie’s. He apologizes. He comes back. He sits with his father, and they begin, slowly, to talk. Not dramatic. Not a grand gesture. Two men learning to say what they mean after a lifetime of silence.

That is harder to swallow for fans than punching your dad in the face. And the show knows it.

Rebecca: The Daughter’s Side of the Same Story

This is not just a show about sons and their fathers. Rebecca Welton spent her entire adult life recovering from a father who cheated on her mother and a husband, Rupert, who did the same thing.

Rebecca’s arc mirrors Ted’s. She uses control the way Ted uses positivity. She bought the football club because it was the thing her ex-husband loved most, then hired an American football coach to destroy it. Her cruelty in season one reflects Ted’s positivity. Both are defense mechanisms built on the same foundation. A parent who taught them the wrong lesson about love.

Rebecca’s healing comes through friendship, not romance. Her relationship with Keeley is one of the show’s great gifts. Two women who build each other up without competition, without the transactional dynamics their fathers and husbands taught them.

By the finale, Rebecca has stopped trying to destroy things Rupert loves and started building things she cares about.

Either All of Us or None of Us

Ted says the most important line of the show is to Coach Beard. Not to the team. Not in a locker room speech. To his best friend, in private, while asking him to do something difficult.

He wants to bring Nate back.

Beard does not want to. And honestly, who would? Nate leaked Ted’s panic attacks. Betrayed the team. Walked out and went to work for Rupert. Beard has every reason to say no.

But Ted has leverage. Not the manipulative kind. The earned kind.

Ted already did this for Beard. After Beard went to jail. After Beard stole Ted’s car. Ted did not cut him off. He gave him a second chance and a job, because that is what you do for the people you believe in, even when they make it hard.

So when Ted looks at Beard and says:

“I hope that either all of us or none of us are judged by the actions of our weakest moments… but rather by the strength we show when, and if we’re ever given a second chance.”

He is not being abstract. He is reminding Beard of something personal. You got your second chance. You are standing here because I gave it to you. Now I am asking you to do the same for someone else.

Ted does not say, “I forgive Nate.” He does not say that what Nate did was fine. He says something far more damning: the standard we use for one person has to be the standard we use for all of them. Beard gets judged by his worst moment? Then so does Nate. So does Rebecca. So does Jamie. So does Ted himself and his dad. If that is the system, nobody survives it.

That is the show’s thesis. Not forgiveness as a feel-good gesture. Forgiveness as a logical framework. If you believe people can grow, you have to believe it about everyone. Not just the people you like. Not just the people whose pain looks sympathetic from the outside. Everyone. Or the whole thing falls apart.

Masculinity, the way most men are taught it, does not work like this. It is about individual performance. Be the strongest. Win on your own. Ask for nothing. Show no weakness. And when someone wrongs you, punish them.

Ted Lasso rejects all of this by showing what happens when men stop performing and start connecting.

Jamie becomes his best self when he passes the penalty kick to Dani Rojas instead of taking it himself. The kid trained by his father to believe individual glory was the only thing worth chasing gives the biggest moment of his career to a teammate. Roy becomes his best self when he hugs a man he spent years hating. Nate becomes his best self when he admits he was wrong and comes home. Ted becomes his best self when he chooses his son over his team.

None of those moments happens alone. Each one requires another person. A teammate. A friend. A therapist. A son. And everyone requires a second chance that someone else chose to offer.

Ted’s relentless optimism nearly destroys him. Nate’s need for approval nearly destroys the team. Jamie’s obsession with proving himself to a man who will never be satisfied nearly destroys his career. They all need others to help them out of these to grow.

What fixes things is not a pep talk. It is the willingness to be honest about the wound. To name it. To sit with it. To stop passing it down. And to extend to others the same grace you hope someone will extend to you when your weakest moment comes. Because it will come. For all of us.

The Cycle Breaks Here

Every father in Ted Lasso is either healing from their own father or repeating him. There is no middle ground. The show argues that this is the central question of masculinity. Not how strong are you? Not how much can you endure? But what are you going to do with the pain your father gave you?

You can do what James Tartt did and turn it into a weapon. You can do what Nate’s father did and bury it so deep that your silence becomes its own kind of violence. You can do what Ted’s father did and let it consume you entirely.

Or you can do what Ted, Jamie, Nate, Roy, and Sam each do in their own way. Look at the wound. Name it. Choose, every single day, not to pass it on.

The show ends with Ted coaching his son’s football team back in Kansas. Not a Premier League squad. A group of kids. His kid.

He went home. He stopped being a father to everyone else and became a father to the person who needed him most.

Cat Stevens was right. It is hard. But it is harder to ignore it.

If you haven’t watched Ted Lasso, please watch it. Not for the soccer. For the lessons it shows you. It reminded me how lucky I am to have my dad. And it taught me to love him for who he is and forgive him for who he is not.

This article is dedicated to my dad.

Share

Joshua's Substack is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

title: Your Doorbell Is a Cop: Ring, Flock, and the Super Bowl Ad Pulled At Your Heart Strings To Sell Big Brother
seoTitle: Your Doorbell Is a Cop: Ring, Flock, and the Super Bowl Ad Pulled At Your Heart Strings To Sell Big Brother
excerpt: How Amazon sold you a lost dog commercial, ICE got a nationwide camera network, and a $60 million movie told you to be fine with it.
metaDescription: How Amazon sold you a lost dog commercial, ICE got a nationwide camera network, and a $60 million movie told you to be fine with it.
bodyHtml:
On February 9, 2026, 113 million people watched Ring sell them a surveillance network disguised as a lost dog commercial.

The Super Bowl. A yellow Lab named Milo goes missing. A little girl cries. Jamie Siminoff, Ring’s founder, narrates as shots of outdoor cameras light up across a neighborhood, scanning footage and using AI to find a match. Andy Jassy, Amazon’s CEO, bragged on X that 99 dogs were reunited in 90 days.

Andy Jassy@ajassy
People ask me all the time about compelling use cases of AI. Here’s a good one. Millions of dogs go missing in the U.S. every year—and options for finding them are often painfully limited. Our Ring team saw an opportunity to use our community and technology to help, so they
3:48 PM · Feb 8, 2026 · 1.24M Views
412 Replies · 496 Reposts · 4.2K Likes

Cute. Wholesome. One of the most blatant pieces of surveillance propaganda ever broadcast on American television.

Here’s what the ad didn’t say. The same AI scanning for Milo can scan for you. The feature, called Search Party, is turned on by default. You don’t even need to own a Ring camera to use it now. As of early February, anyone in the United States can start a Search Party through the Ring app, even without owning a Ring device. People saw through the smoke and generated backlash for the ad.

Three days later, Ring canceled its partnership with Flock Safety, the license plate surveillance company connected to 4,800 law enforcement agencies in 49 states. The press release said the integration needed “significantly more time and resources than anticipated.” The headlines called it a retreat.

It wasn’t.

They sold you a lost dog. They built a panopticon. And when the backlash hit, they didn’t tear down the panopticon. They issued a placating press release.

I wrote about the panopticon before. Michel Foucault’s prison, where you never know if the guard is watching. Palantir built it for the federal government. Ring, just put it on your front porch.

The History They Hoped You’d Forget

Ring’s relationship with law enforcement didn’t start with the Super Bowl ad. It started a decade ago.

In 2016, Ring began courting police departments with free camera giveaways. In 2018, Amazon acquired Ring for $839 million and launched the Neighbors app, with “real-time crime alerts” and law enforcement partnerships built into the product. By 2022, Politico reported that police could request and receive emergency Ring footage without warrants or owner permission. Ring disclosed it had done this at least 11 times in a single year. By 2023, more than 2,600 police departments had formal partnerships with Ring.

In January 2024, Ring announced it would stop letting police request footage directly from users. A brief pivot toward privacy. The headlines were generous.

Then came the reversal. In September 2025, Ring launched Community Requests, a new feature that lets law enforcement agencies request footage from nearby Ring users during active investigations. Ring partnered with Axon, the police tech company known for its first-responder drones, police cameras, and tasers, to run the system.

In October 2025, Ring announced a second partner for Community Requests: Flock Safety, the license plate reader company with the largest surveillance network in the country.

In December 2025, Ring released “Familiar Faces,” a facial recognition feature for doorbells. It scans everyone who approaches a Ring camera and compares them against a list of pre-saved identities. Senator Ed Markey wrote to Amazon demanding they abandon it. Amazon’s response revealed that Ring’s privacy protections apply only to device owners, not to members of the public captured on camera.

Read that again. If your neighbor has a Ring, you have no privacy protections. You didn’t consent. You didn’t opt in. You’re just in the frame.

Thirty percent of U.S. households with internet now have a video doorbell camera, according to Park Associates. Ring is the most popular brand. That means if you walk down a residential street in America, someone’s AI is scanning you. And you have no say in it.

On February 11, two days after the Super Bowl ad, Senator Markey wrote to Amazon again. He called the ad a revelation of “the serious privacy and civil liberties risks attendant to these types of Artificial Intelligence-enabled image recognition technologies.” He demanded Amazon discontinue facial recognition in Ring doorbells.

Amazon has not complied.

The Side Door

This is the section that goes from “maybe you’re overreacting” to “no, this is exactly what they’re doing.”

Flock Safety says it has cameras in 5,000 communities across 49 states. Its cameras conduct 20 billion license plate reads per month. Flock markets itself as a tool to find stolen cars and missing people. A small-town safety solution.

Here’s the problem. Flock says it doesn’t partner with ICE. That’s technically true. But in May 2025, 404 Media broke a story that changed everything.

Researchers obtained Flock’s audit logs through a public records request from a single police department in Danville, Illinois. The data showed search logs from departments around the country. In the “reason” field, officers typed what they were looking for. Over 4,000 searches referenced ICE, immigration, or deportation. Local police around the country were performing lookups in Flock’s nationwide database at the request of federal immigration agents, giving ICE side-door access to a surveillance tool it doesn’t formally contract with.

Flock’s own policies prohibit agencies from using its data for immigration enforcement. The audit logs show they did it anyway. Thousands of times.

The details got worse as more data came out.

Virginia

Nearly 3,000 immigration-related searches on the Flock network over a 12-month period, even though Virginia police departments said they wouldn’t use the system for immigration enforcement. Ninety percent of those searches came from out-of-state agencies. One user in Fairfax County searched data from over 76,000 cameras in a single lookup. The reason field read “DHS case assistance.” The Virginia Center for Investigative Journalism found that images from one small town, Bridgewater, were searched nearly 7 million times during a single 12-month period. Bridgewater has five cameras.

Washington

The University of Washington Center for Human Rights found that at least eight law enforcement agencies shared Flock data directly with U.S. Border Patrol during 2025. Ten more had “back door” access that Border Patrol never formally requested. Senator Ron Wyden discovered that Flock had run a pilot giving data access to Border Patrol, HSI, ICE, and the Secret Service without telling its own law enforcement clients. He asked the FTC to investigate Flock for negligent cybersecurity practices.

Texas

A police officer in Johnson County searched Flock’s nationwide network to track a woman who had self-administered an abortion. The search reason field read: “had an abortion, search for female.” The search pulled data from cameras in Yakima and Prosser, Washington, a state that protects abortion access. Reproductive rights advocates raised the alarm that license plate data from blue states was being weaponized by law enforcement in red states.

Flock said it was “unaware of any credible case” of its tech being used against reproductive healthcare.

The company that claims it doesn’t work with ICE built the infrastructure that lets ICE work through everyone else.

The Cancellation That Didn’t Fix Anything

On February 12, three days after the Super Bowl ad aired, Ring announced it was canceling its partnership with Flock. The statement said the integration “would require significantly more time and resources than anticipated.” Ring’s spokesperson said no customer videos were ever shared with Flock. The integration was never live.

A protest was scheduled for the following day outside Amazon’s Seattle headquarters. Senator Markey’s second letter had arrived. The EFF had published a detailed report on Ring’s surveillance architecture. The backlash hit critical mass.

That sounds like a win. It isn’t.

Ring still partners with Axon through the same Community Requests feature. A Ring spokesperson confirmed the Axon partnership is unaffected. The pipeline from your doorbell to law enforcement didn’t close. It rerouted. Community Requests is still active. Search Party is still on by default. Familiar Faces still scans anyone who walks past your neighbor’s door.

And Flock’s network still exists. Five thousand communities. 4,800 law enforcement agencies. Twenty billion plate reads a month. Forty-nine states. The ICE side-door access didn’t depend on Ring. Local police were running immigration searches through Flock before Ring ever signed the partnership. They’ll keep running them after.

Cities are pushing back. Denver removed its Flock cameras. Colorado, Illinois, Massachusetts, New York, North Carolina, Texas, and Washington have all seen cities terminate Flock contracts or push new regulations. Santa Clara County, where the Super Bowl was played, is weighing ending its Flock contract right now. The Charlottesville Police Department became the first agency in Virginia to cut off all outside access to its Flock data after learning other jurisdictions were running searches for ICE.

But for every city that cancels, Flock signs up more. And when Flock faced scrutiny, it responded by limiting the information available in its audit logs, the same transparency tool that exposed the abuse. When journalists pushed back, Flock CEO Garrett Langley said the company’s critics constituted an “attack on law enforcement.” Flock told 404 Media the concern over their data amounted to “activists trying to let murderers go free.”

The playbook is familiar. When the architecture of surveillance gets exposed, rebrand the critics as the enemy.

Canceling one partnership doesn’t dismantle the architecture.

Amazon Made a Movie About This. That’s the Problem.

In 2002, Spielberg’s Minority Report warned audiences about a future where the government arrests people before they commit crimes, where retina scanners track you through a mall, and where the merger of corporate and government surveillance destroys privacy. The film was distributed by DreamWorks and Fox. Neither company sold surveillance products. The movie was free to be a warning because nobody making it had a financial stake in the thing it warned about.

Twenty-four years later, Amazon made Mercy. And the warning became a sales pitch.

Three weeks before the Super Bowl ad, Amazon MGM Studios released Mercy in theaters. The film is set in near-future Los Angeles. Crime has overwhelmed the city. The government creates the Mercy Court, where an AI judge, Maddox (played by Rebecca Ferguson), presides over 90-minute trials. The defendant sits strapped to a chair. The AI accesses every camera in the city, every phone, every surveillance feed, every social media account. If the defendant can’t prove their innocence within the time limit, they’re executed on the spot. Guilty until proven innocent, adjudicated by algorithm.

ince everything is plugged into the cloud, defendants have access to everyone’s Ring cameras, phone records, and surveillance devices. The film doesn’t frame this as a problem. It frames it as a defense strategy.

Chris Pratt plays Detective Chris Raven, the playmaker of the Mercy Court system. He sold it to the public. When he wakes up accused of murdering his wife, he has to use the same surveillance tools to prove his innocence. His partner, played by Kali Reis, flies around Los Angeles on what is functionally a giant Amazon delivery drone. Ring cameras are deliberately placed in scene after scene. The product placement isn’t subtle. It isn’t trying to be.

The twist reveals that a human villain manipulated the evidence. The AI didn’t fail. Someone fed it bad data. Maddox “learns” and grows throughout the trial. The system works once the human corruption is removed.

The resolution isn't "the surveillance state is wrong." It's "the AI just needed better data." That is Amazon's pitch for Ring. For Alexa. For every product in their ecosystem. Trust the technology. Fix the inputs, and the machine delivers justice.

The Boston Globe’s reviewer put it bluntly: “The movie’s pro-surveillance agenda is troubling, especially as it’s used to justify law enforcement invading everyone’s privacy using face recognition and other potentially biased AI-based tools.”

Think about who made this film. Amazon owns Ring. Ring’s cameras are disproportionately installed in majority-white neighborhoods, creating surveillance coverage gaps that mirror the same policing disparities

And that company produced a $100 million movie that says: don’t worry. The AI will learn.

Critics outside the mainstream press saw it clearly. The Film Verdict called Mercy “actual propaganda for Jeff Bezos’ brand of government compliance,” writing that the Ring product placement “feels even more sinister coming from Amazon MGM Studios,” given the Flock partnership and Flock’s connections to ICE. Awards Radar called it an attempt to make audiences “more submissive to the kind of society megacorporations like Amazon want to build.”

This is what corporate propaganda looks like now. It’s not a billboard. It’s a $100 million action movie distributed by the same company that runs the cameras, partners with the police tech firms, and builds the AI that scans your face when you walk to your mailbox. The movie and the Super Bowl ad aired three weeks apart. The movie says that surveillance is fine, give the AI more data, and it will be better. The ad says, "Look, the AI found your dog.”

Same company. Same message. Different packaging.

“Deleted” Doesn’t Mean Deleted

This is happening right now. Nancy Guthrie, Savannah Guthrie’s 84-year-old mother, disappeared from her Tucson home on February 1, 2026. She had a Google Nest doorbell camera. No paid subscription. The camera was disconnected at 1:47 a.m. local time. Law enforcement initially said the footage was gone.

Then the FBI recovered it.

FBI Director Kash Patel said the video was recovered from “residual data located in backend systems.” He said on Fox News that authorities went into private company systems to “excavate material that people would think would normally be deleted and no one would look for.” Google engineers worked for days on the extraction.

Cybersecurity expert Alex Stamos explained what that means: “Internal storage uses a very lazy deletion mechanism, so the data wouldn’t be available to users who didn’t pay.”

Google kept Nancy Guthrie’s footage on their servers even though she didn’t pay for storage, and the camera was disconnected. The footage wasn’t available to her. But it was available to the FBI.

Two things can be true at once.

The footage matters. It showed a masked, armed person approaching Guthrie’s door the morning she disappeared. That image generated over 4,000 tips. A person was detained for questioning. As of this writing, Nancy Guthrie is still missing. If the footage helps bring her home, that’s a good thing. Her family deserves answers.

And: the FBI just demonstrated that even if you don’t pay for cloud storage, even if your camera is disconnected, the data exists somewhere on a server. Your “deleted” footage isn’t deleted. It’s just not available to you.

With a free Google Nest plan, video should be overwritten within 3 to 6 hours. Nancy Guthrie went missing on February 1. The FBI recovered footage more than a week later. As a former FBI cybercrime agent told NBC News, “Nest/Google deletes billions of data points every hour. To find this data set means that they are finding a single needle in a 10K ft by 10K ft haystack.”

They found it. Which means it was there.

CBS News reported that the recovery “could open Google up to a flood of future law enforcement inquiries.” A cybersecurity analyst told them: “Law enforcement folks are looking at this as a new capability that we could add to our pipeline for when we’re trying to source video footage.”

The gap between what Google tells you about your data and what Google keeps on its servers is the gap between what a product promises and what a system does.

The same system that helps find a kidnapper proves your front porch has never been private.

The Panopticon Has a Subscription Model

I wrote about Foucault’s panopticon in my piece on Palantir, Peter Thiel, and the capture of American information. It is a prison designed so guards could watch inmates at any time, but inmates couldn’t tell when the guards were watching. Foucault’s point was that you don’t need guards in every cell if prisoners believe someone is always watching. The framework of surveillance automatically disciplines behavior.

Palantir built the federal version. Their contracts nearly doubled, from $541 million in 2024 to $970.5 million in 2025. They built what critics call a “master database” of Americans, pulling together IRS records, Social Security files, immigration data, and license plate reader data into a single system. T

Ring is the consumer version. You buy the camera. You install it in your house. You point it at the street. You opt into the network. Then Flock plugs into that network. Then local police plug into Flock. And finally, ICE asks local police to run a search on someone working on a home next door, or a Texas cop uses the system to track a woman who had an abortion.

Nobody forced you to buy a Ring. That’s the point. The panopticon works because you build it yourself.

The Electronic Frontier Foundation wrote on the day of the Super Bowl ad that Ring

“previewed future surveillance of our streets: a world where biometric identification could be unleashed from consumer devices to identify, track, and locate anything, human, pet, and otherwise.”

The architecture is the same at every level. The ring costs $3.99 per month for the basic plan. Flock charges police departments per camera. Palantir charges the federal government per contract. The only difference is who’s paying and who’s being watched.

Shoshana Zuboff, the Harvard scholar, called the corporate version surveillance capitalism, private companies harvest behavioral data to predict and modify human behavior. But Zuboff was writing about corporations selling you products.

What Palantir, Ring, and Flock are building together is something else. The corporate and government surveillance infrastructures aren’t separate systems anymore. They’re integrated.

William Owen, communications director at the Surveillance Technology Oversight Project, put it

“We’re entering an increasingly dystopian era of high-tech surveillance.” He said intelligence sharing between levels of government has “allowed ICE to sidestep sanctuary laws and co-opt local police databases and surveillance tools, including license plate readers, facial recognition and other technologies.”

What You Can Do

Disable Search Party in the Ring app. It’s on by default. Go to the main dashboard, open the menu, go to Control Center, then Search Party.

Opt out of Community Requests. This feature allows law enforcement to request your footage through Ring’s police tech partners. Ring’s partnership with Flock is dead, but the Axon partnership is still active.

Check if your city has a Flock Safety contract. Attend a council meeting. Cities from Denver to Los Angeles to Santa Clara County are canceling these contracts or imposing restrictions on them right now.

Push your representatives to support legislation regulating license plate readers. Montana passed a law requiring warrants for ALPR data. Washington, California, Illinois, and Colorado are all moving on similar legislation.

Support the Electronic Frontier Foundation. They filed the research, published the reports, and raised the alarms that exposed every system detailed in this article. Their work drives the lawsuits and the policy changes. They’re at eff.org.

And watch Minority Report. Not because it’s a fun Tom Cruise movie, though it is. Watch it because in 2002, a film warned you that the merger of corporate and government surveillance would destroy privacy, criminalize prediction, and turn citizens into data points.

Then Amazon made a movie that told you to stop worrying about it.

The difference between those two films is the difference between 2002 and 2026. The warning was right. The product won.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

author: Joshua Smith

title: The NFL Had a Black Head Coach in 1921. It Still Has a Problem.
seoTitle: The NFL Had a Black Head Coach in 1921. It Still Has a Problem.
excerpt: Super Bowl LX just ended. The league enters 2026 with three Black head coaches. In 1921, it had one. The same man built the blueprint they still refuse to follow.
metaDescription: Super Bowl LX just ended. The league enters 2026 with three Black head coaches. In 1921, it had one. The same man built the blueprint they still refuse to follo
bodyHtml:
On Sunday, February 8, 2026, the Seattle Seahawks beat the New England Patriots 29-13 in Super Bowl LX.

By Monday morning, the NFL’s coaching carousel had already stopped spinning. Ten head coaching positions are open in this hiring cycle. Zero went to a Black coach.

The league enters 2026 with three Black head coaches out of 32 teams. Three.

In a league where roughly 70% of the players are Black.

Now that may not surprise you, but here’s what might.

The NFL had a Black head coach in 1921. His name was “Fritz” Pollard. He was 5-foot-9, 165 pounds. He was a member of Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Incorporated. And he did everything first.

First Black player in the famous Rose Bowl. First Black running back named to Walter Camp’s All-America team. First Black player to win a professional football championship. First Black head coach in NFL history. First Black quarterback in NFL history.

That was over a hundred years ago. And the league still hasn’t caught up to where he started.

The Man Who Did Everything First

Frederick Douglass “Fritz” Pollard was born on January 27, 1894, in Chicago. His father, John, was a barber and Union Army veteran. His mother, Catherine, ran a thriving alterations business. The Pollard family was the first Black family to settle in Rogers Park in 1886, and education ran deep in the household. His sister Artissmisia became the first Black registered nurse in Illinois. His sister Naomi became the first Black woman to graduate from Northwestern University.

Fritz attended Lane Tech High School in Chicago, where he played football, baseball, and ran track. He was a three-time Cook County track champion. His classmates compared him to Mercury.

In 1915, Pollard enrolled at Brown University on a Rockefeller Scholarship. He didn’t stumble into an Ivy League school. He earned his way there, just like he earned everything that came after.

His teammates wouldn’t talk to him. Fritz Pollard III, his grandson, told WBUR in 2018 that the players “basically really wouldn’t talk to him or anything until they saw what he could do.”

So he showed them.

Pollard led Brown to the 1916 Rose Bowl, becoming the first Black athlete to play in the game. That same year, he beat both Yale and Harvard in the same week. He scored six touchdowns and ran for over 540 yards. When he took the field, spectators sang “Bye, Bye, Blackbird.”

Walter Camp, the father of American football, called Pollard “one of the greatest runners these eyes have ever seen.”

Building the League While the League Hated Him

In 1919, the same year more than 25 race riots erupted across major American cities, Fritz Pollard signed with the Akron Pros. He was just the sixth Black professional football player in an era when lynchings happened almost daily.

Pollard didn’t care about being liked. He said it himself:

My father had taught me that I was too big to be humiliated by prejudiced whites. If I figured a hotel or restaurant didn’t want me, I stayed away. I didn’t go sniffing around hoping they’d accept me. I was never interested in socializing with whites. I was there to play football and make my money.

In 1920, the Akron Pros joined the American Professional Football Association being one of the first teams to join. Pollard led the team to an undefeated 8-0-3 season and the league’s first national championship. He and Jim Thorpe were the two biggest draws in the sport. The highest-paid players.

In 1921, Pollard became the co-head coach of the Akron Pros while still playing running back. He was the first Black head coach in NFL history.

With the promotion, the NFL had a Black head coach in its second year of existence and after Pollard was pushed out due to segregation, the league wouldn’t hire another Black head coach for 68 years.

In 1923, Pollard became the first Black quarterback in NFL history while playing for the Hammond Pros.

Once he got something, he sought to share the opportunities with others. Pollard brought Paul Robeson into professional football. He organized the first interracial all-star game in 1922 to showcase Black players. He pushed for integrated competition at every level of the sport.

But the NFL pushed back.

George Halas, founder of the Chicago Bears and one of the most powerful figures in early NFL history, helped ensure what came next. Pollard said in a 1971 interview:

“Halas was the greatest foe of Black football players…[Halas] started the ball rolling that eventually led to the barring of blacks from professional football in 1933.”

In 1926, Pollard, along with every Black player in the NFL, was removed from the league.
No explanation. No debate. Just gone.

The NFL banned Black players from 1933 to 1946. That’s 13 years of professional football with a whites-only policy. Fritz Pollard had already built the league by then. He’d already proved everything that needed proving. Didn’t matter.

Fritz Pollard Didn’t Stop

Most people would have walked away. Pollard understood that would have meant the people trying to stop him had won.

In 1928, he organized the Chicago Black Hawks, an all-Black professional football team that played against white teams around Chicago. The Black Hawks became one of the most popular teams on the West Coast. In the 1930s, he founded the Brown Bombers in Harlem.

But Pollard was more than an athlete. He founded F.D. Pollard and Co. in 1922, the first Black investment firm in America. He started the New York Independent News, the first Black-owned tabloid in New York.

In 1954, he became the first Black alumnus elected to the National College Football Hall of Fame.

It wouldnt be for another 50 years the Pro Football Hall of Fame didn’t induct him until 2005. Nineteen years after his death.

His grandson Fritz Pollard III spent the previous 15 years campaigning for the induction. At the ceremony in Canton, Ohio, Pollard III said “He should have been in the founding class. There’s no doubt about it.”

Fritz Pollard was inducted alongside Dan Marino and Steve Young. Both of those men walked into the Hall while they were still alive. Pollard had to wait until he’d been dead for two decades.

The Son Who Defied Hitler

Fritz Pollard’s son didn’t just follow in his footsteps. He ran past them.
