Frederick Douglass “Fritz” Pollard Jr. was born on February 18, 1915, in Springfield, Massachusetts. Like his father, he was a multi-sport athlete. Like his father, he was a member of Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Incorporated.

Pollard Jr. attended Brown University like his father, where he equaled the world record for the 45-yard high hurdles in the spring of 1934. He then transferred to the University of North Dakota, where he became the school’s first Black athlete in football, track, and boxing.

In 1936, while Adolf Hitler prepared the Berlin Olympics as a showcase for Aryan supremacy, Fritz Pollard Jr. was selected for the United States Olympic team. He was one of seven Alpha Phi Alpha men on that team. Seven. Jesse Owens. Ralph Metcalfe. Cornelius Johnson. Dave Albritton. John Woodruff. Archie Williams. Fritz Pollard Jr.

Pollard Jr. won a bronze medal in the 110-meter hurdles, despite suffering a serious leg injury on the ship to Berlin. He was leading the race for most of the way but tripped over the next-to-last hurdle. He still finished third.

After Berlin, Pollard Jr. returned to UND, where he earned All-North Central Conference honors in football in 1937 and 1938. . He graduated in 1939, earned a law degree from the John Marshall Law School in Chicago.

Fritz Pollard III, the son of Pollard Jr. and grandson of the original Fritz, presented his grandfather for induction into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 2005. The family line is a living record of Black excellence.

Share

The Alpha Phi Alpha Thread

This is where the story gets bigger than just football.

Fritz Pollard Sr. was a member of Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Incorporated. So was Fritz Pollard Jr. The fraternity, founded on December 4, 1906, at Cornell University, has a motto: “First of All, Servants of All, We Shall Transcend All.”

The Pollard family lived by that motto before it was written down.

Alpha Phi Alpha’s connection to professional sports and the fight for racial equality in athletics runs deep. The fraternity’s members include Jesse Owens, Phil Edwards, Art Shell, Eddie Robinson, Lenny Wilkens Nate Archibald, and Don Barksdale. When you look at the history of Black athletes who broke barriers and demanded recognition, Alpha men keep showing up.

Seven Alpha men competed in the 1936 Berlin Olympics. Pollard organized all-Black football teams when the NFL shut its doors. Eddie Robinson became the winningest football coach in NCAA history. Art Shell became the second Black head coach in NFL history in 1989, 68 years after Pollard was the first. All of these men represent the spirit of Alpha.

The organization that bears Fritz Pollard’s name, the Fritz Pollard Alliance, was founded in 2003 by attorneys Cyrus Mehri and Johnnie Cochran Jr. Its purpose is to promote diversity and equal hiring practices in NFL coaching and front-office positions. The Alliance helped establish the Rooney Rule, which requires teams to interview at least one minority candidate before filling a head coaching vacancy.

The Rooney Rule exists because of Fritz Pollard’s legacy. Because after he was pushed out in 1926, the NFL went 68 years before hiring another Black head coach. Because the pattern of exclusion he fought against never actually went away.

2026: The Numbers Don’t Lie

The NFL enters the 2026 season with three Black head coaches. Todd Bowles in Tampa Bay, DeMeco Ryans in Houston, and Aaron Glenn with the New York Jets. That’s 9.4% of the league’s head coaches in a league that is 70% Black.

This hiring cycle, ten head coaching positions opened up. Not one went to a Black candidate. Commissioner Roger Goodell acknowledged the results were “disappointing” and said the league would reevaluate its diversity approach. He said it at the same podium where he says it every few years.

A labor economist at the University of Pennsylvania, Dr. Janice Madden, analyzed the data for the Fritz Pollard Alliance in 2002. Her findings, published in the Journal of Sports Economics, found Black head coaches averaged 2.7 more wins in their first season than white head coaches. Black coaches who were fired had better records than white coaches who were retained.

The data hasn’t changed. The hiring hasn’t either.

Mike Tomlin, the longest-tenured head coach in NFL history and longest tenured head coach in North America professional sports , stepped down from the Pittsburgh Steelers this offseason after 18 seasons without a single losing record. Not one. The Steelers hired Mike McCarthy to replace him. Raheem Morris was fired by the Atlanta Falcons. Antonio Pierce was let go by the Raiders after one season. Jerod Mayo lasted one year in New England.

Brian Flores, the former Miami Dolphins head coach, filed a class action lawsuit against the NFL in 2022, alleging systemic racial bias in coaching hiring. The suit is still ongoing. The NFL reportedly wants it heard by the Supreme Court.

What Fritz Pollard Knew

Fritz Pollard died on May 11, 1986, at the age of 92. He never saw his induction into the Pro Football Hall of Fame. He never saw the Rooney Rule. He never saw a Super Bowl with two Black starting quarterbacks, which didn’t happen until Super Bowl LVII in 2023, when Jalen Hurts won (Go Birds). Fifty-seven Super Bowls.

But Pollard knew what was coming because he’d already lived through it. He said it in his own words: “It was evident in my first year at Akron back in 1919 that they didn’t want blacks in there getting that money.”

That’s not a historical observation. That’s the present tense.

The NFL is a $20 billion annual revenue machine built on the labor of Black athletes. Three of its 32 head coaches are Black. Its most successful diversity initiative is named after the first Black coach it for a long time erased from its own history.

Fritz Pollard built professional football. He coached it. He quarterbacked it. He recruited for it. He organized teams when the league banned him. He founded businesses when the country told him he couldn’t. He raised a son who defied Hitler in Berlin.

And a hundred years later, the league he helped build still hasn’t kept its promise.

Two things are true at once. Progress has been made. And the architecture of exclusion that Fritz Pollard fought against in 1921 is still standing.

The question isn’t whether the NFL remembers Fritz Pollard. They named an award after him. They named an alliance after him. They put his bust in Canton.

The question is whether they’ll ever do what he actually asked them to do.

Give people a fair shot. That’s it. That’s the whole thing.

Share

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: How the Wayans Changed the Super Bowl Forever
seoTitle: How the Wayans Changed the Super Bowl Forever
excerpt: In 1992, Alpha Phi Alpha brother Keenen Wayans and In Living Color stole 22 million Super Bowl viewers. The NFL built modern halftime from that loss and erased them.
metaDescription: In 1992, Alpha Phi Alpha brother Keenen Wayans and In Living Color stole 22 million Super Bowl viewers. The NFL built modern halftime from that loss and erased
bodyHtml:
On January 26, 1992, Super Bowl XXVI kicked off at the Metrodome in Minneapolis. Washington led Buffalo 17-0 at halftime. CBS aired “Winter Magic.” Dorothy Hamill and Brian Boitano glided on plastic sheets. Gloria Estefan sang. Kids in MC Hammer pants rapped about Frosty the Snowman. A guy on stilts waved at the camera for seven seconds of primetime real estate.

79.6 million people were watching the Super Bowl.

22 million of them changed the channel.

Fox decided to counterprogram against the biggest sporting event in America. During halftime, they aired a live episode of In Living Color, the sketch comedy show created by a man of Alpha Phi Alpha and comedy legend Keenen Ivory Wayans. Fire Marshall Bill visited a sports bar. The Homeboy Shopping Network set up in a locker room. Damon Wayans and David Alan Grier did “Men on Football,” and it was a hit.

A countdown clock in the corner told viewers when to flip back. Most of them stayed until it hit zero.

George F. Schweitzer, CBS’s senior vice president for communications, called the idea “cute” before the broadcast. They weren’t laughing when they saw the ratings. The Super Bowl lost 22% of its halftime audience to the Wayans family.

The NFL didn’t acknowledge the loss publicly. They did, however, show their hand and went on to hire Michael Jackson for the following year.

This weekend, Bad Bunny performs for over 100 million viewers, continuing a legacy built on what happened 34 years ago. The NFL has never credited the creators who forced them to care.

Before the Wayans: When Halftime Was Dead Air

You need to understand how bad Super Bowl halftimes were to appreciate what In Living Color did.

The first Super Bowl in 1967 featured the University of Arizona and Grambling State University marching bands, along with trumpeter Al Hirt. That was the high point. For the next 25 years, halftime was marching bands, drill teams, and whatever local talent the league could scrape together.

1988 brought 88 grand pianos and Chubby Checker. In 1989, Elvis Presto, an Elvis impersonator, performed card tricks while wearing 3D glasses. The league billed it as the “World’s Largest Card Trick.”

Nobody cared.

By 1990, halftime had become a Peanuts tribute featuring Snoopy conducting a marching band and Charlie Brown twirling a baton.

The league had accepted this as usual. Halftime was when you refilled your snack bowl.

The Fox Gambit

Fox, in the early 1990s, was not a real network. It was an upstart with a few hit shows trying to position itself as the rebellious alternative to CBS, NBC, and ABC. They called themselves the “Bad Boys of Television.” The Simpsons, Married with Children, and In Living Color were their flagship programs.

In Living Color was revolutionary; it featured a majority-Black cast at a time when Saturday Night Live had maybe one Black cast member who rarely appeared. The show launched the careers of Jim Carrey, Jamie Foxx, Jennifer Lopez, David Alan Grier, and Tommy Davidson.

In 1991, Fox president Jamie Kellner walked into a staff meeting with an idea. CBS owned the Super Bowl rights, but nobody owned halftime. What if Fox ran an episode of In Living Color head-to-head against the halftime show?

Dan McDermott, a Fox programming executive, remembered the pitch: “Jamie started talking about how nobody watches the halftime. He said, ‘We should do a live episode of In Living Color. We’ll make a big deal out of it. We’ll convince America to turn the channel at halftime.’”

Keenen Ivory Wayans loved it. “I thought, this is genius,” he said. “The Super Bowl was the biggest thing in television. No one would dare take on the Super Bowl. We have to do that.”

Fox branded the show as “Doritos Zaptime In Living Color Super Halftime Party.” Frito-Lay paid $2 million to sponsor the entire broadcast and promote it aggressively. Fox flooded the airwaves with promos. Fox turned the studio into a party venue. Open bar included.

The Show

Fox ran the special on a slight delay. The network was terrified of what might happen in real time. Fire Marshall Bill's sketches called for pyrotechnics. They took hours to film safely. The Homeboy Shopping Network featured Keenen and Damon Wayans going through Thurman Thomas’s fictional wallet from a football locker. Tommy Davidson did a Sugar Ray Leonard impression he’d learned by watching videos from 7 pm to 7 am the night before.

But the moment everyone remembers was “Men on Football.”

Damon Wayans and David Alan Grier played Antoine Merriweather and Blaine Edwards, their recurring gay film critics characters who reviewed everything with campy commentary. The jokes pushed right up to the line of what 1992 television would allow.

“We were trying to make people flip the channel instead of watching rodeo dancing and stuff,” Wayans said later.

Jim Carrey’s Fire Marshall Bill closed the show by telling viewers they wouldn’t miss any of the game’s “senseless brutality.” The countdown clock hit zero. Viewers switched back to CBS.

The Aftermath

Depending on the source, somewhere between 22 and 29 million viewers chose In Living Color over Winter Magic. That represented 11% of the Super Bowl’s total audience. For half an hour, a sketch comedy show on the fourth-place network beat the most-watched event in American television.

The NFL responded immediately. For Super Bowl XXVII in 1993, the league didn’t book a marching band or an Olympic skater. They booked Michael Jackson.

They negotiated for four months. Jackson's team wanted $1 million. The league doesn't pay halftime performers. They offered to donate $100,000 to his Heal the World Foundation. Jackson wanted the game pushed back three hours so he could perform in the dark. That obviously didn’t happen. But the NFL gave him everything else.

Jackson stood motionless for 72 seconds, some say 90 seconds, after being catapulted onto the stage. That silence alone cost $2 million in advertising time. Then he performed “Jam,” “Billie Jean,” “Black or White,” and “Heal the World” with thousands of children flooding the field.

Ratings increased between halves. First time in Super Bowl history. 133.4 million people watched Jackson’s performance. Guinness World Records named it the most-watched television event ever in the United States.

The modern halftime show was born.

What Came Next

After MJ:

Prince in the rain doing “Purple Rain.” Beyoncé reuniting with Destiny’s Child. The 2022 hip hop show. Rihanna pregnant on stage. Last year, Kendrick pulled 133.5 million viewers and broke Jackson's record

Every single one of them owes a debt to what happened in 1992.

The NFL's official history mentions In Living Color once, maybe twice. The league celebrates halftime milestones by talking about Michael Jackson as the turning point. They discuss how the show evolved from marching bands to superstars. They leave out the part where a Black sketch comedy show humiliated them into caring.

Keenen Ivory Wayans has talked about this pattern. “You can’t do Wayans shit without the Wayans,” he said on a recent podcast. “We have 200 years of comedy between me, Shawn and Keenen, Damon, Kim. We have a lot of years of excellence of what we do.”

He was talking about Scary Movie, a franchise the Weinsteins took from his family after two films. But the statement applies to everything the Wayans built. They created something. Someone else took credit. The original architects got erased from the story.

Alpha Roots

Keenen Ivory Wayans pledged Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity at Tuskegee University in the late 1970s. He earned and maintained an engineering scholarship. He dropped out one semester before graduation to pursue comedy. He moved to New York. Bombed at The Improv. Met Robert Townsend. Moved to Los Angeles. Built an empire.

And there’s a fraternal connection to other pioneers in sports entertainment that the NFL would prefer you forget.

Fritz Pollard, another Alpha, won the first APFA championship in 1920 with the Akron Pros league’s first Black quarterback. The APFA became the NFL. Pollard later became the league's first Black head coach in 1921. They segregated the league after he left, and as such, he was erased. Pollard didn't make the Pro Football Hall of Fame until 2005, 84 years after he coached his first game.

Bad Bunny and the Legacy

This Sunday, Bad Bunny performs at Super Bowl LX. He’ll be one of the first artists to headline primarily in Spanish. Conservative commentators question this selection. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem announced ICE agents will be present at the stadium. Bad Bunny told critics on Saturday Night Live that they had four months to learn Spanish.

You've seen this before. New audiences always face resistance. But the performance itself exists because of what Keenen Ivory Wayans and his siblings built 34 years ago. Before In Living Color, nobody fought for halftime viewers. Now everyone does.

Kendrick Lamar’s record-breaking performance last year proved the halftime show has become bigger than the game itself. His 133.5 million viewers exceeded the 127.7 million who watched the Eagles(GO BIRDS) beat the Chiefs. The halftime show is now the main event for a significant portion of the audience.

None of this happens if Fox airs reruns instead of taking on the Super Bowl. None of this happens if Keenen Ivory Wayans decides the idea is too risky. None of this happens if 22 million viewers don’t change the channel to watch Fire Marshall Bill and Men on Football instead of figure skating on plastic.

What It Means Now

Turning Point USA is staging an alternative halftime show this year with Kid Rock, counterprogramming against Bad Bunny the way Fox counterprogrammed against Winter Magic in 1992. The effort will fail. The conditions are different. The lineup is worse. In 1992, halftime was dead air waiting to be exploited. In 2026, it’s the most anticipated performance of the year.

That transformation took 34 years. It started with a sketch comedy show.

When Bad Bunny takes the stage this Sunday, he’ll perform for an audience that expects spectacle. He’ll follow Kendrick Lamar, who followed Usher, who followed Rihanna, who followed the amazing 2022 hip hop super show, all the way back to Michael Jackson, standing motionless for 72 seconds in 1993.

And Michael Jackson was only there because the year before, people changed the channel to watch a Black sketch show.

The NFL built the modern halftime show because it had no choice. In Living Color forced their hand. Keenen Ivory Wayans and his siblings created something so compelling that America chose it over the biggest sporting event on television.

The league has never acknowledged this. The official history credits Michael Jackson. The institutional memory erases the Black comedy show that made Jackson necessary.

Bad Bunny’s performance on Sunday continues a legacy that started in 1992. The NFL won’t mention the origin of the broadcast. They won’t thank Keenen Ivory Wayans.

But you know now. And that’s worth 22 million channel changes. Now I gotta learn Me porto bonio, before Sunday.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share
author: Joshua Smith

title: A Choice to Ignore Danger: The Erasure of LGBTQ+ Protections at the College of Charleston
seoTitle: A Choice to Ignore Danger: The Erasure of LGBTQ+ Protections at the College of Charleston
excerpt: By removing inclusive housing questions in the name of consistency, the College of Charleston has traded student safety for political neutrality.
metaDescription: By removing inclusive housing questions in the name of consistency, the College of Charleston has traded student safety for political neutrality.
bodyHtml:
On January 30, 2026, I emailed the College of Charleston’s housing office. The template came from some local students on campus, who first reported and raised the issue. Students across campus were sending similar messages. We had a simple question.

The housing application used to include questions about LGBTQ+ students. Questions that helped match queer students with roommates who wouldn’t make their living situation unsafe. Questions that arose because the college understood that living with someone who hates who you are causes real harm.

The Pride Center actually helped create those questions. According to the college’s own Women’s and Gender Studies blog, Dr. Naomi Simmons, then-director of the Gender and Sexuality Equity Center, worked “with other CofC divisions to revise student housing forms” to make them more inclusive. The center launched programs, trained staff, and built a community.

Now those questions are gone.

Flashback to March 2025: President Hsu announced that the Gender and Sexuality Equity Center, aka the Pride Center, would be "realigned" alongside the Multicultural Student Center, the Office of Institutional Diversity, and South Carolina Recruitment and Access Initiatives. A restructured administration absorbed the entire infrastructure that advocated for those housing questions, trained Residence Life staff on LGBTQ+ and minority student needs, and worked to provide the institutional knowledge behind inclusive policy.

The questions didn't just disappear in isolation. They disappeared after the people who fought for them lost their platform.

I wanted to know why. I wanted to know who made that decision. I wanted to know if there was legislation requiring the change, because I couldn’t find any.

Five days later, I got my answer.

President Andrew T. Hsu sent a mass email to “several students” who had raised similar concerns. He wrote about the college’s commitment to being “consistent and fair in the information we collect, focusing on rooming preferences and living considerations that apply equally to all students.”

He never answered my question.

What “Consistent and Fair” Actually Means

Let me show you what the president wrote:

“In developing the application, we aim to be consistent and fair in the information we collect, focusing on rooming preferences and living considerations that apply equally to all students.”

Read that again. The logic is that asking about LGBTQ+ identity creates unfairness because it doesn’t “apply equally to all students.”

The problem arises because LGBTQ+ students don’t face equal risks.

According to the Williams Institute at UCLA, LGBTQ+ students in campus housing are more than twice as likely as their non-LGBTQ+ peers to experience bullying, harassment, or assault. 25.2% of LGBTQ+ students in campus housing reported experiencing harassment or violence, compared to 11.3% of non-LGBTQ+ students.

That’s a population facing more than double the risk.

The questions existed because treating everyone “the same” when people face different dangers is a choice to close your eyes, blind, that benefits whoever faces the least risk.

The Question He Wouldn’t Answer

Students asked,

“why CofC has chosen to remove questions that helped to prevent harm for queer and trans* students who wanted to live on campus.”

“It would be appreciated if you could provide us with some clarity as to why this decision was made, and by whom.”

“I am not currently aware of any active legislation in the state of South Carolina (or federally) requiring that Colleges and Universities remove such questions.”

President Hsu’s response addressed none of this.

He didn’t name the decision-maker. He didn’t cite any legislation. He didn’t explain the timing.

Instead, he wrote

“Our Residence Life staff play an active role in supporting students and addressing concerns when uncomfortable situations arise.”

Basically, he is saying we’ll deal with problems after they happen, once something harms our students or puts them in danger.

Prevention is not the same as response. Matching a queer student with a roommate who won’t harass them is prevention. Intervening after harassment occurs is damage control.

The Timeline

To understand what’s happening at CofC, you need to see the pattern.

February 14, 2025: The Trump Administration’s Department of Education issues a “Dear Colleague” letter claiming that many DEI programs violate Title VI of the Civil Rights Act. The letter threatens federal funding for institutions that don’t comply with its interpretation.

February 25, 2025: CofC’s Board of Trustees convenes a special meeting. President Hsu sends an email to the campus about “proposed legislation” at the state level and the federal Dear Colleague letter. Students notice that the college has taken down the sign in front of the Multicultural Center. A petition to preserve DEI programs begins circulating.

March 4, 2025: President Hsu announces that the administration will “realign DEI-specific support centers.” The Pride Center will be absorbed. The Multicultural Student Center and Programs will be absorbed. The Office of Institutional Diversity will be absorbed. The South Carolina Recruitment and Access Initiatives will be absorbed.

The Board also changes the college’s core value from “Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion” to “Respect for the Individual.”

March 12, 2025: The college held town halls for faculty, staff, and students. The student petition reached 401 signatures.

March 24, 2025: About 30 students gather at Cistern Yard to protest. D’Ajuai Kelley, a member of Students for a Democratic Society, tells reporters: “The state bill hasn’t even been passed yet and the college has already decided to rename things.”

March 26, 2025: Professor Lisa Covert, president of CofC’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors, tells the Post and Courier she wants to see “a more proactive defense of the college’s values” and “more pushback against the assumptions that these efforts are inherently bad.”

August 2025: The college launches the “Life Design Center,” a new program based on Stanford’s Designing Your Life framework. The Multicultural Student Center no longer exists even as a webpage, but the Life Design Center represents the college’s new emphasis. Individual self-improvement rather than cultural community.

Somewhere in this period: Tour ambassadors are reportedly told they can no longer proactively share their pronouns with prospective students unless specifically asked. This change, reported anonymously by students, signals the shift from visibility to erasure.

January 30, 2026: Students notice that the LGBTQ+ questions are no longer on the housing application.

February 4, 2026: President Hsu sends his “consistent and fair” response.

What the Law Actually Says

Here’s what makes this worse: none of this appears to be legally required.

The federal Dear Colleague letter targets race-based preferences in admissions, hiring, and financial aid. The letter was challenged in court almost immediately. On April 24, 2025, two federal district courts issued preliminary injunctions blocking its enforcement. The court in the NEA case found the letter likely violated the Fifth Amendment as “unconstitutionally vague” because it didn’t define what constitutes a DEI program or when such programs violate Title VI.

By January 2026, the Education Department had abandoned its efforts to enforce the letter in its entirety.

But even before those injunctions, the letter focused on race. It did not require universities to remove LGBTQ+ safety questions from housing applications. Housing roommate matching based on sexual orientation or gender identity is not what the letter addressed.

South Carolina has proposed various DEI bills. House Bill 3927 passed the House on April 2, 2025. But as of my email in January 2026 and this article, I couldn’t find legislation requiring the removal of LGBTQ+ housing questions. The bills target DEI offices, training requirements, and race-based preferences. They don’t say “remove questions that help LGBTQ+ students find safe roommates.”

When we asked President Hsu directly if such legislation existed.

He chose not to answer.

If it were voluntary, “consistent and fair” wouldn’t be a legal requirement; it's a choice to put students in danger.

The Stakes

That’s the real calculation queer students now have to make:

Live on campus without the ability to signal your safety needs, or move off campus and lose the community, convenience, and often financial benefits of campus housing.

The Williams Institute research shows that nearly one in five LGBTQ+ college students experienced in-person bullying or harassment. Almost one in five experienced sexual harassment. More than one in ten experienced sexual assault.

And here’s the part that should concern every administrator, faculty, and student at the college. Nearly three-quarters of those who experienced harassment said their college didn’t know it was happening. Only one in five victimized LGBTQ+ students reported that their college provided an accessible, visible procedure for reporting LGBTQ-related bias incidents.

The questions the college removed helped address this problem. They created a mechanism for students to signal their needs before problems occurred. They created data that the college could use to make better matches.

Now that the mechanism is gone, in the name of applying “equally to all students.”

What I Actually Want

I love this school. I’m writing this because I love this school.

I want CofC to answer the question I actually asked: Who made this decision, and why?

I want CofC to explain what legislation, if any, required this change.

I want CofC to acknowledge that treating everyone “the same” when people face different risks isn’t fairness. It’s a choice to ignore documented disparities.

I want CofC to reinstate the questions, or explain in specific terms why they can’t.

And I want students, faculty, and community members to understand what’s happening here. My article isn’t about a single housing application question; it’s about an institutional logic that erases specific protections in the name of universal “fairness.”

When the college treats everyone 'equally' regardless of the risks they face, the people who face the most risk lose the most.

What You Can Do

If you’re a CofC student, faculty member, staff member, or community member concerned about these changes, here’s how to make your voice heard:

Contact the Decision-Makers

President Andrew T. Hsu: Email: president@cofc.edu

Megan Rowe, Director of Campus Housing

Board of Trustees: The Board meets regularly and accepts public comment. The Board posts meeting schedules on the CofC website. You can also contact the Board through the President’s Office.

What to Say

Be specific. Reference the timeline. Ask the questions they haven’t answered yet.

  1. What specific legislation or federal guidance required the removal of LGBTQ+ questions from the housing application?

  2. Who made the final decision to remove these questions, and when?

  3. What data does CofC have on LGBTQ+ student housing experiences, and how did that data inform this decision?

  4. How does CofC plan to ensure LGBTQ+ student safety in housing without these questions?

  5. Will CofC consider reinstating these questions if no legislation actually requires their removal?

  6. Two federal courts have enjoined the federal Dear Colleague letters, and the Education Department has abandoned enforcement. Why is CofC still acting as if it’s in effect?

    Share

Organize

Connect with Local organizations

  • CofC Pride

  • Ttimecofc

  • cofc_qspace

  • Students for a Democratic Society

  • Student Government Association

  • The existing petition to preserve DEI programs (change.org/p/preserve-diversity-equity-and-inclusion-programs-at-the-college-of-charleston)

Document everything. Save emails. Screenshot policy changes. Create a paper trail.

Attend

Town halls happen. The March 2025 sessions drew enough concern that faculty publicly called for “more pushback” from administration. Show up. Ask questions publicly. Make them answer on the record.

Amplify

Share this piece. Talk to local media. The Post and Courier’s Education Lab has covered these changes. Contact state legislators and ask whether any legislation actually requires these changes.

The more people asking the same questions, the harder they are to ignore.

If you are a CofC student, go to the CofC Pride Instagram page. They will have a new email template to send to the admin at the college with your concerns.

A Note on Risk

I’m a junior. I have a year and a half left at this institution. Writing this publicly has risks.

But so does not writing it. When institutions erase protections and call it fairness, someone has to say it out loud.

If you’re a student considering speaking up, know that documentation protects you. Keep records of everything. The email exchange I’ve described is now part of the public record. If anything happens to me academically after this piece publishes, that paper trail exists.

You don’t have to be public to make a difference. Private emails, quiet conversations with faculty allies, anonymous tips to journalists. All of it matters.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share
author: Joshua Smith

title: Seven Men vs. Hitler: How Alpha Phi Alpha Destroyed the Master Race Myth at the 1936 Olympics
seoTitle: Seven Men vs. Hitler: How Alpha Phi Alpha Destroyed the Master Race Myth at the 1936 Olympics
excerpt: The untold story of my fraternity brothers who humiliated the Nazi regime and came home to an American president who wouldn’t send a telegram.
metaDescription: The untold story of my fraternity brothers who humiliated the Nazi regime and came home to an American president who wouldn’t send a telegram.
bodyHtml:
On August 1, 1936, Adolf Hitler opened the Berlin Olympics in front of 110,000 spectators. The Nazi propaganda machine was in overdrive. Germany had spent $30 million on the games. Leni Riefenstahl’s cameras were everywhere. The whole world was watching.

Hitler wanted one thing out of this: proof of Aryan supremacy. He didn’t get it.

The Alphas Who Ran in Berlin

Seven members of my brotherhood, Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity Inc, represented the United States at those games. Jesse Owens. Ralph Metcalfe. Cornelius Johnson. Dave Albritton. John Woodruff. Archie Williams. Fritz Pollard Jr.

When the track and field competition ended, these seven men had combined for thirteen Olympic medals. Not just winning but dominating.

Most people know that Jesse Owens won four gold medals. The 100-meter dash, the 200-meter dash, the long jump, and the 4x100-meter relay. He tied or broke Olympic records in every event he entered.

But Owens wasn’t alone.

Ralph Metcalfe ran the anchor leg on that world-record relay team. He took silver in the 100 meters, just behind Owens. At the time, Metcalfe held the world record. Sports writers called him “the world’s fastest human” before Owens arrived in Berlin.

Cornelius Johnson won gold in the high jump with an Olympic record leap. Dave Albritton, his training partner, took silver in the same event. These two Black men from America swept the top spots in front of Hitler’s stadium.

John Woodruff, a freshman at the University of Pittsburgh, won gold in the 800-meter race that became legendary. Boxed in by competitors, Woodruff nearly stopped running. He dropped to last place, moved to the outside lane, then sprinted past everyone to win. The New York Herald Tribune called it “the most daring move seen on a track.”

Archie Williams won gold in the 400 meters. Fritz Pollard Jr. took bronze in the 110-meter hurdles despite injuring his leg on the ship to Germany.

Seven brothers. Thirteen medals. As John Woodruff recalled in a 2002 oral history interview with former History Center curator Nicholas Ciotola. “We did very well. We destroyed Hitler’s Master Race Theory.”

The Actual Snub

The story everyone knows is wrong.

You’ve probably heard that Hitler refused to shake Jesse Owens’ hand. That’s not what happened.

On the first day of the track competition, Hitler congratulated all the German winners personally. He met them in his private box. Then Cornelius Johnson won the high jump. Hitler left the stadium before the medal ceremony.

The Nazis said Hitler had a scheduling conflict. The IOC wasn’t buying it. The head of the International Olympic Committee told Hitler that he would either congratulate every winner or none. Hitler chose none.

By the time Owens won his first gold the next day, Hitler wasn’t meeting any medalists publicly. So technically, Hitler didn’t single out Owens. He just ignored everyone equally.

Owens understood what really happened. He made it clear in October 1936, speaking to a crowd of a thousand people,

“Hitler didn’t snub me. It was our president who snubbed me. The president didn’t even send me a telegram.”

President Franklin D. Roosevelt never acknowledged Owens’ victories. None of the eighteen Black Olympians from Berlin received any recognition from their own president.

Only the white Olympians got White House invitations.

Roosevelt was running for reelection. He needed Southern Democrats, and inviting a Black man to the White House might cost him votes. So he didn’t.

Owens came home to a ticker tape parade down Fifth Avenue in New York. That same night, he attended a reception at the Waldorf Astoria hotel. He had to use the service elevator. Black people weren’t allowed in the regular elevators.

Four gold medals couldn’t change the rules.

What Happened After

Jesse Owens struggled when he got home. He couldn’t find work. Olympic champions in 1936 couldn’t sign endorsement deals like today. And for a Black champion, the opportunities were even more limited.

Owens ended up racing horses at carnivals. He ran against motorcycles to make money. He filed for bankruptcy. It wasn’t until 1955 that a president acknowledged him. Dwight Eisenhower named Owens “Ambassador of Sports.”

Ralph Metcalfe went into politics. He served as an alderman in Chicago before winning a congressional seat in 1970. He cofounded the Congressional Black Caucus. He introduced legislation to establish Black History Month.

Cornelius Johnson worked as a letter carrier for the Post Office. He joined the Merchant Marine in 1945. He died of bronchopneumonia in 1946 at age 32. The USA Track and Field Hall of Fame later inducted him in 1994.

John Woodruff served in the Army during World War II. He rose to lieutenant colonel. If the 1940 and 1944 Olympics hadn’t been canceled due to the war, many believe he would have won more gold medals.

Archie Williams became one of the first Black military pilots and served in World War II. He later taught high school math for 21 years. In 2021, a California high school was renamed Archie Williams High School in his honor.

Fritz Pollard Jr. earned a law degree and became a Foreign Service officer. His father, Fritz Pollard Sr., also my frat brother, had been the first Black head coach in the NFL. The son followed in that legacy of breaking barriers, becoming one of the first two African Americans to graduate from the University of North Dakota.

The 80 Year Wait

The White House didn't officially recognize the Black Olympians from 1936 until 2016.

President Barack Obama invited their family members to the White House. Relatives representing nine of the eighteen Black athletes attended. Obama said they “put a lie to notions of racial superiority” and “taught them a thing or two about democracy.”

Why This Matters Now

Alpha Phi Alpha was founded in 1906 at Cornell University. By my fratenities Seven Jewels, these men built the first intercollegiate Black Greek letter organization. They chose “First of All, Servants of All, We Shall Transcend All” as our motto.

The seven men who ran in Berlin lived by that motto. They transcended the hate of a fascist regime and the neglect of their own government. They won anyway.

When I write about this history, I think about what Jesse Owens said years later, “The battles that count aren’t the ones for gold medals. The struggles within yourself, the invisible, inevitable battles inside all of us, that’s where it’s at.”

The fraternity that produced them is still doing that work. Still registering voters. Still developing leaders. Still training young men to serve.

Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

What You Can Do

If you want to learn more about this history, read and watch these.

The documentary Olympic Pride, American Prejudice (2016) tells the story of all eighteen Black American athletes at the 1936 games. It includes archival footage and interviews with descendants.

Jeremy Schaap’s book Triumph: The Untold Story of Jesse Owens and Hitler’s Olympics debunks many myths about those games and provides context that most Americans never learned.

The National Archives maintains records from this period, including letters to FDR urging him to recognize Owens. Those letters went unanswered.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share
author: Joshua Smith

title: Who Controls What You Know? Trump, Thiel, and the Quiet Capture of American Information
seoTitle: Who Controls What You Know? Trump, Thiel, and the Quiet Capture of American Information
excerpt: How a billionaire’s database, defunded public media, and a killed CBS investigation are all part of the same system.
metaDescription: How a billionaire’s database, defunded public media, and a killed CBS investigation are all part of the same system.
bodyHtml:
On August 1, 2025, the Corporation for Public Broadcasting announced its closure, and on Monday, January 5, 2026, the CPB board of directors voted to dissolve the corporation entirely. For the first time in nearly 60 years, federal funding for public media was gone. NPR. PBS. Sesame Street. Frontline. All Things Considered. Trump systematically killed the network of media that brought noncommercial news and education to every corner of America.

During the same period, Palantir Technologies, the data-mining company co-founded by billionaire Peter Thiel, saw its federal contracts nearly double, from $541 million in 2024 to $970.5 million in 2025. The company was building what critics called a “master database” of Americans, pulling together IRS records, Social Security files, immigration data, and license plate reader data into a single system.

One entity loses the ability to inform the public. Another gains unprecedented access to track them.

From Manufacturing Consent to Manufacturing Compliance

In 1988, Noam Chomsky and Edward Herman published Manufacturing Consent. The book argues that once a regime is in place, the media doesn’t tell you what to think through direct censorship. It tells you what to think about by filtering information through ownership, advertising, sourcing, and ideology. The public receives pre-filtered information that serves elite interests. You’re “free” to choose, but someone else wrote the menu.

That model assumed you still needed to convince people. The ruling class still needed your consent even if they made the choices.

What happens when you don’t need consent anymore?

A surveillance state doesn’t need to convince you. It needs to predict you. And if it can predict you, it can shape your behavior without ever changing your mind. You comply not because you agree, but because you know someone is watching you.

The shift in consent is from Orwell’s boot-on-the-neck perspective to something more subtle and more dangerous. Michel Foucault wrote about the Panopticon, a prison design in which guards could watch inmates at any time, but inmates couldn’t tell when the guards were watching them. His point was that you don’t need guards in every cell if prisoners believe someone is always watching. The framework of surveillance automatically disciplines behavior.

Shoshana Zuboff, the Harvard scholar, called this surveillance capitalism, where private companies harvest behavioral data to predict and modify human behavior at scale. But Zuboff was writing about corporations selling you products. Possibly the most famous example is Facebook's use of user profiles to deliver personalized ads that influence user preferences and reinforce engagement.

What Trump and Thiel are building is surveillance statism. The conceptual backbone, but pointed at citizens by a government that has made clear it wants to punish critics, deport millions, and eliminate institutions it can’t control.

The combination is the nightmare scenario. A database that knows where you are, who you talk to, what you owe, and where you work. Media companies are too scared to report unfavorably. No public broadcasting alternative outside corporate control—private contractors running the whole thing, insulated from FOIA requests and democratic accountability.

You don’t need to manufacture consent if you can manufacture compliance.

The Database of You

In March 2025, President Trump signed an executive order instructing federal agencies to share data across departments. The administration called it an efficiency measure, but privacy experts begged to differ.

Palantir Technologies got the contract.

Makena Kelly, a senior writer at Wired, reported on what Palatir was building, “The goal was to centralize data all across government.” IRS records. Social Security files. Passport data. Immigration enforcement databases. License plate readers. All of it flows into systems that could cross-reference, analyze, and flag any person in the country.

Kelly told Democracy Now that Palantir is “becoming an operating system for the entire government.”

After Trump got into office, Palantir’s federal contracts exploded, orignally $4.4M (2009) to $541.2M (2024) to $970.5M (2025) which was a securing $795M Pentagon deal and $30M ICE ImmigrationOS for “near real-time” immigrant tracking via targeting, self-deportation, and “lifecycle management” (American Immigration Council), treating humans like user retention.

The connections between Palantir and the Trump administration aren’t subtle. Vice President JD Vance is a Thiel protégé who received $15 million from Thiel for his Ohio Senate campaign. Elon Musk, who led the Department of Government Efficiency before his partial departure, was a co-founder of PayPal, which Thiel also co-founded. At least three DOGE members are former Palantir employees. Stephen Miller, Trump’s chief immigration architect, holds tens of thousands of dollars in Palantir stock. So does senior policy adviser Kara Frederick.

The guy deciding who gets deported has a financial stake in the company tracking deportees.

Paul Graham, the prominent Silicon Valley investor on X, says Palantir is “building the infrastructure of the police state.”

The Panopticon Is a Product

Here’s what makes Palantir different from old-school spy pen-in-pocket surveillance. Their tools don’t just watch. They predict. They find patterns in random data points.

Juan Sebastián Pinto, a former Palantir employee who left without signing the company’s standard non-disparagement agreement, told NPR what he realized about his work there: “What I really was doing is basically helping a company create a monopoly over artificial intelligence decision-making and do so first by targeting the federal government.”

Palantir CEO Alex Karp has been candid about the company's activities. At The New York Times DealBook Summit in December 2024, he acknowledged that Palantir’s products could process data from legal surveillance. He said, “could you put it in our product? Yes. Are our enemies surveilled using data that goes into our product? One hundred percent, and I completely support that.”

The question is, who counts as an enemy?

The Israel Defense Forces already use Palantir’s platforms to strike targets in Gaza. The Los Angeles Police Department used Palantir’s “predictive policing” tools to forecast crime patterns. Now the same technology is being deployed to track immigrants, under a contract that explicitly relies on “external” data sources beyond ICE and DHS databases.

Rep. Jamie Raskin requested an investigation into Palantir’s ties to top Trump officials,

“The history has been that a lot of techniques and technologies are used against noncitizens, and then very quickly, are turned to the citizen population. So it’s never too early to get on top of any Orwellian practices.”

The Stick and the Carrot

While Palantir builds the surveillance infrastructure for the people of America, the Trump administration has been disciplining the media covering it through a combination of threats and rewards.

The stick: Trump has repeatedly threatened to revoke the broadcast licenses of NBC and ABC. The FCC, now led by Trump appointee Brendan Carr, opened investigations into ABC, NBC, CBS, and local outlets. Trump sued Paramount over a “60 Minutes” interview with Kamala Harris; Paramount settled for $16 million and agreed to install a “bias ombudsman” at CBS.

The carrot: Comcast, NBC’s parent company, donated $1 million to Trump’s inaugural committee. They donated to Trump’s $300 million White House ballroom project, making Comcast the only media company on the donor list.

Disney briefly suspended Jimmy Kimmel. Paramount canceled The Late Show with Stephen Colbert outright.

Jim Acosta, the former CNN anchor, called it out on his podcast: “All of our major TV networks right now, they’re compromised in some fashion as the U.S. evolves or mutates into having a state-dominated TV system. NBC is now the latest network to compromise itself.”

It’s not censorship in the traditional sense. It’s corporate media companies voluntarily limiting themselves because they need regulatory approval to survive. The stick doesn’t have to land if everyone flinches.

The Story They Didn’t Want You to See

On December 21, CBS News was supposed to be airing a 60 Minutes investigation. The segment was called “Inside CECOT.” It featured Venezuelan immigrants describing their torture at the courtesy of the Trump administration’s check. It included analysis from UC Berkeley’s Human Rights Center. It was months in the making.

Three hours before the broadcast, CBS News Editor-in-Chief Bari Weiss, whose financial backers include pro-Trump venture capitalists Marc Andreessen and David Sacks, pulled the investigation from airing.

In her internal memo, Weiss said the segment needed “further reporting” and wasn’t “ready.” But the reporting by correspondent Sharyn Alfonsi tells a different story. Alfonsi wrote that she had held powerful testimony of torture from detainees. She had documentation. She had experts. In an Internal Memo, she said:

If the administration’s refusal to participate becomes a valid reason to spike a story, we have effectively handed them a ‘kill switch’ for any reporting they find convenient.

Despite being pulled by CBS, the segment briefly appeared on Global TV, CBS’s Canadian broadcast partner. Viewers screen-recorded it. Despite the challenge of finding a video of it because the company takes down any possible recordings, here is where you can watch it. link.

The government didn’t need to ban the story. It just needed to make the network afraid to tell it. They did the rest themselves.

The Death of Public Broadcasting

On May 1, 2025, Trump signed Executive Order 14290 for “Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media.” The order instructed all federal agencies to “cease Federal funding for NPR and PBS.”

The White House statement said NPR and PBS “receive millions from taxpayers to spread radical, woke propaganda disguised as ‘news.’”

In July, Congress rescinded $1.1 billion in funding it had planned to allocate to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting. On August 1, CPB announced that it would shut down by September 30, 2025. Patricia Harrison, CPB’s CEO, noted it was the first time in nearly 60 years that Congress had refused to fund public broadcasting.

NPR CEO Katherine Maher responded:

America’s founders knew that an informed public is essential to a functioning democracy…The President’s order is an affront to the First Amendment rights of NPR and locally owned and operated stations throughout America.

PBS CEO Paula Kerger warned that the order “threatens our ability to serve the American public with educational programming, as we have for the past 50-plus years.”

What actually gets lost isn’t bias. It’s access.

120 rural stations rely on CPB for at least 25% of their budgets; 33 stations rely on CPB for more than 50% (CPB, 2023). They were sometimes the only news option for communities that couldn’t support a commercial station for needed emergency alert systems. They prepared millions of children for school through programs like Sesame Street.

Rep. Mark Amodei, a Nevada Republican, told reporters: “If you’re mad at the editorial people, that’s fine. But you shouldn’t be mad at the stations. And, oh, by the way, you ought to look at where those stations are, because it’s a lot of Trump country, and they’re not the problem.” While I would argue that the face-eating leopard has made its rounds, what matters is that they're gone anyway under the veil of stopping woke bias.

You can’t defund “bias.” You can, however, defund access to information that advertisers and billionaires don’t control, pushing the poorest further into poverty and disinformation.

The Goosebumps Is the Point

What does the new information atmosphere look like?

Surveillance layer - Palantir is developing a centralized database that consolidates data across agencies, enabling real-time tracking of behavior. A private company, founded by a Trump megadonor, with employees now staffing DOGE and former employees embedded throughout the administration.

Distribution layer - Broadcast networks disciplined through regulatory threats and lawsuits. Corporate media self-censoring to protect merger approvals. Colbert canceled. Kimmel suspended. Networks donating to the White House. A 60 Minutes investigation killed three hours before broadcast.

Access layer - Public broadcasting defunded. The one media system that wasn’t dependent on corporate advertising or billionaire ownership. Eliminated after 60 years.

What remains is media controlled by advertisers who fear government retaliation. Networks whose parent companies need merger approvals from an FCC run by a Trump loyalist. Billionaires who are directly embedded in the administration own these platforms.

Now imagine you’re a journalist. You work for a network whose parent company just donated to the President’s ballroom. Your CEO needs regulatory approval for a merger. Where other networks are dismissing your colleagues for being too critical. Your own network just killed an investigation into torture three hours before air.

And somewhere, there’s a database that can pull up your tax records, your location history, and your associations.

You don’t need censorship. You censor yourself.

The self-censorship is what the ACLU brief in NPR v. Trump gets at: “Government officials should not be able to withhold taxpayer dollars, designated by Congress to promote private speech, from news outlets whose coverage they disapprove.”

But it goes beyond withholding dollars. It’s the combination of financial punishment (defunding, lawsuits, advertising pressure), regulatory threats (FCC investigations, license reviews), and the background knowledge that the surveillance apparatus exists.

Each piece reinforces the others. The surveillance makes the threats credible. The threats make self-censorship rational. The defunding of alternatives means there’s nowhere else to go.

The Oligarch Operating System

Follow the money.

Peter Thiel: Palantir co-founder, $15 million donor to JD Vance(who initially compared Trump to Hitler), and a longtime Trump backer. His company’s federal contracts nearly doubled in 2025.

Elon Musk: DOGE leader, Thiel’s PayPal co-founder. Trump talks about using his AI (Grok) to analyze government data. His platform (X) is where Trump communicates directly. Three former Palantir employees now work at DOGE.

Larry Ellison: World’s second-richest man, Trump supporter. His son’s company (Skydance) acquired Paramount, committed to ending DEI, and installed a bias ombudsman at CBS. Skydance is now eyeing Warner Bros. Discovery, and while Netflix tried to buy, unlike Comcast, the Ellisons have Trump’s blessing.

Stephen Miller: Trump’s immigration architect. Holds tens of thousands in Palantir stock. Designs the policies that Palantir’s tools execute.

Marc Andreessen and David Sacks: Venture capitalists, Trump backers, and financial supporters of Bari Weiss, the CBS News Editor-in-Chief who killed the CECOT investigation.

This oligarchy isn’t corruption in any standard form we've seen before. Nobody’s stuffing cash in envelopes; there is no proverbial black briefcase. It’s architecture. Those in power are rebuilding the country’s information infrastructure to run through companies they own, invest in, or directly control.

The administration isn’t capturing private industry. Private industry is capturing the administration. Or maybe they’ve merged into something that’s neither public nor private. Just power.

Consent Under Surveillance Isn’t Consent

Here’s the philosophical problem at the core of this.

Manufacturing consent worked because people didn’t know elites had rigged the menu. They believed they were making free choices based on good information.
Power manufactured their consent, but people still experienced it as consent.

What happens when people give consent under total surveillance?

If you know the government can see your tax records, your location, your purchases, and your communications. And you know the government has shown willingness to punish critics, fire dissidents, deport people without trial, and sue journalists into submission.

Is your “consent” to that government’s policies actually consent? Or is it compliance dressed up as agreement?

Well, if you haven't heard it in cop shows yet, consent obtained under duress isn’t valid consent. A contract signed with a gun to your head isn’t enforceable.

The surveillance state is a gun that’s never reasonably pointed at you but never quite holstered either. It’s always visible. It’s always there. And its existence shapes what you’re willing to say, do, and think.

Hannah Arendt wrote about totalitarianism's destruction of the private sphere. The space where people could be themselves, away from the state’s gaze. The database destroys that sphere.

Thirteen former Palantir employees signed a letter condemning the company’s work with the Trump administration, in an NPR article. “Democracy faces escalating threats: biometric data collection on immigrant children, journalists being targeted, science programs defunded, and key U.S. allies, like Ukraine, sidelined. Trump’s administration has sought to expand executive powers while greatly alluding to the monarchy greatly. Big Tech, including Palantir, is increasingly complicit, normalizing authoritarianism under the guise of a ‘revolution’ led by oligarchs. We must resist this trend.”

They describe what happens when those in power reorganize a democracy’s information infrastructure to serve themselves rather than citizens.

What Do We Do?

I recommend donating to PBS first, as they have a donation portal that supports local news stations.

But with the ending comes the end of an era of curiosity.

Sesame Street taught kids to read and count. Frontline held the government accountable. All Things Considered was the only news source for communities that couldn’t support a commercial station. That’s gone now.

Its userper is a database that tracks you, run by a company whose founder backs the administration, whose employees staff its efficiency team, and whose CEO brags about building tools to help “the West” achieve “its obvious, innate superiority.”

Support independent journalism. Those in power are capturing the information infrastructure, and the only counter is to build and sustain alternatives outside their system.

Because once you can be watched at all times, tracked by an algorithm, and consume media curated by people who need government regulatory approval for what they’re supposed to cover.

You’re not a citizen anymore. You’re a product bought and sold by the highest bidder.

If you found this piece valuable, please share it. The algorithms don’t favor this kind of work, but you can.

Share
author: Joshua Smith

title: About Time: The Secret Wasn’t Time Travel
seoTitle: About Time: The Secret Wasn’t Time Travel
excerpt: On grief, intentionality, and learning to live the same day better.
metaDescription: On grief, intentionality, and learning to live the same day better.
bodyHtml:
In my senior year of high school, my English teacher, Mr. Gleason, introduced our class and me to the Japanese phrase, Ichi-go ichi-e. 期一会. It roughly translates to "one time, one meeting," or, as he has described it, “every moment is an opportunity,” and it comes from the tradition of the tea ceremony. The idea is to treat every encounter as a once-in-a-lifetime experience, because it is. The people gathered for tea will never gather in exactly this configuration again. The light through the window will never fall at exactly this angle. This moment, right now, is unrepeatable

.

I first watched About Time that same year in 2023. I was 17. I was simple-minded; I took it at face value, a British rom-com with a time-travel gimmick. Sweet movie. Rachel McAdams. Domhnall Gleeson is being awkward and British. A do-over to get the love of your life.

The Setup

If you haven’t seen it, spoilers: Tim, played by Domhnall Gleeson, learns on his 21st birthday that the men in his family can travel back in time. It’s passed down like an heirloom, father to son, explained in the father's study with a drink in hand. Tim uses this power the way most of us would. He fixes awkward conversations. He tries New Years Day kisses and new first impressions until they work. He gets the girl. He betters his life through these do-overs.

For the first hour, the movie lets you believe that’s the point. What would you do with infinite chances to get it right?

Then Tim’s father gets sick.

And Tim learns something brutal about the rules of time travel. If he goes back before his children were born, he risks erasing them from existence. The timeline changes. Different moment of conception. Different child. So at somepoint he can never truly see his father again. The door closes permanently.

This is where the movie stops being about time travel and starts being about how we live with loss. And how we live, period.

You Don’t Get the Rehearsal

Before he dies, Tim’s father, played by Bill Nighy with a quiet British decency that makes you want to call your own dad, shares his approach to living. He lives each day twice. Once, with all the stress, anxiety, and rushing that characterizes normal existence. Then again, the second time through, noticing everything. The small kindness of a stranger holding a door. The meeting with corporate executives. The winning of a court case. The brief conversation he’d otherwise have tuned out.

Tim takes this further on his own after his father dies. He stops living twice altogether. He just lives them once. But he lives them that way the first time. Present. Attentive. Grateful for the ordinary.

That’s the argument the film makes. You don’t need infinite chances or second passes or the ability to correct every mistake, but you do need to live your life with intention.

The Fact of Life

The simple fact of life is that it will never be the way it was. Once something passes, it stays passed. Your father at 57. Your best friend at 20. Your great-grandmother, before dementia, took her vocabulary. That specific Tuesday morning with coffee and dew on the window and nowhere you had to be. Gone means gone.

We act like we don’t know this. We sleepwalk through our days, assuming we’ll have another chance to appreciate them later. Another chance to be present with the people we love.

The Japanese have a phrase for this kind of transient beauty. Mono no aware. An awareness of impermanence that produces a gentle sadness and a deeper appreciation of the passing moments of life. The moment matters because it won’t return.

Ichi-go ichi-e is the practice that flows from mono no aware. If every moment is unrepeatable, then every moment deserves your full presence. Not because you're obligated. Because it's a gift you're giving yourself and everyone around you.

Bill Nighy’s character found his purpose in ordinary life. Reading. Tea. Walks on the beach. Playing ping-pong with his son. Not achievements or accomplishments but the simple experience of being present in a life he’d chosen.

Dead Poets and Seizing Days

Dead Poets Society made a similar argument in a different way. Robin Williams, standing on a desk, telling a room full of prep school boys to seize the day. Carpe diem. Make your lives extraordinary.

I first heard that line as a teenager around the same time Mr. Gleason was teaching us about ichigo ichie. I thought carpe diem meant doing big, dramatic things. Quitting something. Starting something. Making a speech that everyone remembers. The movie itself ends with tragedy, with a student who takes "seizing the day" too seriously or not seriously enough, depending on how you read it.

The simple argument is that extraordinary doesn’t mean dramatic. It doesn’t mean quitting your job to travel, or giving a speech that brings people to tears, or building something the world will remember. Extraordinary means treating ordinary moments as if they won’t come back.

Because they won’t.

The Latin phrase memento vivere, “remember to live,” was always the counterpart to memento mori, “remember you will die.” Most of us only know the death one. We’ve seen it, at least for me, most famously on the Unus Annus YouTube channel started by YouTubers Markiplier and CrankGameplays. We forget the other half of the instruction. Yes, you will die. So while you’re here, remember to actually live.

Not later. Now. This Tuesday. This conversation. This meal with a person you love.

The Always Busy Person

In his book Travels with Epicurus, Daniel Klein writes about a retired friend who fills every day with activities. Golf lessons. Language classes. Volunteer committees. Board meetings for nonprofits. The man is terrified of being still. He’s always doing something, always optimizing, always productive.

“What exactly is he putting off by filling his days like this? The answer is obvious: he’s putting off being old.”

I’m not old. I’m in my twenties. I haven’t graduated yet. But I recognize that pattern in myself and in everyone around me. We fill our days with tasks and activities and responsibilities, not because they all matter, but because stillness is uncomfortable. Being present with nothing to accomplish feels like failure in a culture that values productivity above all.

The tea ceremony takes hours. You're not producing anything that will not be consumed by the end. You're not optimizing anything. You're just present with the people around you, paying attention to water and leaves and steam and silence. You can't rush through a tea ceremony any more than you can rush through a talk with your dying father.

Jon Kabat-Zinn tries to solve the dwindling presence of mindfulness by telling you to “pay attention in a particular way: on purpose, in the present moment, and nonjudgmentally.” Being present isn’t just philosophical advice. It has measurable effects on your brain and your health. A 2024 randomized controlled trial of over 1,200 participants found that just 30 days of digital mindfulness practice significantly improved well-being and reduced depression and anxiety symptoms, effects that persisted at follow-up.

The Argument

You should live with more intentionality. Not because it sounds nice or because some movie made me feel things, but because the alternative is sleepwalking through the only life you get, waiting for some future version of yourself who will finally be present, finally appreciate things, and finally stop rushing.

That person doesn’t show up. There’s only you, now, with whatever’s in front of you.

Tim couldn’t go back and see his father again after a certain point. The rules didn’t allow it. We don’t have time travel, so we don’t even get the option to go back and fix the moments we wasted. We don’t get to re-experience the conversation we half-listened to. We don’t get another version of the morning we rushed through. We just get this one, and then it’s gone.

What would change if you treated that as true instead of just knowing it intellectually?

I think about my own grandmother. The version of her I knew when I was young. The memories we had that I remember, and the many more I’ve absentmindedly forgotten.

I think about my parents at the age they are now, knowing they won’t always be this age, knowing the door will close on this version of them, just as it did for Tim.

I think about Mr. Gleason in that classroom, teaching a bunch of seventeen-year-olds about a Japanese tea ceremony concept, probably knowing most of us wouldn't understand it fully for another decade.

The Practice

This isn’t just philosophy. It’s a practice. And like any practice, it takes work.

Tim in the movie talks about the ordinary parts of his day with a kind of reverence. The walk to the “Tube”. The first cup of tea from the barista at his work. His runs through the courtroom hall every day. He’s not doing anything different externally. He’s just paying attention differently internally.

You can start smaller than that. Pick one meal this week and eat it without your phone. Actually taste it. Actually, notice the texture and temperature, and what it makes you think about.

Pick one conversation and be fully present for it. Not thinking about what you’re going to say next or what you need to do after. Just listening. Just being there with the person in front of you.

There’s a scene late in the movie where Tim is on the train, and someone is listening to music next to him, and he performs air guitar to it. No reason. No purpose. Just appreciation for the fact that they exist and so does he, in the same moment, on the same ordinary day, and there is music playing.

That’s available to you. Right now. No time travel required.

It Will Never Be the Way It Was

Your youth is gone. Certain people who loved you are gone. Certain versions of yourself are gone. You can’t go back.

But that’s not a bad thing. That’s the condition that makes presence possible.

If you could always go back, nothing would matter. Video game logic. No stakes. The weight of a moment comes from its unrepeatable nature. The flowers blooming in your neighbor’s yard won’t look exactly like that again. Next summer, they’ll be different. Or they won’t be there at all. Or you won’t be there to see them.

You’re allowed to just look at them. Right now. For no reason other than they exist, and so do you.

Viktor Frankl, the psychiatrist who survived Auschwitz and wrote Man’s Search for Meaning, argued that humans can endure almost anything if they have a sense of purpose.

“Those who have a ‘why’ to live,” he wrote, “can bear almost any ‘how.’”

People with a clear sense of purpose live longer, experience less cognitive decline, recover faster from illness, and report higher well-being throughout their lives. But purpose doesn’t have to be grand. Frankl himself found meaning in small things during the worst moments of his life. A sunset glimpsed through a window. A piece of bread shared with someone weaker. The decision to think of his wife even when he didn’t know if she was alive.

Tim’s father found purpose in books, family, and playing ping-pong. The old men of Hydra that Klein writes about in Travels with Epicurus find purpose in sitting together, drinking, and talking about nothing in particular for hours. Just existing. Just being present with each other in the time they have left.

Maybe that’s enough. Maybe that’s the whole point.

The Only Day You Get

You don’t need time travel. You don’t need the ability to relive your days. You don’t need another chance to get it right.

You need the willingness to live this day, once, on purpose, as it counts.

Because it does.

The door is going to close. On your parents. On your friends. On yourself eventually. The version of this morning you’re in right now, it won’t come back. The people you love at the ages they are now, they won’t be these ages forever.

Smell the flowers. Watch the light change. Taste the food you’re eating instead of scrolling through your phone while you chew. Tell people what they mean to you while they can still hear it. Notice the texture of the ordinary day.

They’re all saying the same thing.

You’re alive. Right now. This is the day you have.

Live it like you mean it.

Share

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: A Speech for Martin Luther King Jr. Birthday
seoTitle: A Speech for Martin Luther King Jr. Birthday
excerpt: Why Alpha Men Answered Democracy’s Call in 1936
metaDescription: Why Alpha Men Answered Democracy’s Call in 1936
bodyHtml:
Today is Martin Luther King Jr.'s birthday.

King was an Alpha. So was Thurgood Marshall. And Charles Hamilton Houston, the man who trained Marshall and architected the legal strategy that dismantled “separate but equal.”

For those unfamiliar: Alpha Phi Alpha is the oldest intercollegiate historically Black Greek-letter fraternity, founded in 1906 at Cornell University. Every year, chapters across the country compete in oratorical contests. The tradition goes back decades. They pick a theme, you write, and you deliver the speech.

This year, the theme was about democracy.

Not democracy in the abstract. Democracy as a practice. Democracy requires defense, especially now.

What follows is the speech I wrote and presented to them. I cleaned it up for reading.

Alpha’s Democratic Renaissance: Leading America’s Sacred Charge

In 1936, as Adolf Hitler proclaimed Aryan supremacy to the world, seven men of Alpha Phi Alpha stepped onto the track in Berlin.

Jesse Owens. Ralph Metcalfe. Cornelius Johnson. Dave Albritton. John Woodruff. Archie Williams. Fritz Pollard Jr.

When the dust settled and “The Star-Spangled Banner” faded, these Alpha men had won a combined thirteen medals. Not just for America. For democracy itself.

Yet when Jesse Owens returned home, he made an observation that cuts to the heart of our charge today: “Hitler didn’t snub me. It was our president who snubbed me.”

Even in victory, even in proving democracy’s promise, the work remained unfinished.

Eighty-nine years later, that work still calls to us.

The Sacred Inheritance

Brothers, we stand upon the shoulders of giants who understood a central reality: democracy is not a destination. It is a discipline.

When our Seven Jewels founded Alpha Phi Alpha on December 4th, 1906, they planted seeds in frozen Cornell soil that would bloom into a century of democratic leadership.

Consider the harvest.

Charles Hamilton Houston, “The Man Who Killed Jim Crow,” trained one-quarter of all Black lawyers in America and built the legal strategy that toppled “separate but equal.” Belford V. Lawson Jr., our Sixteenth General President, won the first Supreme Court case argued by an African American attorney and secured over 5,100 jobs through his “Don’t Buy Where You Can’t Work” campaign. Thurgood Marshall. Adam Clayton Powell Jr. Martin Luther King Jr.

But perhaps no legacy burns brighter than our revolutionary cry born in 1936: “A Voteless People Is a Hopeless People.”

When only three percent of eligible African Americans in the South could vote, when literacy tests and poll taxes strangled democracy in its cradle, Alpha Phi Alpha declared war on voicelessness itself.

Our citizenship schools registered hundreds of Black voters decades before the Southern Christian Leadership Conference even existed. We didn’t wait for the movement. We were the movement.

Democracy Under Siege

Today, democracy faces threats that would make the architects of Jim Crow smile with recognition.

Project 2025 seeks to criminalize the very voter assistance our predecessors died to provide. Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton raided the homes of 87-year-old civil rights activists, forcing them to stand in their nightgowns while officers searched for the “crime” of helping people vote. Alabama imposed twenty-year prison sentences for ballot assistance. Florida prosecuted citizens who believed they had the right to vote.

Sound familiar? It should. The methods evolve. The mission remains the same: to silence the voice of democracy itself.

But voter suppression is just one front.

Gerrymandering has stolen sixteen House seats through mathematical manipulation. In North Carolina, fair maps that should produce a 7-7 split were redrawn to guarantee a 10-4 Republican advantage. This isn’t politics. This is deliberately created voicelessness.

Meanwhile, misinformation campaigns flood our communities with lies designed to convince us our votes don’t matter, our voices don’t count, and our democracy doesn’t work. Russian disinformation. Domestic conspiracy theories. All conspiring to convince Americans that democracy itself is the enemy.

And it’s working.

Youth voter turnout dropped from 50 percent in 2020 to 42 percent in 2024. Nearly half of young Americans reported hearing “not much” about voting. Sixty percent were never contacted by campaigns.

We are failing to pass democracy’s torch to the next generation.

The Alpha Renaissance

If our forebears could register voters when it meant risking their lives, surely we can register voters when it means risking our comfort.

The question is not whether democracy needs defending. The question is whether we will answer democracy’s call with the same valor as those who came before us.

I envision an Alpha Phi Alpha that leads a Democratic Renaissance. Not just responding to threats, but reconsidering how we safeguard democracy in our time.

We must become digital democracy leaders. Partner with organizations like the Digital Democracy Initiative to create apps that make voter registration as simple as ordering coffee. Develop tools that detect and oppose misinformation in our communities. If they can weaponize technology against democracy, we can weaponize technology for democracy.

We must forge coalitions that reflect democracy’s rainbow. Our Divine 9 partnership registered a quarter million voters in 2024. Imagine multiplying that impact across faith communities, labor unions, corporate partners, and civic organizations. The Civic Alliance already includes 1,252 companies whose civic engagement correlates with better fiscal reports. When democracy wins, everybody wins.

We must meet young people where they are. Not in stuffy conference rooms, but on TikTok, Instagram, and in gaming platforms. Programs like PA Youth Vote achieved a 471 percent increase in civic engagement by connecting daily life to democratic participation. We must make voting as viral as any social media trend.

We must become community democracy defenders. Following the “We Got Us” model, establish Alpha Phi Alpha Democracy Centers in key states. Not just for election seasons, but year-round. Providing voter protection, digital literacy training, and civic education that inoculates our communities against the virus of misinformation.

The Prophetic Vision

I see a future where every Alpha chapter becomes a beacon of democratic light in its community. Where our $100,000 micro-grant program expands to millions, funding democracy defenders in every state. Where “A Voteless People Is a Hopeless People” evolves into “An Educated People Is an Empowered People.”

I see Alpha Phi Alpha leading the coalition that passes the John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act, which ends gerrymandering through fair redistricting, and guarantees every American the sacred right to make their voice heard.

I see us honoring the legacy of Charles Hamilton Houston by training a new generation of social engineers. I see us channeling the spirit of Belford Lawson by winning legal victories that expand the promise of democracy. I see us walking in the footsteps of Martin Luther King Jr. by making real his dream that America would live up to the true meaning of its creed, a government of the people, by the people, and for the people.

The Sacred Charge
