But this vision requires more than hope. It demands action. More than inspiration. It demands organization. More than applause. It demands commitment.

Democracy is not a spectator sport, and Alpha Phi Alpha is not a spectating fraternity. We are the fraternity that chose “First of All, Servants of All, We Shall Transcend All” as our motto because we understood that leadership is a verb, that service is a lifestyle, and that transcendence is a choice.

So I ask today: When democracy’s historians write the story of this moment, will they say Alpha Phi Alpha answered the call? Will they say we chose courage over comfort, action over apathy, democracy over dictatorship?

The choice is ours. The time is now. The charge is sacred.

Let us lead the charge to preserve and safeguard democracy, not because it is easy, but because it is necessary. Not because it is convenient, but because it is required. Not because we have to, but because we get to.

Democracy calls. Alpha answers.

That’s the speech.

I wrote it thinking about what King would recognize if he walked into a room today. The poll taxes are gone. The literacy tests are gone. But the machinery of disenfranchisement has simply gotten more sophisticated. More legalistic. More data-driven.

The question King’s generation asked is the same question we have to answer: What are you willing to risk for democracy?

Happy MLK Day.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share

bodyHtml:
Hey friends,

I’m under the weather today and don’t have the capacity to publish an article I’m proud of. Rather than force it, I’m taking the day to rest and recover.

If you’re still in a sharing mood, I’d really appreciate the support on something that matters a lot to me. This January, I’m running 62 miles to raise money for the American Cancer Society. Every share helps, even if you can’t donate.

👉 https://gofund.me/b90eccf37

Thank you for reading, for caring, and for sticking with me. Writing will be back soon.

— Josh

author: Joshua Smith

title: How to Write Faster Without Writing Worse
seoTitle: How to Write Faster Without Writing Worse
excerpt: How to write and think faster and not sound like you don't know what you are talking about.
metaDescription: How to write and think faster and not sound like you don't know what you are talking about.
bodyHtml:
Every slow writer has the same superstition: if the article comes out fast, it can’t be “serious.”

Wrong.

The people who consistently publish aren’t slower or smarter. They just figured out you can write quickly and think deeply. If you separate making clay from sculpting it.

The trick is building a pipeline that starts with a broad idea (a TikTok about cowboy mythology or a tweet about Sinners) and moves you through research, outlining, and drafting before your inner voice tells you to stop.

Here’s the actual process.

Start with a TikTok, Not a Thesis

The fastest ideas don’t start as theses. They start as reactions.

Instead of waiting for some capital-I Idea, steal what TikTok creators figured out years ago. Get a hook first, then find meaning later.

Watch until something annoys or intrigues you. A video of someone getting murdered by ICE. A clip of James Gunn Superman. A film clip you keep replaying because something about it feels wrong or incredibly right.

Turn that reaction into a working title. “How much a dollar cost and the price of generosity.” “Why Blackkklansman lost the Oscars.” You can change it later. Right now, it just needs to give your brain direction to sprint.

Research Like a Thief, Not a Grad Student.

Once you have your hook, grab just enough research to say something sharper than “this feels bad.” That means raiding Google Scholar and the open web for 20–30 minutes. Not losing an afternoon to 47 PDFs you’ll never read.

On Google, search for phrases of interest, and skim the abstracts for one or two surprising findings.

Use Google dorking to dig up specific documents instead of generic SEO posts:

  • filetype: pdf "Slavery and Sports"

  • filetype: pdf "gamer gate"

  • site:newyorktimes.com "ICE" to see how media are framing the things you think about

You want three things: one concrete stat, one term you can steal (like “transcription skills” or “cognitive load”), and one idea that makes you reconsider what you thought you knew.

That’s it. 30 minutes, three useful pieces, done.

Build the Sprint Skeleton in Google Docs

Before you write sentences, build a bare-bones scaffold that your future self can just fill in. The purpose is to outline lightly so the drafting brain never has to stop and ask, “What now?”

In the Doc, drop in four headings:

Hook: The TikTok/movie/pop culture moment and why it grabbed you.

Problem: Why does this happen? Who does this affect? Why is it an issue?

Evidence: The one or two research nuggets that complicate or confirm the conventional wisdom.

Result: The ending is a way to wrap up what you are talking about, whether that’s positive or negative.

Under each heading, write 2–3 bullet fragments. Not sentences; the topic you would like to address. This will become your subheading.

This takes five minutes. Maybe ten if you’re being precious about it.

Sprint Like It’s a Game, Not a Test

When it’s time to draft, you’re not “being a writer.” You’re doing a writing sprint: short, focused bursts where the only thing that matters is the word count.

Set a 30-40 minute timer. Close everything else. Tell yourself the piece only has to be true to you, not polished. The research is already there. You’re just narrating what you saw and thought.

During the sprint, editing is not permitted. This is the rule. If you don’t know a transition, write “[transition]” and keep going. If you realize a better intro halfway through, write “[better intro: X]” and keep going.

Placeholders keep the momentum up. Once the words are in place, you free up attention for structure, nuance, and style. Plenty of writers report doubling their output once they stop tinkering mid-sentence.

Let Revision Be Slow (On Purpose)

The point of all this isn’t to banish slowness. It’s to put slowness where it belongs.

First drafts want speed. Revisions deserve intention.

That means:

Day 1: Quick hook to quick Scholar/dorking raid to sprint skeleton to one or two 40-minute drafting sprints.

Day 2: Read it as if someone else wrote it. Let your slower, more anxious self fix the sentences, tighten the structure, and add citations using what you already researched.

You’re not trying to become an effortless machine. You’re building a cheap little system.

Pop culture spark. fast research. sprint skeleton. timed draft.

That reduces the number of decisions between “huh, that TikTok or tweet or headlined annoyed me” and “publish.”

Write fast. Think slow. Publish anyway.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share
author: Joshua Smith

title: The Cowboy Was Never Conservative: How Conservative Media Stole America’s Actual Outlaw.
seoTitle: The Cowboy Was Never Conservative: How Conservative Media Stole America’s Actual Outlaw.
excerpt: Hollywood and the Right turned multicultural drifters into symbols of white nationalism. The real cowboy would’ve told them to f off. How Jordan Peele Got it back.
metaDescription: Hollywood and the Right turned multicultural drifters into symbols of white nationalism. The real cowboy would’ve told them to f off. How Jordan Peele Got it ba
bodyHtml:
Two days ago, the DHS posted an ICE advertisement on its Instagram featuring a cowboy. Although this is not new, the Republican Party for years has been using an originally native symbol to bolster nationalism and conservatism.

In 1980, Ronald Reagan put on a cowboy hat and “rode” to the White House.

He wasn’t a cowboy. He was a Hollywood actor from Dixon, Illinois, who purchased a ranch in Santa Barba, California, and learned to pose on horseback. But the costume worked. The white hat or white horse. The ranch photo ops. All of it changed the face of American politics. It is a story about how the multiracial working‑class laborer became the mascot for white grievance politics, and what it means to take him back.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Here's the problem. The actual American cowboy looked nothing like Ronald Reagan.

One in four cowboys was Black. A third were Mexican. The vaqueros invented the whole practice. The terminology, equipment, and culture associated with the American West came from Spanish and Indigenous traditions that predate the United States by centuries.

Who the Cowboys Actually Were

The numbers Hollywood erased.

George W. Saunders, president of the Texas Trail Drivers Association during the 1920s, estimated that out of the 35,000 men who trailed cattle, one-third would have been Mexican. Historians estimate that 20-25 percent of cowboys in the American West were free Black people. William Loren Katz, author of The Black West, wrote: “Right after the Civil War, being a cowboy was one of the few jobs open to men of color who wanted to not serve as elevator operators or delivery boys.”

Many Black cowboys were formerly enslaved people who had learned cattle-handling skills on southern plantations. After emancipation, instead of the North, they headed west. The work was dangerous, the pay was poor, and the conditions were brutal. But it offered something the East didn’t: a chance to be judged by skill rather than skin.

Nat Love was born into slavery in Tennessee in 1854. After the Civil War, he left home and became one of the most famous cowboys of his era. He learned to speak Spanish, won roping championships, and published an autobiography in 1907. He was Black. Hollywood never made his movie. So was Bill Pickett, the Texas cowboy who invented bulldogging and performed in Buffalo Bill’s Wild West Show.

Daniel Wallace invented his own cattle brand and became one of Texas’s most successful ranchers. Jim Perry worked the 3-million-acre XIT Ranch in the Texas Panhandle.

These are the names that don’t appear in John Wayne movies.

The vaqueros invented everything.

The word “cowboy” itself comes from vaquero, which comes from vaca, Spanish for cow. The lasso, the saddle, the spurs, the chaps, the rodeo: all Mexican traditions that predate the American cowboy by centuries.

Pablo A. Rangel, a historian of the vaquero tradition, explained it to the History Channel: “All of the skills, traditions, and ways of working with cattle are very much rooted in the Mexican vaquero. If you are a cowboy in the U.S. today, you have developed what you know from the vaquero.”

The first vaqueros were Indigenous Mesoamerican men trained by Spanish colonizers in the 1500s. By the time Anglo settlers arrived in Texas in the 1820s, vaquero culture had been developing for 300 years. The newcomers didn’t invent cowboy culture. They borrowed it.

Richard King, founder of the legendary King Ranch in Texas, traveled to Mexico in 1854 specifically to recruit entire vaquero families. He knew they understood horses and cattle better than any American.

The Texas State History Museum:

“To the Spanish owners of big ranches in Mexico, the vaquero was a laborer. Most vaqueros were mestizo, American Indian, African American, mulatto, or criollo.”

The cowboy was never a symbol of white American independence. He was multicultural, landless, and transient. He worked for wealthy landowners and owned almost nothing. He rejected marriage, church, property, and law. He was, in every sense, the opposite of what Reagan represented.

"If the real cowboy met Reagan at his ranch, he would've recognized “the boss”.

Hollywood’s Great Whitening

The cowboy in your head was invented in the 20th century, and the people who invented him had an agenda.

Birth of a Nation dropped in 1915. Its visual language infected every Western that followed. White men conquering a savage land. Taming chaos. Defending order against everyone else

By the time John Wayne arrived, the template was finalized.

Wayne became the Hollywood cowboy. Conservative, pro-military, nationalist. He supported the Hollywood blacklist. He opposed the civil rights movement. In a 1971 Playboy interview, he said, “I believe in white supremacy until the Blacks are educated to a point of responsibility.”

That’s the guy we turned into America’s cowboy.

Black cowboys disappeared from films entirely. Mexican vaqueros became bandits and villains. Indigenous people became obstacles to be conquered. Women who ran ranches and worked cattle vanished from the narrative.

The Western film became propaganda. “Cowboys vs. Indians” trained audiences to accept Manifest Destiny as heroic. The “frontier” myth justified displacement and violence against anyone who got in the way.

Look at The Searchers, often called one of the greatest Westerns ever made. John Wayne plays Ethan Edwards, a Confederate veteran who spends years tracking Comanches who kidnapped his niece. The film frames him as a complex antihero, but the subtext is clear: white civilization must rescue itself from the savage other. The Academy nominated it for nothing. It didn't need Oscars. It was already doing the work.

Or look at The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance, where a journalist says: “When the legend becomes fact, print the legend.”

That line is the confession. Hollywood knew what it was doing. It printed the legend and buried the facts.

The Libertarian-to-Conservative Pipeline

Where the cowboy stops being a movie costume and becomes a full-blown political costume.

The actual cowboy ethos was anti-authority. Cowboys were nomadic by nature. They rejected domesticity. They drifted between ranches and refused to settle down. Many were outlaws, rustlers, and gamblers.

That sounds more libertarian than conservative. And for a while, the frontier myth was claimed by both sides.

Theodore Roosevelt used cowboy imagery for progressive politics; you can see the iconic horse-riding imagery in Night at the Museum. Lyndon Johnson, a Texan, invoked it for the Great Society. The cowboy could represent forward motion, reform, and national improvement.

Then something shifted.

Historian Heather Cox Richardson traced the change in her work on what she calls “cowboy individualism.” After the civil rights movement, opponents of integration looked west. They championed the image of the cowboy as a white man who wanted nothing from the government but to be left alone.

“With his cowboy hat and western ranch, Reagan deliberately tapped into this mythology, as well as the racism and sexism in it, when he promised to slash taxes and regulations to free individuals from a grasping government,” Richardson wrote.

The cowboy myth flipped. It stopped representing forward motion and started representing nostalgia. Not a new frontier but a return to the old one. Before civil rights. Before integration. Before Medicare. Before, the government protected anyone who wasn't already protected by being white.

The Marlboro Man became the icon of this shift. From 1954 to 1999, Philip Morris sold cigarettes through fake masculinity dressed in cowboy clothes. The campaign was so effective that “Marlboro Man” became shorthand for a particular kind of American male: silent, rugged, self-reliant.

Reagan completed the transformation. He bought Rancho del Cielo in 1974 and spent years cultivating the cowboy image. He wore boots to press conferences. He posed on a horse for campaign materials. He called his foreign policy “cowboy diplomacy.”

But Reagan’s policies had nothing to do with actual cowboys. He cut taxes for the wealthy. He deregulated industries that harmed workers. He busted unions.

Real cowboys were labor. They worked for wages on capitalist-owned ranches. The cattle drives required cooperation, collective action, and shared meals around the chuckwagon. Cowboys depended on each other. The “rugged individualist” myth ignores that interdependence entirely. Reagan fired 11,000 air traffic controllers in 1981 for striking. Real cowboys would've walked out with them.

And it ignores something else: the Mexican, Indigenous, and Black knowledge that made cowboy culture possible. The “self-made man” narrative pretends that Anglo settlers invented skills they actually borrowed from everyone else.

The conservative cowboy is a contradiction. Real cowboys rejected marriage, church, property, and law. The conservative version defends “traditional values” and property rights. One is anti-institutional. The other is hyper-institutional.

You can’t be a nationalist and a cowboy. The cowboy rejected the nation.

Weaponizing the Myth

The cowboy became a political weapon.

Reagan used it to sell deregulation. He framed cutting government programs as a means of setting Americans free, even though most Americans lack capital and depend on those programs to survive. He cut federal housing assistance by 75%. Food stamp funding dropped by a third. Meanwhile, defense spending doubled.

George W. Bush inherited the playbook. Born in Connecticut, Yale-educated, he bought a Texas ranch and put on cowboy boots. After 9/11, he said of Osama bin Laden: “Wanted dead or alive.” The frontier myth justified imperial violence.

Trump took it further. At his rallies, supporters wear cowboy hats next to “Build the Wall” signs. Kid Rock performs in a cowboy hat while calling for armed border patrols. The symbol that once meant resistance to authority now means deputizing civilians to enforce deportation.

What’s actually being sold is nostalgia for a past that never existed. Independence packaged as resentment. Violence framed as virtue.

“Real America” means white rural America. “Traditional values” means pre-civil rights values. “Self-reliant” means anti-welfare. Meanwhile, rural areas receive massive farm subsidies. The people waving cowboy hats at MAGA rallies depend on government support while voting against government support for everyone else.

Cowboy individualism, as Richardson puts it, “became central to the culture of Republican politics as a way for Republican politicians to convince voters to support the destruction of federal government programs that actually benefited them.”

The conservatives in America had weaponized the symbol against the very people it was supposed to represent.

The Real Outlaw vs. the MAGA Myth

Let’s compare what the cowboy actually was to what conservatives claim.

  • Real cowboys were multicultural / myth is white

  • Real cowboys were landless laborers/myth says property owners

  • Real cowboys created communal systems/myth says individualists

  • Real cowboys learned from Mexican and Indigenous traditions/myth says self-taught

The lexical gap between these two is the whole con.

Conservatives took a symbol of multiracial working-class resistance and turned it into white nationalist nostalgia. They arrested the outlaw and made him a cop.

Jordan Peele and Reclaiming the Black Cowboy

In 2022, Jordan Peele released Nope.

The film stars Daniel Kaluuya and Keke Palmer as siblings who run the only Black-owned horse ranch in California, training horses for Hollywood productions. They’re descendants of the Black jockey in Eadweard Muybridge’s 1878 motion study footage, considered one of the first movies ever made.

The jockey is real. The riders name, however, has been lost to history.

Peele’s thesis is right there in the opening: Black people were there first, and they got erased from the story. The importance of the spectacle is placed above the person's identity.

Keisha Senter, Monkeypaw’s senior vice president of culture and impact on Nope:

“What happened to the Black cowboy? Who erased the Black cowboy?”

“Erasure is a horror story.”

In 2025, that insight became High Horse: The Black Cowboy, a three-part documentary on Peacock examining Black cowboy history and the systematic erasure that followed.

Glynn Turman said, “I’m from the generation where we made great strides. And to see us in a time where the institutions are trying to negate those strides indeed, it’s disheartening.”

Other recent reclamations include The Harder They Fall (2021), a fictional Western grounded in historical Black figures, and Concrete Cowboy (2020), about the Fletcher Street Urban Riding Club in Philadelphia.

Beyoncé’s Cowboy Carter album won Album of the Year at the 2025 Grammys. Lil Nas X’s “Old Town Road” broke streaming records and still holds the record for most time spent at No. 1 at 19 weeks. Solange Knowles’s When I Get Home " centers on Black Texas cowboy culture.

Bun B, one half of UGK, “This is not Black people trying to assimilate with this country's Western lifestyle. Black people across this country have been prevalent in this space for years.”

These projects don’t just correct history. They reclaim the symbol taken by people who hated those who actually did the work.

Share

Taking Back the Outlaw

Conservatives didn’t inherit cowboy culture. They stole it, bleached it, and sold it back to the American public as nationalism.

The real cowboy legacy looks different. It’s about rejecting authority, building multiracial solidarity, and creating culture outside institutional control. Labor, not capital.

What does reclaiming the cowboy actually mean?

Learn the real history. Recognize the theft. Support the actual descendants: Black rodeo, Mexican vaquero traditions, Indigenous horsemen.

It means refusing to let Republicans own symbols they perverted. The outlaw tradition belongs to those who resist, not those who enforce.

The Oakland Black Cowboy Association has been organizing since the 1970s. The Compton Cowboys started in the 1990s. The New York City Federation of Black Cowboys rides through Brooklyn. The Prairie View Trail Ride has been running from Hempstead to Houston since 1957.

These communities never stopped being cowboys. Hollywood stopped filming them.

The cowboy rejected everything America tried to make him. That's the tradition worth keeping, and it’s time we do the same.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: The First Murder: ICE Agent Kills U.S. Citizen Renee Good in Minneapolis “Occupation” Raid
seoTitle: The First Murder: ICE Agent Kills U.S. Citizen Renee Good in Minneapolis “Occupation” Raid
excerpt: A federal agent killed a U.S. citizen sitting in her car. The rest of the country has a choice to make.
metaDescription: A federal agent killed a U.S. citizen sitting in her car. The rest of the country has a choice to make.
bodyHtml:
Here’s how unaccountable police forces form.

They start with fear. Pick a population. Label them dangerous. Propose emergency measures for public safety. “Just this once.” Then those measures don’t end. They become permanent. The targets of the police expand. What started as an exception becomes infrastructure.

ICE was born from this logic. The agency didn’t exist before 2003. It was created inside the Department of Homeland Security after 9/11, designed to treat entire populations as threats. Muslims. Immigrants. Anyone who questioned the war on terror. They gave it broad authority to investigate, detain, and deport. No meaningful oversight. No real accountability. The emergency never ended.

Yesterday, we saw what that infrastructure produces.

On January 7, 2026, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent shot and killed Renee Nicole Good, a 37-year-old U.S. citizen, in south Minneapolis. She was sitting in her car.

DHS claims that she tried to run over the agent, and Mayor Frey calls “bullshit.”

The killing wasn’t a rogue cop acting out of line. It was a federal agent deployed in what ICE called “the largest immigration operation ever.” The border Czar sent two thousand federal agents to Minneapolis. That's the infrastructure they built. That's what it looks like when you weaponize.

Renee Good was not the target of the operation. She was a community member, but more than that, a poet, a writer, a mother of a six-year-old, whose father had died in 2023. She lived in Minneapolis with her wife. Her mom, Donna Ganger, told the Minnesota Star Tribune, “not part of anything like that at all,” referring to protesters challenging ICE agents.

That’s the point. That’s what should terrify you.

What Happened That Day

Early Wednesday morning, ICE agents surrounded a red Honda Pilot on Portland Avenue in south Minneapolis. Less than a mile from where police murdered George Floyd four years ago.

The official DHS narrative: Renee Good's vehicle was blocking the road. Agents approached. She attempted to run over one of them. The agent fired, killing her.

Trigger warning: The video shows the shooting. It's not gratuitous, but it's hard to watch.

@huffpostFederal agents blocked a doctor from helping a woman who was fatally shot by an ICE agent in Minneapolis. NOTE: The date listed is incorrect, as the incident occurred on Jan. 7, 2026.
Tiktok failed to load.

Enable 3rd party cookies or use another browser

A physician who lived nearby rushed to help. According to witness accounts captured on video and reported by Huffington Post, when she offered medical assistance, an ICE agent told her, "I don't care. We have our own medics." When neighbors demanded to see those medics, agents told them to “relax”.

Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara arrived to find a woman with a gunshot wound to the head. He performed CPR because ICE agents refused to let medical personnel in. Renee Good was eventually transported to Hennepin County Medical Center and pronounced dead.

The federal government had just murdered a U.S. citizen. A legal observer. Someone with a child who is in 1st grade. Someone with roots in the community.

The response from federal leadership? Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said the shooting was justified. That the officer “followed his training.”

Noem is lying.

The Department of Justice has a policy on deadly force. Section 1-16.200. It says firearms “may not be discharged at a moving vehicle” unless the vehicle threatens death or serious injury to the officer or others, AND “no other objectively reasonable means of defense appear to exist, which includes moving out of the path of the vehicle.”

Read that again. Federal policy requires agents to consider stepping aside before shooting.

This is where the story breaks. Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey watched the video footage. His response: “after seeing the video myself I want to tell everybody directly that is bullshit.”

He didn’t stop there. At a press conference that afternoon, Frey said directly to federal law enforcement: To ICE, get the fuck out of Minneapolis.

Noem added the officer “took actions to defend himself.”

Defend himself from what, exactly? A woman is sitting in her car.

The Blank Check

The Nazi regime established the Gestapo in 1933. They gave it one job: “Schutzhaft,” to investigate threats to the state. Arrest people based on suspicion. No warrant needed. No court review. No limits.

The Gestapo imprisoned people indefinitely. No trial. No charges. No judicial process. During the Nuremberg Trials, judges asked Nazis why they needed this power; they said it was necessary to combat “all tendencies inimical(harmful) to the State.” Which meant whatever we decided.

That’s not a narrow checked power. That’s a blank check.

ICE operates on a blank check, and we, the American people, are made to pay.

Its officers are masked and anonymous. FOIA exemptions shield their identities, so you can’t find out who killed someone. In March 2025, the Trump administration dismantled the office that handled complaints against DHS officers. They didn’t just ignore complaints. They eliminated the people who process them.

In April 2025, Trump signed an executive order that extends qualified immunity into criminal law. Officers who are criminally liable for their actions now have their legal expenses paid by the federal government. Which made a system that was nearly impossible to hold cops accountable, now functionally impossible.

In October, Deputy White House Chief of Staff Stephen Miller went on Fox News and told ICE agents directly: “You have federal immunity in the conduct of your duties. And anybody who lays a hand on you or tries to stop you or tries to obstruct you is committing a felony.”

Georgetown constitutional law professor Steve Vladeck called Miller’s statement “wrong on its face.” Federal agents can be prosecuted.

But here’s the thing: it doesn’t matter if Miller is legally correct. What matters is that ICE agents believe him. What matters is that the White House told federal agents they can’t be touched. That’s the message. That’s the permission structure.

The agency uses racial profiling as official policy. Tom Homan, Trump’s immigration czar who received $50000 from GEO Group(the United States' largest prison operator), said ICE agents can detain people “based on their physical appearance.” He announced it. That’s what “official policy” means.

Here’s what the numbers tell you.

The Trump administration wanted to carry out one million deportations by the end of last year. The immigration courts have a backlog of 3.6 million cases as of mid 2025. You can’t wait for hearings when you need to move a million people. You can’t provide lawyers; ICE’s detention system prevents access to counsel. You can’t verify immigration status carefully. You just move bodies, and the dehumanization never stops with the people labeled as “dangerous”.

To be a Citizen or To not be a Citizen

Renee Good’s citizenship didn’t protect her.

That’s the line that’s been crossed. Not metaphorically. Literally. A federal agent deployed in a 2,000-person operation murdered an innocent citizen sitting in her car, and the federal government responds that the officer followed protocol.

What used to be “this could happen to anyone” is now “this is happening to everyone.”

Mayor Frey understood something: when federal agents are authorized to treat anything as probable cause, when they're deployed in massive numbers, when they operate without accountability, when they can kill citizens with the government's blessing, it is no longer law enforcement. It is an active occupation.

The Choice

The question is whether other cities follow Minneapolis or accept the framing that this is normal. That deportations are necessary and that the camps are efficient.

I’m telling you what it is.

It’s the first documented time an ICE agent killed a U.S. citizen in these operations, but it won't be the last.

It is the update to the status quo that, citizen or not, the government can kill you without consequence.

What Happens Next

And now the last layer of accountability is gone.

On January 8, 2026, Trump signed an executive order withdrawing the United States from 66 international organizations. The UN Human Rights Council. The UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. The International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance. The UN Office for Children in Armed Conflict.

This isn’t random. In February 2025, he withdrew from the UN Human Rights Council. He quit UNESCO. He’s withdrawing from the World Health Organization. He defunded UNRWA.

The pattern: any organization that could document, report on, or hold the United States accountable for human rights violations is being eliminated. Internal oversight is gone. International oversight is gone. The only remaining accountability mechanism is local resistance.

That’s why Minneapolis matters.

Minneapolis showed what the first stages of governmental resistance look like. The mayor said no. The governor said no.

That’s the framework. Not believing that the system will hold itself accountable.

It won’t.

The system was built specifically so it wouldn’t. That’s the point of militarized enforcement. Unaccountable agents who wear masks and corporate contractors like CoreCivic are managing detention.

So the question is whether you and your community will say no to it.

Whether Minneapolis was the line in the sand or just the first escalation.

Whether we’ll watch these agencies consolidate power, build camps, kill citizens, and do nothing.

Or whether we’ll understand that what happened to Renee Good, sitting in her car, a U.S. citizen, is what happens when we let fear and politics replace law and accountability.

And that it can happen to any of us.

Thanks for reading. This is my most important Substack article. Please share if you can.

Share

What You Can Do

Know what’s happening in your city. Contact your mayor, your governor, and your state legislators. Ask them directly. Make it a public question and force them to answer.

Document everything. If you see ICE operations or witness enforcement actions, record them. Screenshot them. Share them. Know your rights. You have the right to film police and federal agents in public. Know this. These agencies operate in darkness. Darkness protects them. When you film, document, or share, you take away the darkness.

Support organizations fighting this. The ACLU, the National Immigration Law Center, and local immigrant rights organizations like SC United with Immigrants. They’re doing the work on the ground. Support them financially if you can. Amplify their message if you can’t.

Vote. The Trump administration did this. They built the infrastructure. They deployed the agents. They murdered Renee Good, and they cannot remain in power. Even if it seems hopeless, the midterms are in November. Start campaigning in federal, local, and state elections; they are essential.

Understand what you’re looking at. The Occupation isn’t coming. It isn't something you can wish away or pray doesn’t come. It’s here. The camps are open. Trump has deployed the agents. The first citizen has been murdered on video. The only question is what happens next.

And that question doesn’t have a federal answer. It has a local one.

It has a Minneapolis answer, one I share.

To tell ICE “to get the fuck out.”

Rest In Peace, Renee Nicole Good.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Kendrick Lamar and Talking to God: How Much a Dollar Really Cost
seoTitle: Kendrick Lamar and Talking to God: How Much a Dollar Really Cost
excerpt: On the myth of the deserving poor, religion and all the reasons we've invented to turn a blind eye.
metaDescription: On the myth of the deserving poor, religion and all the reasons we've invented to turn a blind eye.
bodyHtml:
I remember my first time listening to Kendrick Lamar's album To Pimp a Butterfly. I was researching some of my favorite artists and came across TPAB.

One song stood out to me then, and it stands out even more now as Trump cuts SNAP and VA benefits and comes for daycares and social programs. The title is How Much a Dollar Costs the eleventh track on the album. James Fauntleroy and Ronald Isley provided vocals. LoveDragon produced the song. It tells the real story of an encounter between KDot and a homeless man when he was working on this album.

The Story

A man stands at a gas station in South Africa. He has money. He has a luxury car. He has made it.

Another man approaches and asks for ten rand—about 0.60 USD.

Kendrick Lamar says no.

The man reveals himself as God. And the cost of that dollar turns out to be Kendrick’s spot in heaven.

Barack Obama called it the best song of that year. Critics have dissected its religious imagery. Fans have debated its theology.

But the twist isn’t the reveal. The twist is what Kendrick already knew before he said no.

He knew he was looking for an excuse. He smelled alcohol on the man’s breath. He assumed crack addiction. He calculated that this person didn’t deserve his help.

The song isn’t about God appearing to Kendrick Lamar. The song is about all the reasons we, as a society, invent to look away.

The Perfect Poor

The “deserving poor” myth is the belief that only certain impoverished people merit help based on their behavior, circumstances, or moral character.

We have a fantasy about who deserves help.

The deserving poor must be humble. Grateful. Sober. Mentally stable. Polite. Not asking for too much. Not asking too often and not asking in a way that makes us uncomfortable. Must be quiet in their asking, like monks.

They must perform their poverty correctly, or we revoke our sympathy.

A 2024 Pew Research survey found that 46% of Republicans believe the government should provide less assistance to people in need. But even among Democrats, who broadly support expanding aid, attitudes shift when the word “welfare” enters the conversation. Research consistently shows that Americans support federal spending to help people experiencing poverty but oppose federal welfare spending. Same population. Same programs. Different word, different response.

The difference is who we picture.

“Welfare” conjures a specific image in the brains of the American public. Study after study documents how media portrayals have associated welfare with Black recipients at rates far higher than reality. Political scientist Martin Gilens at Stanford University documented this in his research: the excessive representation of Black faces in poverty coverage shaped white attitudes about who deserves help and who doesn’t.

The mirage of benevolence

When we, as a society, imagine a single mother who needs food assistance, we’re already running her through a checklist. Does she have too many children? Is she working hard enough? Does she have a smartphone? Is she buying the wrong groceries?

This framework divides people experiencing poverty into those who “deserve” assistance (because they work hard, stay sober, and display proper gratitude) and those who don’t. Research shows this distinction justifies policies that restrict access to benefits rather than addressing poverty itself.

We don’t ask these questions about corporate bailouts or about lobbying. We don’t mean test PPP loans. We don’t drug test farmers who receive agricultural subsidies.

Only the poor must prove their poverty and prove they are without sin when we are the ones casting the stone.

The Gospel According to Kendrick

Kendrick Lamar didn’t invent this parable. He inherited it.

Matthew 25 tells a similar story. Jesus describes the final judgment, in which God separates the righteous from the damned. The criteria isn’t theology. It isn’t church attendance. It’s whether you fed the hungry, clothed the naked, visited the sick, and imprisoned. In verse 40, he says:

“Whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine,” Jesus says, “you did for me.”

The homeless man at the gas station is that verse made flesh.

Kendrick has wrestled with Christianity across his entire career. Good Kid, m.A.A.d. City opens with a prayer of repentance and closes with a baptism. To Pimp a Butterfly examines what salvation means when your community is still suffering and asks whether personal success is worth anything amid systemic racism. DAMN the Pulitzer winner. Explores the tension between faith and fear, titled after the damned, the question of a "damned if I do, damned if I don't" status quo, but the questions remain the same.

What does being saved mean? And what does that salvation require of you?

“How Much a Dollar Cost” sits at the center of this theological arc. The song dramatizes what happens when faith in something intangible meets actual practice. The man doesn’t ask Kendrick to give up everything. He’s asking for less than a dollar. Kendrick refuses.

“I smell grandpa’s old medicine reeking from your skin,” he raps. “Moonshine and gin.”

He compares the man to Shaq in Kazaam, calling his story a mediocre gimmick. He insists that his own selfishness is what got him here, so why should he apologize? Why should he give what he earned?

The homeless man responds with scripture. “Have you ever opened up Exodus 14? A humble man is all that we ever need.”

Kendrick keeps refusing.

The reveal comes in the final verse. The man tells Kendrick that knowing the truth will set him free. He identifies himself as the Messiah. And then the judgment: “I’ll tell you just how much a dollar cost. The price of having a spot in heaven. Embrace your loss. I am God.” His greed has cost him his eternity.

The song doesn’t end with redemption or a revelation. It ends with that line hanging there. No reconciliation. No second chance. Just the verdict and the outro, done by Ronald Isley, which mirror Matthew 27:24, dictating the regrets of Pontius Pilate, the man who sentenced Jesus to death, saying, “I washed my hands, I said my grace, What more do you want from me?”

Seeing Everyone as Worthy of Love

And there lies the more complex theological claim that the song makes.

It’s not “help people who deserve it.”

It’s “everyone deserves it.”

That includes the man who smells like alcohol. That consists of the person you've decided is running a con. The person who might spend your dollar on something you disapprove of, and the ones it is uncomfortable to look at.

The song’s structure forces Kendrick to watch himself fail. He doesn’t get a redemption arc. He doesn’t learn a lesson and then demonstrate growth. He fails the test. The song ends.

The abruptness is what makes it so uncomfortable. We want the narrative where the protagonist realizes his mistake, changes his behavior, and earns his salvation. That’s not what we get.

We get Kendrick looking at a human being in need, running through every justification for why he shouldn’t help, and walking away.

And then finding out who he was really talking to, the “least of [his] brothers.”

The question the song asks is whether we can recognize the divine in people we’ve decided don’t deserve recognition. Not in the abstract, but in the actual moment when someone asks us for something and we start calculating whether they’ve earned it.

Why We Test People

Making people prove their pious poverty is American policy.

As of 2026, 17 states require drug testing for welfare applicants or recipients, often based on reasonable suspicion or prior convictions. Rep. David Rouzer (R-NC) reintroduced the federal Drug Testing for Welfare Recipients Act (H.R. 372) in January 2025, mandating tests for TANF, SNAP, and housing aid. Yet programs cost more than they save; CLASP, a nonprofit policy organization, found Missouri spent $336,000 testing 108 applicants in 2017, with just 11 positives, and found drug use rates below the general population.

In states that implemented drug testing, less than 1% of applicants tested positive. Florida’s program found that welfare recipients used drugs at lower rates than the general population. Courts have repeatedly struck these laws down as unconstitutional. The programs cost more to administer than they save.

But that’s never been the point.

The point is the testing itself. The point is making people prove they’re worthy of something Christianity says is inextricable from being human. The fact is the suspicion.

The same logic applies to disability documentation, asylum claims, and housing assistance applications. Every program that serves the unfortunate comes with hoops to jump through. Missing a deadline, losing paperwork, failing to provide documentation you can’t afford to obtain: any of these can disqualify you from the help you need to live.

The system's design isn’t meant to help people. It’s designed to make them prove they deserve help. In that process, many fail, and ultimately, the system maintains the status quo.

What the Song Asks of Us

“How Much a Dollar Cost” doesn’t ask you to give away everything you have.

It asks a smaller, more direct question.

It asks you to notice when you’re looking for an excuse to turn your eyes blind.

Notice when you smell alcohol and decide that answers everything. Notice when you calculate that someone might mispend your money on pills or alcohol. Notice when you demand that people perform their suffering in ways you find acceptable before you’ll acknowledge it.

The dollar isn’t the point. The looking away is.

Kendrick Lamar told a TMZ reporter that the song’s message is simple: “Help as many people as you can, man, if you want to live forever.”

That’s the whole thing. In essence, the entire song is about the deadly sin of greed and the virtue of generosity. He tells MTV

“These are moments in my life deeper than just handing somebody a dollar. These are actually moments of integrity. Me talking to him was simply a thank you from God. And I felt God speaking through him to get at me.”

Not help people who pass your test. Not help people who prove their worthiness. Not help people who will use your assistance in ways you approve of.

Help as many people as you can.

The song forces us to sit with our own calculations. Every time we've walked past someone asking for help. Every time we've decided, they probably didn’t really need it or deserve to be there. Every time we've been given a reason.

The homeless man doesn't argue with Kendrick. He doesn’t try convince him. He asks for the dollar, receives the rejection, and renders divine judgment.

To Pimp a Butterfly won Best Rap Album that year. Kendrick told the world he failed the test, and we gave him a Grammy for it. That’s what a dollar and his soul costs.

Whether the divine element is literal or metaphorical, Kendrick leaves it open. That's the structure of the test.

Kendrick failed the test. Will you?

Whether you are religious or not, will you choose to see the person in front of you or not?

Share

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Why Dark Superman Is a Symptom of the Male Loneliness Epidemic
seoTitle: Why Dark Superman Is a Symptom of the Male Loneliness Epidemic
excerpt: The connection between gritty superhero reboots, Gamergate, and a generation of men who feel like the world wasn’t built for them even if it was.
metaDescription: The connection between gritty superhero reboots, Gamergate, and a generation of men who feel like the world wasn’t built for them even if it was.
bodyHtml:
When I was 8, I sat down in the theatre and watched as Zack Snyder gave us a Superman who didn’t smile; Henry Cavill’s Man of Steel was brooding, conflicted, and isolated. He snapped General Zod’s neck and helped level Metropolis, barely acknowledging the lives lost. He spent most of the movie looking like he’d rather be anywhere else. I felt the same in the theater. Critics called it “grim and gritty.” Snyder argued it was “relatable.” Deborah Snyder doubled down on Forbes, adding:

“He’s going through real problems that we go through as people every day,” / “To me, that’s not dark, that’s life.”

But here’s the thing: whose life? Whose “real problems”? And why, in 2013, did Hollywood decide that the most powerful being on Earth needed to be sad, alienated, and alone?

The choice had less to do with comics than with the mood of the decade: a generation of young men beginning to feel the same sense of isolation.

The Loneliness Numbers

Let’s start with the data. If Superman is a cultural mirror, this is what it was reflecting.

According to Gallup’s 2023-2024 survey, 25% of American men aged 15-34 say they feel lonely a lot of the previous day, a full seven points above the national average. The American Institute for Boys and Men found that the number of men reporting having no close friends has increased fivefold in the decades since 1990, an explosion in social isolation.

A 2023 study from Equimundo found that two-thirds of men aged 18-23 agreed with the statement: “No one really knows me well.”

In that same study, 44% of men reported suicidal thoughts or ideation in the previous two weeks.

Male loneliness isn’t a gender war problem. It is an everything problem. Young men are more likely to feel stuck—out of school, out of work, and out of answers about what their lives are for. As Richard Reeves, president of the American Institute for Boys and Men, put it: “Many men, and especially younger men, feel disconnected and very often uncertain of their purpose in life.”

But here’s what’s crucial to understand: this loneliness isn’t just something that happened to men. Much of it is trained into boys and then reinforced by other men.

Patriarchy, the system men built and enforced for centuries, taught boys that emotional vulnerability is weakness. That real men don’t cry, that needing others makes you weak. Parents, peers, coaches, and culture taught boys to bottle up their feelings. Boys policed other boys for being “too emotional” or “acting like a girl.” The masculine ideal was stoic, self-sufficient, and independent to the point of isolation.

The data confirms this isn’t abstract theory. A 2018 Plan International study found that 41% of boys say society expects them to be aggressive or violent when they feel angry, while 35% say the status quo is to “suck it up and be a man.”

A systematic review published in the Journal of Men’s Health found that traditional masculinity norms were “consistently linked to a wide range of negative mental health outcomes among men,” with emotional suppression identified as the primary barrier to seeking help.

The consequences are measurable. According to the CDC, women are 1.6 times more likely than men to receive mental health treatment. Nearly 1 in 10 men experience depression or anxiety, but less than half receive treatment.

Men aren’t seeking help because they were taught that seeking help makes them less of a man. Women, by contrast, are more often socialized to build emotional support networks. They were encouraged to share feelings, maintain friendships, and seek help when struggling.

Not always, not for everyone. But as a pattern, men are offered status; women are offered connection. And status without connection turns into a cage.

Now look at Snyder’s Superman again. An alien who can’t connect with humans. A god-like figure whom the world fears and resents. A man with unlimited power who still can’t seem to save himself from loneliness.

The Manosphere Pipeline

Around the same time Snyder was filming Man of Steel, something was brewing in the corners of the internet.

The “manosphere”: a loose network Men’s Rights Activists, pickup artists, incels, and MGTOW, was gaining traction. These groups share a core belief: feminism corrupted society, that men are the actual victims of modern life, and that the only way forward is to “take the red pill.”

The red pill metaphor originates in The Matrix, where the protagonist must choose between a comfortable illusion (blue pill) and a harsh reality (red pill). In manosphere communities, “taking the red pill” means accepting that feminism has rigged the game against men. It entails viewing women as adversaries rather than partners. It means trading hope for grievance.

Here’s the bitter irony: the manosphere diagnoses a real problem, “male loneliness,” and then prescribes the exact thing that caused it, essentially the worst version of “hair of the dog” in the world, a hangover cure that leads to nowhere.

Men are lonely because Patriarchy taught them that vulnerability is weakness. The manosphere says: be even less vulnerable. Double down on emotional walls. Treat relationships as transactions. View women as conquests, not companions. Become so “alpha” that you don’t need anyone.

Researcher Shawn P. Van Valkenburgh’s analysis of these communities found that red pill ideology “superficially resolves a contradiction between hegemonic masculinity’s prescriptive emotional walls and an inherent desire for connection by constructing women as exchangeable commodities.” Translation: lonely men who can’t figure out how to talk to women are being taught that the problem isn’t the fact that the masculine norms that isolated them, but it’s women. It’s feminism. It’s everyone except the system that created the problem in the first place.

The ultimate consequence of this cycle is written in the mortality data. Men die by suicide at four times the rate of women, even though women attempt more. The Pan American Health Organization reports that 1 in 5 men in the Americas won’t live to see their 50th birthday. The report linking many of these early deaths to harmful masculine norms like risk-taking, emotional suppression, and avoiding healthcare.

And the visual language of that exploitation looked a lot like Zack Snyder’s DC Universe: dark, cynical, obsessed with power, and convinced that the world doesn’t deserve its heroes.

Gamergate: The Rehearsal

If the manosphere were an ideology, Gamergate was its cultural hearth.

In 2014, a harassment campaign targeting women in the video game industry, including developer Zoe Quinn and critic Anita Sarkeesian, exploded across social media under the guise of “ethics in games journalism.” What it actually was: a coordinated attack on women who dared to critique the culture that young men had claimed as their own.

Researcher Adrienne Massanari documented how platforms like Reddit enabled this “toxic technoculture,” where misogynistic language and harassment became normalized.

As Karen Lee Ashcraft wrote in Logos Journal, Gamergate “refined the makeover of misogyny, from ‘crusty old guy’ to ‘edgy anarchist’ .” It recruited young men who weren’t interested in politics but were gripped by gaming and shaped them into what can only be described as footsoldiers.

While the men’s rights movement didn’t create Gamergate, they appropriated it. The through line: lonely, alienated young men, online all day, convinced that they were the real victims, looking for enemies to blame.

The Snyder Cult*

Which brings us back to Superman.

After Batman v Superman underperformed and Justice League was taken away from him, Zack Snyder’s fans launched a years-long campaign to #ReleaseTheSnyderCut.

And in 2021, they won.

Warner Bros. spent $70 million to finish and release Snyder’s four-hour version on HBO Max.

But the victory came with a cost. The Snyder Cut movement was plagued by the same tactics that defined Gamergate: coordinated harassment, death threats against executives and journalists, and an online culture so toxic that WarnerMedia CEO Ann Sarnoff had to publicly condemn it.

WarnerMedia-commissioned reports later found that a meaningful share of the campaign came from inauthentic accounts.

The parallels to Gamergate weren’t coincidental. As one critic wrote, the Snyder Cut movement had “far more in common with abusive right-wing campaigns like Gamergate than with most of mainstream geek culture.”

What did these fans want?

Proof that their dark, cynical worldview was correct. Confirmation that the brooding, alienated Superman they identified with was the real one

What Superman Was Supposed to Be

Here’s what’s easy to forget: Superman wasn’t created to be dark.

The “grim and gritty” turn came in the 1980s, when Frank Miller’s The Dark Knight Returns reimagined Batman as a brutal vigilante, and Alan Moore’s Watchmen deconstructed the entire superhero genre. These were brilliant works. They were also explicitly meant as critiques, not templates.

Snyder’s Superman was all deconstruction. A symbol of hope that inspired no hope. A savior who couldn’t save himself from his own alienation. A hero stripped of the very thing that made him heroic in the first place.

Kindness.

The Course Correction

On July 11, 2025, James Gunn released a new Superman movie. It was bright. It was colorful. Superman smiled and wore tights. He saved squirrels. He told jokes.

Critics called it exactly what audiences needed.

“We’re talking about how this character of Superman fits into our cultural zeitgeist now, when I would argue, there is more of a craving for a ‘just strictly good’ character without ulterior motives, without a darker backstory,” Northeastern University professor Steve Granelli told reporters.

You can see the divide in comment sections: “Snyder fans want dark, gritty, Randian power fantasy. Gunn fans want a fun, hopeful, humanistic story.”

The Randian power fantasy: the exceptional man above the crowd, owing nothing, softened by nothing.

Snyder’s Superman fit that mold. He was a god among insects, tortured by his own superiority, surrounded by humans who didn’t deserve him.

Gunn’s Superman is something else. He’s powerful, yes.

But his power comes from choosing kindness when cynicism would be easier. He’s not a Randian superman. He’s just a good person trying to help.

Why Corenswet Gets It Right

David Corenswet’s Superman isn’t just a course correction. It’s a return to origins.

In the film’s climactic confrontation with Lex Luthor, Corenswet delivers a line impossible in the Snyderverse: “I’m as human as anyone. I love, I get scared. I wake up every morning… and I try to make the best choices I can.”

His greatest strength. Not the heat vision. Not the flight. Not the invulnerability. His humanity.

That’s the Superman Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster created in 1938. Two Jewish kids from Cleveland, sons of immigrants who fled pogroms in Eastern Europe, who made a hero out of their own wish fulfillment.

In his unpublished memoir, Siegel wrote that Samson, the biblical judge with superhuman strength, and the Golem of Prague, a legendary creature created to defend Jews from persecution, were the inspirations for the Big Blue Boy Scout. Superman wasn’t just a strongman. He was a champion of the oppressed.

As Jules Feiffer wrote in the New York Times: “It wasn’t Krypton that Superman came from; it was the planet Minsk or Lodz or Vilna or Warsaw.”

Snyder’s Superman lost this. His Kal-El was a god wrestling with whether humans deserved his protection. The immigrant narrative was replaced by messianic imagery: Superman as a Christ figure, dying and resurrecting, floating with his arms outstretched in cruciform poses.

Corenswet’s Superman gets it back. James Gunn told The Times of London:

“Superman is the story of America. An immigrant that came from other places and populated the country, but for me it is mostly a story that says basic human kindness is a value and is something we have lost.”

This isn’t just theological hair-splitting. It’s the difference between a hero you worship and a hero you can become.

Snyder’s Superman was aspirational in the way the manosphere is aspirational—you could never be him, but you could fantasize about having his power. Corenswet’s Superman is aspirational in a different way. He’s a model of positive masculinity: strong but gentle, larger than life but kind, capable of emotion, willing to save a squirrel because every life matters.

And that’s the whole point. Superman was never supposed to be a power fantasy for lonely men who feel the world owes them something. He was supposed to be proof that the powerless can become powerful.

Share

The Real Question

Zack Snyder obviously didn’t cause male loneliness, and watching Man of Steel doesn’t turn you into an incel, or you wouldn’t be reading this. Culture doesn’t work like that. But culture does reflect how you or the society around you feels. When a generation of young men feels lonely, disconnected, and unsure of their place, the stories they consume matter.

The manosphere gives you one answer. It tells you the world is rigged, women are the problem, and power is all that matters. The Dark Superman aesthetic agrees: even gods stand alone, and hope appears childish. Both messages lie to you. They come from the same system that taught people to treat emotion as weakness, then acted shocked when men ended up isolated. But that’s not who Superman was made to be or what he should be.

Superman was made to be the voice of those who could not always speak for themselves. He cares. He fails. He saves squirrels because every life matters. His real strength isn’t force. It’s humanity. It's my dad, my mom, my brother, my best friends, and my neighbor.

That’s what strength was supposed to look like. Being Human.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Why You Can’t Have Nice Things: Super PACs, Lobbying, and the Death of American Democracy
seoTitle: Why You Can’t Have Nice Things: Super PACs, Lobbying, and the Death of American Democracy
excerpt: Your Vote Counts. Their Money Counts More.
metaDescription: Your Vote Counts. Their Money Counts More.
bodyHtml:
In 2014, two political scientists at Princeton and Northwestern asked a question that should have been asked decades earlier: Does the government represent the people?

Martin Gilens and Benjamin Page looked at more than 20 years of data. Nearly 1,800 policy issues. They compared what Americans wanted to what the government did.

Their conclusion:

“The preferences of the average American appear to have only a minuscule, near-zero, statistically non-significant impact upon public policy.”

It doesn't matter what Americans want. Whether 20% or 80% of the public wants something, the probability of Congress passing it remains approximately 30%. Your opinion, statistically speaking, makes no difference.

But when economic elites want something? When do business interest groups push for a policy? Those preferences strongly predict whether laws pass.

The Before Times

Here’s what you need to understand: it wasn’t always this bad.

From the New Deal through the mid-1970s, American democracy was actually moving in the right direction. Not perfect. Never perfect. But moving.

The minimum wage hit its peak purchasing power in 1968. Union membership meant a factory worker could buy a house, send kids to college, take a vacation. Social Security (1935); the Clean Air Act(1963), Civil Rights Act (1964); Medicare and Medicaid (1965); Voting Rights Act(1965); the EPA(1970); and OSHA(1971). For forty years, when Americans organized and demanded change, they sometimes got it.

Then came the counterrevolution.

Post-Watergate reforms tried to limit money in politics. The Federal Election Campaign Act put caps on contributions. The idea was simple: if a candidate needed a million small donors instead of ten big ones, they’d have to listen to a million small voices.

Buckley v. Valeo in 1976 started chipping away. The Supreme Court ruled that spending money is speech. Limits on independent expenditures violate the First Amendment. The door cracked open.

It took 34 more years to blow it off the hinges.

January 21, 2010

Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission is the original sin of modern American corruption and the moment the rot became law.

A conservative nonprofit wanted to air a documentary titled “Hillary: The Movie” attacking Hillary Clinton before the 2008 primary. Campaign finance law said no. The case went to the Supreme Court. Five justices decided this was their moment to rewrite the rules.

The decision could have been narrow. It wasn’t. Justice Anthony Kennedy, writing for the majority, declared that corporations have First Amendment rights. That unlimited political spending can’t be considered corruption. The government has no business limiting how much money flows into elections.

“The fact that a corporation, or any other speaker, is willing to spend money to try to persuade voters presupposes that the people have the ultimate influence over elected officials,” Kennedy wrote.

He was wrong. The Princeton study would prove it four years later.

But the genie was out of the bottle, and it only granted wishes to corporations.

Two months after Citizens United, a federal appeals court applied the same logic in SpeechNow.org v. FEC. The ruling created Super PACs, groups that can raise and spend unlimited money as long as they don’t “coordinate” with candidates. Which they do. Constantly. With no enforcement.

The late Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg called Citizens United the worst ruling of her time on the Court. Twenty-two states and hundreds of cities have voted to support a constitutional amendment to overturn it.

The ruling stands.

$15 Billion

The 2024 election cost nearly $15 billion, adjusted for inflation. That’s the official number from OpenSecrets, the nonpartisan group that tracks political money.

Super PACs raised over $5 billion. Dark money, which means contributions where the source is secret, hit a record $1.9 billion in federal races alone.

One dark-money group, Future Forward USA Action, spent $304 million.

One in every six dark-money dollars in the election came from this single organization.

The scale matters. As Daniel Weiner, director of the Brennan Center’s elections program

The scale matters. As Daniel Weiner, director of the Brennan Center's elections program “There is a big difference between $16 billion coming from millions of Americans in small increments versus just a significant portion of it coming from a handful of billionaires.”

The Brennan Center found that in the 2022 midterms, just 21 of the biggest donor families contributed $783 million. Billionaires provided 15% of all federal election financing. They outspent the combined total of millions of small donors giving to House and Senate candidates.

Since 2010, outside groups have spent billions on federal elections. In 126 congressional races, outside spending has actually surpassed candidate spending. Super PACs now wage their own ad wars, funded by people you’ve never heard of, pushing candidates who will do what they’re told.

The Villains Roster

So where does the money go? And what does it buy? Let's look at the receipt and introduce you to the industries buying your government.

Pharmaceuticals

The pharmaceutical industry spent $294 million on lobbying in 2024. That’s up from $100 million in 2000. The industry has spent more on lobbying than any other sector for decades, averaging $379 million per year in recent years.

Americans pay nearly triple what other wealthy nations pay for the same drugs. When Medicare was finally allowed to negotiate drug prices, the pharmaceutical industry had spent decades and billions killing the proposal. When it finally passed, it was watered down by the same members of Congress who'd taken pharma money.

You want cheaper medicine. Pharma wants profits. Pharma wins.

Fossil Fuels

Oil and gas companies spent $219 million influencing the 2024 election. According to OpenSecrets, 88% went to Republicans. More than 70% has gone to Republicans in every election cycle since 1996. This cemented climate denial as Republican orthodoxy.

Donald Trump received nearly $23 million from the industry. When he asked oil executives to raise $1 billion for his campaign, he told them it would be a “deal” given the taxes and regulations they’d avoid.

But its not only presidential elections in 2024, Koch Industries spent $11 million on lobbying. Chevron spent $9 million. Exxon spent $6.8 million. That’s just the direct lobbying. Add the Super PAC spending, the dark money, the astroturf organizations, and you’re looking at hundreds of millions flowing into climate denial while the planet burns.

Guns

About 90% of Americans support universal background checks, including 83% of all gun owners and 72% of the NRA members.

The policy has overwhelming bipartisan support from voters.

It never passes.

The gun lobby spent $89 million on federal candidates, parties, and outside spending groups from 1989 through 2024. The NRA alone spent $54 million on independent expenditures in the 2016 cycle.

They’ve lobbied against background checks, assault weapons bans, and red flag laws. They’ve pushed for the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act, which shields gun manufacturers from lawsuits. They funded the Dickey Amendment, which for years blocked federal research on gun violence.

Four months after twenty children were murdered at Sandy Hook, the background check amendment came to a vote. Polls showed 90% support. It failed by six votes. Most of the senators who voted against it had received contributions from the gun lobby.

AIPAC

The American Israel Public Affairs Committee spent more than $100 million on the 2024 election cycle. Their Super PAC, United Democracy Project, paid $30 million specifically to defeat two members of Congress: Jamaal Bowman and Cori Bush.

Both lost their primaries, which became two of the most expensive House Democratic primaries in history.

Nine of the top ten funders to AIPAC have regularly given to Republican candidates and causes. Republican megadonors are spending millions in Democratic primaries to choose which Democrats get elected.

AIPAC endorsed 109 congressional Republicans who voted to overturn the 2020 election, including 37 Republicans who refused to affirm Joe Biden’s election on Jan. 6, 2021.

A recent Gallup poll found 60% of U.S. adults disapprove of Israel’s military actions in Gaza. Among Democrats, 92% disapprove. Yet when the Democratic National Committee considered a resolution calling for recognizing Palestinian statehood and halting arms shipments, it was blocked. The mechanism is simple:

AIPAC funds primaries against dissenters. Politicians learn that criticizing Israel means facing a multimillion-dollar campaign in their next election. So they don’t criticize Israel. The money continues to flow to actions that most Americans oppose.

Auto Industry

But corporate capture of American democracy didn't start with Citizens United. The template was set decades earlier.

In 1949, General Motors and several related companies were convicted of conspiring to monopolize the sale of buses and supplies to National City Lines. Between 1936 and 1950, NCL, with investment from GM, Firestone, Standard Oil, Phillips Petroleum, and Mack Trucks, acquired streetcar systems in about 45 cities.

What happened to those streetcar systems? They were dismantled and replaced with buses. At the time, 90% of all trips in America were by rail. There were 1,200 separate electric street and interurban railways, with 44,000 miles of track.

The penalty for destroying American public transportation? General Motors was fined $5,000.

The company's treasurer was fined one dollar.

Three years later, GM’s president became Secretary of Defense under Eisenhower and advocated for a nationwide freeway-building program. The head of federal highways was a DuPont, whose family owned the largest share of GM. The 1956 Federal-Aid Highway Act authorized the construction of 41,000 miles of highways funded by the gas tax. More highways meant more driving, meant more gas taxes, meant more highways.

This is how you end up in a country where a car is required to participate in the economy. The auto and oil industries shaped the physical infrastructure of American life to ensure permanent dependence on their products.

What Americans Want But Can’t Have

Let’s make a list. Policies that have majority support, often overwhelming support, that Congress refuses to pass:

Universal background checks for gun purchases. 90%+ support for decades. Never happens.

Higher minimum wage. The federal minimum wage has been $7.25 since 2009. Most Americans support raising it. Nothing moves.

Drug price negotiation. Americans have wanted Medicare to negotiate drug prices for twenty years. Congress passed a gutted version in 2022, after pharmaceutical companies spent hundreds of millions fighting it.

Climate action, Getting money out of politics, Tax increases on the wealthy. All Failed

The pattern is consistent: when average Americans want something that wealthy donors don’t like, average Americans lose. When wealthy donors wish to do something that average Americans don’t like, wealthy donors win.

It’s the documented, peer-reviewed finding of political science research. Gilens and Page:

“Economic elites and organized groups representing business interests have substantial independent impacts on U.S. government policy, while mass-based interest groups and average citizens have little or no independent influence.”

The System is Working as Designed

Here’s the thing about Citizens United: it didn’t break democracy. It formalized a transfer of power that was already underway.

The 2024 election was the most expensive in history. But elections have been getting more expensive every cycle since we started tracking them. The average cost of a winning House campaign has risen from under $1 million in 1990 to over $2 million today. Senate races cost tens of millions. Presidential campaigns cost billions.

When campaigning costs that much, candidates spend most of their time fundraising rather than researching policy or meeting with constituents. The average member of Congress spends 30 hours a week on call time, dialing donors. The people they talk to, the people whose calls they return, the people they see at fundraisers, are not their constituents. They’re the donor class.

You can’t spend 30 hours a week talking to millionaires and billionaires without absorbing their worldview. You can’t depend on their money for your career without internalizing their priorities.

Citizens United didn't create this dynamic. It just floored the gas and cut the brakes. It legalized what had previously been done through workarounds and loopholes.

The Supreme Court said unlimited spending doesn’t corrupt. The data says it does. The Supreme Court noted transparency would protect the public. Dark money has exploded. The Supreme Court held that independent expenditures would remain independent.

Super PACs now function as extensions of campaigns, see SpaceX, which was Trump’s biggest donor and has received over $1 billion annually in government contracts, loans, subsidies, and tax credits since 2016.

Share

What You Can Do

In all of my writings there is going to be a what you can do section but I’m not going to pretend there’s an easy fix. There isn’t.

Overturning Citizens United requires either a Supreme Court reversal or a constitutional amendment. The Court won't reverse itself - the conservative supermajority made this bed. A constitutional amendment requires two-thirds of Congress. That won't happen while the people who benefit from the system control the system. More than half of former members of Congress now work as lobbyists. The job in Congress is the audition; K Street is the career.

But that doesn’t mean you’re powerless.

Support state and local reform. Some states have public financing systems that work. Some cities have enacted transparency requirements. Change often starts locally.

Vote in primaries. Low-turnout primaries are where money exerts the most significant influence. A relatively small number of engaged voters can overcome a spending advantage.

Pay attention to who funds what. When you see a political ad, look up who paid for it. OpenSecrets makes this easy. Know whose interests you’re being asked to serve.

Support organizations fighting for reform. Common Cause, End Citizens United, RepresentUs, and the Brennan Center. These groups are doing the work.

And stop believing the narrative that your voice doesn’t matter because there’s nothing to be done. They want you to believe that. Your disengagement is their victory.

The Gilens and Page study found that average citizens have “near-zero” influence on policy. But that’s not because citizens are inherently powerless. It’s because the system has been rigged to minimize citizen power.

The good news is rigged systems can be un-rigged. It's happened before. The New Deal. The civil rights movement. The labor movement of the 1930s. None of those changes came from powerful interests deciding to be nice. They came from ordinary people organizing, voting, protesting, and refusing to accept that the system couldn't change

You can't buy the government. But you can fire the people who sold it.

If enough people do that, the math changes.

author: Joshua Smith

title: Book Review : Ways To Live Forever.
seoTitle: Book Review : Ways To Live Forever.
excerpt: On grief, lists, and the need to leave proof you were here
metaDescription: On grief, lists, and the need to leave proof you were here
bodyHtml:
I picked up Ways to Live Forever by Sally Nicholls in November. Free from the library. I wasn’t looking for anything in particular. Just browsing the shelves the way you do when you don’t want to go home yet, or in my case, on the walk back from classes.

The book follows an eleven-year-old boy named Sam, who has leukemia. He knows he’s dying. So he makes lists. Questions he wants answered. Things he wants to do before the end. Facts about death and dying. He writes it in a journal because he wants proof he existed.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

It’s a children’s book. 200-something pages that carry on long after the hours it takes to read.

Nicholls doesn’t make dying poetic. Sam isn’t wise beyond his years in that annoying way fictional sick kids often are; he doesn't give advice. He’s frustrated. He’s scared. He wants to break a world record, see a ghost, and figure out why people say “I’m sorry” when someone dies. He’s still a kid, even when he’s running out of time.

One thing that stuck with me is that his “bucket list” isn’t cinematic. It’s kid-stupid. It takes the Stand By Me approach but flips it on its head, showing someone dealing with adult challenges facing playground dares. Sam dares himself to do things like run up a down escalator, like the universe is a video game and he’s trying to beat it on a technicality. And it’s funny, until it isn’t, where he becomes tired, because you realize that’s the point. When you don’t get control over the big thing, you start taking it everywhere you can: one escalator, one rule broken, one little victory that proves your body still belongs to you, and illness is not everything you are.

I was at the tail end of 10 months of grieving when I found this book. I won’t get into the details. But I was stuck in that phase where I wasn’t crying anymore, yet everything still felt too heavy to name. Reading about an eleven-year-old making sense of mortality through lists and facts and small rebellions like sneaking out and climbing a tree so he could go to the moon helped me more than anything written for adults.

Children’s books about death often don’t try to explain it. They sit with it; there is no answer, nor any explanation, at that age.

Sam asks the question I couldn’t: “Is it better to live a short life that’s full of things you like doing, or a long life spent being sensible?”

I don’t know the answer. Neither does he. But asking it out loud felt like enough.

If you’re carrying something heavy right now, find this book at your local library or watch the Ways to Live Forever (2010), one of the only movie adaptations I really love.

Share

If you really love my writing, please share and comment, and, most importantly, please share or donate to my GoFundMe. I'm running 2 miles every day in January for the American Cancer Society. Anything helps.
