title: The Asterisk People: How Variety Covered Sinners vs. Marty Supreme and What That Tells Us About Black Art in America
seoTitle: The Asterisk People: How Variety Covered Sinners vs. Marty Supreme and What That Tells Us About Black Art in America
excerpt: The same publication that questioned a $368 million hit celebrated a $45 million one. The difference? You already know.
metaDescription: The same publication that questioned a $368 million hit celebrated a $45 million one. The difference? You already know.
bodyHtml:
On April 20, 2025, Variety posted its box office summary for Ryan Coogler’s Sinners. The film had just opened to $63 million globally. It beat A Minecraft Movie for the number one spot. It was the biggest opening for an original film since Jordan Peele’s Us in 2019.

Here’s what Variety wrote

Variety@Variety
"Sinners" has amassed $61 million in its global debut. It's a great result for an original, R-rated horror film, yet the Warner Bros. release has a $90 million price tag before global marketing expenses, so profitability remains a ways away. tinyurl.com/5crb5f83
5:25 PM · Apr 20, 2025 · 37.5M Views
3.73K Replies · 538 Reposts · 6.82K Likes

Ben Stiller quote-tweeted it: “In what universe does a 60 million dollar opening for an original studio movie warrant this headline?”

Patrick Schwarzenegger responded: “It’s opening weekend.”

Luke Cage showrunner Cheo Hodari Coker put it more directly: “Basically… ‘Uh uh, n---s. Not yet.’ Which is why we need to see it again.”

Coker, a veteran Black showrunner, was naming what Variety's headline implied: the goalposts move when Black filmmakers win.

Sinners went on to gross $368 million worldwide. It became the first original film to cross $200 million domestically since Coco(a project backed by Disney) in 2017. It earned a 97% on Rotten Tomatoes. It was the only horror film in 35 years to receive an “A” CinemaScore.

Eight Months Later

When $27 million gets better press than $63 million

Fast-forward to December: Timothée Chalamet’s Marty Supreme opened on Christmas Day. It made $27 million over the four-day holiday weekend. That’s solid: A24’s second-biggest opening ever.

Here’s how Variety covered the opening

The headline: "'Marty Supreme’ Cements Timothée Chalamet as Christmas Box Office King.”

No asterisks. No questions about profitability. No hedging about whether a $70 million A24 film (their most expensive ever) would break even—just a celebration.

The tweet is

Sinners made $368 million. Variety questioned whether it would be profitable.

Marty Supreme has made $45 million so far. Variety crowned Chalamet a king.

These framings don't just shape public perception; they determine which directors get final cut, which films get greenlit, and which stories Hollywood decides are worth telling.

I’m not saying Marty Supreme is a failure. It’s not. Chalamet is a movie star. I love his work in Dune, Bones and All, and Call Me By Your Name; he is one of the greats, arguably one of the only movie stars of this era. Josh Safdie made a film people want to see. The marketing was clever. All of that is true.

But all of it had little to do with the movie's foundation, which followed what I like to call “The Safdie Formula.”

The Safdie Formula

The Safdie Formula is simple: a morally gray New York hustler sprints through nonstop crises while electronic music blares and the camera refuses to calm down. Good Time, Uncut Gems, and Marty Supreme swap actors and hustles but keep the same scaffolding. Written with the same co-writer (Ronald Bronstein), scored by the same sound designer (Daniel Lopatin), shot in the same anxious, handheld style, and paced as if the film itself is late to the box office.

At some point, Safdie's movies feel less like films and more like blood pressure tests with nice music. How many times have both you and the character risked a heart attack?

Each film follows the same template: take a real-life hustler, loosen the facts, and film their downfall in the same anxious style.

One critic wrote that Marty Supreme is “basically Uncut Gems again, to the point where I’m convinced that Marty must be related to Adam Sandler’s character.”

Another called it a “greatest hits” of Safdie’s previous work.

I’m not saying a formula is bad. Martin Scorsese has explored variations on the same themes for fifty years and is a master at it, with his films winning 20 Oscars on 101 nominations. Safdie found something that works, and he is good at the archetype. Good for him.

But here’s what I’m pointing out: critics notice when Josh Safdie repeats himself. They call it a formula. They write about how his “shoe prints are everywhere.”

When Ryan Coogler makes something new, he gets questioned about profitability before his film has finished its opening weekend.

Coogler made Fruitvale Station. A biographical drama about Oscar Grant. Then Creed. A boxing movie that reinvented Rocky for a new generation. Then Black Panther. A superhero film that became a cultural phenomenon, earning over $1.3 billion globally. Then Black Panther: Wakanda Forever. A sequel that dealt with grief and loss. Then Sinners. An original horror film about vampires, blues music, and the forces that have drained Black communities for generations.

Five films. Five different genres. Five distinct visions.

Safdie made three films about charismatic New York hustlers who talk fast, make bad decisions, and run from the consequences.

Which filmmaker is taking more risks? And how that worked out for Coogler.

The American Prospect ran a piece in December called “The Hit Hollywood Didn’t Want.”

The argument: Sinners threatened too many industry narratives at once. It proved that original films can still succeed. It proved R-rated horror can be prestige. It proved that a Black filmmaker can negotiate ownership of his own work. It proved that audiences will show up for something that isn’t a sequel, a remake, or a video game adaptation,

All of those things are inconvenient for studios that have spent a decade telling us audiences only want already established IP, only to face a year of groundbreaking original movies. “One Battle After Another”, “Weapons”, “Sorry, Baby”, and yes, “Sinners”, all of which are in Oscar Conversation.

So the coverage hedged. The asterisks appeared. The questions about profitability started before the film had finished its first weekend.

Meanwhile, Marty Supreme gets coverage about Chalamet’s marketing genius. Standing ontop of a blimp gets articles about his stardom and “reign” over Christmas.

Both framings shape how audiences perceive these films. When you tell people a movie is struggling, they believe it. When you tell people a star is ascending, they think that too, and that informs decisions on whether to see a bad movie if you can only afford one.

What Sinners Actually Did

Let me tell you what Ryan Coogler accomplished with Sinners because the trade coverage doesn’t.

He made an original film that was not adapted from a book or play and was not a sequel or remake. A period horror film set in 1933 Mississippi, about twin brothers who open a juke joint and, because of the power of music, are plagued by vampires.

That premise should not work. A $90 million R-rated horror movie set during Jim Crow with no pre-existing audience? Studio executives at WB should have laughed Coogler out of the room.

Instead, Sinners became a cultural event enough to warrant three separate release runs.

He first screened it in Clarksdale, Mississippi, where the story is set, and then it opened to $48 million domestically, $63 million globally. It held strong through May and June. It crossed $200 million domestic in its fourth weekend, something no original film had done in eight years. It earned $368 million worldwide on a $90 million production budget.

Michael B. Jordan played twin brothers. Both of them. He acts against himself throughout the film, delivering two distinct performances and consulting real-life twins before delivering two of his best.

Ludwig Göransson, a longtime friend and collaborator of Coogler and a two-time Oscar-winning composer, produced a score that David Ehrlich at IndieWire called “inextricable from the texture of the film.” He was right from the moment Miles Canton’s Sammy steps on screen with a broken guitar to the ending score. The last time I saw the sun is the film’s heartbeat. Blues, gospel, and Delta South sounds that were made to feel both modern and fitting in their time. Göransson has worked with Coogler since they were in USC film school together, and their connection shines in this work.

Coogler shot the film on 70mm IMAX, being the first film to combine 70mm IMAX and Ultra Panavision 70. That format choice was a statement: Black life in Jim Crow Mississippi deserved the same cinematic grandeur Nolan gives to space travel, and Tarantino gives to revenge fantasies.

He hired everyone from Hoodoo Consultants, to Railroad, to Gambling consultants to make sure this film was as accurate a period piece as possible.

Warner Bros. offered Coogler a deal under which the rights would revert to him after 25 years. Anonymous studio executives told Vulture this was “dangerous” and could “topple the studio system.”

A Black filmmaker securing ownership of his own work is described as dangerous. Think about that.

Coogler told IndieWire that his ownership deal mattered because of the film’s subject matter—a story of Black sharecroppers developing Black art and culture. The irony of making that film and not owning it would have been obscene.

Share

IP While Black

There is a simple test: did they cover Once Upon a Time in Hollywood the same way?

ContrabandCamp
With ‘Sinners,’ White Hollywood Is Moving Goalposts on Black Cinema Again
This Easter weekend, Ryan Coogler’s Black vampire epic Sinners rose past projected box office placement and earnings to open at No. 1 with $48 million domestically and $63 million worldwide in ticket sales. While this is the biggest opening weekend for an original film since …
Read more
a year ago · 354 likes · 53 comments · Brooke Obie

Same 25-year reversion deal. Same studio (different era).

The coverage was celebratory. No asterisks. No concerns about profitability after three days. No anonymous executives calling Tarantino’s deal “dangerous,” and earned about the same amount as sinners at $377 million worldwide.

Brooke Obie wrote in the newsletter Contraband Camp: “White media has always conspired to spin the narrative and move the goalposts about Black films, Black filmmakers and their successes.”

The message is clear: stay in your lane, or expect scrutiny.

What Sinners Is Actually About

Sinners is a vampire movie where the vampires are white. That’s not subtext. That’s the text. Coogler uses horror to talk about extraction, subjection, and forced assimilation— a question about who takes from Black communities, how they do it, and how long they’ve been doing it.

These vampires don’t just want blood. They want culture. Music. Bodies. History. A connection with the ancestors. When the Irish vampire dances or praises the blues, it’s clear he wants to own, not share, the gift of the connection.

That’s what great horror does. It puts history in your body instead of explaining it to your head.

Michael B. Jordan plays brothers, the “Smoke Stack Twins,” representing balance and chaos respectively, who left Mississippi and came back changed, one haunted by loss, the other by love.

The cast is flawless. The music feels excavated from 1933 and somehow futuristic at the same time. Coogler balances horror, history, music, and character for over two hours without losing momentum, creating a never-before-seen format.

That's filmmaking at the highest level. That's the film Variety questioned

@screenoffscriptFrom Super 8 mm to IMAX, director Ryan Coogler breaks down different film formats ahead of the release of #SinnersMovie (via Kodak) #RyanCoogler #BlackPanther #Creed #IMAX #Filmmaking
Tiktok failed to load.

Enable 3rd party cookies or use another browser

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

The Oscar Question

As I write this, Sinners is expected to lead Oscar nominations. It could get 14 nods across categories. Best Picture, Director, Actor, Supporting Actress, Original Screenplay, Cinematography, Score, Song, Costume, Production Design, Editing, Sound, Casting.

Ryan Coogler has never won a competitive Oscar despite a Best Picture nomination for Judas and the Black Messiah. Neither has Michael B. Jordan.

No Black director has won Best Director in the Academy’s 98-year history

.

The specific absence of Black winners in that category is a choice the Academy has made for nearly a century.

Coogler could be the one to break that streak. Sinners is the film that deserves it. And the Oscar story for Sinners is the same as the box office story: an achievement that keeps getting asterisked.

But if history is any guide, the Academy will find a way to give him everything except the win, amounting to nominations without the crown as they did with Spike Lee.

Meanwhile, Chalamet is the frontrunner for Best Actor for playing another Safdie hustler—the role Adam Sandler wasn't nominated for in Uncut Gems.

Is Chalamet more talented than Sandler? Maybe. Is Marty Supreme better than Uncut Gems? They’re basically the same movie.

The difference is that Chalamet is Chalamet. He’s a star. He plays the game. He stands on top of the Sphere and in the blimps.

He gives interviews about craft and talks about his collaboration with Michael B. Jordan since Fruitvale Station, which helped make them a modern-day Spike Lee and Denzel Washington.

Which approach gets rewarded? Who gets the crown?

Follow the Pattern

I keep coming back to the Variety coverage because it crystallizes something.

When Sinners opened, Variety’s headline emphasized the obstacles, and it wasn't the only one. When Marty Supreme opened, Variety’s headline emphasized the triumph with the marketing team.

One film has made eight times as much as the other has so far. The coverage inverts that reality.

This article isn’t about Variety specifically. The New York Times did the same thing. So did Business Insider. The pattern is industry-wide.

Share

What You Can Do

See Sinners if you haven’t. It’s one of the best films of 2025 and in my opinion the best film. It’s available to stream now on Hulu, Amazon, and HBO Max, but if it’s still playing near you, see it on the biggest screen possible. Coogler shot it in IMAX to showcase the movie's beauty and the beauty of the black experience. The experience matters.

Pay attention to how films are covered. When you see a box office piece that hedges on success, ask yourself: Would they write this about Tarantino? Would they write this about Nolan?

Support Black filmmakers who take risks. Coogler made something nobody else could have made. Jordan gave a performance nobody else could have given. That's what deserves celebration.

And next time someone tells you an original film can’t succeed, remember: Sinners made $368 million. The problem was never the audience.

The problem was who was counting.

If this or any of my other work landed for you, please consider going paid. Real talk, a lot of research, writing, and thinking go into these, even if they seem to be put out quickly. Your support means I can keep doing this work seriously. And if you’re already paying, thank you so so much, it means a lot.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: Mohammed Bati: The NCAA and the Commodification of Black bodies
seoTitle: Mohammed Bati: The NCAA and the Commodification of Black bodies
excerpt: How Mohammed Bati’s $6,000 tuition fund exposed the lie of “student-athlete”
metaDescription: How Mohammed Bati’s $6,000 tuition fund exposed the lie of “student-athlete”
bodyHtml:
As you are this I have either ran or are getting ready to run two miles every day for the rest of January for American Cancer Society if you can please donate.

On Christmas Eve 2025, Mohammed “Mo” Bati posted a message on Strava announcing the end of his collegiate career. The NCAA had ruled him ineligible. His violation: accepting $6,000 from his community to pay tuition.

I’ll come back to Bati. But first, I need to talk about the system that punished him, because his case only makes sense once you understand what college athletics was built to do.

Forty Million Dollar Slaves

In 1997, New York Knicks forward Larry Johnson got into a dispute with the NBA over talking to reporters. Frustrated, he told the press that he and some teammates were “rebel slaves.”

The next season, during a game in Los Angeles, a fan yelled at him from the stands: “Johnson, you’re nothing but a $40 million slave.”

New York Times columnist William Rhoden heard about that moment and spent years turning it into a book. “Forty Million Dollar Slaves: The Rise, Fall, and Redemption of the Black Athlete” argues that Black athletes’ journey through American sports has been a migration from literal plantations to figurative ones. The shackles are invisible now. The power relationship hasn’t changed.

Rhoden traces how sports were introduced on plantations as diversions to prevent revolutionary stirrings. He documents how Black jockeys dominated horse racing until the Jockey Club stopped licensing them, and how they won 15 of the first 28 Kentucky Derbies. He shows how integration enriched white institutions while destroying Black ones.

The conveyor belt, Rhoden calls it. A system that pulls Black kids from poor neighborhoods into big-time programs, cuts them off from their roots, extracts their labor, and discards them when they’re used up.

In today's world, college sports sit at the center of this belt. And the economics tell the story plainly.

The Business of Black Bodies

Black athletes make up nearly 60% of football and basketball rosters at Power Five schools. Those two sports produce almost all the money. At major programs, football and men’s basketball account for 90% of team-specific revenue. The television deals, the bowl games, the March Madness brackets, the jersey sales. Black labor makes the machine run.

A 2020 National Bureau of Economic Research study tracked where the money goes. Football and basketball players come from school districts with median family incomes around $58,000. Athletes in non-revenue sports such as golf, tennis, and lacrosse come from districts with average incomes of $80,000. Black players make up half of revenue-sport rosters but only 11% of non-revenue sports.

In essence, predominantly Black athletes from lower-income backgrounds generate billions. That money subsidizes predominantly white athletes from wealthier families playing sports that lose money. Less than 7% of football and basketball revenue returns to the players who create it.

But what happens to the players who produce this? Until the House v. NCAA settlement last year, they got scholarships and meal plans. Now Division I athletes can receive up to $20.5 million per school in revenue sharing. Progress, finally.

But Mohammed Bati doesn’t play Division I football. He runs cross-country at a small Lutheran school in Minneapolis. And the rules that govern billion-dollar programs also govern him.

The Architecture of Control

Walter Byers ran the NCAA for 36 years. He coined the term “student-athlete” in the 1950s for one reason: to prevent athletes from being classified as employees eligible for workers’ compensation.

The triggering case was a football player who died during a game. His widow filed for benefits. The NCAA needed language to establish that athletes were students first, not workers.

“We crafted the term student-athlete,” Byers later wrote, “and soon it was embedded in all NCAA rules and interpretations as a mandated substitute for such words as players and athletes.”

And it worked: the athletes' labor was for the school, not the players.

But here’s what Byers wrote in 1995, after he retired: the NCAA operates on a “neoplantation belief that the enormous proceeds from games belong to the overseers (the administrators) and supervisors (coaches).”

The man who built the system called it a [neo] plantation.

Taylor Branch, the Pulitzer Prize-winning civil rights historian, extended the analysis in his 2011 Atlantic essay “The Scandal of NCAA College Sports.” He wrote that surveying college athletics is “to catch an unmistakable whiff of the plantation.” But what does this have to do with Mo Bati losing his benefits?

The Logic of Impermissible Benefits

NIL changed part of this. Since 2021, athletes have been able to sign endorsements, post sponsored content, and get paid for appearances. Commerce is now permitted.

But care is not.

If Mohammed Bati had partnered with a running store for $6,000 in sponsored content, that’s legal. If he had given a speech to the community, it would be legal. If his community had paid him $6,000 to appear at charity events, that’s legal. Or, but give him $6,000 because you don’t want him to drop out? Impermissible benefit.

The NCAA has spent decades making this distinction. In 2013, three Oklahoma football players donated $3.83 each to charity after eating too much pasta at a graduation banquet. A soccer recruit donated $9.95 after ordering internet at a hotel during her campus visit. A Stanford receiver donated $400 because his summer landlord gave him movie tickets.

Every enforcement sends the same message: you do not control what you receive. Your community, regardless of the amount or intent, cannot support you outside approved channels.

Mohammed Bati

Now, let me tell you who they punished.

Bati came to America from Ethiopia in 2018 with no formal education and no English. He enrolled at a St. Paul high school as a nontraditional student, learned the language, and learned to run. At Augsburg University, he became one of Division III’s greatest distance runners. Four consecutive conference titles. Two-time national runner-up. A 2:12 marathon that qualified him for the 2028 Olympic Trials.

He did this while working overnight shifts at an assisted living facility, 11 PM to 7 AM, five nights a week. He ran twice a day. He studied nursing. He told the Star Tribune he doesn’t sleep. He takes naps.

Augsburg’s tuition is $45,992 this year. Division III doesn’t allow athletic scholarships. Bati paid his way by working graveyard shifts and hoping the math worked out.

Last semester, the math didn’t work out, even after nights of sleeplessness working for his education. So his community helped. Friends. Teachers. People from the running world who’d watched him sacrifice everything for a spot at being the best. Gathering about $6,000. Enough to finish the semester.

“I didn’t get paid,” Bati wrote after the ruling. “I didn’t get something crazy. Just help to stay in school. And because of that, I’m not allowed to run.”

Share

What This Is Really About

Mohammed Bati’s case is small in the economy of college sports. Division III cross-country doesn’t matter to the NCAA’s bottom line.

But small cases reveal what significant cases obscure.

The NCAA didn’t punish Bati because his community’s support threatened competitive balance. They punished him because the support happened outside approved channels. Because he received something without authorization.

Larry Johnson made $40 million, and a fan still called him a slave. Mohammed Bati worked overnight shifts to afford tuition, and when his neighbors helped him stay in school, the NCAA took his season.

The amounts are different. The logic is the same. Black bodies producing value for institutions that control what those bodies can receive.

“Sometimes rules don’t see the human behind the story,” Bati wrote. “But I hope one day, things like helping someone won’t be a reason to stop them from doing what they love.”

He plans to keep training. He’ll pursue the Olympic Trials regardless. The NCAA can take his final collegiate season, but they can’t take his 2:12 marathon, his nursing degree, the community that showed up when he needed them, or his undaunted human spirit.

“I never give up,” he told the Star Tribune. “I’m not the kind of person.”

The system did what it does. Mohammed Bati is still standing.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.
author: Joshua Smith

title: Book Review: Travels with Epicurus: A Book Review
seoTitle: Book Review: Travels with Epicurus: A Book Review
excerpt: I realized I can post stuff like this here too.
metaDescription: I realized I can post stuff like this here too.
bodyHtml:
I realized I can post stuff like this here too.

I found Daniel Klein’s Travels with Epicurus in the free book section at the Charleston Public Library. Shoutout to whoever donated it. You gave away something worth more than you probably realized.

The premise is simple. Klein is 73. His dentist offers him a choice: get implants and maintain the teeth of a younger man, or get dentures and accept that he’s old. Most Americans would choose the implants you get shiny new teeth that don’t come out, you aren’t restricted to soup nor the implication of age. Klein chooses dentures. Then he flies to a Greek island to figure out why.

That decision is the whole book in miniature. What does it mean to age authentically instead of just clinging to youth until you can’t anymore?

The Setup

Klein packs a suitcase full of philosophy books and heads to Hydra, a small island in the Aegean Sea. No cars. Donkeys carry the groceries. Old men sit in tavernas drinking ouzo and talking about nothing in particular for hours.

He watches these men. They seem happy. Not productive-happy. Not optimizing-their-remaining-years happy. Just… happy. Content. Present.

And Klein starts wondering if Americans have gotten the whole “aging” thing completely wrong.

The Philosophy (But Make It Digestible)

Here’s where I expected the book to lose me. Philosophy books can be insufferable I love philosophy it’s one of my many passions, and yet you try reading Marx for more than an hour and you will realize why most philosophers have bags under their eyes. This one isn’t.

Klein walks you through Epicurus, the Stoics, Sartre, Erikson, and a bunch of other great thinkers of the modern and ancient age. But he does it the way a good bartender tells stories. Tells about mothers giving lessons to daughters, and a man drinking under an olive tree, and the spiritual experience of classical music out loud.

Epicurus, as the title suggests, gets the most attention. And his core idea is wild for how simple it is: pleasure is the highest good, but the best pleasures are the simplest ones. In fact in his garden is carved “'Stranger, here you will do well to tarry; here our highest good is pleasure.” Tarry means to linger longer than you should, and he lingered on the simple hedonistic pleasures. Friendship. Good food and drink if that’s your thing. A sunny afternoon. Not achievement. Not accumulation. Not legacy.

Klein quotes Epicurus directly: “Of all the means to a blessed life, by far the most important is the winning of friends.”

Not money. Not success. Friends.

The Greek word is ataraxia. It means tranquility. Freedom from anxiety. Epicurus thought this was the point of everything.

In the last of the pages, he is invited to a Easter feast and gives a toast "It is a great privilege to be here," which he immediately follows with the profound realization: "In fact, it is a great privilege simply to be.”

The Old Men of Hydra

The best parts of the book aren’t the philosophy. They’re Klein watching Tasso and Dimitri, two old Greek guys who spend their days sitting, talking, and watching the world go by.

They’re not trying to be useful for any other reason that enjoying each other company. They’re just existing. And they seem more at peace than any retired executive I’ve ever met although I could only count them on one hand.

Klein writes: “Dimitri and Tasso were role models for the kind of old man I wanted to become.”

There’s a scene where he joins them for ouzo. The conversation goes nowhere. Nothing gets accomplished. And Klein realizes that’s the point. The sitting is the thing. The being together is the thing.

Americans don’t know how to do this. We feel guilty if we’re not optimizing something.

Age is not linear

I’m not 73 in fact I’m a 20 year old who hasn’t even graduated college. I’m not staring down the barrel of “old age” in the way Klein is. But something about this book stuck with me anyway.

We’re all sold this story that life is about accumulation and achievement. More money. More status. More followers. More whatever. And then you die. The end.

The writer and by way the book challenges this idea by putting forth stores of Kleins youth and his decision to leave his master program and travel after seeing old men dance.

He quotes Erikson’s stages of development. The final stage is “integrity vs. despair.” You either make peace with the life you lived, or you don’t. There’s no third option.

The old men of Hydra have integrity. They’re not scrambling. They’re not pretending to have control over the eventual deterioration of their health which is described in the book as the age before the “old old age” where you return to an infant like reality. They’ve accepted what they are and they’re enjoying it.

The Always Busy Old Person

Klein writes about his friend, a retired businessman who fills every day with activities. Golf lessons. Language classes. Volunteer committees. He’s terrified of being still.

Then Klein writes this: “What exactly is he putting off by filling his days like this? That answer is obvious: he’s putting off being old.”

Should You Read It?

Yes. It’s short relatively. You can finish it in an afternoon(I finished it in a week by walking in between classes).

It won’t change your life the way a face lift would. But it’ll make you think. About what you’re chasing. About what actually makes you happy. About whether any of us know how to just sit and be.

The book is from 2012, so you can probably find it cheap. Or free, if your local library has a good donation shelf.

Charleston Public Library, thanks for the hookup. If whoever donated this is reading: you made my week.

Final Thought

There’s this idea in the book that old age is actually the best time to do philosophy, in the Hindu tradition which he talks about there are 4 stages of life and the last is where you learn the most. Known as the Sannyasa the purpose is complete detachment in pursuit of spiritual liberation (Moksha).

When you’re young, you’re too busy building your life to think about what it means. When you’re old, you finally have time to sit with the big questions.

Maybe you don’t need to wait until you’re 73 to start I know I didn’t and even I feel behind.

Klein ends with a line from Epicurus: “The wise man does not fret about the future, nor does he long for the past, but lives in the present.”

That’s free advice from a guy who died 2,300 years ago from kidney stones. Still works.

Subscribe
author: Joshua Smith

title: Big Oil Owns America. Venezuela Is Just the Latest Victim.
seoTitle: Big Oil Owns America. Venezuela Is Just the Latest Victim.
excerpt: Yes. Maduro Is a Dictator. That's Not Why We're Bombing Venezuela.
metaDescription: Yes. Maduro Is a Dictator. That's Not Why We're Bombing Venezuela.
bodyHtml:
On January 3, 2026, the United States launched airstrikes on Caracas. President Trump announced on Truth Social that Nicolás Maduro had been “captured and flown out of the Country.”

The supposed justification for bombing of military sites? Drug trafficking. The Cártel de los Soles. National security.

But here’s what Stephen Miller, Trump’s Homeland Security Advisor, said three days earlier: Venezuela’s oil belongs to the United States.

Two things can be true at once. Maduro is a bad man. He stole an election. He imprisoned dissidents. He ran a corrupt government that drove 7.7 million people out of their own country.

And US-led regime change always makes things worse.

I’ve watched this movie before. So have you. Iran. Iraq. Libya. The script doesn’t change. Only the cast.

The Pattern

Venezuela sits on the largest proven oil reserves in the world. 304 billion barrels. More than Saudi Arabia. More than anyone.

In the 1990s, Venezuela was selling 1.5 to 2 million barrels per day to the United States. American companies like Exxon, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips operated across the country. Then Hugo Chávez nationalized the industry. He kicked out the foreign companies. He used oil revenue to fund social programs.

The United States has been trying to reverse that decision ever since.

And this is non unique for the American empire, and fitting for our 250 year, happy birthday America? You get the same gift of the same stuff youve been getting.

Lets look at the countries where America has launched military interventions since World War II. Iran. Iraq. Libya. Venezuela. What do they have in common?

Oil. And leaders who tried to keep it for their own people.

Iran, 1953: The Blue Print

Mohammad Mosaddegh was the democratically elected Prime Minister of Iran. His crime was nationalizing Iranian oil, which had been controlled by the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company since 1908. That company would later become British Petroleum. BP. (Yes the oil spill people that said it wasnt that big of a deal cause the ocean is big)

The British were furious. They convinced the CIA that Mosaddegh was soft on communism. In August 1953, Operation Ajax overthrew the elected government and installed the Shah.

The aftermath? Twenty-six years of dictatorship. A revolution that created the Islamic Republic. Seventy years of hostility between Iran and the West.

All because one country wanted to keep its own oil.

The CIA formally acknowledged its role in 2013. Secretary of State Madeleine Albright called it a “setback for democratic government” in Iran. But by then, the template was set. Nationalize oil, get overthrown.

Iraq, 2003: Part 2 Electric Boogaloo

Before the invasion, Iraq’s oil industry was fully nationalized and closed to Western companies. Saddam Hussein was negotiating contracts with China, Russia, and France. American and British oil companies were shut out.

Bush’s Treasury Secretary Paul O’Neill later revealed that the first two National Security Council meetings of the administration discussed invading Iraq. He was given briefing materials titled “Plan for post-Saddam Iraq” that envisioned dividing up Iraq’s oil wealth. A Pentagon document dated March 5, 2001 was titled “Foreign Suitors for Iraqi Oilfield Contracts.”

This was before September 11.

General John Abizaid, former head of US Central Command, admitted in 2007: “Of course it’s about oil; we can’t really deny that.”

The war cost at least 200,000 Iraqi civilian lives. Some estimates put the total death toll, including indirect deaths from the destruction of healthcare and infrastructure, at over 4.5 million.

Iraq’s oil is no longer nationalized.

Libya, 2011: The Threequel

Muammar Gaddafi nationalized Libyan oil in the 1970s after seizing power. He used the revenue to build Africa’s highest Human Development Index. Free healthcare. Free education. A literacy rate of 88 percent.

He was also a brutal dictator who funded terrorism and murdered his opponents. Both things are true.

When the Arab Spring reached Libya, NATO intervened. So under the guise of protecting civilians Gaddafi was captured and killed by rebels in October 2011.

What followed? A decade of civil war. ISIS establishing its largest base outside Syria and Iraq. A country divided between rival governments and warlords.

A French opposition newspaper published a secret contract between Libya’s new transitional government and France guaranteeing 35% of Libya’s oil contracts to French companies.

If you didnt catch that the intervention that NATO said wasn’t about oil ended with Western companies getting Libya’s oil.

Show me the Money

The fossil fuel industry spent $219 million to influence the 2024 US election. According to OpenSecrets, 88% of oil and gas money went to Republicans infact more than 70% goes to republicans every election cycle since 96’. Donald Trump received nearly $23 million from the industry for his campaign and supporting PACs.

Oil companies spend over $100 million each year lobbying politicians incuding Koch at 7 Million, OP at 7M, Exxon at 6.5M and Chevron at 6.3M. In the 2024 cycle, oil and gas companies spent nearly $60 million lobbying Republican Party members alone.

When you follow the money and look at the history and who says “coal is beautiful” to children talking to santa and who ran on “drill baby drill” you find out why. telling

When Trump asked oil executives in 2024 to raise $1 billion for his campaign, he told them it would be a “deal” given the taxes and regulations they’d avoid under his presidency. They responded by making fossil fuel interests one of his top corporate backers.

Now he’s ordering military strikes on the country with the world’s largest oil reserves. And his administration is openly claiming that Venezuela’s oil “belongs” to the United States.

The Venezuela Playbook

The escalation followed a familiar sequence.

First, economic warfare. Sanctions began under Obama, expanded under Trump’s first term, and continued under Biden. These measures, targeting Venezuela’s oil sector and key state officials, not only isolated the nation financially but also severely restricted its access to crucial international markets and investment. Consequently. The result: Venezuela’s oil exports collapsed from $90 billion annually to $2.3 billion. Production fell from 3.6 million barrels per day to under a million.

A 2021 US Government Accountability Office report concluded that sanctions “likely contributed to Venezuela’s economic decline.”

The Financial Times described them as devastating to ordinary Venezuelans.

Second, the drug trafficking narrative. In March 2020, the Department of Justice indicted Maduro for narco-terrorism. The State Department designated the Cartel de los Soles as a foreign terrorist organization. This designation, officials acknowledged, enabled “new options” for military action; see where I’m going with this.

Third, the military buildup. Starting in August 2025, the US deployed the largest military force in the Caribbean in decades. Dubbing it “"Operation Southern Spear” The USS Gerald R. Ford. F-35s to Puerto Rico. Over 15,000 personnel. A naval blockade of oil tankers.

Fourth, the strikes. Since September 2025, the US has killed over 110 people in airstrikes on boats the administration claims were trafficking drugs. No evidence has been made public. The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights warned that the strikes violate international human rights law. Probably because the administration did a second strike on people hanging on to pieces of their already recked boat.

Now the bombs are falling on Caracas.

The Human Cost

Before the current intervention, 7.7 million Venezuelans had already fled their country.

Inside Venezuela, 14.2 million people need humanitarian aid. The minimum wage is $3.60 per month partly due to sanctions. Ninety percent of the population experiences water shortages.

Now we’re adding airstrikes.

We've been here before. Iraq proved it: you can't bomb your way to stability. The intervention creates the insurgency. The insurgency creates the civil war. The civil war creates the refugee crisis and with escalating humanitarian costs to keep it simple its expensive to cover up a murder.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

The Lie We Keep Telling

Every time, the justification changes. Communism in Iran. Weapons of mass destruction in Iraq. Humanitarian intervention in Libya. Drug trafficking in Venezuela.

Nobody is watching Green Book now(click to read my other essay). Nobody will remember the drug trafficking narrative in ten years. What they’ll remember is that the United States attacked another oil-rich country that refused to let American companies control its resources.

Maduro is a dictator. He should face justice for his crimes against the Venezuelan people as Fannie Lou Hamer says "Nobody's free until everybody's free,". I want all my people free. But that’s not what this is about. If America only attacked dictators, we’d have a much longer list and wouldn’t be gifting a bald eagle statue to Putin.

We attack the ones sitting on oil and that cannot be won over with gifts like Kim John Un and a autographed CD of Elton John.

Why are they doing this now?

Stephen Miller already told you silly. He believes Venezuela’s oil belongs to America. Not because of any legal claim. Not because of any historical right. But because American companies used to extract it, and American consumers used to burn it, and somewhere along the line, that became the same as ownership.

I don’t know how this ends. But I’ve seen enough of these movies to know the sequel is always worse than the original.

What You Can Do

Call your representatives. The Senate voted against requiring Congressional approval for military action against Venezuela. Twice. But the pressure needs to continue.

Donate to humanitarian organizations working with Venezuelan refugees. UNHCR. International Rescue Committee. The people fleeing this crisis need help now, not after the bombs stop.

And stop believing the narrative. When they tell you it’s about drugs, remember: they told us Iraq had weapons of mass destruction. When they tell you it’s about democracy, remember: we overthrew democracy in Iran to protect oil profits.

Follow the oil. It’s always about the oil.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share

author: Joshua Smith

title: Sprinkles Cupcakes; The Plague of Private Equity and Sympathy
seoTitle: Sprinkles Cupcakes; The Plague of Private Equity and Sympathy
excerpt: How private equity destroys businesses and why founders who knowingly sell don't get to play victim
metaDescription: How private equity destroys businesses and why founders who knowingly sell don't get to play victim
bodyHtml:
On December 31, 2025, Candace Nelson posted a video to TikTok.

She looked directly into the camera, voice soft with emotion, and announced that Sprinkles Cupcakes was closing. All locations. Effective immediately. She called it “surreal.” She said she thought it would be her legacy. She asked fans to share their favorite Sprinkles memories in the comments.

The comments did not go the way she planned.

“If you wanted a legacy, private equity was a bad choice.” 103,000 likes.

“You betrayed that legacy by selling to private equity, doesn’t make much sense to mourn it now.” 21,800 likes.

“You’re a former investment banker. You knew exactly what you were doing.” 6,000 likes.

One former employee wrote that after 18 years working there, they got one day’s notice. No severance. No benefits. Just a check for work already done. Nelson replied: “Devastating. I’m incredibly sorry.”

9,491 comments. Most of them some version of: what did you think was going to happen?

The Investment Banker Who Sold Her Soul (and Her Cupcakes)

Here’s what you need to know about Candace Nelson.

Before cupcakes, she worked in investment banking in San Francisco. She rode the dot-com boom. Had the prestigious job, the expense account, the whole thing. Then the bubble burst in 2001, she got laid off, and apparently 9/11 gave her a “personal revelation.” She went to pastry school. Opened a bakery.

That part of the story is actually cool. Career pivots are hard. She took a risk. It worked.

In 2005, Nelson opened the first Sprinkles in Beverly Hills. 600 square feet. Just cupcakes. People thought she was crazy. A whole bakery for one product? But she was early to something real. Sprinkles basically invented the gourmet cupcake trend. Lines out the door. Celebrity fans. Oprah showed up. They expanded nationwide.

In 2012, Sprinkles introduced the Cupcake ATM. A vending machine that dispensed fresh cupcakes 24/7. Kids loved it. It was weird and fun and felt reminiscent of the future. Who doesn’t want a cupcake at 2 am? I’ve had my fair share. A sprinkle of delight in an otherwise destitute strip mall.

Also in 2012, Nelson sold the company to KarpReilly, a private equity firm based in Greenwich, Connecticut.

That’s the part her TikTok glosses over.

She didn’t just “start Sprinkles and then it closed.” She started Sprinkles, built it into something people loved, sold it to private equity for what reportedly helped build her current net worth of $10-20 million, walked away, and then acted surprised when the new owners did exactly what private equity always does.

Nelson is now a “serial entrepreneur,” angel investor, guest shark on Shark Tank, bestselling author, and keynote speaker who gets booked at Goldman Sachs and Taco Bell corporate events. She’s doing fine.

The employees who got one day’s notice on New Year’s Eve? They’re not doing fine.

What Private Equity Actually Does

Allow me explain the private equity playbook because it keeps showing up like a bad movie sequel nobody asked for Im looking at you “Son of the Mask”.

Step one: PE firm buys a company mainly using borrowed money. The debt doesn’t go on the PE firm’s books. It goes on the company’s books. The company they just bought now owes the money used to buy it. This is called a leveraged buyout and yes it’s as dumb as it sounds.

Step two: Cut costs. Fire workers. Reduce quality. Close locations. Sell off real estate and lease it back. Every dollar saved goes toward servicing that debt or paying the PE firm’s fees.

Step three: Extract value. The PE firm pays itself dividends, management fees, “consulting fees,” whatever they can bill. They pull money out of the company they supposedly invested in.

Step four: Exit. Either sell the hollowed-out company to someone else, take it public, or let it collapse into bankruptcy. By then the PE firm has already made its money back plus profit. The debt stays with the corpse.

Brendan Ballou, author of Plunder: Private Equity’s Plan to Pillage America, told CNBC: “Companies that are owned by private equity firms are significantly more likely to go bankrupt than those that aren’t. There’s a lot of incentives for the private equity firm to take short-term extractive strategies towards restaurants that oftentimes can lead to their harm, or, in extreme cases, to their destruction.”

The data backs this up. According to the Private Equity Stakeholder Project, PE firms were involved in 70% of the largest U.S. corporate bankruptcies in 2024. Moody’s reports that PE-backed companies default at twice the rate of non-PE-backed companies.

In the first quarter of 2025 alone, 7 out of 10 bankruptcies at companies with over $1 billion in liabilities were private equity owned. That’s 70% of the biggest failures coming from 6.5% of the economy (the share of US GDP produced by PE).

The list of PE casualties reads like a tour of places you used to go: Red Lobster. TGI Fridays. Party City. Big Lots. Joann Fabrics. 99 Cents Only Stores. Claire’s (they went bankrupt twice). Forever 21. And now Sprinkles.

The Comment Section Knew

What made the Sprinkles situation go viral wasn’t the closure. Businesses close all the time. It was the audacity of the founder’s framing.

Nelson’s video presented herself as a victim of circumstances. As someone blindsided by events beyond her control. “I learned a few days ago” and “this isn’t how I thought the story would go” and “I thought it would be my legacy.”

The internet wasn’t having it.

One comment with 57,300 likes showed a confused dog meme with the caption: “’It was gonna be my legacy’ and ‘I sold out to private equity.’” The contradiction was the whole joke.

Another: “Surreal? Its what private equity does. They destroy businesses.” 8,655 likes.

Another: “At least you got your money, right? Who cares about all the employees so long as you got your millions of dollars. Private equity only destroys.” 6,216 likes.

A former employee who did training at Sprinkles in Disney Springs in 2020 wrote: “It’s so weird how much they talk about Candace Nelson like she still owns and runs it when she’s sold in 2014.”

(The dates vary in reports between 2012 and 2014. Point stands either way. She’s been gone over a decade.)

The brutal honesty of these comments reflects something shifting in public consciousness. People understand the PE playbook now. They’ve watched it destroy too many things they cared about. They’re not buying the “I had no idea this would happen” narrative from someone who used to work in finance.

You don’t get to cash out and then cosplay as a mourner at the funeral.

Sprinkles Was a Third Space (Sort Of)

Here’s what actually makes this story worth writing about beyond the schadenfreude(the concept of getting pleasure from others misforture or comeuppance) watching a comment section roast a millionaire.

Sprinkles was third-place-ish.

Sociologist Ray Oldenburg coined that term in 1989. Third Space are the social spaces that aren’t home (first place) or work (second place). The coffee shop where you see regulars or are one. The barbershop where coversations of lebron vs jordan trail on so much your 20 mintue haircut is an hour. The park bench where strangers become neighbors.

Oldenburg identified their characteristics: neutral ground, accessible, don’t cost much, conversation is the main activity, regulars set the tone, playful mood, feels like home away from home. He called them “the living room of society.”

Sprinkles wasn’t a classic third space like a neighborhood bar. But those cupcake ATMs created something. Kids dragged their parents there. Families made it a tradition. People formed memories around a pink machine that dispensed frosted sugar. It was weird and fun and became part of people’s routines.

That’s worth something. Maybe not to a PE firm calculating returns per square foot. But to the humans who built their lives around these small seemly inconciquencial rituals.

When private equity guts a business like Sprinkles, they’re not just eliminating jobs and closing storefronts. They’re removing gathering points from the social fabric. One more place people used to go, gone.

America Is Running Out of Places to Go

The Sprinkles closure fits a bigger pattern.

According to research from the National Establishment Time-Series, almost all categories of third space declined after 2011 and 2012. That’s the turning point. Commercial establishments and privately(family)-run organizations got hit hardest.

Bowling centers dropped 32% from 2005 to 2023. From 2014 to 2019, time Americans spent with friends fell 37%. During the pandemic, social interaction among teens dropped 70% compared to two decades earlier.

The Urban Institute put it plainly: “The decline of third places in recent years has also coincided with rising loneliness in US cities and a weakened sense of well-being in communities.”

U.S. Surgeon General Vivek Murthy declared loneliness a public health epidemic in 2023, comparing its health effects to smoking 15 cigarettes daily which is about as much as a heavy smoker and doubles your risk of cadiovascular disease.

There’s a direct line from “we closed all the places people used to gather” to “everyone is lonely and nobody knows why.”

The Employees Got One Day’s Notice

Let’s go back to the comments on Nelson’s video.

One person claiming to be an 18-year employee wrote: “After 18 years working there, they notified us one day in advance, without any benefits, only with a check for the work already done. So sad good memories. Happy new year for me.”

Nelson replied: “Devastating. I’m incredibly sorry.”

Another commenter: “Some of us don’t have backup plans we loved sprinkles and planned to be here forever.”

Nelson replied: “I’m so sorry this is heartbreaking.”

Sorry doesn’t pay rent. Sorry doesn’t replace the health insurance that vanished on New Year’s Eve. Sorry doesn’t give back the 18 years someone invested in a company that private equity dismantled for parts.

A Sprinkles spokesperson told KTLA: “After thoughtful consideration, we’ve made the very difficult decision to transition away from operating company-owned Sprinkles bakeries.”

Notice the passive voice. “We’ve made the decision.” Not “KarpReilly decided to shut it down.” Not “The private equity firm that bought this company 13 years ago extracted what they could and now they’re done.” Just “we’ve made the very difficult decision” like it was an act of nature rather than a business choice.

The Business of Banking

I keep coming back to one detail: Nelson was an investment banker.

She knows how finance works. She understood what selling to private equity meant. She chose the payday anyway.

That’s her right. Nobody forced her to run Sprinkles forever. Founders sell companies all the time. Sometimes they have good reasons.

But you forfeit the right to public mourning when you take the check.

Nelson didn’t just sell. She’s spent the past decade building a media presence, partly based on the Sprinkles story. The cookbook. The speaking gigs. The TV appearances. She monetized the legacy while someone else hollowed out the company.

When that company finally collapsed, she posted a video asking for memories. As if the community she’d sold could give her something back.

The comment section’s response was basically: you already got what you wanted. You got paid. Now you want sympathy too?

I’m good.

The Bigger Question

Private equity will keep doing this. They’ll buy your favorite restaurant, your childhood store, your mom and pop shop and your local gym. They’ll load it with debt, cut costs, extract fees, and let it die. Then they’ll move on to the next target.

The question is whether we’re going to keep acting surprised.

Store closures doubled in 2025 compared to 2024. The PE bankruptcy rate keeps climbing. The loneliness epidemic gets worse every year. Third places keep disappearing. These facts are connected.

Every time we lose a place where people gathered, we lose more than a business. We lose a node in the social network.

Sprinkles closing doesn’t matter because cupcakes are important(although they are). It matters because it’s one more data point in the systematic destruction of American community.

Candace Nelson will be fine. She’ll keep giving keynotes about entrepreneurship and branding and passion. She’ll probably write something thoughtful about “saying goodbye” to her creation. The media will quote her as a founder reflecting on loss.

The employees who got one day’s notice will figure something out. They always do. Nobody will ask them for quotes.

And somewhere, a private equity firm in Greenwich, Connecticut is looking at its next acquisition.

Thanks for reading Joshua's Substack! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share

What You Can Do

Shop local when you can. The independent coffee shop. The family-owned restaurant. The bookstore that’s been around for 40 years. These places are inefficient by design. They are made specifically to let you linger and ask the owner why they love coffee so much that they only make a small profit. They build community. Private equity doesn’t want them because the margins are thin.

That’s precisely why they matter.

Pay attention to who owns what and make sure that owners are supported and know they don’t have to sell. When a chain you like gets acquired by a PE firm, the clock starts ticking. You’ll see the quality decline in both care and product, the cost-cutting, and the hollowing out. Eventually, the closure announcement. Knowing this in advance won’t save the business, but it helps you understand what’s happening and why.

Support policy changes that limit PE extraction. Caps on debt loading. Restrictions on dividend recapitalizations. Requirements to maintain employment levels.

And when a founder who sold to private equity posts a tearful video asking for sympathy, you’re allowed to say no.

What do you think? Have you watched a place you loved get gutted by private equity? Reply to this email. I read everything.

Leave a comment
author: Joshua Smith

title: The Problem With the 2019 Oscars: How Green Book Beat BlacKkKlansman and What That Has to Do With The Blind Side
seoTitle: The Problem With the 2019 Oscars: How Green Book Beat BlacKkKlansman and What That Has to Do With The Blind Side
excerpt: Hollywood keeps making the same movie. And the Academy keeps rewarding it.
metaDescription: Hollywood keeps making the same movie. And the Academy keeps rewarding it.
bodyHtml:
On February 24, 2019, Julia Roberts opened an envelope and announced that Green Book had won Best Picture. Spike Lee waved his arms in disgust and tried to leave the Dolby Theatre. Security stopped him at the doors. When he returned to his seat, he turned his back to the stage.

Backstage, a reporter asked Lee about his reaction. He held up a glass of champagne and said “This is my sixth glass, and you know why. I’m snakebit. Every time somebody is driving somebody, I lose.”

He was talking about 1990. That year, Driving Miss Daisy won Best Picture. His film, Do the Right Thing, wasn’t even nominated.

History repeated itself. And it will keep repeating until we understand why.

The Pattern Nobody Wants to Name

Green Book and The Blind Side are the same movie.

I don’t mean conceptually. I mean structurally. A white protagonist enters the life of a Black person. The white person learns something. The Black person gets saved. The audience feels good. The credits roll. The Academy votes.

The pattern has a name: the white savior trope. Sociologist Matthew Hughey, author of “The White Savior Film,” defines it this way: a white character rescues a Black or nonwhite character from circumstances like poverty or overt racism. The message is that proximity to whiteness solves Black problems.

These films win Oscars. The Blind Side earned Sandra Bullock her first Academy Award. Green Book won three, including Best Picture. The Help received four nominations. Driving Miss Daisy won four Oscars, including Best Picture.

Spike Lee’s Do the Right Thing? Zero.

What Green Book Actually Did

Green Book tells the story of Don Shirley, a Black classical pianist, and Tony Vallelonga, an Italian-American bouncer hired as his driver for a concert tour through the Jim Crow South in 1962. The film is named after The Negro Motorist Green Book, a real guide created by Victor Hugo Green in 1936 that listed safe establishments for Black travelers.

Here is the problem: the film is not about Victor Hugo Green. It is not about the Green Book. It is about a racist white guy who becomes slightly less racist by the end.

Brooke Obie, writing for Shadow and Act, accused the film of erasing the very object it was named after. The Root said it “spoon-feeds racism to white people.” The New York Times wrote that the film has “very little that can’t be described as crude, obvious and borderline offensive.” IndieWire labeled Shirley’s character a “Magical Negro” whose sole purpose was to change a white man for the better.

The critics were right.

The Family Speaks

Don Shirley’s family did not mince words.

Maurice Shirley, Don’s only living brother, called the film “a symphony of lies.” He disputed the central premise that Shirley was estranged from his family and the Black community. At the time depicted in the film, Shirley had three living brothers with whom he was always in contact.

Edwin Shirley III, Don’s nephew, told Shadow and Act that the depiction of his uncle being uncomfortable with his Blackness was “just 100% wrong.” He pointed out that Shirley had marched at Selma. He was friends with Martin Luther King Jr., Duke Ellington, and Nina Simone.

Karole Shirley Kimble, Don’s niece, told StudyBreaks: “The thing that bothers our family is that the focus of the film is about a white man who is an extreme racist and who was still a racist at the end.”

The family was never consulted during production. Director Peter Farrelly later told Variety that he didn’t think their input would have changed the movie. “It’s not about him and his family. It really isn’t.”

Read that sentence again.

A film about a Black man’s life is not about his family, with whom the movie makes him estranged. That’s the whole problem in one quote.

The Fried Chicken Scene

The changes from real life to the movie ultimately come down to one point. There is a scene where Vallelonga teaches Shirley how to eat fried chicken.

In the film, Shirley claims he has never eaten fried chicken. Vallelonga, shocked, insists that Black people love fried chicken. He forces Shirley to try it. Shirley eventually enjoys it. They throw the bones out the window. Feel-good music plays.

Maurice Shirley told the radio show 1A that his brother wasn’t introduced to fried chicken by Vallelonga. He said, “No one, EVER, had to teach my brother how to eat fried chicken. Nor would he have allowed ‘lessons’ of such by a white man (given stereotypes).”

Yet they turned a racist stereotype into a heartwarming bonding moment by, if it wasn't clear, making a black man eat fried chicken. They invented it. Then they built a movie around it.

Who Made This Film

Peter Farrelly directed Green Book.

His previous credits include Dumb and Dumber, There’s Something About Mary, Shallow Hal, and Movie 43. He built his career on gross-out comedies. He is not known for nuanced explorations of race in America.

In 1998, Newsweek reported that Farrelly “liked to use ruses to get people to look at Farrelly’s penis” as a joke on set. Those who had been tricked included actress Cameron Diaz and film executive Tom Rothman. When the story resurfaced in 2019, Farrelly apologized.

Nick Vallelonga, Tony’s son, co-wrote the screenplay. In 2015, he tweeted support for Donald Trump’s debunked claim that Muslims cheered during the September 11 attacks.

Mahershala Ali, who won Best Supporting Actor for playing Shirley, called the family to apologize. He told them, “If I have offended you, I am so, so terribly sorry. I did the best I could with the material I had. I was not aware that there were close relatives with whom I could have consulted to add some nuance to the character.”

Ali did the best he could. The material was the problem.

The Real Green Book Story

Victor Hugo Green was a postal worker in Harlem. In 1936, he saw a problem: Black travelers in America faced humiliation, violence, and death on the road. Sundown towns banned Black people after dark. Hotels refused service. Gas stations turned customers away.

Green created The Negro Motorist Green Book. He used his network of fellow postal workers to compile information on safe establishments. The guide listed hotels, restaurants, gas stations, and beauty parlors that would serve Black customers.

Hollywood chose not to tell that story. They told a story about a racist white guy who drives a Black man around instead.

What BlacKkKlansman Did Differently

Spike Lee’s BlacKkKlansman tells the true story of Ron Stallworth, the first Black detective in the Colorado Springs Police Department. In the early 1970s, Stallworth infiltrated the Ku Klux Klan by phone while a white colleague attended meetings in person.

The film does not shy away from complexity. Stallworth is a cop working within a racist system to fight racists outside it. The film asks uncomfortable questions about complicity, assimilation, and the limits of individual action.

BlacKkKlansman ends with footage from the 2017 Unite the Right rally in Charlottesville. Heather Heyer’s murder. Donald Trump’s “very fine people on both sides” comment. The film connects its 1970s story to the present moment.

Green Book ends with Vallelonga inviting Shirley to Christmas dinner. Everyone smiles. The audience leaves feeling good.

One film challenges its audience. The other comforts them.

The Academy chose comfort.

Why This Keeps Happening

White savior films are popular because they serve a psychological function. Matthew Hughey explains: “White savior films are particularly attractive or even seductive for a lot of white people who want to think of themselves as a good white person yet not really have their own whiteness or the racial order interrogated too deeply.”

The Blind Side did the same thing. A wealthy white family takes in a homeless Black teenager. They give him a home, education, and a football career. The white family becomes heroes. The Black teenager becomes a prop in their story.

Michael Oher, the real person depicted in The Blind Side, has been vocal about his discomfort with the film. He told ESPN in 2015: “People look at me and they take things away from me because of a movie. They don’t really see the skills and the kind of player I am.”

In 2023, Oher filed a petition alleging the Tuohy family never legally adopted him and instead tricked him into a conservatorship. The feel-good story collapsed. The saviors, it turned out, may not have been saviors at all.

The Real Consequence

White savior films do not just distort history. They shape how audiences understand race.

Hughey told NBC News: “It’s a common theme in which the Black person being saved has to be almost rescued from Blackness itself. The message is, ‘The closer to whiteness you become, the more your problems are solved.’”

These films flatten complex people into simple archetypes. Don Shirley becomes a lonely genius who needs a white friend to teach him how to be human; he even eats fried chicken with his fingertips. Michael Oher becomes a gentle giant who needed a white mother to teach him discipline. The Help turns civil rights history into a story about a white journalist who permits Black maids to speak.

The pattern reduces systemic racism to individual prejudice. If the problem is one racist white guy, then the solution is for that guy to become less racist. No structural change required. No policy reform necessary. Just personal growth.

That is not how social change happens.

What Spike Lee Said

At the 2019 Oscars press room, Lee told reporters: “Whether this film won Best Picture or not, we’re on the right side of history.”

The Test

Here is how to identify a white savior film. Ask three questions:

  1. Whose story is this? If a film claims to be about a Black person but centers a white character’s emotional journey, it fails.

  2. Who has agency? If the Black character exists primarily to help the white character grow, it fails.

  3. What does the audience learn? If the takeaway is “racism was(not is) bad but some white people were good,” it fails.

Green Book fails all three. So does The Blind Side. So does Driving Miss Daisy.

Where We Go From Here

The problem is not that Green Book exists. The problem is that it won Best Picture while BlacKkKlansman lost. The problem is that Hollywood keeps making these films, and the Academy keeps rewarding them.

Victor Hugo Green deserves a real movie. Don Shirley deserves a film that treats him as the protagonist of his own life. Black stories deserve to be told by Black filmmakers who understand them.

Until that happens, we’ll keep watching the Academy hand trophies to films where white people drive [with or driven by] Black people around and learn valuable lessons along the way.

Spike Lee will keep losing. And he’ll keep being right.

title: The Algorithm Won't Save Us (But Maybe We Can)
seoTitle: The Algorithm Won't Save Us (But Maybe We Can)
excerpt: We’re living in an era where AI writes poetry, markets crash on a tweet, and everyone has an opinion, but nobody has a philosophy.
metaDescription: We’re living in an era where AI writes poetry, markets crash on a tweet, and everyone has an opinion, but nobody has a philosophy.
bodyHtml:
I’m Josh, a computer scientist by training, photographer by passion, and chronic overthinker by nature, but I prefer the term modern philosopher. I’ve spent the last few years building tech systems at startups, traveling and photographing the world and its people, and, for a few years before that, loudly questioning everything I’ve been told about the status quo, technology, and the “right” way to think.

Why This Newsletter Exists

Because I'm tired of two things: tech bros who think AI will solve inequality while ignoring the humans building it, and a culture that mistakes loudness for insight, to the point that we make golden calves of the loudest podcaster and silence the scientist. I want a space to think out loud, about the ethics of the tools we're building, the systems we're building them in and around, and most importantly, the art and philosophy we've stopped making time for.

What to expect here:

  • AI for Good (and its contradictions): Real talk about technology’s promise, where it’s failing us, and the implications for the future of tech.

  • Political & economic criticism: In an era of misinformation, I would like to make a cited critique analysis of systems, incentives, and who benefits.

  • Philosophy & art: The stuff that makes us human when the algorithm can’t, as John Keating says, “poetry, beauty, romance, and love are what we stay alive for.”

  • Personal essays: Often unfiltered and hastily written essays surrounding current events, policy changes, and pop culture.

I'll be posting weekly, sometimes more, when the news cycle picks up or Doctor Who returns. Free subscribers get everything. If I ever go paid, it'll be for bonus deep dives, not paywalled essentials.

This space is for you if:

You’re skeptical of easy answers even when they are comfortable. You want to think critically without being cynical. You believe technology was designed for humans to create art, not the other way around.

(me in my favorite NPR(National Public Radio)

Drop your questions below, and I may answer them in an upcoming article with my thoughts!

Leave a comment
